Anabelian Perspectives in GT Theory Collas reviews the interface between braid/mapping-class groups, Galois-Teichmuller theory, moduli spaces of curves, Nakamura-style anabelian geometry, and combinatorial reconstruction of G_Q. Use to explain why GT and IUT are both discussed through algorithmic, group-theoretic reconstruction, while keeping their technical claims separate.
Anabelian Geometry for Deligne-Mumford Curves Collas, Philip, and Yamaguchi develop reconstruction results for Deligne-Mumford curves, including stack inertia, rigidification, coarsification, and low-step solvable quotient detection. Use as current background for orbicurve and stack-inertia themes relevant to anabelian formalization; do not read it as an IUT 3.12 proof input.

Algorithmic reconstruction

The common thread is not just group comparison, but reconstruction by explicit procedures. This matches the species/mutation emphasis in IUT IV and Mochizuki's formalization report.

Container caution

Collas' GT paper explicitly discusses group-theoretic containers. In this wiki, such containers are orientation tools unless the IUT source supplies the exact comparison and numerical bridge.

Formalization relevance

The Deligne-Mumford curve paper explains why stack inertia and low-step quotient information matter. This is adjacent to LANA's anabelian-library work before direct IUT formalization.

Sources: CMY 2025 Collas GT 2026 CPY 2026 Related: Collas category view anabelian background LANA species and mutations

GT Species Perspective

Mochizuki's October 2025 NGR talk notes, joint work in progress with Tsujimura, compare three Teichmuller-type situations: classical complex Teichmuller theory, IUT, and Grothendieck-Teichmuller theory. For this wiki the value is diagnostic: it explains IUT's multiradial representation as an arithmetic common-container analogue, while keeping the later GT statements separate and explicitly marked as expected where the source does so.

Three common-container patterns complex Teichmuller, IUT, and GT
Source sectionConceptWiki reading
§1 Two holomorphic structures on the same topological surface embed into the common sheaf of complex-valued real analytic functions. This is the classical model for a common container that is larger than either holomorphic structure.
§2 The N-th power map gives a multiplicative monoid isomorphism that is not compatible with addition and hence not a ring homomorphism. Mochizuki uses this as an elementary analogue for theta-link-type gluing: common multiplicative/coric data does not identify ring histories.
§2 IUT's multiradial representation is described as a common container for distinct arithmetic holomorphic structures, built using anabelian geometry, logarithms, theta-function Galois evaluation, and Kummer theory. This reinforces the wiki rule that multiradial comparison is not a direct ring isomorphism between the two sides of a theta link.
§3 The expected Theorem A states faithfulness of the natural outer action of GT on a genus-zero quotient of the geometric fundamental group of the tripod. This is a GT-side anabelian analogue, not an established input to the four IUT papers.
§4 The expected FICSP/COF route characterizes G_Q inside GT by synchronization of decomposition groups; the p-adic variant is stated as GT_p = G_Qp. The page records this as current adjacent research into anabelian/GT rigidity, with the source's expected-status caveat intact.
Varying Hues §3-4 Combinatorial algebraization theory uses idempotents in function algebras, Chebotarev/Hilbertian inputs, and combinatorial anabelian geometry to compare conjugates of arithmetic groups inside GT or Out(Pi_X). This extends the common-container pattern from IUT toward a GT-side Teichmuller theory; it is recorded as joint work in progress in the source.

Complex common container

Section 1 reviews the classical pattern O_H1 subset O_R superset O_H2: two holomorphic structures on one topological surface are compared inside a larger real analytic sheaf.

§1Teichmuller

N-th power gluing model

Section 2 uses the N-th power map on a multiplicative monoid as a simple non-ring-theoretic gluing. It is compatible with multiplicative data and group action, but not with addition.

§2monoid gluing

IUT multiradial container

The IUT paragraph identifies common multiplicative monoid and abstract group structures as the logical-AND input for the multiradial representation of the theta-pilot, with ring structures remaining distinct.

IUT §2multiradial

GT genus-zero quotient

The expected GT theorem concerns the action of GT on the genus-zero quotient Pi_X^0 attached to the tripod. The paper treats this through combinatorial anabelian geometry.

§3expected

CAT idempotent function theory

Varying Hues §3 states an idempotent lemma for subalgebras of function spaces on finite etale covers. After restricting to an infinite active subset, the nonzero idempotents form a maximal orthogonal family with transitive action by the cover's Galois group.

Varying Hues §3CAT

CAT miraculous rotation

Varying Hues §4 compares CAT with IUT's theta-link-to-log-link rotation: a mysterious gap between distinct ring theories attached to GT conjugates is rotated to a harmless gap between conjugates of finite characteristic quotients.

Varying Hues §4GT analogy

FICSP synchronization

The FICSP/COF condition asks whether restrictions of GT-decomposition groups to conjugates of an arithmetic Galois group are synchronized. Under this condition, the expected result characterizes G_Q inside GT.

§4decomposition groups

p-adic GT analogy

The notes state an expected p-adic analogue GT_p = G_Qp and a tempered absolute anabelian result for graphic cuspidalizable outer automorphisms. These are recorded as adjacent anabelian research, not as wiki prerequisites for IUT I-IV.

§4p-adic
Status caveat: the GT-side Theorem A, Theorem B, and Corollaries C-D are labelled "Expected" in the source. The wiki therefore treats this page as an explanatory and research-context bridge, not as a settled theorem page.
Sources: GT species paper Varying Hues Related: anabelian background species and mutations logical structure multiradiality

GT Common Containers

Mochizuki's 2025 GT notes compare three common-container patterns. Classical complex Teichmuller theory embeds two holomorphic structures into a real analytic sheaf. IUT builds a multiradial common container for theta-linked arithmetic holomorphic structures. The GT direction seeks function-space containers for distinct conjugates of G_Q inside GT.

Three container patterns Mochizuki-Tsujimura NGR2025 route
CaseContainerWarning
Complex Teichmuller Real analytic functions contain the two holomorphic sheaves. The container is larger than either holomorphic structure.
IUT Multiradial representation of the theta-pilot contains data from distinct arithmetic holomorphic structures. The source says the ring structures remain distinct.
GT Function spaces are recovered from decomposition groups and genus-zero data. The GT theorems in the note are marked expected.
Sources: GT species paper §§1-4 Related: GT species perspective Collas category view multiradiality

GT N-Power IUT Analogy

Section 2 of the GT notes gives an elementary arithmetic analogue for the theta-link situation. On the multiplicative monoid of an integral domain, the N-th power map is an isomorphism onto its image when viewed multiplicatively, but it is not compatible with addition and does not arise from a ring homomorphism. This is the source's simple model for non-ring-theoretic gluing.

Compatible with multiplication, not with addition the elementary model in GT species §2
Formalization warning. This example is useful exactly because the multiplicative comparison fails to be a ring map. A formal model that silently upgrades it to ring equality loses the source point.
Sources: GT species paper §2 Related: theta/Frobenius pictures theta links Collas GMS categorification

GT FICSP and Decomposition Groups

Sections 3 and 4 of the GT notes move from the IUT analogy to Grothendieck-Teichmuller theory. The expected Theorem A concerns faithfulness of the natural outer action of GT on a genus-zero quotient of the tripod fundamental group. The expected Theorem B uses a decomposition-group synchronization condition, FICSP/COF, to characterize G_Q inside GT.

Expected GT route genus-zero quotients, decomposition groups, synchronization
Source itemStatus in notesWiki handling
Theorem A Marked expected. Record as current GT research context, not as an IUT dependency.
FICSP/COF Condition used for Theorem B. Useful as a synchronization analogue for readers, but separate from 3.12.
Corollaries C-D Marked expected and p-adic/tempered. Adjacent anabelian research, not part of the four-paper proof spine.
Status caveat. This page preserves the source's expected labels. It should not be cited as a settled theorem page in the wiki.
Sources: GT species paper §§3-4 Related: GT species perspective anabelian background Collas current anabelian work

GT Genus-Zero Quotient

In the 2025 GT species notes, Mochizuki defines a genus-zero quotient Pi_X^0 of the geometric fundamental group of a genus-zero hyperbolic curve. The expected Theorem A says that, for the tripod over Q, the natural action GT -> Out(Pi_X^0) is faithful.

Genus-zero quotient route GT species §3
Source objectMeaningWiki handling
Pi_X^0 Quotient of Pi_X by the intersection of open subgroups corresponding to genus-zero finite etale covers. Keep separate from the full geometric fundamental group.
Out_C(Pi_X) Outer automorphisms preserving cuspidal inertia subgroups. Cuspidal data remains part of the typed GT object.
Expected Theorem A Faithfulness of GT -> Out(Pi_X^0). Record as expected/work-in-progress context, not a settled source theorem.
Source: GT species paper §3 Related: GT FICSP/decomposition Collas current anabelian work CAT Chb/Hlb tools

GT Function Spaces from Decomposition Groups

GT species §4 explains how, assuming the FICSP/COF synchronization condition, G-decomposition groups can reconstruct the point set of genus-zero finite etale covers, recover the field structure through S_3 symmetries, and produce function spaces Fn(Y(Q), Q) with algebraic functions inside them.

Decomposition groups

GT-style decomposition groups are obtained by combinatorial arithmetic Belyi cuspidalization.

Point and field recovery

The point set and Q-field structure are read using genus-zero S_3 symmetries.

Function-space container

The recovered points support Fn(Y(Q), Q), standard coordinate functions, and a natural action.

Construction stepSource roleIUT analogy boundary
Belyi cuspidalization Builds decomposition groups in a purely combinatorial/group-theoretic fashion. Analogous in spirit to reconstruction, but not part of IUT III Step (xi).
S_3 symmetries Operations such as t -> t^{-1} and t -> 1-t recover field structure. Genus-zero symmetry replaces any direct ring-structure identification.
Function spaces Standard coordinate functions sit inside Fn(Y(Q), Q). The notes compare these with degree-one theta functions, but this is GT context.
The function-space construction is conditional on FICSP/COF and appears in expected GT-side results. It should be used as a comparison layer, not as a replacement for the IUT multiradial construction.
Source: GT species paper §4 Related: GT FICSP/decomposition GT common containers CAT miraculous rotation

GMS/GCG Motivation

Mochizuki's survey and logical-structure notes use global multiplicative subspaces and global canonical generators as the elementary model for why IUT has to build Hodge theaters, theta links, log links, and multiradial representations. In the special case where a global multiplicative subspace exists, height control is visible through an isogeny; in the general case, IUT is described as simulating the missing GMS/GCG package.

From actual GMS to simulated GMS/GCG motivation for Hodge theaters and theta/log machinery
SourcePointWiki consequence
Mutually Alien Copies §2.3 A global multiplicative subspace is a rank-one l-torsion subspace defined globally and matching the multiplicative Tate-uniformization subspace at bad multiplicative places. It is a special extra structure, not generally available for arbitrary elliptic curves.
Mutually Alien Copies §2.3 Quotienting by such a subspace makes the new q-parameters behave like q -> q^l, while dlog(q) scales by l. This gives the prototype for inequalities of the form l*h <= h + C.
Logical Structure I-V §1 Mochizuki summarizes IUT as an attempt to simulate global multiplicative subspaces and global canonical generators by theta functions and anabelian geometry. The later Hodge-theater and theta-link layers should be read as replacing a missing global geometric convenience, not as arbitrary bookkeeping.
Anabelian Gateway 2025 §2 The N-th power map model separates common multiplicative/abstract-group data from incompatible ring structures. This explains why deleting labels too early turns a simultaneous AND relation into an unhelpful OR ambiguity.

Global multiplicative subspace

In the motivating special case, a rank-one subspace of E[l] is globally defined and agrees locally with the multiplicative subgroup from Tate uniformization at the bad multiplicative places.

Alien §2.3GMS

Global canonical generators

The logical-structure notes state that the theta-function route needs not only a GMS simulation but also global canonical generators. In IUT these are represented through label/cusp and theta-value machinery rather than a literal global section.

Logical I-V §1GCG

q-parameter l-th power

The quotient by an actual GMS changes bad-place q-parameters by an l-th power. Mochizuki repeatedly uses this as the prototype for the Frobenius-like dilation that IUT tries to simulate.

q -> q^lFrobenius-like

dlog height container

The differential relation dlog(q) -> l*dlog(q) supplies the common container in the special isogeny argument. In IUT the analogous role is played by logarithmic/anabelian machinery and log-shells.

dloglog link

Simulation problem

For arbitrary elliptic curves the required GMS generally does not exist. This absence is one reason IUT introduces distinct Hodge-theater copies, non-ring-theoretic theta links, log links, and multiradial algorithms.

general caseHodge theaters

Formalization obligation

A Lean model should not assume a hidden global multiplicative subspace in the general IUT case. The source route requires explicit replacement data: labels, theta values, Kummer comparison, log-shell containment, and multiradial invariance.

Leanno hidden GMS
Sources: Mutually Alien Copies Logical Structure I-V Anabelian Gateway 2025 Related: initial theta data Hodge theaters theta/log links multiradiality

Projective-Line Gluing Analogy

Mochizuki's 2025 Anabelian Gateway notes use the construction of P1 from two labeled affine lines as an elementary model for a recurring IUT warning: a gluing can be meaningful at one structural level while being incompatible with stronger ring-structure identifications. The page records this as an analogy for labels, indeterminacy, and multiradial transport, not as a proof of any IUT estimate.

Two charts, one glued object labels prevent false self-identification

Affine line as ring scheme

For a field or ring k, the affine line A1 = Spec(k[T]) carries its usual ring-scheme structure. Its open subscheme Gm = Spec(k[T,T^-1]) carries the multiplicative group-scheme structure of units.

Gateway 2025 §3A1/Gm

Inversion is only multiplicative here

Between labeled copies there is a group-scheme isomorphism dagger Gm -> double-dagger Gm sending dagger T to double-dagger T^-1. Mochizuki stresses that this does not extend to an isomorphism of the two affine-line ring schemes.

Gateway 2025 §3multiplicative not ring

P1 as two-chart gluing

The standard construction of P1 glues two affine-line charts along Gm by inversion. On the overlap, one rational function is read as dagger T from one chart and as double-dagger T^-1 from the other.

Gateway 2025 §3gluing

Why label deletion is dangerous

If the two labels are erased and both charts are treated as one copy of A1, the gluing forces the standard coordinate T to agree with T^-1. Mochizuki presents this as an elementary source of apparent consistency problems in noncommutative gluing diagrams.

Gateway 2025 §3labels

Riemann-sphere picture

Over C, the same gluing is pictured as the Riemann sphere assembled from northern and southern hemispheres along the equator. An oriented equatorial flow appears clockwise from one hemisphere and counterclockwise from the other.

Gateway 2025 §3orientation

Transport replaces naive identification

Rather than deleting hemisphere labels, the Gateway notes relate the two sides by metric/geodesic geometry. Mochizuki identifies this, at the level of analogy, with the anabelian-geometry-based multiradial algorithm for the theta-pilot.

Gateway 2025 §3multiradial analogy
Formalization use. This analogy is a guardrail for the wiki and Lean work: it supports explicit labels for copied structures, separate interfaces for multiplicative and ring data, and recorded indeterminacy when a comparison passes through a weaker common interface.
Sources: Anabelian Gateway 2025 Essential Logical Structure Mutually Alien Copies Related: GMS/GCG motivation mutually alien copies indeterminacies multiradiality GT species perspective

IUT I Gluing Models

IUT I Introduction §I2 explains how multiple theta-Hodge theaters are glued into a Frobenius-picture. Each theater is a miniature model of conventional scheme theory, but the theta-link between neighboring theaters is not a scheme-theoretic morphism: it is a Frobenioid/monoid-level gluing of theta-values to q-parameters. This page records the source analogies because they are a guardrail against modeling theta-links as ring homomorphisms.

Gluing miniature scheme worlds IUT I §I2 analogies for non-scheme-theoretic comparison

Miniature scheme-theory models

Mochizuki describes a theta-Hodge theater as a system of Frobenioids intended to function as a small model of conventional scheme theory. The Frobenius-picture is formed by gluing many such theaters {n HT} along theta-links.

IUT I §I2Hodge theaters

Theta-link outside schemes

The source text states that the essential correspondences n theta_v -> (n+1) q_v do not arise from ring homomorphisms. The relation between neighboring theaters therefore cannot be expressed as an ordinary scheme-theoretic relation.

IUT I §I2not ring maps

Topological surface analogy

IUT I compares conventional scheme theory to Euclidean space and the Frobenius-picture to a topological manifold built by gluing local Euclidean pieces. The figure in §I2 depicts twice-punctured genus-one surfaces glued along tubular neighborhoods of cycles.

IUT I Fig. I2.1analogy

Cantor-function alien ring example

The second analogy glues two real lines R0 and R1 by a bijection from the Cantor set C0 to the interval I1. Describing structures native to the alien ring structure of R0 using only the ring structure of R1 is presented as highly nontrivial.

IUT I §I2alien structure

Forgetting the integral monoid

For the pair G acts O-delta, IUT I recalls an absolute-anabelian result: forgetting O-delta gives a bijection on automorphism groups. In this case no extra unit-like ambiguity remains after forgetting the monoid.

AbsTopIII inputbijection

Forgetting the unit monoid

For G acts O^x, forgetting the unit monoid only gives a surjection on automorphism groups; the kernel is the natural Zhat^x-action on O^x. This is the closer model for why theta-link gluing retains a nontrivial ambiguity.

AbsTopIII inputsurjection

Kummer gluing cycle

Kummer theory supplies the precise specification of the gluing cycle in each theater. IUT I stresses that the same cycle receives quite different precise descriptions on the two sides of a theta-link, and that Galois-compatible rigidity leaves Kummer theory as the acceptable specification mechanism.

IUT I §I2Kummer

Formalization obligation

The wiki and Lean model should represent theta-links as typed cross-theater interfaces, not as hidden equality of ring or scheme histories. Any later Corollary 3.12 experiment must preserve this distinction when it moves from Frobenius-like to etale-like data.

Lean guardrailtyped histories
Scope. These examples are explanatory analogies from IUT I, not independent proofs of the later inequalities. Their role in the wiki is to fix the intended type discipline before the log-theta lattice and Corollary 3.12 pages reuse theta-link data.
Sources: IUT I Related: theta-link Hodge theaters projective-line gluing mutually alien copies theta/log links log-theta lattice Corollary 3.12 corridor

Basepoints and Inter-universality

IUT I Introduction §I3 explains why theta-links and log-links are called inter-universal. A non-ring/scheme-theoretic filter does not preserve the auxiliary basepoint and fiber-functor data used to construct an etale fundamental group. Across such a filter, the common object is only the underlying abstract topological group, so anabelian reconstruction and species-level type discipline become structural necessities.

What survives a non-ring filter IUT I §I3: from scheme histories to abstract topological groups

Non-ring filter

IUT I §I3 describes the theta-link, and later the log-link, as filters between distinct theaters that are not compatible with the respective ring structures. This is the concrete setting for "surgery on the number field."

IUT I §I3filter

Fundamental-group basepoint

Etale fundamental groups are constructed from a basepoint, equivalently a geometric point and fiber functor into separably closed field data. That construction depends on the surrounding scheme/ring structure.

IUT I §I3fiber functor

Abstract topological group interface

Since the non-ring filter is not a scheme morphism, ordinary functoriality of etale fundamental groups is unavailable. Mochizuki's point is that the shared type that still makes sense on both sides is the abstract topological group underlying the etale fundamental group.

IUT I §I3etale-like

Fiber functors and universes

The phrase inter-universal refers here to relating distinct set-theoretic universes associated with distinct fiber functors and basepoints on the two sides of a non-ring/scheme-theoretic filter.

IUT I §I3inter-universal

Absolute anabelian pressure

Once only the abstract topological group can be compared, the project is forced toward absolute anabelian geometry: understand which ring/scheme structures can be recovered from the topological group alone.

IUT I §I3reconstruction

Species type discipline

IUT I explicitly says this discussion depends on distinguishing different types of mathematical objects, and points forward to the language of species developed in IUT IV. This is why the wiki treats species as part of the core formalism rather than as later notation.

IUT I §I3species

Conjugate synchronization

The same source paragraph points to synchronization between conjugacy indeterminacies of distinct copies of local Galois groups. This is a basepoint-sensitive inter-universal phenomenon that later reappears in theta evaluation and log-Kummer transport.

IUT I §I3synchronization

Theta-function rigidity

IUT I also flags rigidity properties of the etale theta function as an inter-universal phenomenon needed in later papers. This connects §I3 to the Hodge-Arakelov evaluation and theta-value Kummer pages.

IUT I §I3theta rigidity

Formalization rule

A formal model should not identify etale fundamental groups by silently transporting basepoints through theta-links or log-links. It should expose the weakening to abstract topological groups and state which anabelian reconstruction theorem restores which structure.

Lean guardrailno hidden basepoints
Sources: IUT I Related: IUT I gluing models anabelian background theta±ell relay theta evaluation route species and mutations Collas category view

IUT I Teichmuller Analogy

IUT I Introduction §I4 explains the word "Teichmuller" in the theory's name. The analogy is not merely decorative: complex Teichmuller theory varies a holomorphic structure inside a fixed real-analytic container, while IUT varies an arithmetic holomorphic structure inside mono-analytic containers. The theta direction dilates value-group data; the log direction rotates the additive and multiplicative dimensions.

From complex dilation to log-theta lattice IUT I §I4: fixed containers, varied holomorphic structures

Complex template

IUT I recalls the classical deformation z = x + iy -> Kx + iy: one real-analytic dimension is dilated while the other is held fixed. The varying object is the holomorphic structure; the fixed object is the real-analytic container.

IUT I §I4complex template

Arithmetic combinatorial dimensions

Mochizuki interprets a number-field ring structure as one arithmetic holomorphic dimension made from two combinatorial dimensions: addition and multiplication. Locally, these are read through unit groups and value groups.

IUT I §I4+ / x

Theta dilation of value groups

The theta-link is described as a Teichmuller deformation relative to a theta factor: value-group data are dilated by theta/q data, while local unit groups remain fixed up to isomorphism. This is the source-level reason the wiki separates unit and value-group interfaces.

IUT I §I4theta-link

Theta and log dimensions

IUT I says the later log-theta lattice has two dimensions matching the two combinatorial dimensions of a ring. The theta direction is made of Frobenius-picture deformations; the log direction rotates the additive and multiplicative dimensions while the arithmetic holomorphic structure is held fixed.

IUT I §I4lattice

Canonical bound in a fixed container

The same section points forward to Theorem 3.11 and Corollary 3.12: a priori unbounded theta deformations should admit canonical bounds once described inside a fixed mono-analytic container.

IUT I §I43.11 / 3.12

p-adic Teichmuller correspondence

Figure I4.1 compares IUT with p-adic Teichmuller theory: number field with punctured elliptic curve corresponds to a hyperbolic curve with nilpotent ordinary indigenous bundle; the log-theta lattice corresponds to canonical lifting data equipped with Frobenius action.

IUT I Fig. I4.1p-adic analogy

Witt-vector arrow analogy

IUT I identifies theta arrows with the mixed-characteristic extension structure of a ring of Witt vectors, and log arrows with the Frobenius morphism in positive characteristic. Their failure to commute models the noncommutativity of theta and log links.

IUT I §I4Witt/Frobenius

Anabelian reconstruction contrast

Mochizuki contrasts the relatively straightforward construction of canonical liftings with their nontrivial absolute-anabelian reconstruction. This mirrors IUT: constructing the log-theta lattice is not the hard part; describing an alien arithmetic holomorphic structure is.

IUT I §I4reconstruction

Formalization rule

A formal model should keep separate the fixed mono-analytic container, the arithmetic holomorphic structures being deformed, and the numerical bound extracted from the container. This is the type-level analogue of the complex Teichmuller fixed-container/varying-structure pattern.

Lean guardrailcontainer vs structure
Sources: IUT I Related: classical roots route Invitation deformation log-theta lattice theta/log links tensor/log-volume corridor Corollary 3.12 corridor

CAT Idempotent Function Theory

In Varying Hues §3, Mochizuki states an elementary idempotent lemma for combinatorial algebraization theory. Starting from a finite etale Galois cover and several algebra copies inside a function algebra on an infinite active point set, one may restrict to an infinite subset so that the nonzero idempotents form a maximal orthogonal family and the Galois group acts transitively on them.

Idempotents after restricting the active set Varying Hues §3, source lemma for CAT
Lemma componentContentCAT use
Finite etale Galois cover Provides the finite group Q acting on the function space. Finite quotient side of the later CAT rotation.
Active infinite subset The construction may restrict to an infinite subset after excluding finitely many points. Stabilizes the idempotent pattern used for algebraization.
Maximal orthogonal idempotents Nonzero idempotents correspond to connected components after restriction. Converts algebra copies into combinatorial component data.
Transitive action Q acts transitively; supports look like cosets qHy. Feeds the finite-quotient side of the CAT comparison.
Status. This page records a lemma from an ICMS 2025 talk note connected to manuscripts in preparation. It is current adjacent source material, not a dependency of the published IUT I-IV proof.
Sources: Varying Hues §3 Related: CAT miraculous rotation GT common containers GT species perspective

CAT Miraculous Rotation

Varying Hues §4 presents combinatorial algebraization theory as another Teichmuller-type theory, now for conjugates of arithmetic groups inside GT or Out(Pi_X). The source compares CAT's rotation from a mysterious gap between distinct ring theories to a harmless finite-quotient gap with IUT's rotation from the theta-link gap to the log-link gap.

CAT rotation compared with IUT rotation Varying Hues §4

CAT input

The source studies conjugates of G_Q or mapping-class-group images inside GT or Out(Pi_X).

Technical tools

Chebotarev and Hilbertian inputs compute tensor-product actions at decomposition subgroups; combinatorial anabelian geometry supplies the configuration-space transition.

IUT analogy

The analogy is structural: CAT rotates a difficult GT-conjugate gap to a finite-quotient gap, while IUT rotates the theta-link gap to the log-link gap.

Wiki handling. CAT is a useful comparison lens for the common-container theme. The page should remain linked to GT context and not be folded into the core IUT I-IV proof spine.
Sources: Varying Hues §4 Related: CAT idempotents GT FICSP/decomposition Collas log-theta wandering

CAT Chb/Hlb Tools

Varying Hues §4 singles out Chebotarev-type arguments and the Hilbertian property as technical inputs for combinatorial algebraization theory. Their role is to give enough pointwise decomposition-subgroup control to compare multiplicative tensor-product actions with additive scheme-theoretic actions.

Pointwise control for CAT algebraization Chb/Hlb in Varying Hues §4
ToolSource roleWiki interpretation
Chebotarev Applied over number fields and over function fields over finite fields. Supplies enough decomposition subgroups of points to test the action.
Hilbertian property Used for rational varieties over number fields. Controls specialization points in the Out(Pi_X) setting.
Configuration-space anabelian geometry Uses isomorphisms between Out_F(Pi_{X_{n+1}}) and Out_F(Pi_{X_n}). Moves from toral/multiplicative nodes to tripod/additive symmetries.
Cyclotomic Belyi cuspidalization Provides group-theoretic cuspidalization data. Plays the CAT analogue of theta-function/elliptic cuspidalization input.
Boundary. This is a CAT comparison page, not a claim that Chebotarev or Hilbertian arguments by themselves prove the IUT Corollary 3.12 estimate. The relevance is methodological: both CAT and IUT need a way to replace an alien comparison by data visible in a fixed combinatorial container.
Sources: Varying Hues §4 Related: CAT miraculous rotation CAT idempotents GT function spaces GT genus-zero quotient

Hoshi Introduction Route

Hoshi's Japanese introduction is organized as a pedagogical route into IUT: first cyclotomes and object types, then the link-based strategy for Diophantine inequalities, then local/additive machinery, then global Frobenioid and multiplicative Hodge-theater machinery. The wiki uses it as a reader's map of dependencies, not as a substitute for the four IUT papers.

Survey route from cyclotomes to full Hodge theaters

Cyclotomes first

Hoshi begins with cyclotomes because IUT repeatedly needs to compare cyclotomic structures arising from different sources: roots of unity, curve cohomology, inertia at cusps, and Frobenioid monoids. The survey presents cyclotomic synchronization and rigidity as a prerequisite for later transport.

Hoshi §1cyclotomes

Types of objects

The early sections distinguish etale-like parts from Frobenius-like parts. In the local model, the Galois group is the symmetry-bearing etale side, while monoids such as local units or integral multiplicative monoids form the quantity-bearing Frobenius side.

Hoshi §2etale/Frobenius

Kummer transport

Hoshi's local toy model explains Kummer theory as a way to transport a Frobenius-like monoid through etale containers. The inevitable ambiguity is split into Kummer-detachment indeterminacy and etale-transport indeterminacy.

Hoshi §3transport

Link approach

Sections 4-12 describe the strategy for obtaining Diophantine consequences through links: protect q-parameter and field data from destructive identifications by handling them as special values inside appropriate containers rather than as bare numbers in one fixed ring.

Hoshi §§4-12links

Core objects

The survey frames core objects as the portions that remain usable across transport. This matches the wiki's distinction between the data retained by prime-strips, log-shells, and Hodge theaters, and the ring/scheme histories deliberately not transported as-is.

Hoshi §§5-6cores

Multiradial algorithms

Hoshi isolates multiradiality before the technical Hodge-theater construction. In this wiki, that order is important: multiradial algorithms are the formal reason output may be read from many theater positions after allowing the specified indeterminacies.

Hoshi §7multiradiality

Hulls and log links

The middle survey route introduces hulls and log links before the full global setup. Hulls absorb indeterminacy into controlled regions; log links provide the vertical movement later visible in the log-theta lattice.

Hoshi §§8-9hulls/log links

Mild indeterminacies

Hoshi's route explicitly pauses on the "mild" indeterminacies before numerical output. This is a useful reading warning: Ind1, Ind2, and Ind3 are not cleanup details after the comparison, but part of the comparison interface itself.

Hoshi §10Ind1/2/3

Additive and multiplicative theaters

The later sections split the construction into additive Hodge theaters built from local/log-theta data and multiplicative Hodge theaters built from global Frobenioid and Kummer data. Section 26 explains the combined Hodge theater as the interface between these two symmetry packages.

Hoshi §§13-26Hodge theaters
Sources: Hoshi introduction Related: theta cyclotome theta/log links Hodge theaters indeterminacies

Hoshi Cyclotome Synchronization

Hoshi begins his introduction with cyclotomes because IUT repeatedly compares Tate-twist objects that arise from different mathematical sources. The same symbol Zhat(1) may be represented by roots of unity, by curve cohomology, by cusp inertia, or by Frobenioid data. The mathematical work is to control when these presentations may be synchronized.

One cyclotomic type, several sources Hoshi §1, used as prerequisite for later transport
Source presentationWhy it mattersWiki link
Roots of unity Provides the elementary Tate-twist model used in local field and Kummer settings. theta cyclotome
Curve cohomology Connects cyclotomic orientation to degree and Chern-class conventions. notation primitives
Cusp inertia Supplies a group-theoretic cyclotomic source in the anabelian setting. cusp recognizers
Frobenioid monoids Keeps multiplicative data compatible with the categorical line-bundle package. Frobenioid package
Reader warning. Hoshi's first section is not preliminary notation only. It is a guardrail against silently identifying cyclotomic structures that arise from different containers.
Sources: Hoshi introduction §1 Related: Hoshi route Kummer transport Kummer-ready/blind

Hoshi Kummer Transport

Hoshi's early local model presents Kummer theory as a transport mechanism: Frobenius-like monoid data can be moved through etale-like containers only after the cyclotomic comparison has been fixed. The result is never a raw equality of ring histories. It is a controlled passage with specified indeterminacy.

Etale container, Frobenius-like payload Hoshi §3 before the full Hodge-theater setting

Etale-like carrier

The Galois or fundamental-group side carries symmetry information and permits transport without preserving a chosen ring structure.

Frobenius-like payload

Unit monoids and integral monoids carry quantity data. Kummer maps expose them to the etale carrier but do not turn them into scheme-identical data.

Indeterminacy is part of transport

The local toy model already shows why later IUT comparisons must track indeterminacies as typed structure, not as post-proof noise.

Sources: Hoshi introduction §3 Related: cyclotomes Log-Kummer correspondences indeterminacies

Hoshi Link and Indeterminacy Route

Hoshi's sections 4 through 12 explain the strategic middle of IUT before the full Hodge-theater construction. The aim is to protect q-parameter data, field data, and hull/log-link containers while moving through links. The indeterminacies are introduced before the numerical output because they are necessary to state what remains comparable.

From link strategy to controlled output Hoshi §§4-12 as the pedagogical middle route
Survey section groupRole in the routeDetailed wiki target
§§4-6 Set up the link approach and isolate core objects that survive transport. base bridges/evaluation sections
§7 Introduces multiradial algorithms before the full technical theater setup. multiradiality
§§8-9 Introduce hulls and log links as controlled containers and movements. theta/log links
§10 Explains mild indeterminacies before numerical and Hodge-theater output. indeterminacies
3.12 relevance. This Hoshi route supports the wiki's main warning about the disputed corridor: the q-pilot and theta-pilot comparison is not a comparison of bare numbers before the link, hull, and indeterminacy apparatus is applied.
Sources: Hoshi introduction §§4-12 Related: Hoshi route Collas log-theta wandering Corollary 3.12 corridor

IUT I Notation Primitives

IUT I Section 0 fixes conventions that later pages use constantly. These are not optional stylistic choices: poly-isomorphisms, capsules, pseudo-monoids, and connected temperoids determine what kind of comparison is being made when a theorem says "isomorphism" or "full poly-isomorphism."

Conventions before the construction IUT I §0: monoids, categories, temperoids, valuations

Pseudo-monoid

A topological pseudo-monoid is a topological space with a partially defined multiplication obtained by embedding it in an ambient topological abelian group and restricting the group law to products that remain inside the embedded subspace.

IUT I §0monoids

Divisible and cyclotomic cases

Divisibility is defined by the existence and compatibility of roots in the ambient group. Cyclotomic pseudo-monoids carry a torsion subgroup isomorphic to Q/Z and stable under multiplication with the pseudo-monoid.

IUT I §0cyclotomic

Category-isomorphism convention

Mochizuki uses "isomorphism of categories" to mean an isomorphism class of equivalences of categories. This convention matters whenever later pages speak about full poly-isomorphisms between categories or Hodge-theater components.

IUT I §0categories

Poly-morphism and full poly-isomorphism

A poly-morphism is a collection of morphisms between two objects; a poly-isomorphism is a collection of isomorphisms. The full poly-isomorphism is the collection of all isomorphisms. Composites ignore multiplicity and retain the set of all pairwise composites.

IUT I §0poly

Capsule of objects

A capsule is a finite indexed family of objects in a category. Morphisms between capsules use injections of index sets and component morphisms; capsule-full poly-isomorphisms use bijections of the index sets.

IUT I §0capsules

Finite etale covering categories

For a connected noetherian algebraic stack of the stated type, B(X) denotes finite etale coverings over X; B(X)_0 denotes the connected-object subcategory. These categories feed the anabelian side of the later Hodge-theater definitions.

IUT I §0coverings

Connected temperoid

Btemp(Pi) is the category of countable discrete sets with continuous action by a tempered topological group Pi. For Galois-countable cases, the group Pi can be reconstructed from the category up to inner automorphism.

IUT I §0temperoids

Temperoid morphisms

Morphisms of connected temperoids are represented contravariantly by functors preserving finite limits and countable colimits. For suitable tempered groups, this matches continuous outer homomorphisms between the groups.

IUT I §0outer homs

Abstract equivalence

Two functors are abstractly equivalent when they differ by isomorphisms of their source and target categories. The wiki treats this as a warning that many later comparisons are invariant up to the specified categorical equivalence, not up to literal equality.

IUT I §0equivalence

Valuations and curve notation

Section 0 fixes abbreviations such as NF, MLF, CAF, valuations V(F), completions F_v, residue characteristics, and the curve/orbicurve terminology used by the initial theta data.

IUT I §0notation
Formalization use. These conventions justify why the Lean and wiki layers should represent collections of possible isomorphisms, capsule indices, and reconstructed groups explicitly instead of replacing them by single chosen maps or definitional equalities.
Sources: IUT I Related: IUT I atlas prime strips and bridges theta/log links anabelian background species and mutations

Pseudo-Monoids in IUT I §0

IUT I begins by allowing monoid-like objects that are only partially closed under multiplication. A pseudo-monoid is represented inside an ambient abelian group, and multiplication is defined only when the product remains inside the chosen subset. This is the source convention behind later rational-function pseudo-monoids and coric function packages.

Partial multiplication inside an ambient group IUT I §0, monoids and categories
TermSource meaningDownstream role
Pseudo-monoid A subset embedded in an ambient topological abelian group, with multiplication defined by the ambient law only when the product remains in the subset. Models later partially closed function and monoid packages.
Divisible Root extraction is compatible with membership: an element belongs exactly when its relevant powers belong, in the ambient group. Prepares the divisibility conditions used in coric rational functions.
Cyclotomic The ambient group may be chosen with torsion subgroup isomorphic to Q/Z, stable under multiplication with the pseudo-monoid. Connects pseudo-monoids to cyclotomic rigidity and Kummer data.
Formalization consequence. A pseudo-monoid should not be encoded as an ordinary monoid unless the source has supplied global closure. The ambient group and the membership condition are part of the structure.
Sources: IUT I §0 Related: notation primitives kappa-coric functions Frobenioids/log-shells

Poly-Isomorphisms in IUT I §0

A poly-morphism is a set of morphisms between two objects. A poly-isomorphism is a set of isomorphisms, and the full poly-isomorphism is the set of all isomorphisms. Composing poly-morphisms means taking all pairwise composites and ignoring multiplicity. This convention is essential in theta links, bridge morphisms, and Hodge-theater comparisons.

Sets of possible maps, not chosen maps IUT I §0
ConventionDefinitionGuardrail
Poly-morphism A collection of morphisms A -> B. Do not replace by one selected morphism unless the source chooses one.
Poly-isomorphism A poly-morphism all of whose members are isomorphisms. The data is a set of possible isomorphisms.
Full poly-isomorphism The collection of all isomorphisms between the two objects. This is common in theta-link and bridge statements.
Composition All composites g_j o f_i, with multiplicity ignored. Set-level composition changes proof obligations in Lean.
Failure mode. Many incorrect simplifications of IUT-style interfaces start by replacing a full poly-isomorphism with a canonical-looking single isomorphism. The source convention explicitly avoids that move.
Sources: IUT I §0 Related: theta links D-ThetaNF bridges symmetries/processions

Capsules and Temperoids in IUT I §0

Section 0 defines two categorical devices that recur throughout IUT I: capsules, which are finite indexed families of objects, and connected temperoids, which encode countable discrete sets with continuous action by a tempered topological group. These conventions control later prime-strip capsules and anabelian reconstruction interfaces.

Finite indexed families and reconstructible action categories IUT I §0
DeviceSource definitionDownstream role
Capsule A finite indexed family {A_j}_{j in J} of objects of a category. Used for labeled families of D-prime-strips and Hodge-theater components.
Capsule morphism An injection of index sets plus component morphisms. Index movement is explicit; labels are not erased.
Capsule-full poly-isomorphism A capsule-full poly-morphism whose index injection is a bijection. Controls later processions and full bridge comparison data.
Btemp(Pi) Countable discrete sets with continuous Pi-action. Provides the category-theoretic language for tempered fundamental groups.
Connected temperoid A category equivalent to Btemp(Pi) for a tempered topological group. For Galois-countable cases, Pi is reconstructible up to inner automorphism.
Formalization consequence. Capsule index sets and temperoid reconstruction hypotheses are part of the mathematical interface. Later pages should link here whenever they use labeled capsules or recovered topological groups.
Sources: IUT I §0 Related: D-prime strips and labels symmetries/processions Galois-countability repairs

IUT I Section 1: Orbicurve Cover Package

IUT I begins by fixing a cyclic covering package attached to a once-punctured elliptic curve and a distinguished cusp. The point is not only to have curves, but to have coverings, quotient orbicurves, cusp labels, and fundamental groups that can later be reconstructed group-theoretically.

Section 1 Source Package cyclic covers, quotient orbicurves, cusps, and reconstruction
Source itemConstruction or statementWiki/formalization consequence
Setup before Definition 1.1 Starting from a hyperbolic orbicurve built from a once-punctured elliptic curve, the paper forms finite etale cyclic coverings and quotient orbicurves. The displayed covering diagram has Galois groups Z/ell Z, Z/2Z, and Z/2ell Z in the indicated layers. The base curve data carries a rigid cyclic-cover hierarchy before theta data is packaged.
Definition 1.1 Defines hyperbolic orbicurves of type (1,ell-tors arrow) and (1,ell-tors arrow)^{±} by arising, up to isomorphism, from the constructed X-arrow or C-arrow package. These are source-defined curve types, not informal labels for arbitrary elliptic-curve covers.
Remark 1.1.1 The arrow notation records ordered archimedean labels 1,2,... determined by the distinguished epsilon on the relevant plus-minus orbits. Label order enters already at the orbicurve-cover layer; later label discipline is not introduced only at Gaussian or log-theta stages.
Remark 1.1.2 The constructed X-arrow and C-arrow are determined, up to k-isomorphism, by (X/k, C, epsilon). The cover package is canonical relative to the chosen source data.
Corollary 1.2 For k an NF or MLF, there is a functorial group-theoretic algorithm to reconstruct the indicated fundamental groups, together with cusp decomposition-group conjugacy classes, from the top-level group. The reconstructed groups are viewed as subgroups of the relevant automorphism group. The later anabelian interface has an explicit reconstruction theorem behind it: groups and cusp classes are recovered from group structure.
Remark 1.2.1 The automorphism groups of the constructed covers identify with the corresponding cyclic Galois groups. The cover automorphism data is exactly the cyclic symmetry data used in the package.
Remark 1.2.2 Corrects background cusp-reconstruction algorithms by characterizing cusp inertia through cyclic pro-ell coverings that are totally ramified at a cusp. Cusp recovery requires the repaired total-ramification test, not a vague assertion that cusps are visible.
Remark 1.2.3 Repairs related combinatorial anabelian graph-cover arguments, including characterizations of cuspidal and nodal edge-like subgroups and verticial subgroups by ramification properties. The graph-of-anabelioids input to tempered covering theory relies on corrected recognizers for cusps, nodes, and vertices.

Cyclic cover hierarchy

Section 1 organizes the elliptic curve, its quotient orbicurve, and cyclic covers into a hierarchy whose Galois groups include Z/ell Z, Z/2Z, and Z/2ell Z.

IUT I §1cyclic covers

Ordered cusp labels

The arrow in the notation records ordered labels determined by the chosen cusp data. This is an early source of the label discipline that later appears in processions and theta-value labels.

Remark 1.1.1labels

Group-theoretic reconstruction

Corollary 1.2 reconstructs the lower fundamental groups and cusp decomposition classes from the top group, functorially with respect to topological-group isomorphisms.

Corollary 1.2anabelian

Cusp inertia test

Remark 1.2.2 repairs the cusp algorithm by using cyclic coverings whose degree-ell quotient is totally ramified at a cusp. The total-ramification condition is then group-theoretic.

Remark 1.2.2repair

Edge and vertex recognizers

Remark 1.2.3 repairs the combinatorial graph-cover background by replacing several informal descent and ramification claims with explicit group-theoretic characterizations of cuspidal, nodal, and verticial data.

Remark 1.2.3CombGC

Formalization duty

A faithful formalization should eventually expose cyclic cover maps, Galois group identifications, cusp decomposition classes, and repaired total-ramification recognizers. A bare record saying that the cover exists would miss the Section 1 content used downstream.

Lean targetsource interface
The role of this page is foundational: it records the first anabelian reconstruction mechanism in the series. Later Hodge theaters should be read as carrying this repaired curve/cover/cusp recognition layer, not as untyped copies of elliptic-curve data.
Sources: IUT I, §1 Related: anabelian background IUT I atlas tempered covering complements initial theta data Hodge theaters

IUT I Atlas: From Initial Data to Hodge Theaters

IUT I builds the starting universe of the theory. Its central move is not yet the Corollary 3.12 comparison, but the construction of typed theaters, prime strips, label classes, and Frobenioid-theoretic links that make later alien comparisons possible.

Punctured elliptic curves and orbicurves

The curve X_F is a once-punctured elliptic curve over a number field. The quotient by the involution gives the orbicurve C_F. These curves provide the hyperbolic objects whose fundamental groups and coverings are reconstructed anabelianly.

IUT I §1initial data

Tempered coverings

IUT I records complements on tempered coverings and decomposition subgroups. This is part of the absolute anabelian input: local and global curve data must be recoverable from group-theoretic information.

IUT I §2anabelian

Initial theta data components

Definition 3.1 fixes an algebraic closure Fbar/F, a once-punctured elliptic curve, a prime l >= 5, a cover C_K, valuation sets, bad reduction places, and a distinguished cusp. It also imposes torsion, Galois, and mod-l image conditions.

Definition 3.1input package

Bad and good valuation sets

The distinction between V_bad and V_good controls where multiplicative reduction, theta evaluation, local logarithms, and log-volume estimates behave in different ways.

valuationslocal data

Frobenioids at bad primes

At bad nonarchimedean places, Frobenioids encode base-field and divisor-like arithmetic data. Later log-volume estimates read this material through monoids, units, realifications, and Kummer-compatible actions.

IUT I §3Frobenioids

Global realified Frobenioids

Realification passes Frobenioid data into a form where degrees and log-volumes become real-valued. This is one reason the theory must track exactly which real-valued quantity is being compared.

realificationdegree

Theta-Hodge theater

Definition 3.6 defines a theta-Hodge theater as valuation-indexed local categories, Frobenioid/Kummer data, and global modular Frobenioid data attached to the fixed initial theta data. It is a miniature arithmetic world built category-theoretically.

Definition 3.6theater

Theta-link

Corollary 3.7 gives full poly-isomorphisms between theta-Hodge theaters. The point is that the link is Frobenioid-theoretic and does not live inside ordinary ring/scheme morphisms.

Corollary 3.7horizontal motion

Frobenius-picture chains

Corollary 3.8 arranges theta-Hodge theaters in integer-indexed chains. These chains are the first visible form of the later lattice-like world of mutually alien but structurally linked copies.

IUT I §3chains

D-prime-strips

A holomorphic base-prime-strip is a valuation-indexed collection of local objects. Its mono-analyticization produces a D⊢-prime-strip, a recurring interface for stripping away holomorphic history.

Definition 4.1prime strips

Label classes of cusps

IUT I attaches F_l-torsor structures to cusp label classes. These labels are later essential when Gaussian and j^2-weighted theta data are averaged or descended.

Proposition 4.2labels

Base-NF and base-theta bridges

Model base bridges describe how prime-strips and label classes are moved while preserving only the structures allowed at that stage. They are early prototypes of the later bridge discipline needed for Corollary 3.12.

IUT I §4bridges
Sources: IUT I Related: initial theta data Hodge theaters theta links

Source Coverage Map

This page groups the detailed wiki pages into larger source units. It is a navigation device and an audit device: it shows which parts already have concept-level pages and which parts still need page-by-page expansion against the source papers.

Current source-map layer hub pages organize the detailed concept pages below them
ClusterCurrent stateMain holes still visible
IUT I initial theta cluster Detailed pages exist for Definition 3.1, Remark 3.1.2, Remarks 3.1.3-3.1.6, and Remark 3.1.7. Needs continued cross-checking against later uses in IUT I §4-5 and against EtTh/AbsTopIII source dependencies.
IUT I Frobenioid/theater cluster Pages exist for bad, good, archimedean, global realified, theta-side, and theater/link constructions. Needs finer pages for every reconstruction claim and every correction remark in Examples 3.2-3.5 and Definition 3.6.
IUT I prime-strip/bridge cluster Pages exist for D-prime-strips, labels, local analytic sections, theta evaluation, and D-ThetaNF bridges. Needs systematic source-order expansion through all bridge propositions and their indeterminacy constraints.
Corollary 3.12 corridor cluster The wiki has a diagnostic corridor, proof-step pages, and supporting pages for Hodge theaters, log-links, Kummer correspondences, and IUT IV consequences. IUT II and IUT III still need much denser page-by-page coverage before the corridor can be called source-complete.
IUT III §1 log-theta cluster Pages exist for local log-links, log-shell variants, Hodge-theater log-links, Gaussian log-theta lattices, vertical/horizontal coricity, and bi-cores. Needs continued source-order expansion of the fine remarks, especially the single-isomorphism synchronization and bi-coricity restrictions.
IUT III Corollary 2.3 cluster Pages now group the radial/coric algorithm, spoke symmetry, polar-coordinate interpretation, ∞κ-coric analogue, theta/number-field container comparison, and the EtTh/mono-theta decision tree of Remark 2.3.4. Needs continued expansion through the transition into the F⊥/pilot-object pages and later uses in Theorem 3.11.
IUT IV output cluster Section 1 estimate pages, Theorem 1.10, Corollary 2.2, Corollary 2.3, and Section 3 species pages now have grouped routes plus atomic subpages. Needs deeper references to the external GenEll and explicit-estimates inputs, and eventual Lean-facing theorem-interface extraction.
Context papers and formalization Local PDF library and pages for Mochizuki formalization, RIMS route, LANA, Scholze-Stix, Mochizuki response, Collas, Hoshi, and related papers exist. Needs deeper extraction of each paper's distinct contribution and tighter links from context pages back to exact IUT source concepts.
Sources: IUT I IUT II IUT III IUT IV Related: source library concept index 3.12 corridor cluster

IUT I §3.1 Source Cluster: Initial Theta Data

This cluster groups the pages that explain the input package for the IUT series. The unit starts with Definition 3.1, then records the theta-approach reconstruction route, the dependency remarks, the stable-model and stack-theoretic checks, the odd-ell correction, and the κ-coric function package.

Definition 3.1 package

Field/moduli data, valuation section, bad/good split, cover data, and the distinguished cusp.

overview field/moduli valuations cusp/covers

Theta approach

Recover Pi_XK from Pi_CK, apply AbsTopIII through that subgroup, and identify the cyclotome output.

overview Pi_XK function field cyclotome

Dependency and hygiene remarks

Remarks 3.1.3-3.1.6 control which named data are independent and which local or EtTh hypotheses must be carried forward.

3.1.3 3.1.4 3.1.5 3.1.6

κ-coric function layer

The function-field side introduces critical points, κ-coric rational functions, infinity variants, κ-solvable elements, and automorphism control.

overview critical points definition infinity variants disjointness automorphisms

Why this is one unit

The later Hodge-theater construction does not start from a bare elliptic curve. It starts from this package of fields, valuations, covers, reconstructible groups, local models, and Kummer-compatible functions.

source guardrailinput package

Next expansion target

Each external dependency cited here, especially AbsTopIII, EtTh, FrdI, and FrdII, should eventually have a linked dependency page explaining the exact theorem interface used by this source cluster.

holedependency interfaces
Sources: IUT I §3.1 Related: coverage map Frobenioid/theater cluster

IUT I §3.2-§3.9 Source Cluster: Frobenioids and Theta-Hodge Theaters

This cluster groups the local and global Frobenioid pages that turn initial theta data into theta-Hodge theaters and chains of theaters. It keeps the valuation strata separate because the source construction differs at bad nonarchimedean, good nonarchimedean, and archimedean places.

Bad-place package

Bad nonarchimedean places use the Tate-curve theta-function theory of EtTh, including the theta value and split Frobenioid indeterminacies.

overview base category theta value split Frobenioids reconstruction

Good and archimedean packages

Good nonarchimedean places use valuation-monoid Frobenioids; archimedean places use Aut-holomorphic orbispaces, Kummer structures, and log compatibility.

overview good base formal theta Aut-holomorphic Kummer/log

Global realified package

Example 3.5 builds global realified Frobenioids and theta/log-shell versions that exchange local divisor currencies across valuations.

overview Cmod realification theta version D/log-shell version

Theater and link output

Definition 3.6 packages the local/global data into a theta-Hodge theater; Corollaries 3.7-3.9 organize theta links and Frobenius/etale pictures.

theater pictures theta links Frobenius etale

Why this is one unit

These pages explain the construction of the first Hodge-theater worlds: local categories, divisor monoids, Kummer structures, realifications, and link pictures are assembled before the Section 4 prime-strip machinery.

construction chaintheater output

Next expansion target

The remaining holes are the theorem-interface details behind each reconstructibility assertion and the exact source dependencies on FrdI, FrdII, and EtTh.

holereconstruction proofs
Sources: IUT I §3.2-§3.9 Related: initial theta cluster prime-strip/bridge cluster

IUT I §4-§5 Source Cluster: Prime-Strips, Labels, and Bridges

This cluster groups the pages where theta-Hodge theaters are converted into prime-strip interfaces, label torsors, local analytic sections, theta evaluation sections, and bridge constructions. This is the part of IUT I that prepares the later log-Kummer and theta-link transport machinery.

D-prime-strips and labels

Section 4 starts by extracting valuation-indexed prime-strip objects and synchronizing cusp label classes.

overview definition label torsors monoanalytic global polymorphisms synchronization

Local analytic sections

Local section data introduces NF automorphism groups, local poly-morphisms, D-capsule actions, and obstruction warnings.

overview NF automorphisms local polymorphisms D-capsule action obstructions

Theta evaluation and label transport

The theta evaluation pages distinguish bad-place and good-place bridges and then track how labels move through base-bridge interfaces.

overview evaluation points bad bridge good bridge label transport outer factorization

D-ThetaNF bridges

The later bridge pages introduce the D-NF bridge, the D-Theta bridge, the D-ThetaNF theater, bridge label transport, and global label/Kodaira-Spencer bookkeeping.

overview D-NF bridge D-Theta bridge D-ThetaNF theater bridge labels global labels

Why this is one unit

These pages form the interface layer between constructed Hodge theaters and later inter-theater comparisons. The repeated issue is which structures survive after holomorphic or ring/scheme history is discarded.

interface layersurvival of structure

Next expansion target

The source still needs page-by-page treatment through the later Section 5 Kummer/Frobenioid bridge material, with each indeterminacy and reconstructibility assertion linked back to its exact proposition.

holeSection 5 details

IUT III §1 Source Cluster: Log-Links, Log-Shells, and Bi-Cores

This cluster groups the detailed pages for IUT III §1. The section builds the vertical log-link side of the log-theta lattice, replaces ring/scheme history by log-shell and mono-analytic data, and then identifies the bi-coric objects that can be carried through both vertical and horizontal directions.

Local log-link mechanism

Proposition 1.1 and its surrounding remarks explain the vertical passage through local logarithms: unit data moves through additive log images, while ordinary ring compatibility is not part of the transported object.

1.1 route tautological/full link unit domain upper-semi Log-Kummer

Log-shell replacements

Proposition 1.2 supplies the mono-analytic log-shell interfaces. These are the objects that can serve as common receptacles after the log-wall has removed the original ring/scheme interpretation.

1.2 variants F⊢×μ shells mono-analytic shells coric shells log-wall warning

Hodge-theater log-links

Proposition 1.3 lifts the local log-link mechanism to Theta±ellNF-Hodge theaters. The source emphasizes that the construction is first made for a single theater isomorphism before full poly-isomorphism language is imposed, so synchronization data is not lost.

1.3 theater link Frobenius-picture root-unity indeterminacy reference coricity

Gaussian log-theta lattice

Definition 1.4 arranges Hodge theaters in a two-dimensional lattice. Vertical arrows are log-links; horizontal arrows are theta links. The noncommutativity of these directions is the structural reason the wiki does not collapse the diagram into a single chain of ordinary morphisms.

1.4 lattice lattice overview coricity overview basepoint triangle

Bi-coric output

Theorem 1.5 identifies the objects that remain coric in both directions: certain F⊢×μ prime-strips, mono-analytic log-shells, and global realified Frobenioids. It also records negative information, such as the absence of a full bi-coric solvable/ring reconstruction object.

1.5 coricity prime-strips log-shells realified Frobenioids bi-cores

Why this is one unit

The local log-link, log-shell, lattice, and bi-core pages all describe what survives when horizontal theta movement and vertical log movement are both present. This is the first local form of the discipline later needed near Theorem 3.11 and Corollary 3.12.

survival disciplineIUT III §1
Source: IUT III §1 Related: IUT III atlas 3.12 corridor cluster coverage map

Corollary 3.12 Corridor Source Cluster

This cluster groups the pages most directly relevant to the disputed Theorem 3.11 to Corollary 3.12 corridor. It is not a claim that the corridor is source-complete; it is a map of the current pages that should be read together when auditing how the wiki explains the passage from Hodge-theater transport to log-volume comparison.

Hodge-theater and link background

These pages explain why Hodge theaters, theta/log links, and Kummer-compatible transport are central to the intended comparison.

Hodge theaters theta/log links log-Kummer indeterminacies

IUT II and IUT III inputs

Current pages cover the Kummer evaluation square, theta values q^{j^2}, Frobenius/etale versions, and radial taxonomy, but this remains a sparse layer compared with the source papers.

IUT II atlas evaluation square theta values radial taxonomy IUT III atlas

The 3.12 corridor pages

These pages are the current diagnostic center: they separate the stated corridor, its proof steps, and the formalization diagnostics used to avoid silently assuming the disputed comparison.

Corollary 3.12 proof steps diagnostics Theorem 3.11

IUT IV output side

IUT IV pages explain how Corollary 3.12 is used in log-volume estimates, explicit consequences, and set-theoretic species/foundation pages.

IUT IV atlas IUT IV consequences canonicality N-species monoid Frobenius

Critique and response context

The corridor should be read with the Scholze-Stix critique, Mochizuki's response material, and the formalization pages, but these are context layers rather than replacements for the source proof.

IUT IV Source Cluster: Estimates, Output Gates, and Species

This cluster groups the IUT IV pages into the source units a reader should traverse before using the detailed leaf pages. The atomic pages remain available for search and direct citation; this page explains how they fit together in the paper's route from the IUT III log-volume comparison to Diophantine output and then to the set-theoretic language of species.

§1 local and global estimates

Propositions 1.1-1.9 build the numerical environment: tensor-product differents, nonarchimedean and archimedean log-volume estimates, prime distribution, weighted averages, elliptic torsion input, and arithmetic divisor degrees.

local estimates prime/averaging elliptic torsion arithmetic divisors

Theorem 1.10 gate

Theorem 1.10 is the numerical point where the IUT III comparison machinery is consumed. Its pages separate hypotheses, local estimate steps, and the final theta-pilot bound so that a formalization does not hide the Corollary 3.12 dependency inside elementary estimates.

1.10 hub hypotheses local steps final bound

Corollary 2.2 grouped gate

Corollary 2.2 is best read as one application gate: lambda-line input, bounded-discrepancy height comparison, finite exceptions, auxiliary-prime and Galois-image selection, and the C1/C2 numerical output.

unified page lambda/j BD heights exceptions prime/Galois C1/C2

Corollary 2.3 output

Corollary 2.3 transfers the lambda-line output to the Vojta-style Diophantine inequality, with GenEll marking the external boundary between IUT IV's estimate and general hyperbolic-curve consequences.

2.3 hub statement GenEll boundary comparison remarks

§3 species and mutations

Section 3 is not a numerical afterthought. It supplies the formal vocabulary for species, mutation, model-free specimens, and universe changes that later formalization discussions use to describe inter-universal transport without identifying incompatible structures.

species hub universes 0/1-species mutations ring incompatibility

Why this is one unit

IUT IV has two connected functions in the wiki: it records how the Corollary 3.12 comparison is turned into explicit Diophantine inequalities, and it records the set-theoretic language used to keep inter-universal object types separate.

application layertype discipline
Reading rule: for Corollary 2.2-style material, prefer the grouped page first. Use the leaf pages when checking a particular hypothesis, finite-exception step, or numerical output condition.
Source: IUT IV Related: IUT IV atlas Corollary 2.2 unified gate 3.12 corridor cluster coverage map

Tempered Covering Complements

IUT I Section 2 supplies group-theoretic control over tempered covering subgroups before the Hodge-theater construction starts. The point is to recognize decomposition and inertia data inside tempered and profinite fundamental groups, so that later anabelian reconstruction has a rigid source interface.

Tempered subgroup control from sub-semi-graphs to arithmetic exact sequences
Source itemStatement shapeWiki consequence
Setup before Proposition 2.1 The tempered fundamental group Pi_G^tp embeds naturally in the pro-hat Sigma completion hat Pi_G. A connected sub-semi-graph H subset G gives decomposition subgroups Pi_H^tp and hat Pi_H. Decomposition subgroups are not informal labels; they are subgroups inside the ambient tempered/profinite group system.
Proposition 2.1 If a profinite conjugate of a nontrivial compact subgroup of Pi_G^tp lands back in Pi_G^tp, then the conjugating element already lies in Pi_G^tp. This prevents hidden profinite motion from producing a new tempered realization indistinguishable at the compact-subgroup level.
Proposition 2.2 The decomposition subgroups associated to sub-semi-graphs are commensurably terminal in the corresponding ambient groups. Commensurators do not enlarge the subgroup beyond the subgroup itself; this is the rigidity used by later reconstruction arguments.
Corollary 2.3 For a stable hyperbolic curve over an MLF, sub-semi-graphs determine subgroups Delta_X,H and Pi_X,H, their profinite closures, and exact sequences over G_k under the stated slimness hypotheses. The curve-level arithmetic subgroup is tied to the graph-level component data, so later local prime-strip inputs can be read group-theoretically.
Corollary 2.3(vi) A cusp inertia group lies in a conjugate of the subgroup attached to H exactly when the corresponding cusp of the stable model meets a component contained in H. Inertia data detects geometric incidence with selected components; it is not merely external decoration on the group.
Proposition 2.4 The profinite-conjugacy rigidity of Proposition 2.1 has arithmetic analogues for the geometric and arithmetic tempered fundamental groups of X/k. The same rigidity principle survives the passage from graph coverings to arithmetic curve fundamental groups.
Corollary 2.5 In the full profinite case, a profinite decomposition or inertia group is contained in the tempered fundamental group exactly when it is the corresponding tempered decomposition or inertia group. A profinite conjugate of the tempered group containing such a subgroup must equal the tempered group. This is the theorem-level form of Theorem B from the introduction. Later applications use especially the cusp-inertia portion.
Theorem 2.6 A discrete analogue is proved for groups virtually free or orientable surface groups, with a stronger conclusion when the relevant intersection is nonabelian. The section records both the tempered arithmetic result and the classical group-theoretic shadow behind it.

Profinite conjugacy rigidity

Proposition 2.1 is the central local rigidity lemma of the section. It says that a profinite conjugator that sends a nontrivial compact subgroup back into the tempered group is already a tempered element.

Proposition 2.1compact subgroup

Commensurably terminal subgroups

Proposition 2.2 turns the conjugacy rigidity into commensurator control for decomposition subgroups attached to connected sub-semi-graphs. This keeps the subgroup boundary sharp under profinite completion.

Proposition 2.2decomposition

Sub-semi-graph subgroups

Section 2 treats a connected sub-semi-graph as determining compatible tempered and pro-completed subgroups. These are the group-theoretic form of selecting components in the stable reduction graph.

IUT I §2 setupsubgraph H

Arithmetic compact conjugates

Proposition 2.4 repeats the rigidity phenomenon for arithmetic compact subgroups inside the tempered fundamental group of a hyperbolic curve over a mixed-characteristic local field.

Proposition 2.4MLF curve

Tempered decomposition recognition

Corollary 2.5 identifies which profinite decomposition and inertia groups actually come from the tempered fundamental group. It also states that a profinite conjugate of the tempered group containing one such subgroup is already the original tempered group.

Corollary 2.5Theorem B

Inertia cusp test

Corollary 2.3(vi) gives the useful incidence test: membership of a cusp inertia group in the selected subgroup is equivalent to the corresponding stable-model cusp meeting a component contained in the selected subgraph.

Corollary 2.3(vi)cusps

Discrete analogue

Theorem 2.6 isolates a parallel statement for groups with finite-index free or orientable surface subgroups. Its role in the wiki is diagnostic: the tempered statement has a recognizable classical group-theoretic analogue, but the IUT application uses the tempered arithmetic version.

Theorem 2.6free/surface groups
Formalization note: this page should eventually become a library interface for subgroup recognition and terminality lemmas. It should not be replaced by an opaque field saying that decomposition subgroups exist, because Section 2 proves how they behave inside the ambient completed groups.
Sources: IUT I, §2 Related: IUT I atlas IUT I coverings/symmetries anabelian background prime strips and bridges

IUT I Propositions 2.1-2.2: Tempered Terminality

Propositions 2.1 and 2.2 are the first precise rigidity layer of IUT I Section 2. They say that a profinite motion that returns nontrivial compact tempered subgroup data to the tempered world is already a tempered motion, and hence that the relevant decomposition subgroups have no larger commensurator inside the completed group.

From compact subgroup test to commensurable terminality IUT I, Propositions 2.1-2.2
Source itemPrecise roleDo not weaken to
Setup before Proposition 2.1 The tempered fundamental group Pi_G^tp is regarded as a subgroup of its pro-Sigma completion Pi_G. For a connected sub-semi-graph H, the groups Pi_H^tp and Pi_H are decomposition subgroups, well-defined up to conjugacy. A mere label saying “the subgroup associated to H exists.” The ambient inclusions and completions are part of the statement.
Proposition 2.1 If Lambda is a nontrivial compact subgroup of Pi_G^tp, gamma in Pi_G, and gamma Lambda gamma^-1 subset Pi_G^tp, then gamma in Pi_G^tp. A general claim that all profinite conjugators are tempered. The compact subgroup hypothesis is the detector.
Proof of Proposition 2.1 The proof passes through universal tempered and pro-Sigma semi-graphs. Compact subgroups lie in verticial subgroups; the nontrivial intersection after conjugation forces the corresponding vertices to be equal or adjacent; temperedness of the conjugated vertex forces the conjugator to be tempered. A black-box conjugacy principle detached from graph vertices and adjacency.
Proposition 2.2 Pi_H and Pi_H^tp are commensurably terminal in the corresponding ambient groups, and Pi_G^tp is commensurably terminal in Pi_G. Normalizer control only. The source statement is commensurator control, hence stronger in the intended subgroup boundary sense.
Remarks 2.2.1-2.2.2 Mochizuki records generality beyond the displayed PSC-type case and notes that, for all primes, the proof gives an alternative route to normal terminality of finitely generated free groups in their profinite completions. Treating Section 2 as a disposable side lemma. It is a reusable rigidity mechanism behind later arithmetic recognition.

Tempered vertex detector

The source proof detects whether a pro-Sigma vertex is tempered by comparing it with images of vertices from the universal tempered covering. This is the geometric content behind the compact-subgroup test.

Prop. 2.1 proofvertices

Nontrivial compact subgroup test

The subgroup Lambda must be nontrivial and compact. This lets the proof place both Lambda and its conjugate inside verticial subgroups before invoking the equal-or-adjacent vertex criterion.

Prop. 2.1compactness

Commensurator chain collapse

Proposition 2.2 uses Proposition 2.1 over open subgroups of verticial subgroups, then combines the resulting terminality with the pro- Sigma analogue of the subgroup equality C(Pi_H)=Pi_H.

Prop. 2.2commensurator
Sources: IUT I, §2, Propositions 2.1-2.2 Related: tempered covering complements subgraph arithmetic groups 2.3 arithmetic recognition 2.4-2.5

Compact Conjugator Test in IUT I Proposition 2.1

Proposition 2.1 is the local detector behind Section 2. If a nontrivial compact subgroup of the tempered fundamental group is moved by a profinite element and lands back inside the tempered group, then the moving element was already tempered. The proof reads compact subgroup data through verticial subgroups in universal tempered and pro-Sigma covering graphs.

Landing back in the tempered group forces a tempered conjugator IUT I Proposition 2.1
HypothesisMeaningUsed for
Nontrivial compact subgroup Lambda subset Pi_G^tp is compact and not the trivial subgroup. Places Lambda inside a verticial subgroup of the tempered covering.
Profinite conjugator gamma in Pi_G and gamma Lambda gamma^-1 subset Pi_G^tp. Creates two verticial subgroups whose images intersect nontrivially.
Vertex criterion The corresponding pro-Sigma vertices are equal or adjacent. Shows the conjugated vertex is tempered, hence gamma is tempered.
The theorem is not a generic conjugacy-closedness statement. Its detector is the nontrivial compact subgroup and the proof depends on verticial subgroup containment in the universal covering graphs.
Sources: IUT I Proposition 2.1 Related: tempered terminality commensurable terminality arithmetic tempered recognition

Commensurable Terminality in IUT I Proposition 2.2

Proposition 2.2 turns the compact conjugator test into commensurator control. For a connected sub-semi-graph H, the associated decomposition subgroups Pi_H and Pi_H^tp are commensurably terminal in their ambient completed and tempered groups.

Commensurator chains collapse to the subgroup itself IUT I Proposition 2.2

Stronger than normalizer control

The proposition controls commensurators, not only normalizers. This matters because the subgroup boundary is being tested up to finite-index overlap.

Subgroup source discipline

Pi_H^tp and Pi_H are not arbitrary subgroups; they come from a connected sub-semi-graph and are treated as decomposition subgroups inside Pi_G.

Sources: IUT I Proposition 2.2 Related: compact conjugator test subgraph arithmetic groups tempered covering complements

IUT I Corollary 2.3: Sub-Semi-Graph Arithmetic Subgroups

Corollary 2.3 transports the Section 2 terminality mechanism from an abstract semi-graph of anabelioids to the tempered and profinite fundamental groups of a hyperbolic curve over a mixed-characteristic local field.
