Orbit route Delta-valuations are those mapping through the distinguished V^±un orbit associated to the cusp-label transport. The definition depends on cusp-label transport, not merely on a place.
D-NF route The same valuations are read from the unique D_j whose label torsor sends delta to label 1. This is the bridge-native formulation needed for later localization.
Delta-valuations are not an arbitrary chosen subset of valuations. They are reconstructed from the D-NF bridge and the global cusp-label torsor.
Sources: IUT I, Example 5.4(iii) Related: D-ThetaNF bridges F|delta localization global cusp labels

F|delta Localization in IUT I 5.4

Example 5.4(iv) localizes an F-prime-strip at delta-valuations to construct F|delta. The resulting object is well-defined only up to isomorphism, because the map from the universal valuation set to the model valuation set is not injective and no canonical representative is specified.

1. Delta-valuations

The valuation subset is read from the D-NF bridge and cusp-label data; no fiber representative is selected.

2. Localized F|delta

Localizing at that subset gives an F-prime-strip well-defined up to isomorphism.

3. ∞κ compatibility

Associated Kummer classes restrict to the local/global coric structures across the induced D-side poly-morphism.

This page is the guardrail against turning localization into a function that chooses representatives. The paper's construction is relational up to isomorphism.
Sources: IUT I, Example 5.4(iv) Related: delta-valuations NF poly-morphism local κ-coric structures

NF Poly-Morphism in IUT I 5.4

Example 5.4(v) applies Corollary 5.3(ii) to obtain a unique poly-morphism psi^NF : F_J -> F lying over the D-NF bridge phi^NF. The uniqueness is inherited from the D-prime-strip-to-F-prime-strip rigidity already established in Corollary 5.3.

InputOutputDependency
phi^NF on D-prime-strips psi^NF on F-prime-strips Corollary 5.3(ii)
Capsule F_J Target F D-NF bridge, not D-Theta alone
Formalization consequence: this is a good boundary for replacing an oracle arrow by a theorem using the earlier rigidity interface.
Sources: IUT I, Example 5.4(v) Related: Corollary 5.3 rigidity theta poly-morphisms restriction functors

Restriction Functors in IUT I 5.4

Example 5.4(vi) extracts isomorphism classes of restriction functors from the poly-morphism data. The construction is independent of the chosen poly-morphism inside its F_l-conjugacy class; the output is therefore an isomorphism class rather than a selected functor.

Diagonal route

A poly-morphism F_<J> -> F induces restriction from the global/model Frobenioid toward the local constituents.

Example 5.4(vi)diagonal

Capsule route

The same construction is stated for F_J -> F_J and for each F_j -> F_j.

capsulelocal factors

Realification

The paper also records the analogous realified restriction functors, matching the realified Frobenioid prime-strip layer.

realifiedF⊩
The independence from F_l-conjugate choices is part of the statement. A model with a chosen representative has added structure unless it also quotients or proves independence.
Sources: IUT I, Example 5.4(vi) Related: F|delta localization pivotal distribution realified strips

Pivotal Distribution in IUT I 5.4

Example 5.4(vii) names the restriction-functor objects at the distinguished label j = 1 the pivotal distributions, together with their realified versions. The definition depends on the preceding capsule, localization, NF poly-morphism, and restriction-functor stages.

StageNeeded before pivotal distributionReason
Labels F_j, F_J, F_<J> The distinguished label 1 must live in the capsule structure.
Localization F|delta up to isomorphism The local Frobenioid strip is obtained from delta-valuations.
Restriction Isomorphism classes of restriction functors The pivotal distribution is one named output of this restriction machinery.
Sources: IUT I, Example 5.4(vii) Related: restriction functors labeled F-copies ThetaNF bridges

Average/Sum Convention in IUT I 5.4.2

Remark 5.4.2 fixes a sign-sensitive bookkeeping convention for log-volume computations. One may read degrees attached to F_<J> as averages over J ~= F_l, producing a factor of l relative to a single F_j. When the Frobenioid isomorphisms are used directly, the same comparison is read as a sum over labels, without dividing by l.

Average reading

The diagonal object records degree data as an average over the label set. This is the convention that displays the factor relative to one labeled component.

averagedegrees

Sum reading

Under Frobenioid isomorphisms, the comparison may be written as a sum over the labels. The paper explicitly says not to divide by l in this reading.

sumlog-volume
This convention is important downstream: log-volume pages should state whether a displayed expression uses the averaged diagonal convention or the summed Frobenioid-isomorphism convention.
Sources: IUT I, Remark 5.4.2 Related: pivotal distribution tensor/log-volume corridor packet/procession log-volume

IUT I Definition 5.5 and Corollary 5.6: ThetaNF Bridges

Definition 5.5 is the Frobenioid-theoretic lift of Definition 4.6. It defines NF-bridges, Theta-bridges, and ThetaNF-Hodge theaters over a shared capsule of F-prime-strips. Corollary 5.6 proves that their isomorphism theory is governed by the associated D-level bridges.

Frobenioid lift of D-ThetaNF Definition 5.5 and Corollary 5.6
Source itemPaper contentWiki consequence
Def. 5.5(i) An NF-bridge consists of an F-prime-strip capsule F_J, Frobenioid categories F and F*, the morphism F -> F*, and a poly-morphism lifting a D-NF bridge. This is the Frobenioid lift of the number-field side.
Def. 5.5(ii) A Theta-bridge consists of F_J, F_>, a Theta-Hodge theater, and poly-morphisms determined by a D-Theta bridge. This is the Frobenioid lift of the theta/evaluation side.
Def. 5.5(iii) A ThetaNF-Hodge theater glues the NF and Theta bridges, requiring their associated D-data to form a D-ThetaNF-Hodge theater and requiring morphisms to induce the same bijection on F-capsule indices. The shared capsule condition survives the Frobenioid lift.
Cor. 5.6(i) Isomorphisms of Theta-Hodge theaters are bijective with isomorphisms of their associated D-prime-strips. The underlying D-layer controls theater isomorphism.
Cor. 5.6(ii) Isomorphisms of NF-bridges, Theta-bridges, and ThetaNF-Hodge theaters are bijective with isomorphisms of the associated D-NF, D-Theta, and D-ThetaNF bridge data. The F-level bridge package is rigid over the D-level bridge package.
Cor. 5.6(iii) Compatible gluing poly-isomorphisms between the F-prime-strip capsules of an NF-bridge and Theta-bridge form an F_l-torsor. Gluing indeterminacy is inherited from Section 4.
Remark 5.6.1 The functorial dynamics of Section 4 have analogues for the Section 5 NF-bridges, Theta-bridges, and ThetaNF-Hodge theaters. Section 5 is a controlled lift, not a separate combinatorial theory.
Encoding rule: every ThetaNF-Hodge theater page should expose the associated D-ThetaNF-Hodge theater. Corollary 5.6 makes that associated D-data the controlling isomorphism layer.
Sources: IUT I, Definition 5.5, Corollary 5.6, Remark 5.6.1 Related: ThetaNF Frobenioid lift D-ThetaNF bridges Section 4 symmetries/processions Theta±ell bridges

NF-Bridge Definition in IUT I 5.5

Definition 5.5(i) defines an NF-bridge as the Frobenioid lift of a D-NF bridge. It consists of an F-prime-strip capsule F_J = {F_j}_{j in J}, two Frobenioid categories modeled on the global categories of Example 5.1(iii), the structural arrow between their base categories, and a uniquely lifted poly-morphism over a D-NF bridge.

ComponentDefinition 5.5(i)Audit point
F_J A capsule of F-prime-strips indexed by a finite set J, with associated D-capsule D_J. The F-capsule and D-capsule must be tracked together.
F, F* Categories equivalent to the global Frobenioid categories of Example 5.1(iii), with base categories D, D*. The bridge is not just a map of labels; it carries Frobenioid structure.
psi^NF A poly-morphism lifting uniquely, by Corollary 5.3(ii), a D-NF bridge phi^NF : D_J -> D. The F-level arrow is controlled by D-level rigidity.
Sources: IUT I, Definition 5.5(i) Related: NF poly-morphism 5.4 Corollary 5.3 rigidity bridge rigidity

Theta-Bridge Definition in IUT I 5.5

Definition 5.5(ii) defines a Theta-bridge as the Frobenioid lift of a D-Theta bridge. It combines the same kind of F-prime-strip capsule F_J, a Theta-Hodge theater HT^Theta, its tautologically associated theta-side F-prime-strip F_>, and the family of poly-morphisms determined by a D-Theta bridge.

Capsule

The source capsule is again {F_j}_{j in J}, with associated D-prime-strips D_J.

Def. 5.5(ii)(a)capsule

Theater side

The target object F_> is tautologically associated to the Theta-Hodge theater, with associated D-strip D_>.

Def. 5.5(ii)(b-c)Theta

Lifted arrows

The family psi^Theta_j : F_j -> F_> is determined by the D-Theta bridge family phi^Theta_j : D_j -> D_>.

Def. 5.5(ii)(d)lift
Sources: IUT I, Definition 5.5(ii) Related: theta poly-morphisms 5.4 Theta-Hodge theater 3.6 Theta isomorphisms

ThetaNF-Hodge Theater in IUT I 5.5

Definition 5.5(iii) glues an NF-bridge and a Theta-bridge over the same F-prime-strip capsule. The associated D-data {phi^NF, phi^Theta} must form a D-ThetaNF-Hodge theater, and a morphism of ThetaNF-Hodge theaters is a compatible pair of NF-bridge and Theta-bridge morphisms inducing the same bijection on capsule indices.

1. NF side

F <- F_J is an NF-bridge lifting a D-NF bridge.

2. Theta side

F_J -> F_> -> HT^Theta is a Theta-bridge lifting a D-Theta bridge.

3. Shared index motion

Bridge morphisms must induce the same bijection on the capsule index set.

The shared capsule is structural. A formal model that stores independent NF-side and Theta-side index maps has not yet encoded Definition 5.5(iii).
Sources: IUT I, Definition 5.5(iii) Related: NF-bridge Theta-bridge D-ThetaNF theater

Theta-Hodge Isomorphisms in IUT I 5.6

Corollary 5.6(i) states that the functorially induced map from isomorphisms of Theta-Hodge theaters to isomorphisms of their associated D-prime-strips is bijective. The proof uses the fact that the associated F-prime-strip contains all but the explicitly listed global and bad-place tempered Frobenioid data, and then applies Corollary 5.3.

Proof ingredientSource statementConsequence
Associated F_> Most Theta-Hodge theater data is contained in the associated F-prime-strip. The theater isomorphism problem descends to the strip interface.
Global data recovery The remaining global data is obtained by the functorial algorithm of Remark 5.2.1(ii). No extra arbitrary choices are added at this stage.
Corollary 5.3 Parts (ii) and (iv) control associated F-prime-strips and bad-place tempered Frobenioids. The induced map to D-prime-strip isomorphisms is bijective.
Sources: IUT I, Corollary 5.6(i) Related: Theta-Hodge theater F-to-D algorithms Corollary 5.3 rigidity

Bridge Isomorphism Rigidity in IUT I 5.6

Corollary 5.6(ii) extends the rigidity statement from Theta-Hodge theaters to NF-bridges, Theta-bridges, and ThetaNF-Hodge theaters. In each case, the natural map from F-level isomorphisms to the corresponding D-level bridge isomorphisms is bijective.

F-level objectD-level controllerReason this matters
NF-bridge D-NF bridge The number-field side remains rigid over its D-prime-strip data.
Theta-bridge D-Theta bridge The theta-side lift carries no independent isomorphism freedom.
ThetaNF-Hodge theater D-ThetaNF-Hodge theater Compatibility of the two bridge sides is checked at the D-level.
This is the Section 5 analogue of the anti-drift rule used throughout the wiki: F-level bridge records should expose their associated D-level record as the isomorphism controller.
Sources: IUT I, Corollary 5.6(ii) Related: NF-bridge Theta-bridge ThetaNF theater

Gluing Torsor in IUT I 5.6

Corollary 5.6(iii) states that, given an NF-bridge and a Theta-bridge, the capsule-full poly-isomorphisms between their F-prime-strip capsules that allow the two bridges to glue into a ThetaNF-Hodge theater form an F_l-torsor.

Input

One NF-bridge and one Theta-bridge, each with an F-prime-strip capsule.

givenbridges

Allowed gluings

Capsule-full poly-isomorphisms that make the combined data a ThetaNF-Hodge theater.

poly-isomorphismscompatibility

Structure

The set of such gluings is an F_l-torsor.

Cor. 5.6(iii)torsor
This page intentionally uses F_l-torsor, following the local IUT I text for Corollary 5.6(iii). The earlier multiplicative Section 4 labels use the quotient-by-sign notation on separate pages.
Sources: IUT I, Corollary 5.6(iii) Related: ThetaNF theater Section 4 symmetries bridge rigidity

Prime Strips and Bridges

Prime-strips are valuation-indexed interfaces extracted from Hodge-theater data. They are not decorative labels: D-prime-strips, their mono-analytic versions, F-prime-strips, and bridge data determine which information is retained when the theory moves between holomorphic and mono-analytic views.

Prime-strip interface holomorphic data, mono-analyticization, and bridges

D-prime-strip

IUT I Definition 4.1 defines a D-prime-strip as a collection {D_v}_{v in V}. At nonarchimedean places these are categories equivalent to the local D-data; at archimedean places they are Aut-holomorphic orbispaces.

Mono-analyticization

The associated D⊢-prime-strip keeps the mono-analytic information obtained by passing to the appropriate subcategories or topological-monoid objects. This is one controlled way to weaken holomorphic history.

Label classes

Label classes of cusps are reconstructed from the prime-strip and carry an F_l-torsor structure together with a canonical class determined by the initial cusp datum.

D-NF bridge

A D-NF bridge is a poly-morphism from a capsule of D-prime-strips to a number-field side D-prime-strip. It tracks the number-field component of a bridge without conflating it with theta evaluation.

D-Theta bridge

A D-Theta bridge is a poly-morphism from a capsule of D-prime-strips to a theta-side D-prime-strip. At bad places it transports the label information used later by theta-value and Gaussian constructions.

D-ThetaNF theater

A D-ThetaNF-Hodge theater combines compatible D-NF and D-Theta bridges over the same capsule. This compatibility is an early instance of the "same interface, different permitted histories" discipline.

Formalization checkpoint: a Lean model should distinguish the strip object, the mono-analyticized strip, the capsule index set, and the two bridge directions. Collapsing these loses exactly the bookkeeping needed later in the log-theta lattice.
Prime-stripBad-prime model in IUT I, Fig. I1.2Interpretive roleLater use
D Pi_v, via tempered coverings at bad places and finite etale coverings at good places. Holomorphic local arithmetic structure attached to the curve data. Base object for D-NF and D-Theta bridges.
D⊢ G_v, via the corresponding Galois-category data. Mono-analyticization of D; keeps the underlying arithmetic-real-analytic information. Processions of D⊢-prime-strips generate tensor packets in IUT III.
F O_Fv^▹ with its natural group action. Frobenioid/monoid interface for divisor-like multiplicative data. Theta links compare F-prime-strips built from theta values and q-parameters.
F⊢ O_Fv^× × q_v^N. Mono-analytic Frobenioid interface retaining unit and q-power data. Input to weakened F⊢× and F⊢×μ variants.
F⊢× O_Fv^×. Unit portion after dropping q-power value data. Appears in the original theta-link preservation statements.
F⊢×μ O_Fv^× / O_Fv^μ. Unit quotient modulo roots of unity. Central weakened portion in Theta×μ, Gaussian, and LGP links.
F⊢×μ + q (O_Fv^× / O_Fv^μ) × q_v^N. Unit quotient together with q-power value data. Tracks the split between unit data and value-group data.

Local analytic sections

Remark 4.3.1 interprets D-prime-strips as arithmetic collections of local analytic sections of Spec(K) -> Spec(F_mod). This is how IUT simulates a global multiplicative subspace and a global canonical generator without an actual global scheme-theoretic section.

Cost of the simulation

The same remark emphasizes that this simulation dismantles the global prime structure into valuation-indexed local pieces. The resulting bookkeeping is not cosmetic: it is why later bridge, label, Frobenioid, and log-shell structures must remain carefully typed.

Single global object

Remark 4.9.2 explains why a D-NF bridge uses one global D-object rather than independent globalizations for each local prime-strip. The single global object synchronizes the capsule index set with the labels 1,...,l* up to the controlled F_l^*/±-action.

Remark 4.9.2synchronization

Why labels must be compared

Remark 4.9.1 says the later theta evaluation produces arithmetic line bundles at different labeled D-prime-strips, and the theory needs to compare their arithmetic degrees as log-volumes. This is the reason for passing to non-labeled output data.

Remark 4.9.1log-volumes

Cyclic versus full symmetry

Proposition 4.9 distinguishes the cyclic F_l^*/±-symmetry retained by the D-NF bridge from the full symmetric-group freedom obtained after forgetting the cyclic label structure of the capsule. These are different comparison regimes.

Proposition 4.9forgetful functor

Processions minimize indeterminacy

After labels are hidden enough to compare different D-prime-strips, IUT I introduces processions to reduce the resulting label indeterminacy without giving up the symmetry needed for comparison. This is the first appearance of the procession discipline used later in IUT III.

Proposition 4.11processions

Global label classes

Proposition 4.7 constructs a unique global cusp class whose images identify the global label-class torsor with the capsule index set. This replaces a fragile local label choice by data native to the global side of the bridge.

Proposition 4.7LabCusp

Torsor/uniqueness split

Proposition 4.8 locates ambiguity precisely: D-NF bridge isomorphisms and compatible gluings form F_l^*/±-torsors, while D-Theta bridge and D-ThetaNF theater isomorphism sets have cardinality one. The wiki treats this as a formalization boundary.

Proposition 4.8ambiguity
Sources: IUT I, Definition 4.1 and Definition 4.6 IUT II, Definition 4.9 IUT III, Definition 1.1 Related: base bridges/evaluation sections Hodge theaters theta/log links Corollary 3.12

IUT I Section 4: Base Bridges and Evaluation Sections

Section 4 develops the multiplicative combinatorial Teichmuller theory used before thetaNF-Hodge theaters are built. It defines D-prime-strips, label-class torsors, model base-NF and base-theta bridges, bad-prime evaluation sections, and processions that reduce label indeterminacy.

Base bridge route Definition 4.1 through Corollary 4.12

D-prime-strips

Definition 4.1 defines a holomorphic base-prime-strip D = {D_v}_{v in V}. At nonarchimedean places the entries are categories equivalent to the local D-data; at archimedean places they are Aut-holomorphic orbispaces. Its mono-analyticization is a D^⊢-prime-strip.

Definition 4.1(i),(iii)prime strip

Label classes of cusps

Definition 4.1(ii) reconstructs the cusp label classes of each local D_v. Each LabCusp(D_v) is an F_l^* / {±1}-torsor and contains the canonical element determined by the initial cusp epsilon.

Definition 4.1(ii)LabCusp

Global label identification

Proposition 4.2 canonically identifies the local LabCusp(D_v) across all valuations by requiring compatibility with the canonical epsilon classes and the torsor structures. This yields LabCusp(D) and a natural bijection with F_l^* / {±1}.

Proposition 4.2canonical labels

Why X_v, not only C_v

Remark 4.2.1 warns that if good-place D_v were defined from C_v instead of the arrow-decorated X_v data, the canonical system of label bijections would fail for suitable decomposition groups.

Remark 4.2.1source choice

Model base-NF bridge

Example 4.3 constructs the model D_j -> D from the automorphism subgroup fixing epsilon. The quotient Aut(C_K)/Aut_epsilon(C_K) is identified with F_l^* / {±1}, producing the label poly-action on the capsule.

Example 4.3NF side

Local analytic sections

Remark 4.3.1 interprets these D-prime-strips as arithmetic collections of local analytic sections of Spec(K) -> Spec(F). They simulate a global multiplicative subspace, but they do not extend to ring homomorphisms, prime-tree splittings, or global Galois sections.

Remark 4.3.1no global section

Bad-place evaluation points

Example 4.4(i) labels cusps of C_v by |F_l| and translates them by the two-torsion point mu_-. These translated cusps are the evaluation points of X_v.

Example 4.4(i)bad places

Theta values and j^2

At a bad-place evaluation point with label j, the value of the Frobenioid-theoretic theta function lies in the mu_{2l}-orbit of q_v^{j^2}. The points above these evaluation points in the theta cover are defined over K_v.

Example 4.4(i)j squared

Evaluation sections

The K_v-rational points over evaluation points determine sections G_v -> Pi_v. Example 4.4 records that these evaluation sections are group-theoretically reconstructible from isomorphs of Pi_v.

Example 4.4(i)sections

Model base-theta bridge

Example 4.4(ii)-(iv) uses the evaluation sections at bad places and full poly-isomorphisms at good places to build the poly-morphism D_j -> D_>. The target label classes in D_> are held fixed by arbitrary automorphisms.

Example 4.4(ii)-(iv)theta side

Transport of label classes

Example 4.5 composes the base-NF and base-theta bridge restrictions to obtain phi_j^LC : LabCusp(D) -> LabCusp(D_>). The initial epsilon class is characterized as the unique class whose evaluations recover the assignments j -> j.

Example 4.5transport

No artificial synchronization

Remark 4.5.1 analyzes independent conjugacy indeterminacies in local and global groups. At bad places, Corollary 2.5 supplies the needed factorization uniqueness without choosing a synchronization that would amount to an impossible global prime-tree splitting.

Remark 4.5.1indeterminacy

D-ThetaNF theater

Definition 4.6 packages a base-NF bridge D <- D_J, a base-theta bridge D_J -> D_>, and a shared capsule index set. Isomorphisms must induce the same bijection on the bridge capsule indices.

Definition 4.6bridge pair

The zeta bijection

Proposition 4.7 constructs the bijection zeta : LabCusp(D) -> J, identifying global cusp labels with capsule indices. Remark 4.7.2 interprets its inverse as a combinatorial Kodaira-Spencer morphism relating arithmetic labels to geometric cusp data.

Proposition 4.7Kodaira-Spencer

Forgetful symmetries

Proposition 4.9 separates three outputs: the D-NF bridge with cyclic F_l^* / {±1}-symmetry, the holomorphic capsule with full symmetric-group freedom after labels are forgotten, and the mono-analytic capsule with the same label-indeterminacy behavior.

Proposition 4.9forget labels

Processions

Definition 4.10 and Proposition 4.11 replace a bare capsule by nested subcapsules 1 <= ... <= j <= ... <= l*. This reduces the label indeterminacy from the possibilities of Proposition 4.9(ii) to l*!, without abandoning comparison symmetry.

Definition 4.10, Proposition 4.11procession

D-ThetaNF etale-picture

Corollary 4.12 defines the D-ThetaNF link by passing a D-ThetaNF theater to its mono-analytic target strip D_>^⊢. Chains of D-linked D-ThetaNF theaters then share this mono-analytic core and admit arbitrary permutation symmetries among spokes.

Corollary 4.12etale-picture
Section 4 objectSource roleWhy it matters later
LabCusp(D) Canonical torsor of label classes, synchronized by epsilon. Prevents labels from becoming arbitrary names.
D <- D_J NF bridge: local analytic sections of the global number-field side. Simulates the missing global multiplicative subspace.
D_J -> D_> Theta bridge: evaluation-section side, especially at bad primes. Feeds Hodge-Arakelov evaluation and later theta values.
zeta Identifies global cusp labels with capsule indices. Supplies globalizability of labels without local ad hoc choices.
Procession Nested subcapsules of the bridge capsule. Minimizes label indeterminacy before log-volume comparison.
Formalization warning: Section 4 should not be modeled as a generic labeled family of prime-strips. The source distinguishes the NF bridge, theta bridge, evaluation sections, global label-class torsor, and procession operation.
Sources: IUT I, Section 4 Related: prime strips and bridges theta evaluation route Galois theta evaluation additive base bridges multiplicative/additive bridges Corollary 3.12 corridor

IUT I Section 6: Additive Base Bridges

Section 6 starts the additive combinatorial Teichmuller theory. Before the Frobenioid lift of Definition 6.11, Mochizuki defines signed additive label structures, D-Theta± bridges, D-Thetaell bridges, their theater, and the functorial route back to ordinary D-Theta bridges.

Additive base route Definition 6.1 through Corollary 6.10
Source itemDefinition or resultFormalization consequence
Definition 6.1(i) F_l^± = F_l ⋊ {±1}. An F_l^±-group is a signed additive group object; an F_l^±-torsor is acted on by affine maps z -> ±z + lambda. The group/torsor distinction is typed data. Translations, signs, and positive automorphisms are separate interfaces.
Definition 6.1(iii) LabCusp±(D_v) has a zero element and a ±-canonical element. Removing zero and quotienting by ± recovers LabCusp(D_v). The zero label is not cosmetic. It must remain present until the explicit nonzero/quotient step.
Definition 6.1(v) D± = B(X_K)^0. From this category one reconstructs the relevant automorphism quotients, the F_l^± torsor of ±-label classes, and V±; Remark 6.1.1 identifies V± = V±un. The additive target is global elliptic geometry, not an arbitrary relabeled D-prime-strip.
Examples 6.2 and 6.3 The model D-Theta± bridge uses positive +-full maps D_t -> D≻. The model D-Thetaell bridge maps D_t -> D± and carries the natural F_l^± poly-action. The two bridge models have the same capsule but different target logic: signed positive maps on one side, elliptic torsor equivariance on the other.
Definition 6.4 A D-Theta±ell theater is the compatible span D≻ <- D_T -> D±. The construction also forms the quotient capsule D_|T| and the nonzero subcapsule used later. A formal model needs the full capsule, the ± quotient, and the nonzero subcapsule as related but distinct objects.
Propositions 6.5 and 6.6 Proposition 6.5 transports ±-label classes through both bridges. Proposition 6.6 identifies the isomorphism and gluing torsors: {±1} x {±1}^V, F_l^±, {±1}, and F_l^± x {±1}^V. These torsor sizes are audit targets: they locate exactly which indeterminacy is supposed to remain.
Proposition 6.7 From a D-Theta± bridge one functorially constructs an ordinary D-Theta bridge by passing to the nonzero quotient part, identifying D_0 and D≻, and replacing +-full maps by the appropriate full/evaluation maps. This is the formal hinge between additive and multiplicative bridge theory; it cannot be replaced by a bare forgetful map.
Propositions 6.8-6.9, Corollary 6.10 Forgetful functors expose F_l^± and symmetric group symmetries, processions reduce the (l±)^(l±) ambiguity to l±!, and D-Theta±ell links produce an etale picture with a mono-analytic core. The wiki keeps labels, quotient capsules, processions, and mono-analytic cores as separate pages because later comparison arguments use different forgetful levels.

Group versus torsor

In Definition 6.4, T is an F_l^±-group for the D-Theta± bridge, but an F_l^±-torsor for the D-Thetaell bridge. The theater uses the torsor structure determined by the group structure.

Definition 6.4typed labels

Zero label

The zero label is the point that lets Section 6 compare zero-labeled and nonzero-labeled prime-strips before the nonzero labels are matched with the multiplicative Section 4 system.

Definition 6.1zero/nonzero

Functorial hinge

Proposition 6.7 is the step that turns the signed additive bridge into a D-Theta bridge. It uses both the quotient capsule and the evaluation maps of Section 4 at bad places.

Proposition 6.7bridge conversion

Additive processions

Proposition 6.9 builds processions from the ordered subsets {0,...,t-1} of |F_l|. This is the additive counterpart of the Section 4 procession discipline.

Proposition 6.9l±!

Additive etale-picture

Corollary 6.10 sends linked D-Theta±ell theaters to full poly-isomorphisms between the associated mono-analytic D_>^⊢ cores. The spokes retain distinct holomorphic structures.

Corollary 6.10mono-analytic core

Modeling warning

A generic signed capsule misses the source distinction between positive +-full maps, the global D± target, the F_l^± torsor action, and the later nonzero quotient used by Proposition 6.7.

formalization boundary
Sources: IUT I, Section 6 Related: multiplicative/additive bridges Theta±ell bridges 6.11 Theta±ellNF 6.13 base bridges/evaluation sections Theta±ell relay

IUT I Definition 6.1: Signed Additive Labels

Definition 6.1 introduces the additive label structure used in Section 6: signed affine motion by F_l^± = F_l ⋊ {±1}, ±-label classes of cusps, positive and negative automorphisms, and the global double cover D± = B(X_K)^0.

Signed additive label data Definition 6.1(i)-(vii)
ClauseSource contentWiki/formalization consequence
6.1(i) F_l^± is the semidirect product F_l ⋊ {±1}. Groups use signed linear coordinates; torsors use signed affine coordinates. Group and torsor pages must not be merged. The bridge definitions use both structures for different purposes.
6.1(ii)-(iv) A D-prime-strip determines D_v^±; LabCusp±(D_v) carries zero, ±-canonical class, and positive/negative automorphism data. The zero label and sign quotient are part of the mathematical object, not merely display conventions.
6.1(v)-(vii) D± = B(X_K)^0 reconstructs automorphism quotients, cusp classes, valuations, and archimedean Aut-holomorphic morphism targets. The global target page must cite elliptic-curve reconstruction and the category-theoretic origin of the torsor, not just label arithmetic.
Guardrail: Definition 6.1 is not a generic additive labeling device. It packages signed affine motion, cusp reconstruction, the zero/nonzero transition, and the global D± target that later receives the Thetaell bridge.
Sources: IUT I, Definition 6.1 Related: Section 6 atlas Theta±/Thetaell models D-Theta±ell base theater zero/nonzero relation

F_l± Groups and Torsors in IUT I 6.1

Definition 6.1(i) introduces F_l^± = F_l ⋊ {±1}. An F_l^±-group is a set equipped with a {±1}-orbit of bijections to F_l; an F_l^±-torsor is a set equipped with an F_l^±-orbit of bijections to F_l, where the action is affine: z -> ±z + lambda.

StructureDefinition 6.1(i)Why separate it
F_l^±-group Signed linear coordinate ambiguity; it induces an F_l-module structure. Used for automorphism groups and sign-sensitive linear data.
F_l^±-torsor Signed affine coordinate ambiguity z -> ±z + lambda. Used for label sets where there is no preferred origin.
Aut^+(T) The translation subgroup z -> z + lambda, with Aut^+(T) subset Aut^±(T) of index two. Positive automorphisms define the later +-full poly-isomorphism interface.
Sources: IUT I, Definition 6.1(i) Related: ± cusp labels +-full poly-isomorphisms model additive bridges

D_v± Reconstruction in IUT I 6.1

Definition 6.1(ii) assigns to a D-prime-strip D = {D_v}_{v in V} a collection D^± = {D_v^±}_{v in V}, functorially determined by D. At nonarchimedean places this is obtained from the topological group corresponding to X_v; at archimedean places it is obtained as an Aut-holomorphic orbispace.

Nonarchimedean

pi_1(D_v) determines the larger topological group corresponding to X_v, and hence D_v^±.

6.1(ii)V_non

Archimedean

The Aut-holomorphic reconstruction uses the K_v-core argument to obtain the orbispace corresponding to X_v.

6.1(ii)V_arc

Natural morphism

The source records natural morphisms D_v -> D_v^±, assembled across V.

functoriallocal
Sources: IUT I, Definition 6.1(ii) Related: additive labels D-prime-strips ± cusp labels

± Cusp Labels in IUT I 6.1

Definition 6.1(iii) defines LabCusp±(D_v) as ±-label classes of cusps of D_v lying over a single cusp of D_v^±. These label classes carry a zero element, a ±-canonical element well-defined up to sign, and a natural F_l^±-group structure.

Label datumDefinition 6.1(iii)Consequence
Zero element eta_v^0 in LabCusp±(D_v). The additive label set includes a distinguished zero class.
±-canonical element eta_v^± is well-defined up to multiplication by ±1. The sign ambiguity is part of the object, not a notation issue.
Relation to Section 4 labels (LabCusp±(D_v) minus {eta_v^0})/{±1} ≃ LabCusp(D_v). The additive labels refine the earlier nonzero quotient labels.
Automorphism signs Aut(D_v) maps to {±1}, defining positive and negative automorphisms. Sign classes feed directly into +-full poly-isomorphisms.
Sources: IUT I, Definition 6.1(iii) Related: F_l± groups/torsors Section 4 cusp labels +-full poly-isomorphisms

+-Full Poly-Isomorphisms in IUT I 6.1

Definition 6.1(iv) defines a +-full poly-isomorphism as the orbit of an isomorphism under positive automorphisms. For one local component, the self-case has exactly two such poly-isomorphisms, positive and negative. For a full D-prime-strip, self +-full poly-isomorphisms are indexed by {±1}^V.

LevelDefinition 6.1(iv)Formal guardrail
Local component Orbit under Aut^+(D_v); in the self-case there are positive and non-positive classes. Do not identify all full poly-isomorphisms with positive ones.
Whole strip Self +-full poly-isomorphisms correspond naturally to {±1}^V. Signs vary by valuation.
Capsules Capsule-+-full poly-morphisms use +-full poly-isomorphisms between corresponding constituents relative to an injection of index sets. Index motion and sign motion are distinct fields.
Sources: IUT I, Definition 6.1(iv) Related: ± cusp labels Theta± model bridge D-Theta±ell base

Global D± Cover in IUT I 6.1

Definition 6.1(v) defines D± = B(X_K)^0, a finite etale double covering of D = B(C_K)^0. From D±, the source reconstructs the outer homomorphism to GL_2(F_l)/{±1}, the rank-one quotient associated to the covering X_K -> X_K, and the subgroup Aut^±(D±).

1. Double cover

D± -> D is the category-theoretic cover attached to X_K -> C_K.

2. Torsion action

The l-torsion Galois action gives Aut(D±) -> GL_2(F_l)/{±1}.

3. Cusp choice

The chosen cusp epsilon supplies the F_l^±-structure on global cusp labels.

Definition 6.1(v) is global and cusp-sensitive. The F_l^±-torsor on LabCusp±(D±) is reconstructed from D± together with the initial cusp datum.
Sources: IUT I, Definition 6.1(v) Related: cyclic cover reconstruction epsilon quotient Thetaell model bridge

D± Valuations and Archimedean Targets in IUT I 6.1

Definition 6.1(vi)-(vii) extends the global D± package to arbitrary categories isomorphic to it. The source reconstructs cusp classes, the algebraic closure of the base field, valuation sets, quotient valuation sets, and archimedean Aut-holomorphic orbispaces from pi_1(D±).

ObjectDefinition 6.1(vi)-(vii)Use downstream
LabCusp±(D±) For any category isomorphic to D±, cusp and ±-cusp-label classes make sense. Thetaell bridges can target global ± labels without choosing a model copy.
V(D±) Valuations of the reconstructed algebraic closure, with natural fundamental-group action. Controls valuation-indexed components of additive bridge data.
X(D±, w) For archimedean w, an Aut-holomorphic orbispace reconstructed from pi_1(D±). Defines morphisms from arbitrary Aut-holomorphic orbispaces to D±.
Sources: IUT I, Definition 6.1(vi)-(vii), Remark 6.1.1 Related: global D± cover arch Aut-holomorphic Frobenioids D-Theta±ell base

IUT I Examples 6.2-6.3: Model Theta± and Thetaell Bridges

Examples 6.2 and 6.3 give the model objects for the two additive bridge directions. The same capsule D_T is sent upward to a signed D-prime-strip target D≻ and downward to the global elliptic target D±.

Two model bridge directions Examples 6.2 and 6.3
ExampleConstructionWhat must remain visible
6.2(i) The model base-Theta± bridge uses copies D_t indexed by F_l and positive +-full poly-isomorphisms D_t -> D≻. Positivity is not decoration: it controls which signed automorphisms survive in the model.
6.2(ii)-(iii) The order-two operation -1_{F_l} sends t to -t and uses negative local signs; {±1}^V acts by local signed +-full automorphisms. The page separates the global sign operation from placewise sign choices.
6.3 The model base-Thetaell bridge maps the capsule to D±, after fixing a torsor identification LabCusp±(D±) ≃ F_l. The target is the global elliptic object. Its automorphism quotient is identified with F_l^± only after the torsor choice.
Sources: IUT I, Examples 6.2-6.3 Related: additive labels D-Theta±ell base theater Theta±ell Frobenioid lift

Theta± Model Bridge in IUT I 6.2

Example 6.2(i) treats F_l as an F_l^±-group and forms copies D_t = {D_{v,t}}_{v in V} of the tautological D-prime-strip. For each t in F_l, the map phi_t^{Theta±} : D_t -> D≻ is the positive +-full poly-isomorphism relative to the tautological identifications.

ComponentExample 6.2(i)Consequence
D_t Copies of the tautological D-prime-strip indexed by t in F_l. The additive capsule is indexed by a signed additive group, not by multiplicative Section 4 labels.
D≻ A copy of the same tautological D-prime-strip used as the Theta± target. The model bridge compares copies through +-full poly-isomorphism classes.
phi^{Theta±}_± The collection of positive +-full poly-isomorphisms {phi_t^{Theta±}}_{t in F_l}. Positivity is part of the model data.
Sources: IUT I, Example 6.2(i) Related: +-full poly-isomorphisms Theta± sign actions D-Theta±ell base

Theta± Sign Actions in IUT I 6.2

Example 6.2(ii)-(iii) records two different sign operations on the model Theta± bridge. The global order-two operation -1_{F_l} sends t to -t and uses negative signs at every valuation. Separately, each alpha in {±1}^V gives a placewise signed automorphism that acts trivially on the F_l index set.

Global sign

-1_{F_l} acts on labels by multiplication by -1 and on each strip by negative +-full poly-automorphisms at all valuations.

Example 6.2(ii)order two

Placewise signs

alpha^{Theta±} for alpha in {±1}^V fixes the label set and acts by the corresponding signed +-full poly-automorphism at each valuation.

Example 6.2(iii)placewise
The global label negation and the valuation-wise sign action are different symmetries. Keeping them separate prevents a later collapse of additive label motion into one undifferentiated sign flag.
Sources: IUT I, Example 6.2(ii)-(iii) Related: Theta± model +-full poly-isomorphisms ± cusp labels

Thetaell Model Bridge in IUT I 6.3

Example 6.3(i) treats F_l as an F_l^±-torsor and maps the same model capsule D± = {D_t}_{t in F_l} to the global elliptic target D ±. After fixing an F_l^±-torsor isomorphism LabCusp±(D ±) ≃ F_l, the automorphism quotient Aut^±(D ±)/Aut_csp(D ±) is identified with F_l^±.

CaseSource construction of phi_{v,0}^{Thetaell}Role
Good nonarchimedean Restriction functor from the natural composite X_v -> X_K. Finite etale covering route to the global target.
Bad nonarchimedean The analogous tempered/local route through X_v -> X_K. Bad-place version of the same global elliptic target map.
Archimedean The tautological Aut-holomorphic morphism to X(D ±, v). Archimedean compatibility for the additive bridge.
Sources: IUT I, Example 6.3(i) Related: global D± cover D± valuations/arch Thetaell equivariance

Thetaell Equivariance in IUT I 6.3

Example 6.3(ii) defines a natural poly-action of F_l^± on the capsule D± and on the target D ±. The bridge phi^{Thetaell}_± : D± -> D ± is equivariant for these actions.

1. Capsule action

gamma in F_l^± sends D_t to D_{gamma(t)} with sign equal to gamma.

2. Target action

The quotient Aut^±(D ±)/Aut_csp(D ±) supplies the target poly-action.

3. Equivariance

phi^{Thetaell}_± is compatible with both actions, producing an F_l^±-action on the whole model data.

Sources: IUT I, Example 6.3(ii) Related: Thetaell model F_l± groups/torsors D-Theta±ell base

IUT I Definition 6.4 and Propositions 6.5-6.6: D-Theta±ell Base Theaters

Definition 6.4 turns the two model bridge directions into the base D-Theta±ell theater. Propositions 6.5 and 6.6 record the label transport and the torsors of isomorphisms and gluing choices.

Compatible additive span Definition 6.4, Proposition 6.5, Proposition 6.6
SourceStatementAudit target
Definition 6.4(i)-(iii) A D-Theta± bridge is D_T -> D≻; a D-Thetaell bridge is D_T -> D±; a D-Theta±ell theater is the compatible span D≻ <- D_T -> D±. Compatibility means both bridge sides induce the same capsule poly-isomorphism, not just the same index cardinality.
Proposition 6.5 The two bridge sides transport ±-label classes by well-defined bijections compatible with F_l^±-group or torsor structures. Label transport is a theorem-level structure for later pages, not an implicit consequence of naming labels the same way.
Proposition 6.6 Isomorphisms and gluing choices form the stated torsors over {±1} x {±1}^V, F_l^±, {±1}, and F_l^± x {±1}^V. These torsors are the additive indeterminacy ledger. They must remain explicit when the Frobenioid lift is introduced.
Sources: IUT I, Definition 6.4 and Propositions 6.5-6.6 Related: additive labels model bridges additive to D-Theta Theta±ell bridges 6.11

D-Theta± Bridge in IUT I 6.4

Definition 6.4(i) promotes the model of Example 6.2 to an abstract D-Theta± bridge. The index set T is an F_l^±-group, the capsule D_T = {D_t}_{t in T} is mapped to a D-prime-strip D≻, and the model comparison is required to identify the index sets as F_l^±-groups.

Definition 6.4(i)DataConsequence
Bridge map phi^{Theta±}_± : D_T -> D≻. The bridge target is the additive Theta± D-prime-strip target.
Quotient capsule |T| = T/{±1} gives an l±-capsule, and the nonzero part gives the later l⋇-capsule. The zero/nonzero split is built into the bridge, not added later.
Morphisms Capsule-+-full poly-isomorphisms whose index maps are F_l^±-group isomorphisms. Signed additive structure controls bridge isomorphisms.
Sources: IUT I, Definition 6.4(i) Related: Theta± model +-full poly-isomorphisms D-Theta±ell theater

D-Thetaell Bridge in IUT I 6.4

Definition 6.4(ii) promotes the model of Example 6.3 to an abstract D-Thetaell bridge. The same capsule type is now indexed by an F_l^±-torsor, and the target is a category equivalent to D ±. Target morphisms are taken up to the Aut_csp(D ±)-orbit of isomorphisms.

Torsor index

The index set T is an F_l^±-torsor, not merely an additive group.

Def. 6.4(ii)torsor

Global target

The target D ± is a copy of the global elliptic double-cover category.

D±global

Morphisms

The target arrow is an Aut_csp-orbit, reflecting the cusp-fixing indeterminacy.

Aut_csporbit
Sources: IUT I, Definition 6.4(ii) Related: Thetaell model global D± cover D-Theta±ell theater

D-Theta±ell Theater in IUT I 6.4

Definition 6.4(iii) glues a D-Theta± bridge and a D-Thetaell bridge over a common capsule. The data have the span shape D≻ <- D_T -> D ±, where the F_l^±-group structure on T induces the torsor structure needed by the Thetaell side.

1. Theta± side

D_T -> D≻ is a D-Theta± bridge with group-indexed capsule.

2. Thetaell side

D_T -> D ± is a D-Thetaell bridge using the induced torsor structure.

3. Common capsule

Morphisms are compatible only when both bridge sides induce the same capsule poly-isomorphism.

Sources: IUT I, Definition 6.4(iii) Related: D-Theta± bridge D-Thetaell bridge ± label transport

± Label Transport in IUT I 6.5

Proposition 6.5 is the intrinsic version of the model constructions in Examples 6.2 and 6.3. A D-Theta±ell-Hodge theater supplies well-defined bijections of LabCusp± sets along both bridge sides, compatible with the relevant F_l^±-group or torsor structures.

PartTransport statementUse
6.5(i) The Thetaell side induces zeta^{Thetaell}_{v,t} from local ± cusp labels to global LabCusp±(D ±). Globalizes additive labels through the elliptic target.
6.5(ii) The Theta± side induces zeta^{Theta±}_{v,t} from local ± cusp labels to the D≻ target, with compatible identifications across valuations. Synchronizes additive labels on the Theta± side.
6.5(iii)-(iv) The assignment t -> zeta^{Thetaell}_t(0) identifies T with global ± cusp labels; post-composition by target automorphisms is tracked explicitly. Records how changing the Thetaell target action changes the transported labels.
Proposition 6.5 prevents treating the labels on the two bridge sides as merely "the same names". Their equality is replaced by explicit transported bijections.
Sources: IUT I, Proposition 6.5 Related: D-Theta±ell theater ± cusp labels additive to multiplicative

Additive Isomorphism Torsors in IUT I 6.6

Proposition 6.6(i)-(iii) computes the torsors of isomorphisms for D-Theta± bridges, D-Thetaell bridges, and D-Theta±ell-Hodge theaters. These torsors are the additive analogue of the Section 4 bridge dynamics.

ObjectTorsor in Proposition 6.6Interpretation
D-Theta± bridges {±1} x {±1}^V Global label negation plus valuation-wise sign choices.
D-Thetaell bridges F_l^±-torsor, up to the stated outer isomorphism. Affine signed motion of the global elliptic target labels.
D-Theta±ell theaters {±1}-torsor. Compatibility of both bridge sides reduces the remaining theater isomorphism freedom.
Sources: IUT I, Proposition 6.6(i)-(iii) Related: Theta± sign actions Thetaell equivariance gluing algorithm

Additive Gluing Algorithm in IUT I 6.6

Proposition 6.6(iv)-(v) describes the gluing choices and construction algorithm. Given a D-Theta± bridge and a D-Thetaell bridge, compatible capsule-+-full poly-isomorphisms that glue them into a D-Theta±ell-Hodge theater form a torsor over F_l^± x {±1}^V. Given a D-Thetaell bridge, there is a functorial construction of a D-Theta±ell theater up to F_l^±-indeterminacy.

Gluing choices

The group F_l^± x {±1}^V combines the Thetaell affine signed motion with the Theta± valuation-wise sign data.

6.6(iv)gluing

Construction from Thetaell

The source records a relatively simple functorial algorithm constructing a D-Theta±ell theater from a D-Thetaell bridge, up to F_l^±-indeterminacy.

6.6(v)algorithm
Sources: IUT I, Proposition 6.6(iv)-(v), Figure 6.1 Related: isomorphism torsors D-Theta±ell theater additive to multiplicative

IUT I Propositions 6.7-6.10: From Additive Bridges to D-Theta Etale-Pictures

Propositions 6.7-6.10 are the hinge from the additive Section 6 bridge system back to the multiplicative Section 4 D-Theta bridge system. The construction passes through the nonzero quotient capsule, processions, and a mono-analytic etale-picture core.

Additive bridge conversion Proposition 6.7 through Corollary 6.10
ResultMathematical moveWhy this page is separate
Proposition 6.7 A D-Theta± bridge functorially gives a D-Theta bridge by replacing the capsule with the nonzero quotient, identifying D_0 with D≻, and switching to the full/evaluation morphisms required by Section 4. This is not mere forgetting. It is the explicit source bridge between additive and multiplicative combinatorial Teichmuller theories.
Proposition 6.8 Forgetful functors expose F_l^± symmetry on D-Thetaell outputs and S_{l±} symmetry on holomorphic or mono-analytic capsules. The wiki tracks the symmetry level before and after label forgetting.
Proposition 6.9 Additive processions are nested subcapsules indexed by S_t^± = {0,1,...,t-1}. They reduce the displayed ambiguity from (l±)^(l±) to l±!. Processions are the finite combinatorial discipline that makes the bridge comparison auditable.
Corollary 6.10 D-Theta±ell links are full poly-isomorphisms between the mono-analyticized D_>^⊢ cores; the resulting etale-picture admits arbitrary permutation symmetries among spokes. This is the additive analogue of the Section 4 etale-picture and the immediate precursor to the Frobenioid lift of Definition 6.11.
Sources: IUT I, Propositions 6.7-6.9 and Corollary 6.10 Related: D-Theta±ell base Section 4 processions multiplicative/additive bridge comparison Theta±ell bridges 6.11

D-Theta from Theta± in IUT I 6.7

Proposition 6.7 constructs an ordinary D-Theta bridge from a D-Theta± bridge. The algorithm passes from the additive capsule to the nonzero quotient capsule, identifies D≻ with the zero component D_0, and replaces the local +-full maps by the full or evaluation maps required by Definition 4.6(ii).

StepProposition 6.7Meaning
Nonzero quotient Replace the signed additive capsule by the nonzero quotient capsule. Returns from additive labels to Section 4 multiplicative labels.
Target identification Use phi^{Theta±}_0 to identify D≻ with D_0, forming D_>. The target is not arbitrarily chosen; it is tied to the zero label.
Local replacement At good places use full poly-morphisms; at bad places use the group-theoretic maps of Example 4.4. This is a real bridge back to the Section 4 D-Theta construction.
Sources: IUT I, Proposition 6.7 Related: D-Theta± bridge bad-place theta bridge D-Theta bridge

Thetaell Forgetful Symmetry in IUT I 6.8

Proposition 6.8(i) sends a D-Theta±ell-Hodge theater to its underlying D-Thetaell bridge. The output admits an F_l^± symmetry, more precisely a finite group with natural outer isomorphism to F_l^±, acting doubly transitively on the capsule index set.

This is the symmetry before forgetting to the quotient capsule. It remembers the full signed affine label motion on T.
Sources: IUT I, Proposition 6.8(i) Related: D-Thetaell bridge F_l± groups/torsors capsule symmetry

Capsule Symmetry in IUT I 6.8

Proposition 6.8(ii)-(iii) forgets labels from a D-Theta±ell-Hodge theater to the l±-capsule D_|T|, and then to its mono-analyticization. The output has full symmetric-group symmetry S_{l±} on the quotient index set |T|.

OutputSymmetryIndeterminacy statement
Holomorphic l±-capsule S_{l±} Before the functor, a given quotient index has precisely l± possible labels.
Mono-analytic l±-capsule Same label symmetry as the holomorphic output. Mono-analyticization does not repair the forgotten label information.
Sources: IUT I, Proposition 6.8(ii)-(iii) Related: Thetaell forgetful symmetry additive processions Section 4 forgetful symmetries

Additive Processions in IUT I 6.9

Proposition 6.9(i)-(ii) builds l±-processions from nested subcapsules of the quotient capsule D_|T|. For the holomorphic procession, the subsets are S_t^± = {0,1,...,t-1}. The same construction has a mono-analytic version after applying mono-analyticization.

Holomorphic procession

Nested subcapsules of D_|T| are indexed by the initial segments S_t^±.

6.9(i)holomorphic

Mono-analytic procession

Applying mono-analyticization gives the corresponding procession of D⊢-prime-strips with the same label indeterminacy behavior.

6.9(ii)mono-analytic
The source records the numerical gain: processions reduce the displayed label indeterminacy from (l±)^(l±) possibilities to l±! possibilities.
Sources: IUT I, Proposition 6.9(i)-(ii) Related: capsule symmetry Section 4 processions procession compatibility

Procession Compatibility in IUT I 6.9

Proposition 6.9(iii) states that the additive procession functors are compatible with the multiplicative procession functors of Proposition 4.11, relative to the D-Theta bridge construction of Proposition 6.7. The natural inclusions {1,...,j} -> {0,1,...,t-1}, with t = j + 1, give the corresponding natural transformations.

Sources: IUT I, Proposition 6.9(iii) Related: D-Theta from Theta± additive processions Section 4 procession indeterminacy

D-Theta±ell Link in IUT I 6.10

Corollary 6.10 defines the D-Theta±ell link. First one assigns to a D-Theta±ell-Hodge theater the D-prime-strip D_> obtained from Proposition 6.7, then passes to the mono-analyticized D_>^⊢. A link between two theaters is the full poly-isomorphism between these mono-analytic cores.

Corollary 6.10StatementPicture
Link HT_{D-Theta±ell} -> D_> -> D_>^⊢; links are full poly-isomorphisms between the D_>^⊢ outputs. Mono-analytic core.
Infinite chain The output D_>^⊢ forms a constant invariant of a chain of linked theaters. Core with spokes.
Etale-picture The holomorphic processions remain on spokes; arbitrary permutations of spokes preserve the core. Additive analogue of the Section 4 etale-picture.
Sources: IUT I, Corollary 6.10 Related: Section 4 etale-picture Theta±ell bridge lift Frobenius/etale pictures

Multiplicative and Additive Bridge Theory

IUT I separates two combinatorial directions before the later theta-value estimates are attempted. Section 4 develops a multiplicative F_l-label theory for D-ThetaNF theaters. Section 6 develops an additive F_l^±-label theory for D-Theta±ell theaters and then glues the additive and multiplicative sides into Theta±ellNF theaters.

Bridge split in IUT I multiplicative labels, additive labels, and the glued theater
LayerSymmetryBridge/theater objectWhat is synchronized
Multiplicative side F_l-symmetry on labels 1,...,l. D-NF bridge, D-Theta bridge, D-ThetaNF-Hodge theater. Capsule index set, global cusp labels, and number-field/theta-side D-prime-strips.
Additive side F_l^±-symmetry on signed labels including the zero label. D-Theta± bridge, D-Thetaell bridge, D-Theta±ell-Hodge theater. ± cusp labels, elliptic/torsion-side structure, and geometric basepoint data.
Glued side Compatibility of F_l and F_l^± labels. Theta±ellNF-Hodge theater and D-Theta±ellNF-Hodge theater. Nonzero signed labels ±t are identified with the corresponding multiplicative label.
Forgetful side Coarser symmetric-group actions after label structure is hidden. Unlabeled capsules and processions. Enough combinatorics is retained to compare objects without full holomorphic label history.
IUT I source item Data or conclusion Why the wiki separates it
Definition 4.6(i): D-NF bridge A poly-morphism D_J -> D, where D is equivalent to B(C_K)^0 and D_J = {D_j}_{j in J} is a finite capsule of D-prime-strips, modeled on phi^NF. This is the number-field bridge; its isomorphism set is an F_l-torsor, so it carries controlled label ambiguity.
Definition 4.6(ii): D-Theta bridge A poly-morphism D_J -> D>, where D> is a D-prime-strip and D_J is a finite capsule modeled on phi^Theta. This is the theta-side bridge; unlike the D-NF bridge, isomorphisms between D-Theta bridges are unique once the source data is fixed.
Definition 4.6(iii): D-ThetaNF theater A compatible span D <- D_J -> D> whose two arrows are the D-NF and D-Theta bridges and whose morphisms induce the same bijection on capsule indices. Compatibility is not just sharing a domain object; it synchronizes the two bridge histories through the same capsule index set.
Proposition 4.7 The D-Theta side determines a bijection chi : J ~= F_l; the two bridge sides determine isomorphisms of label-class torsors LabCusp(D) ~= LabCusp(D>); a unique global cusp class [epsilon] then identifies LabCusp(D) with J. This is the first precise synchronization theorem: global cusp labels, local capsule indices, and theta labels become one controlled structure.
Proposition 4.8 D-NF bridge isomorphisms and compatible gluings form F_l-torsors; D-Theta bridge and D-ThetaNF theater isomorphisms are unique. The torsor/uniqueness split tells a formalization where label ambiguity lives and where it has already been rigidified.
Additive source item Data or conclusion Why it is not the same as the multiplicative side
Definition 6.1(i): F_l^± F_l^± is the semidirect product F_l ⋊ {±1}. An F_l^±-group has a natural F_l-module structure; an F_l^±-torsor is acted on by affine maps z -> ±z + lambda. The additive side has signs and translations. Its automorphisms include positive automorphisms and full signed automorphisms, not only the multiplicative F_l-torsor action.
Definition 6.1(iii): ±-label classes LabCusp±(D_v) contains a zero class and a ±-canonical class; after removing zero and quotienting by ±, it maps to LabCusp(D_v). The zero label is real data. Forgetting it collapses the additive bridge to a multiplicative-looking object too early.
Definition 6.4(i): D-Theta± bridge A poly-morphism D_T -> D≻, where T is an F_l^±-group. It also defines D_|T| by quotienting indices by ± and D_T from the nonzero part. The quotient capsule keeps track of the passage from signed additive labels to the nonzero unsigned labels used by later comparison with the multiplicative side.
Definition 6.4(ii): D-Thetaell bridge A poly-morphism D_T -> D±, where T is an F_l^±-torsor and D± = B(X_K)^0. This is the elliptic side; its target is tied to the double-cover and additive cusp-label structure rather than to C_K alone.
Definition 6.4(iii): D-Theta±ell theater A compatible span D≻ <- D_T -> D± whose two arrows are the D-Theta± and D-Thetaell bridges and whose morphisms induce the same poly-isomorphism of capsules. The compatibility condition is stronger than sharing labels: it ties the signed group structure and the torsor structure through one capsule.
Propositions 6.5 and 6.6 Proposition 6.5 transports ±-label classes through the two additive bridges. Proposition 6.6 locates the remaining ambiguity: D-Theta± bridge isomorphisms form a {±1} × {±1}^V-torsor, D-Thetaell bridge isomorphisms form an F_l^±-torsor, and D-Theta±ell theater isomorphisms form a {±1}-torsor. These torsors explain why the additive bridge cannot be formalized by reusing the multiplicative D-ThetaNF interface with an extra sign flag.

Multiplicative side: D-ThetaNF

IUT I Definition 4.6 defines a D-NF bridge, a D-Theta bridge, and the D-ThetaNF-Hodge theater that combines them over the same capsule of D-prime-strips. Proposition 4.7 extracts a label bijection J ≃ F_l and a global cusp-label bijection LabCusp(D) ≃ J.

The mathematical role of this side is synchronization: one global object D = B(C_K)^0 prevents independent relabeling of each capsule component. Mochizuki explicitly contrasts this with nonsynchronized label choices in Remark 4.9.2.

IUT I, Definition 4.6Proposition 4.7

Forgetful functors and comparability

Proposition 4.9 records three outputs: the underlying D-NF bridge with simply transitive F_l-symmetry, the unlabeled capsule with full symmetric-group freedom, and the mono-analytic capsule. The point is not to forget information casually, but to expose exactly which comparisons become available after labels are hidden.

Proposition 4.11 then organizes the unlabeled capsule into processions. Processions retain enough order to reduce independent label ambiguity to a factorial procession ambiguity, while preserving the comparisons needed for later log-volume computations.

Proposition 4.9Proposition 4.11

Additive side: D-Theta±ell

IUT I Definition 6.1 introduces F_l^±-groups, F_l^±-torsors, positive automorphisms, and ±-label classes of cusps. Definition 6.4 uses this language to define D-Theta± bridges, D-Thetaell bridges, and D-Theta±ell-Hodge theaters.

Proposition 6.5 supplies the additive analogue of Proposition 4.7: the capsule index set is tied to global ±-label classes of cusps. Proposition 6.8 gives the corresponding F_l^±-symmetry and the coarser symmetric-group symmetry after labels are forgotten.

Definition 6.1Definition 6.4Proposition 6.5

Gluing: Theta±ellNF

Proposition 6.7 constructs a D-Theta bridge from a D-Theta± bridge by a functorial algorithm. Definition 6.13 then glues a Theta±ell-Hodge theater to a ThetaNF-Hodge theater along the associated Theta bridges; the gluing is unique in the sense cited in Remark 6.12.2.

Remark 6.12.6 describes the result as a relay between geometric and arithmetic basepoints. This relay is why the wiki treats the bridge theory as structural, not as peripheral notation.

Proposition 6.7Definition 6.13basepoints
For the IUT formalization project, this page marks a concrete boundary: labels, torsors, capsules, processions, and mono-analyticization must be represented as separate interfaces. A Lean model that identifies them too early cannot express the distinction between synchronized F_l/F_l^± data and unlabeled symmetric-group data.
Sources: IUT I, §§4, 6 Related: prime strips theta/log links log-theta lattice Corollary 3.12 corridor

Theta±ell Bridges in IUT I 6.11

Definition 6.11 is the Frobenioid lift of the additive D-bridge formalism developed earlier in Section 6. It defines Theta±-bridges, Thetaell-bridges, and Theta±ell-Hodge theaters, then Corollary 6.12 reads their isomorphism theory back through the associated D-bridges.

Definition 6.11 additive Frobenioid lift Theta± bridge + Thetaell bridge = Theta±ell-Hodge theater
Source itemDefinition or theoremFormalization consequence
Definition 6.11(i) A Theta±-bridge is a poly-morphism psiTheta± : F_T -> F_≻, where T is an F_l^±-group, lifting a D-Theta± bridge. The quotient capsule F_|T| is obtained by quotienting labels by {±1}. The signed additive labels and the quotient-by-sign capsule must both be represented; the quotient constituents are only well-defined up to positive automorphism.
Definition 6.11(ii) A Thetaell-bridge is a D-Thetaell bridge F_T -> D±, where T is an F_l^±-torsor and D± is equivalent to the additive D± category. Unlike the Theta± bridge, the target is D-level additive geometry, so the bridge is not an F-prime-strip target on both sides.
Definition 6.11(iii) A Theta±ell-Hodge theater is the compatible span F_≻ <- F_T -> D±. Its associated D-data must form a D-Theta±ell-Hodge theater. Compatibility means the two bridge morphisms induce the same poly-isomorphism between their F-prime-strip capsules.
Corollary 6.12(i) Isomorphisms of Theta±-bridges, Thetaell-bridges, and Theta±ell-Hodge theaters map bijectively to isomorphisms of the associated D-bridges and D-theaters. Once the D-level additive bridge data is fixed, the Frobenioid lift has no extra hidden isomorphism choice.
Corollary 6.12(ii) Compatible capsule-+-full gluings of a Theta±-bridge and a Thetaell-bridge form a torsor over F_l^± x {±1}^V. The first factor corresponds to the induced isomorphism of F_l^±-torsors. The gluing ambiguity is larger than the Section 5 F_l-torsor: it includes signed affine label motion and place-indexed sign choices.

Theta± bridge

The Theta± bridge is the F-prime-strip lift of the additive D-Theta± bridge. It is indexed by an F_l^±-group and lands in F_≻.

Def. 6.11(i)signed labels

Thetaell bridge

The Thetaell bridge uses an F_l^±-torsor indexed F-prime-strip capsule but maps to the additive D-category D±.

Def. 6.11(ii)elliptic side

Theta±ell theater

The theater is not a single bridge; it is the compatible gluing of the Theta± and Thetaell bridges over the same F-prime-strip capsule.

Def. 6.11(iii)gluing

Quotient capsules

The notation F_|T| records quotienting labels by the {±1} action; the nonzero subcapsule is the part later compared with the multiplicative label system.

Def. 6.11(i)± quotient

Isomorphism bijection

Corollary 6.12(i) is the additive analogue of the Section 5 rigidity: isomorphisms of lifted bridges are read from the associated D-level bridge isomorphisms.

Cor. 6.12(i)rigidity

Gluing torsor

Corollary 6.12(ii) identifies the gluing choices as an F_l^± x {±1}^V-torsor, not merely an F_l-torsor.

Cor. 6.12(ii)torsor
Sources: IUT I Related: Theta±ell relay multiplicative/additive bridges ThetaNF Frobenioid lift Gaussian Frobenioids

IUT I Definition 6.11 and Corollary 6.12: Theta±ell Frobenioid Lift

Definition 6.11 lifts the additive D-Theta±ell bridge package to F-prime-strips. Corollary 6.12 then says that the lifted isomorphism theory is read bijectively from the associated D-bridges, with gluing choices forming an F_l^± x {±1}^V-torsor.

Lift from D-bridges to F-prime-strip bridges Definition 6.11 and Corollary 6.12
Source itemContentFormalization note
Definition 6.11(i) The Theta± bridge is a poly-morphism F_T -> F_≻ lifting a D-Theta± bridge. It forms F_|T| by quotienting the label set by {±1} and also forms the nonzero subcapsule. The quotient capsule is part of the lifted structure, not a later convenience. Its positive-automorphism ambiguity must be explicit.
Definition 6.11(ii) The Thetaell bridge keeps an F-prime-strip capsule but maps to D±; its associated D-prime-strip capsule is the D-Thetaell bridge. The right-hand target remains D-level. A wiki or Lean model that forces both bridge targets to be F-prime-strips changes the definition.
Definition 6.11(iii) A Theta±ell-Hodge theater is the compatible span F_≻ <- F_T -> D±, whose associated D-data forms a D-Theta±ell-Hodge theater. Compatibility is checked through induced poly-isomorphisms of the F-prime-strip capsules.
Corollary 6.12 Lifted isomorphism sets map bijectively to D-level isomorphism sets, and compatible gluings form an F_l^± x {±1}^V torsor. This is the additive lift analogue of Section 5 rigidity: the new information is the signed gluing torsor, not hidden extra lift choices.
Sources: IUT I, Definition 6.11 and Corollary 6.12 Related: D-Theta±ell base Proposition 6.7 hinge Theta±ellNF gluing

Theta± Bridge Lift in IUT I 6.11

Definition 6.11(i) defines a Theta± bridge as an F-prime-strip poly-morphism F_T -> F_≻ that lifts a D-Theta± bridge by Corollary 5.3(ii). The index set T is an F_l^±-group, and the quotient capsule F_|T| is formed by identifying sign-paired components through the bridge morphisms.

Lifted datumDefinition 6.11(i)Consequence
F_T Capsule of F-prime-strips indexed by an F_l^±-group. The additive index structure is preserved at F-level.
F_|T| The quotient by {±1}; constituents are well-defined up to positive automorphism. The same sign-pairing ambiguity from Definition 6.4 appears after lift.
Morphisms Pairs of poly-isomorphisms lifting morphisms of associated D-Theta± bridges. F-level isomorphisms are constrained by the D-level bridge.
Sources: IUT I, Definition 6.11(i) Related: D-Theta± bridge Theta±ell rigidity Theta±ell theater lift

Thetaell Bridge Lift in IUT I 6.11

Definition 6.11(ii) defines a Thetaell bridge. It keeps an F-prime-strip capsule F_T indexed by an F_l^±-torsor, but its target remains the D-level category D ±. Its associated D-prime-strip capsule supplies the D-Thetaell bridge.

The right-hand target of a Thetaell bridge is not an F-prime-strip. It is the global D ± category. This is an important typing distinction in Definition 6.11(ii).
Sources: IUT I, Definition 6.11(ii) Related: D-Thetaell bridge Thetaell model Theta±ell theater lift

Theta±ell Theater Lift in IUT I 6.11

Definition 6.11(iii) glues a Theta± bridge and a Thetaell bridge into a Theta±ell-Hodge theater F_≻ <- F_T -> D ±. The associated D-data must form a D-Theta±ell-Hodge theater, and morphisms must induce the same poly-isomorphism on the F-prime-strip capsule.

1. Theta± bridge

F_T -> F_≻ lifts the D-Theta± bridge.

2. Thetaell bridge

F_T -> D ± carries the F-capsule to the global D-target.

3. D-theater check

The associated D-data must be a D-Theta±ell-Hodge theater.

Sources: IUT I, Definition 6.11(iii) Related: Theta± bridge lift Thetaell bridge lift D-Theta±ell theater

Theta±ell Rigidity in IUT I 6.12

Corollary 6.12(i) states that isomorphisms of Theta± bridges, Thetaell bridges, and Theta±ell-Hodge theaters map bijectively to isomorphisms of their associated D-Theta± bridges, D-Thetaell bridges, and D-Theta±ell-Hodge theaters.

Lifted objectD-level controllerConsequence
Theta± bridge D-Theta± bridge F-level signed bridge isomorphisms are read through D-level data.
Thetaell bridge D-Thetaell bridge The mixed F-capsule/D-target type remains rigid over the D bridge.
Theta±ell-Hodge theater D-Theta±ell-Hodge theater The lifted theater has no hidden independent isomorphism freedom.
Sources: IUT I, Corollary 6.12(i) Related: Theta±ell theater lift Section 5 bridge rigidity D-level additive torsors

Theta±ell Gluing Torsor in IUT I 6.12

Corollary 6.12(ii) states that, given a Theta± bridge and a Thetaell bridge, the capsule-+-full poly-isomorphisms between their F-prime-strip capsules that glue them into a Theta±ell-Hodge theater form a torsor over F_l^± x {±1}^V.

This is the F-level analogue of Proposition 6.6(iv). The first factor corresponds to isomorphisms of F_l^±-torsors between capsule index sets; the second factor records valuation-wise signs.
Sources: IUT I, Corollary 6.12(ii) Related: Theta±ell rigidity D-level gluing algorithm Theta±ellNF gluing

Theta±ellNF Hodge Theaters in IUT I 6.13

Definition 6.13 glues the additive Theta±ell-Hodge theater of Definition 6.11 to the multiplicative ThetaNF-Hodge theater of Definition 5.5. The gluing is unique in the sense of Remark 6.12.2, and the same definition has a D-level version using D-Theta±ell and D-ThetaNF theaters.

Definition 6.13 combined theater additive Theta±ell side glued to multiplicative ThetaNF side
Definition 6.13 itemDataWhy it matters
6.13(i)(a) A Theta±ell-Hodge theater, i.e. the additive theater from Definition 6.11(iii). Supplies the signed additive label system, the zero/nonzero comparison interface, and the geometric basepoint side.
6.13(i)(b) A ThetaNF-Hodge theater, i.e. the multiplicative Frobenioid lift from Definition 5.5(iii). Supplies the NF/theta bridge pair, the number-field construction, and the multiplicative label system.
6.13(i)(c) The necessarily unique gluing isomorphism between these two theaters, cited back to Remark 6.12.2. The combined theater is not a free product of structures; the two sides are connected through the functorial Theta± to Theta algorithm.
6.13(ii) The D-Theta±ellNF-Hodge theater is the analogous triple of a D-Theta±ell-Hodge theater, a D-ThetaNF-Hodge theater, and the unique D-level gluing isomorphism. The D-level shadow must be available because later rigidity and multiradial constructions repeatedly read lifted data through D-theater isomorphisms.

Additive side

The Theta±ell side carries the F_l^± additive symmetry, including the zero label and the sign quotient structure.

Def. 6.13(i)(a)additive

Multiplicative side

The ThetaNF side carries the Section 5 Frobenioid package: the NF bridge, the theta bridge, and their common F-prime-strip capsule.

Def. 6.13(i)(b)multiplicative

Unique gluing

Remark 6.12.2 says the relevant gluing isomorphism is unique, using Proposition 6.7 and the isomorphism rigidity of Corollary 5.6.

Rem. 6.12.2unique

D-level version

Definition 6.13(ii) repeats the construction at D-level. This is the version used when later arguments work only with mono-analytic or D-prime-strip structure.

Def. 6.13(ii)D-theater

Frobenioid inventory

Figure 6.6 lists the Frobenioids appearing in the combined theater: local F-prime-strip data at places, tempered Frobenioids over D_> at bad places, and global Fmod and F layers.

Fig. 6.6inventory

Relay meaning

Remark 6.12.6 reads the combined theater as a relay between geometric basepoints for theta evaluation and arithmetic basepoints for constructing Fmod.

Rem. 6.12.6(v)relay
Sources: IUT I Related: Theta±ell bridges 6.11 ThetaNF Frobenioid lift Theta±ell relay Gaussian Frobenioids

Theta±ellNF Definition in IUT I 6.13

Definition 6.13(i) defines a Theta±ellNF-Hodge theater as a triple: a Theta±ell-Hodge theater, a ThetaNF-Hodge theater, and the necessarily unique gluing isomorphism between them. The additive side supplies the F_l^± geometry; the multiplicative side supplies the ThetaNF Frobenioid package.

Triple componentSource referenceRole
Theta±ell-Hodge theater Definition 6.11(iii) Signed additive bridge system, including zero/nonzero label relation.
ThetaNF-Hodge theater Definition 5.5(iii) Multiplicative Frobenioid bridge system over NF and theta sides.
Unique gluing Remark 6.12.2(i)-(ii) Connects the Theta± bridge to the Theta bridge through Proposition 6.7.
Sources: IUT I, Definition 6.13(i) Related: Theta±ell theater lift ThetaNF theater unique gluing

D-Theta±ellNF Definition in IUT I 6.13

Definition 6.13(ii) gives the D-level analogue: a D-Theta±ell-Hodge theater, a D-ThetaNF-Hodge theater, and the necessarily unique D-level gluing isomorphism. This D-level package is the controlling shadow for later comparisons that pass through D-prime-strips and mono-analytic structures.

Sources: IUT I, Definition 6.13(ii) Related: D-Theta±ell theater D-ThetaNF theater bridge rigidity

Unique Gluing in IUT I 6.12.2

Remark 6.12.2 explains how a Theta bridge may be glued to a Theta± bridge when the Theta part is obtained from the Theta± bridge through the functorial algorithm of Proposition 6.7. For the combined Theta±ellNF theater, the relevant gluing isomorphism is unique by Proposition 4.8(ii) and Corollary 5.6(ii).

Definition 6.13 does not introduce an arbitrary new comparison map. The gluing is forced once the Theta±/Theta relation has been fixed by the Proposition 6.7 algorithm.
Sources: IUT I, Remark 6.12.2 Related: D-Theta from Theta± Theta±ellNF definition Theta±ellNF gluing

Basepoint Relay in IUT I 6.12.6

Remark 6.12.6 summarizes the conceptual role of the combined theater: it is an intricate relay between geometric basepoints, suited to Hodge-Arakelov theta evaluation, and arithmetic basepoints, suited to constructing Fmod. In this relay, additive F_l^± and multiplicative F_l symmetries remain independent.

SideBasepoint roleUse
Additive F_l^± Geometric single basepoint at each place, suited to theta evaluation. Controls Hodge-Arakelov evaluation of the etale theta function.
Multiplicative F_l Arithmetic basepoints tied to Borel translates of valuation sets. Controls the construction of Fmod from the NF side.
Combined theater Relay between geometric and arithmetic basepoints. Resolves additive/multiplicative dimensions of the ring-like prototype.
Sources: IUT I, Remark 6.12.6 Related: Theta±ell relay Fmod reconstruction theta evaluation route

Frobenioid Inventory in IUT I Figure 6.6

Figure 6.6 lists the Frobenioids appearing in a Theta±ellNF-Hodge theater. The inventory includes local F-prime-strip data at places, tempered Frobenioids over the D_> part at bad places, and the global non-realified Fmod, F, and associated Frobenioid layers of the NF side.

This inventory matters for formalization: the combined theater is not a single category but a typed package of local, bad-place, and global Frobenioid components tied together by the bridge structure.
Sources: IUT I, Figure 6.6 Related: Theta±ellNF definition ThetaNF Frobenioid lift global Frobenioids 5.1

IUT I Definition 6.13: Theta±ellNF Gluing

Definition 6.13 combines the additive Theta±ell theater with the multiplicative ThetaNF theater. The gluing is along the Theta±/Theta bridge relation supplied by Proposition 6.7 and Remark 6.12.2, and is described by Mochizuki as a relay between geometric and arithmetic basepoints.

Combined additive/multiplicative theater Definition 6.13 with Remarks 6.12.2 and 6.12.6
SourceStatementInterpretive constraint
Remark 6.12.2(i) A Theta bridge portion can be regarded as constructed from a Theta± bridge by the functorial algorithm parallel to Proposition 6.7. The final gluing depends on the explicit additive-to-multiplicative bridge conversion, not on an arbitrary identification of label sets.
Remark 6.12.2(ii) Gluing a Theta±ell-Hodge theater to a ThetaNF-Hodge theater along their associated Theta±/Theta bridges yields the Theta±ellNF-Hodge theater; the gluing isomorphism is unique. The combined theater has one additive component, one multiplicative component, and a specified unique compatibility witness.
Definition 6.13(i)-(ii) The Theta±ellNF theater and its D-level analogue are triples consisting of the two component theaters and their unique gluing isomorphism. The F-level and D-level versions should be linked but not conflated. Later arguments may read only D-level or mono-analytic information.
Remark 6.12.6(v) The combined theater is presented as a relay between geometric basepoints suited to theta evaluation and arithmetic basepoints suited to constructing Fmod. This page records why both additive and multiplicative sides are present before later Hodge-Arakelov and Gaussian Frobenioid material is read.
Sources: IUT I, Remark 6.12.2, Remark 6.12.6, Definition 6.13 Related: Theta±ell lift ThetaNF Frobenioid lift Theta±ell relay Theta±ellNF atlas

Theta±ell Relay

The additive side of IUT I is not merely the multiplicative bridge with signs attached. Section 6 introduces signed additive torsors, zero-labeled prime-strips, global ±-synchronization, and a geometric basepoint tied to X_K. These features are later needed for Hodge-Arakelov theta evaluation.

Additive and multiplicative basepoints Section 6 separates the geometric and arithmetic roles
IUT I source item Precise role Formalization consequence
Definition 6.1(i) F_l ⋊ {±1} acts on F_l by affine maps z -> ±z + lambda. Positive automorphisms are the translation part; full signed automorphisms also include the sign. The additive symmetry should be typed as an affine signed action, not as a cyclic multiplicative torsor with an added Boolean.
Definition 6.1(iii) LabCusp±(D_v) has a zero element and a ±-canonical element. Removing zero and quotienting by ± recovers the ordinary cusp-label set. The zero label must remain explicit until the theory deliberately passes to nonzero/quotiented labels.
Definition 6.1(v) The global additive object is D_o± = B(X_K)^0, a finite etale double cover of D_o = B(C_K)^0. Its automorphisms reconstruct the relevant signed affine structure using the distinguished cusp. Additive labels are anchored in the elliptic cover and its cusp data, while multiplicative labels are anchored in the orbicurve/field-of-moduli side.
Examples 6.2-6.3 The model D-Theta± bridge carries signed full poly-isomorphisms, while the D-Thetaell bridge carries a poly-action of F_l ⋊ {±1} on both the capsule and D_o±. The additive bridge has two interacting layers: signs at places and affine translations of labels.
Remark 6.12.4 Synchronization of labels comes from a single global component/basepoint, via bijections between capsule indices and global ±-label classes of cusps. Independent local relabelings are not source-accurate. A model must express the global origin of the label bijections.
Remarks 6.12.5-6.12.6 The additive symmetry relates the zero-labeled strip to nonzero-labeled strips, but this also means it does not insulate the nonzero labels from the zero label. The glued theater relays between this geometric basepoint and the arithmetic basepoint used to construct F_mod. The later Gaussian layer must remember that nonzero theta values depend on both the additive relation to zero and the multiplicative number-field structure.

Signed affine action

The additive symmetry acts by z -> ±z + lambda. Positive automorphisms are translations; negative automorphisms include the sign reversal.

Def. 6.1(i)affine

±-label classes

A ±-label class is a cusp class of a D-prime-strip lying over a single cusp of the additive cover. It remembers both a zero class and a ±-canonical class.

Def. 6.1(iii)cusps

Global additive object

D_o± = B(X_K)^0 replaces the multiplicative B(C_K)^0 base. The choice of cusp supplies the signed affine structure on global cusp labels.

Def. 6.1(v)X_K

Global ± synchronization

Remark 6.12.4 says the ±-indeterminacies over the various v in V are globally synchronized. This is a prerequisite for the additive translation action to compare labels coherently.

Rem. 6.12.4global sign

Zero/nonzero relation

Remark 6.12.5 identifies the zero label as the feature that lets the additive network compare zero-labeled and nonzero-labeled prime-strips. It is also the source of a later insulation problem for Gaussian data.

Rem. 6.12.5zero label

Gaussian insulation issue

The Gaussian Frobenioids of IUT II use nonzero theta values. The additive theory can relate zero and nonzero strips, but the later multiplicative theory is needed to keep the nonzero Gaussian package from being confused with the zero strip.

Rem. 6.12.5IUT II input

Geometric basepoint
