The additive side uses one geometric basepoint: a single connected component of the local elliptic object. This is why it is suited to Hodge-Arakelov theta evaluation.

Rem. 6.12.6(ii)theta evaluation

Arithmetic basepoint

The multiplicative side uses an arithmetic basepoint and F_l^*/± symmetry. This is why it is suited to constructing F_mod.

Rem. 6.12.6(iii)F_mod

Combinatorial resolution of Z

Remark 6.12.6 summarizes the glued theater as a relay between additive and multiplicative basepoints, or as a global combinatorial resolution of the two operations on the ring Z.

Rem. 6.12.6(v)add/mult split
Source warning: the nonzero labels used by the multiplicative side are not simply a subset of the additive theory in ordinary set-theoretic memory. Section 6 relates them through signed torsors, quotienting by ±, and a later gluing to the ThetaNF theater.
Sources: IUT I, Section 6 and Remarks 6.12.4-6.12.6 Related: multiplicative/additive bridges ThetaNF Frobenioid lift Gaussian Frobenioids theta/log links

Mono-Theta Environments in IUT II 1.1

Definition 1.1 of IUT II starts from a mod-N mono-theta environment MTheta. From this object one functorially recovers the topological groups, geometric kernels, interior and exterior cyclotomes, and the cyclotomic rigidity isomorphism needed for Kummer-compatible theta evaluation.

Section index

Read this parent page as the Definition 1.1 hub. The child pages isolate the recovery package, cyclotomes, commutator rigidity, and construction routes used later by theta evaluation.

recovery cyclotomes commutator routes

Source order

Definition 1.1 names the mono-theta environment and recovered cyclotomes; Remark 1.1.1 explains rigidity; Propositions 1.2-1.5 give construction, compatibility, theta functions, and projective systems.

1.1 -> 1.5theta evaluation input

Reading rule

Do not identify mono-theta rigidity with the earlier rational-function Kummer route. IUT II uses a line-bundle/curvature-style cyclotome comparison that later stabilizes theta-value evaluation.

distinct rigidityKummer-compatible
Definition 1.1 recovery package mono-theta data controls both group and cyclotome layers
IUT II source itemContentWhy it matters
Definition 1.1(i) From MTheta one functorially constructs Pi_Y, Pi_X, G, the kernels Delta_MTheta, Delta_Y, Delta_X, the interior object l·DeltaTheta, and Pi_mu. Theta evaluation is not attached directly to labels; it is mediated by a recovered group-and-cyclotome package.
Definition 1.1(ii) The interior cyclotome is l·DeltaTheta; the exterior cyclotome is Pi_mu. The mono-theta cyclotomic rigidity isomorphism identifies their mod- N reductions. This is the first explicit Kummer bridge in IUT II between the interior theta-section side and the exterior cyclotomic side.
Remark 1.1.1 The rigidity isomorphism may be reconstructed as the difference between the theta-section splitting and an algebraic commutator splitting; the construction is stable under the relevant F_l^± symmetry. The source separates line-bundle-style mono-theta rigidity from the rational-function Kummer classes used earlier for Frobenioids.
Proposition 1.2 Mono-theta environments can be constructed functorially either from a topological group isomorphic to Pi_X^tp or from the tempered Frobenioid associated to the curve. This gives both the etale-like and Frobenioid-like access routes that the later Kummer square compares.
Proposition 1.3 The mono-theta cyclotomic rigidity isomorphism agrees with the corresponding base-field/Galois cyclotomic rigidity isomorphism. It prevents the mono-theta bridge from introducing an incompatible cyclotome convention.
Propositions 1.4-1.5 The etale theta functions of standard type and projective systems of mono-theta environments give the pro-level version used by later theta-value evaluation. The projective system supplies a stable cyclotome and theta-function package across moduli.

Mono-theta environment

A mod-N mono-theta environment is the input object of IUT II §1. Its structure determines the topological group Pi_MTheta and the theta-section data used for cyclotomic rigidity.

Def. 1.1MTheta

Interior/exterior cyclotomes

The interior cyclotome is the theta-side subquotient l·DeltaTheta; the exterior cyclotome Pi_mu is the kernel of the map to Pi_Y.

Def. 1.1(ii)cyclotomes

Commutator splitting

Remark 1.1.1 reconstructs the rigidity isomorphism from two sections: the theta-section splitting and the algebraic commutator splitting.

Remark 1.1.1splittings

Two construction routes

Proposition 1.2 supplies both a group-theoretic construction from Pi_X^tp and a category-theoretic construction from the tempered Frobenioid.

Prop. 1.2two routes

Cyclotomic compatibility

Proposition 1.3 says the mono-theta and base-field/Galois cyclotomic rigidity isomorphisms coincide under the stated correspondences.

Prop. 1.3compatibility

Projective system

Proposition 1.5 passes to projective systems of mono-theta environments, producing a stable exterior cyclotome and rigidity isomorphism for the later pro-level theta theory.

Prop. 1.5pro-system
Source warning: the mono-theta cyclotomic rigidity isomorphism is not the same construction as the Frobenioid rational-function Kummer rigidity of IUT I Example 5.1. IUT II explicitly contrasts the line-bundle/curvature-style mono-theta route with the rational-function route.
Sources: IUT II Related: theta evaluation route recovery package interior/exterior cyclotomes commutator rigidity mono-theta rigidity Theta±ellNF theaters Gaussian Frobenioids multiradiality

Mono-Theta Recovery Package

Definition 1.1(i) starts from a mod-N mono-theta environment MTheta and records a functorial recovery package. The output is not just one group: it includes quotient groups, geometric kernels, a theta-side subquotient, an exterior cyclotome, and actions by the recovered local group.

Recovered objectDefinition 1.1 roleLater use
Pi_Y(MTheta), Pi_X(MTheta), G(MTheta) Quotient and ambient groups; Pi_Y is a normal open subgroup of Pi_X. Provides the local group context for theta evaluation.
Delta_MTheta, Delta_Y, Delta_X Closed normal kernels of the maps to G(MTheta). Tracks the geometric tempered subgroup data.
l·DeltaTheta A subquotient of Pi_Y, abstractly Z(1), with natural Pi_X-action. Interior cyclotome for theta-section comparison.
Pi_mu Kernel of Pi_MTheta -> Pi_Y, abstractly (Z/NZ)(1), with natural action. Exterior cyclotome for the target of rigidity.
Formal reading. A wiki or Lean model should keep these outputs typed separately. Collapsing them into one unlabeled local group loses the distinction that Definition 1.1 needs for cyclotomic rigidity.
Source: IUT II, Definition 1.1(i) Related: mono-theta environments cyclotomes

Interior and Exterior Cyclotomes

Definition 1.1(ii) names two cyclotomes associated to MTheta. The interior cyclotome is l·DeltaTheta; the exterior cyclotome is Pi_mu. The theorem-level content is the functorial cyclotomic rigidity isomorphism between their mod- N reductions.

Definition 1.1(ii) theta-side cyclotome to exterior cyclotome
Source: IUT II, Definition 1.1(ii) Related: commutator rigidity cyclotomic compatibility

Commutator Rigidity in Remark 1.1.1

Remark 1.1.1 explains that the mono-theta cyclotomic rigidity isomorphism is reconstructed as a difference of two sections over l·DeltaTheta: the theta-section portion of the mono-theta environment and an algebraic section obtained from a bilinear commutator map.

Two sections, one difference Remark 1.1.1(iii)
Source componentMeaningConstraint
s_Theta The theta-section portion restricts to a subgroup mapping isomorphically to l·DeltaTheta. It depends on mono-theta environment structure.
Commutator map A bilinear map from Delta_X/Delta_Y and Delta_Y^ell into the same subquotient. It constructs the algebraic splitting s_alg.
Difference The difference between s_Theta and s_alg reconstructs cyclotomic rigidity. The isomorphism is not inserted as an arbitrary external label.
Source: IUT II, Remark 1.1.1(iii) Related: cyclotomes F_l± symmetry

F_l± Symmetry of Mono-Theta Rigidity

Remark 1.1.1(iv)-(v) states that the constructions underlying the mono-theta cyclotomic rigidity isomorphism are stabilized by the natural action of Delta_C/Delta_X ~= F_l^±. Mochizuki then contrasts this mono-theta route with the rational-function Kummer-class route: the mono-theta construction behaves more like line-bundle or curvature data.

Naive action becomes natural

The action of Pi_C on the relevant subquotient coincides with the natural conjugation action arising from the model embedding into a cyclotomic semidirect product.

F_l± stabilization

The commutator map and its section data are stabilized by Delta_C/Delta_X, the quotient identified with F_l^±.

Line bundle contrast

Mochizuki contrasts this with rational-function Kummer classes: the mono-theta construction admits more symmetry, like line-bundle or first Chern class data.

Source: IUT II, Remark 1.1.1(iv)-(v) Related: conjugate synchronization ThetaNF Fl symmetry

Group-Theoretic Mono-Theta Route

Proposition 1.2(i) constructs a mod-N mono-theta environment from a topological group Pi isomorphic to the tempered fundamental group Pi_X^tp. The construction is functorial up to the stated isomorphism indeterminacy.

InputAlgorithmic outputIndeterminacy
Pi ~= Pi_X^tp MTheta(Pi) and a functorial isomorphism back to Pi_X(MTheta(Pi)). Automorphisms modulo mu_N-conjugacy classes, order 1 if N is odd and order 2 if N is even.
Source: IUT II, Proposition 1.2(i) Related: Frobenioid route recovery package

Frobenioid-Theoretic Mono-Theta Route

Proposition 1.2(ii) constructs a mod-N mono-theta environment from a category equivalent to the tempered Frobenioid determined by the curve. This is the category-theoretic access route matching the Frobenius-like side of IUT II.

Source precision. The input category carries a natural Frobenioid structure over a base category equivalent to Btemp(Pi_X^tp)^0. The constructed MTheta(C) recovers the corresponding base category through Pi_X(MTheta(C)).
Source: IUT II, Proposition 1.2(ii) Related: Frobenius/etale versions IUT I Frobenioid package

Cyclotomic Compatibility in Proposition 1.3

Proposition 1.3 checks that the mono-theta cyclotomic rigidity isomorphism arising from a tempered Frobenioid agrees with the corresponding base-field/Galois cyclotomic rigidity isomorphism from the MLF-Galois pair framework. Both identifications coincide with the conventional scheme-theoretic cyclotome when the Frobenioid arises from familiar scheme objects.

IsomorphismSourceCompatibility claim
(*mono-Theta) Interior cyclotome subquotient of Aut(S_bs) to mu_N(S). The two cyclotomic rigidity isomorphisms coincide.
(*bs-Gal) MLF-Galois pair cyclotome comparison through mu_Z identifications. The same compatibility assertion applies to the base-field/Galois route.
Source: IUT II, Proposition 1.3 Related: interior/exterior cyclotomes Frobenioid route

IUT II Propositions 1.2-1.5: Mono-Theta Construction Routes

After Definition 1.1, Mochizuki records two ways to construct the mono-theta package, checks cyclotome compatibility, isolates the etale theta functions of standard type, and passes to projective systems. These results are the local input for the later multiradial theta-evaluation machinery.

From local data to projective mono-theta environments Propositions 1.2-1.5
Source resultConstructionWiki/formalization consequence
Proposition 1.2(i) A functorial group-theoretic algorithm constructs a mod N mono-theta environment from a topological group Pi isomorphic to Pi_X^tp, with only the stated mu_N-conjugacy indeterminacy. The group route is not a placeholder for geometry; it is a named reconstruction algorithm with controlled automorphism ambiguity.
Proposition 1.2(ii) A tempered Frobenioid category C associated to X_k also functorially constructs a mono-theta environment, recovering the base tempered category. The wiki separates group and Frobenioid access routes because later Kummer squares compare etale-like and Frobenioid-like structures.
Proposition 1.3 The mono-theta cyclotomic rigidity isomorphism agrees with the base-field/Galois cyclotomic rigidity isomorphism under the Frobenioid/MLF-Galois pair correspondences. This fixes the cyclotome convention before theta evaluation begins. Two routes are compatible because both coincide with the ordinary scheme-theoretic identification in the model case.
Proposition 1.4 and Remark 1.4.1 From Pi one constructs Pi_Ydd(Pi), lDeltaTheta(Pi), and the set of etale theta functions of standard type. Standard type is characterized by the value condition at the pointed inversion data over mu-. The normalization data is part of the construction. It cannot be replaced by an arbitrary ring-dependent unit choice.
Proposition 1.5 A projective system MTheta_* is uniquely determined up to isomorphism, has constant Pi_X, and yields a projective exterior cyclotome and the exterior theta package theta_env, infty theta_env. Later pages should refer to the projective system when working with pro-level Kummer classes and constant-multiple rigidity.
Sources: IUT II, Propositions 1.2-1.5 Related: mono-theta environment etale theta standard type projective systems cores and cuspidalizations mono-theta rigidity Galois theta evaluation

Etale Theta Functions of Standard Type

Proposition 1.4 constructs, from a group Pi ~= Pi_X^tp, the open subgroup Pi_Ydd(Pi), the subquotient l·DeltaTheta(Pi), and a set theta(Pi) inside H^1(Pi_Ydd(Pi), l·DeltaTheta(Pi)). This set is the group-theoretic version of the reciprocal of the standard etale theta function orbit, up to mu_l-multiples.

AlgorithmOutputRole
Pi -> Pi_Ydd(Pi) Open subgroup corresponding to the tempered covering Ydd. Domain for the cohomological theta function.
Pi -> l·DeltaTheta(Pi) The theta-side cyclotomic subquotient. Coefficient object for H^1.
Pi -> theta(Pi) Set of mu_l-multiples of the reciprocal standard etale theta function orbit. Input for projective and exterior-cyclotome theta packages.
Source: IUT II, Proposition 1.4 Related: pointed inversion theta_env package

Pointed Inversion Automorphism

Remark 1.4.1 recalls the normalization behind standard etale theta functions. The unique two-torsion point mu- lying on the same special-fiber component as the zero cusp is lifted through the tempered covering, and the relevant order-two inversion automorphism determines a decomposition group D_{mu-}.

Standard-type normalization restriction to D_mu- is a 2ell-th root of unity
Source: IUT II, Remark 1.4.1 Related: etale theta standard type theta evaluation points

Projective Systems of Mono-Theta Environments

Proposition 1.5 passes from individual mod- M mono-theta environments to a projective system MTheta_*. The system is uniquely determined up to isomorphism by the curve, its recovered Pi_X groups are identified through transition isomorphisms, and its exterior cyclotomes admit a projective limit.

Proposition 1.5 itemStatementConsequence
(i) The projective system is uniquely determined up to isomorphism by X_k. Uses discrete rigidity to prevent uncontrolled system choices.
(ii) The transition morphisms on Pi_X(MTheta_M) are all isomorphisms. One may write a single Pi_X(MTheta_*).
(iii) The exterior cyclotomes have a projective limit Pi_mu(MTheta_*) and a unique rigidity isomorphism from l·DeltaTheta(MTheta_*). Provides the stable exterior-cyclotome target for theta classes.
Source: IUT II, Proposition 1.5(i)-(iii) Related: theta_env package cyclotomes

Theta_env and Kummer Compatibility

Proposition 1.5(iii)-(iv) transports the standard etale theta package along the projective cyclotomic rigidity isomorphism, producing theta_env(MTheta_*) and infty theta_env(MTheta_*) with exterior-cyclotome coefficients. If the system arises from a tempered Frobenioid, this agrees with the Kummer construction from roots of the Frobenioid-theoretic theta function.

Remark 1.5.2. Tautological Kummer classes give a natural Pi_X(MTheta_*)-equivariant injection Pi_mu(MTheta_*) tensor Q/Z into the direct-limit cohomology group, with image equal to the torsion subgroup. This records the torsion control needed for the pro-level theta package.
Source: IUT II, Proposition 1.5(iii)-(iv), Remark 1.5.2 Related: etale theta standard type Frobenius/etale versions

Core Group Algorithm

Proposition 1.6(i) constructs, from the local tempered group Pi, a topological group Pi_C(Pi) containing Pi as an open subgroup and augmented to Pi/Delta. In the model case this recovers the inclusion Pi_X^tp subset Pi_C^tp.

Remark 1.6.1. Mochizuki describes the construction as computing centralizers of images of open subgroups of Pi/Delta in outer automorphism groups of open subgroups of Delta. This should be represented as a group-theoretic algorithm, not as an existential black box.
Source: IUT II, Proposition 1.6(i), Remark 1.6.1 Related: cores/cuspidalizations elliptic cuspidalization

Elliptic Cuspidalization Algorithm

Proposition 1.6(ii) constructs, for every positive integer N, a group Pi_U_N(Pi) surjecting to Pi. In the model case this is the elliptic cuspidalization coming from the open immersion determined by the N-torsion points of a once-punctured elliptic curve double-covering C_k.

Remark 1.6.1. The cuspidalization construction is described by compatible outer isomorphisms between subquotients of Delta, compatible with outer actions of open subgroups of Pi/Delta. This is the group-theoretic input for later recovery of evaluation-point decomposition groups.
Source: IUT II, Proposition 1.6(ii), Remark 1.6.1 Related: core group algorithm Galois theta evaluation

IUT II Proposition 1.6: Cores and Elliptic Cuspidalizations

Proposition 1.6 recalls the group-theoretic reconstruction of the core group and elliptic cuspidalizations from a local tempered group. These constructions are later used to recover evaluation points and decomposition groups without choosing a scheme-theoretic coordinate system.

Group-theoretic elementary operations Proposition 1.6 and Remark 1.6.1
Source itemConstructionUse downstream
Proposition 1.6(i) From Pi, construct a topological group Pi_C(Pi) containing Pi as an open subgroup and augmented to Pi/Delta. In the model case this is the core inclusion Pi_X^tp into Pi_C^tp. This gives the core group needed for comparing local curve data through the group alone.
Proposition 1.6(ii) For each positive integer N, construct Pi_U_N(Pi) with a surjection to Pi; in the model case this is the elliptic cuspidalization associated to N-torsion points. This is later used in the theta-evaluation route to reconstruct the decomposition groups attached to evaluation points.
Remark 1.6.1 The core algorithm is described in terms of centralizers of images of open subgroups in outer automorphism groups. Cuspidalization is described through compatible outer isomorphisms between subquotients of Delta. The page treats these as specific group-theoretic algorithms, not as an abstract existence assertion. This matters for later formalization.
Sources: IUT II, Proposition 1.6 Related: mono-theta construction routes Galois theta evaluation evaluation decomposition groups radial/coric framework

IUT II Examples 1.7-1.9: Radial, Coric, and Multiradial Data

Examples 1.7-1.9 introduce the language used to say when constructions can be transported across alien arithmetic holomorphic structures through a shared mono-analytic core. This is the formal source of the later multiradial/uniradial distinction.

Radial data over a common coric core Examples 1.7-1.9 and Remarks 1.7.1, 1.9.1, 1.9.2
Source itemDefinition or exampleWhy it matters
Example 1.7(i)-(ii) A radial environment is a category of radial data R, a category of coric data C, and an essentially surjective functor Phi : R -> C. It is multiradial when Phi is full, and uniradial otherwise. Fullness is the precise condition that the radial algorithm loses no rigidity relevant to gluing over the coric core.
Example 1.7(iii)-(v), Remark 1.7.1 Multiradiality allows switching and parallel transport between fibers over the same coric data. Mochizuki compares this to a fibration equipped with a connection. The wiki uses this page as the reference for "parallel transport across alien spokes"; it is not informal metaphor but source terminology.
Example 1.8 Concrete radial environments include the basic (Pi,G,alpha) -> G environment, TM-pair and unit-pair versions, cyclotomic and co-cyclotomic quotients O^mu, O^xmu, and log-shell versions. These are the actual coric interfaces later used by Corollaries 1.10-1.12 and by the IUT III log-shell machinery.
Example 1.9 A functorial group-theoretic algorithm can be represented by its graph. Depending on the radial environment chosen, the same algorithm may be regarded tautologically as multiradial or as uniradial. This explains why Mochizuki later emphasizes the content of an algorithm, not merely the existence of some reconstruction theorem.
Remarks 1.9.1-1.9.2 The purpose is to compute which output data depends essentially on an arithmetic holomorphic structure and which portion depends only on the mono-analytic core, possibly after explicit indeterminacies are allowed. This is the source-level framework for the debated "history-erasure" or "alien comparison" issues: forgetting is only allowed with a specified radial/coric account of what survives.
Guardrail: a multiradial statement is not a claim that all radial structures are identical. It is a claim that the relevant gluing isomorphisms over the coric core can be lifted or transported in the specified category, with the specified indeterminacy.
Sources: IUT II, Examples 1.7-1.9 Related: mono-theta rigidity radial environments multiradial fullness multiradiality log-theta lattice Corollary 3.12 corridor

Radial Environment

Example 1.7 begins with a type of radial data, a type of coric data, and a functorial algorithm from the former to the latter. Concretely, the radial data form a category R, the coric data form a category C, and the algorithm gives an essentially surjective functor Phi : R -> C.

Example 1.7(i) radial data mapped to a shared coric category
Source: IUT II, Example 1.7(i) Related: multiradial fullness radial taxonomy

Multiradial Fullness

In Example 1.7(ii), a radial environment is multiradial exactly when Phi : R -> C is full. If it is not full, the environment is uniradial: an object may lose rigidity when mapped to C, while a chosen lift back to R adds rigidity.

ConditionSource meaningUse in IUT
Full Phi Every coric isomorphism between images lifts to a radial isomorphism. No loss of rigidity under the radial algorithm.
Not full The output in C has less data than a chosen radial lift. Uniradial; simultaneous execution across alien spokes is not automatic.
Fiber product test Example 1.7(iii) characterizes multiradiality by behavior of the switching functor on R x_C R. Turns "can switch spokes" into a categorical condition.
Source: IUT II, Example 1.7(ii)-(iii) Related: parallel transport multiradiality

Tautological Multiradialization

Example 1.7(ii) also defines a tautological multiradialization. An object of R_mtz is a triple (R, C, alpha), where alpha : Phi(R) ~= C is a full poly-isomorphism. The radial algorithm then sends (R,C,alpha) to C.

Interpretation. Mochizuki describes this as forgetting the uniradiality of the original environment in a minimal possible fashion. For the wiki, this means a claim of multiradiality may be genuine fullness or a tautological upgrade; the source must say which one is being used.
Source: IUT II, Example 1.7(ii) Related: multiradial fullness poly-isomorphisms

Radial Parallel Transport

Remark 1.7.1 interprets fullness as a connection-like condition. A uniradial environment is analogous to a fibration without a connection; a multiradial environment is analogous to a fibration with a connection, allowing parallel transport between fibers of Phi : R -> C.

Uniqueness caveat. Lifts of coric isomorphisms are unique only up to the indeterminacy coming from the failure of Phi to be faithful. This is why multiradiality does not mean "all radial copies are identical".
Source: IUT II, Remark 1.7.1 Related: radial environments mutually alien copies

Concrete Radial Examples in Example 1.8

Example 1.8 gives concrete multiradial environments built from topological groups, MLF-Galois TM-pairs, unit monoids, cyclotomic and co-cyclotomic quotients, exterior cyclotomes, groupifications, and log-shell versions. The repeated pattern is: radial data includes a spoke-side object and a comparison to a coric G-side object.

Example itemCoric outputRole
1.8(i)GDifferent arithmetic holomorphic structures on a single coric G.
1.8(ii)G O^triangleright(G)Monoid version after sacrificing ring structure.
1.8(iii), 1.8(iv)G O^x(G), G O^xmu(G)Unit and co-cyclotomic variants with allowed isometry/orbit indeterminacy.
1.8(v), 1.8(vi)G O^xmu(G)Torsion and exterior-cyclotomic routes into the co-cyclotomic coric object.
1.8(vii)-(viii)G O^xmu(G)Groupification/profinite-completion variant tied back to the same coric output.
1.8(ix)Log-shell versionAdds the log-shell I(G) subset O^xmu(G).
Remark 1.8.1separation of automorphism sourcesAut(G)-induced automorphisms of O^xmu(G) do not coincide with nontrivial Gamma-actions.

Basic spoke/core layer

Start with (Pi,G,alpha)->G, then add the MLF-Galois TM-pair. These two pages explain the basic spoke-to-core pattern before unit and cyclotomic quotients enter.

Pi/G TM-pair

Unit quotient layer

The unit, cyclotomic, co-cyclotomic, and exterior-cyclotomic variants determine which scalar or torsion ambiguity is allowed in a multiradial comparison.

units quotients exterior

Log-shell layer

The final variants prepare the IUT III log-shell machinery by replacing bare O^xmu(G) data with (O^xmu(G), I(G)).

groupification log-shell Aut/Gamma
Source: IUT II, Example 1.8 Related: Frobenioids/log-shells theta_env package

IUT II Example 1.8(i): Basic Pi/G Radial Environment

The first concrete radial environment consists of radial data (Pi,G,alpha): a topological group Pi isomorphic to the local tempered group, a topological group G isomorphic to the base-field Galois group, and a full poly-isomorphism alpha : Pi/Delta ~= G. The coric data is just the isomorphism class of G.

PieceSource roleConceptual reading
Pi One spoke: a topological group isomorphic to the tempered fundamental group. Represents one arithmetic holomorphic structure.
G The common coric output, known only up to isomorphism. Represents the underlying mono-analytic core shared by the spokes.
alpha A full poly-isomorphism from Pi/Delta to G. Records how the spoke is viewed from the coric core.
This is the formal version of several arithmetic holomorphic structures arranged around one coric G. It is the base model for the later radial environments of Example 1.8.
Source: IUT II, Example 1.8(i) Related: Example 1.8 overview radial environment algorithm graphs

IUT II Example 1.8(ii): TM-Pair Radial Environment

The second environment replaces the bare group comparison by MLF-Galois TM-pair data. From Pi one has Pi MTM(Pi); from G one has G O^triangleright(G). The comparison alpha^triangleright is induced by the quotient Pi -> Pi/Delta and the basic Pi/Delta ~= G poly-isomorphism.

AlgorithmOutputSource warning
Pi -> Pi MTM(Pi) MLF-Galois TM-pair with a natural ring structure on the monoid plus zero. The ring structure belongs to the spoke-side anabelian reconstruction.
G -> G O^triangleright(G) MLF-Galois TM-pair attached to the base-field Galois group. Mochizuki says this route sacrifices the ring structure.
radial algorithm (Pi MTM(Pi), G O^triangleright(G), alpha^triangleright) -> G O^triangleright(G) The coric output is the G-side monoid package, not the full spoke ring history.
Source: IUT II, Example 1.8(ii) Related: basic Pi/G environment algorithm content

IUT II Example 1.8(iii): Unit-Orbit Radial Environment

Example 1.8(iii) restricts the monoid packages to their invertible parts. A closed subgroup Gamma subset Z^x acts trivially on G and by its natural action on O^x(G). The comparison alpha^x is the Gamma-orbit of the unit part of the TM-pair comparison.

ObjectRoleWhy separate it?
M_TM^x(Pi) Unit part of the spoke-side reconstructed monoid. It is still tied to the arithmetic holomorphic structure of Pi.
O^x(G) Coric unit object on the G-side. It is compared only up to the permitted Gamma-multiple.
Gamma-orbit Allowed ambiguity in the unit comparison. This is the first explicit scalar indeterminacy in the Example 1.8 ladder.
Source: IUT II, Example 1.8(iii) Related: cyclotomic/co-cyclotomic variant MLF-Galois indeterminacies

IUT II Example 1.8(iv): Cyclotomic and Co-Cyclotomic Variants

Example 1.8(iv) forms the torsion and torsion-quotient variants of the unit environment. The cyclotomic version uses M_TM^mu and O^mu(G). The co-cyclotomic version uses M_TM^xmu = M_TM^x/M_TM^mu and O^xmu(G)=O^x(G)/O^mu(G).

VariantConstructionAllowed ambiguity
cyclotomic Replace unit objects by their torsion submodules. Uses the corresponding torsion comparison.
co-cyclotomic Quotient units by roots of unity. May replace Gamma by a closed subgroup Gamma^xmu subset Ism.
Ism All G-equivariant isometries of O^xmu(G) preserving invariant lattices. Contains both the full isometry choice and the image of Z^x.
The co-cyclotomic quotient is central later because roots of unity have been divided out. This is why Corollary 1.10 and Corollary 1.12 repeatedly use O^xmu-style coric data.
Source: IUT II, Example 1.8(iv) Related: coric unit quotient Aut/Gamma separation

IUT II Example 1.8(v): Torsion-to-Co-Cyclotomic Environment

Example 1.8(v) deliberately maps torsion-side spoke data into the co-cyclotomic coric object. The radial data is (Pi M_TM^mu(Pi), G O^xmu(G), alpha^{mu,xmu}), where alpha^{mu,xmu} is determined by the basic Pi/Delta ~= G comparison and the trivial homomorphism M_TM^mu(Pi) -> O^xmu(G).

Torsion input

M_TM^mu(Pi) records the spoke-side torsion submodule.

Co-cyclotomic output

O^xmu(G) has already quotiented by roots of unity.

Trivial map

The torsion contribution maps trivially into the quotient coric object.

Source: IUT II, Example 1.8(v) Related: co-cyclotomic quotient Corollary 1.10

IUT II Example 1.8(vi): Exterior-Cyclotomic Version

Example 1.8(vi) replaces the spoke-side torsion module M_TM^mu(Pi) of Example 1.8(v) by the exterior cyclotome package Pi_mu(MTheta_*(Pi)) tensor Q/Z. This is the radial environment used directly by Corollary 1.10 for mono-theta cyclotomic rigidity.

ReplacementReasonDownstream use
M_TM^mu(Pi) replaced by Pi_mu(MTheta_*(Pi)) tensor Q/Z The exterior cyclotome is recovered from the projective mono-theta environment. Corollary 1.10 compares it with l·DeltaTheta(Pi).
Source: IUT II, Example 1.8(vi) Related: Corollary 1.10 theta_env package

IUT II Example 1.8(vii)-(viii): Groupification Variant

Example 1.8(vii) passes from monoids to groupifications and to inductive limits of profinite completions of invariant groupifications. Example 1.8(viii) then uses this groupified comparison to build a radial environment whose radial data contains Pi MTM(Pi), G O^gp(G), and a diagram of poly-morphisms connecting MTM, groupifications, units, and O^xmu(G).

StageConstructionCoric output
1.8(vii) Form M_TM^gp, O^gp, and the profinite-completed inductive-limit variants. Provides the groupified comparison alpha^gp.
1.8(viii) Use a diagram from MTM(Pi) through groupifications and units to O^xmu(G). The radial algorithm still lands in G O^xmu(G).
This variant is important because it records that the same co-cyclotomic coric output can be reached through a larger monoid/groupification diagram, not only through direct unit quotients.
Source: IUT II, Example 1.8(vii)-(viii) Related: torsion-to-co-cyclotomic route log-shell version

IUT II Example 1.8(ix): Log-Shell Radial Environment

Example 1.8(ix) attaches the log-shell I(G) subset O^xmu(G) to the co-cyclotomic coric object. Replacing G O^xmu(G) by the pair (G O^xmu(G), I(G) subset O^xmu(G)) gives log-shell versions of the environments of Example 1.8(v), (vi), and (viii).

Added dataSource constructionWhy it matters
I(G) The log-shell inside O^xmu(G), constructed from the G-invariants of O^x(G). This is the bridge to the log-shell machinery of IUT III and AbsTopIII.
log-shell versions Apply the replacement to the co-cyclotomic, exterior-cyclotomic, and groupification variants. The radial/coric framework now carries the analytic container used for log-volume estimates.
Source: IUT II, Example 1.8(ix) Related: Frobenioids/log-shells mono-analytic log-shells log-theta lattice

IUT II Remark 1.8.1: Aut/Gamma Separation

Remark 1.8.1 states that automorphisms of O^xmu(G) induced by elements of Aut(G) do not coincide with automorphisms induced by elements of Gamma whose image in Z_p^x is nontrivial.

Action sourceEffect detected by the sourceConsequence
Aut(G) Acts trivially on the Q_p quotient obtained from the p-adic logarithm of the cyclotomic character. Aut-induced ambiguity is not the same as scalar Gamma-ambiguity.
Gamma subset Z^x Acts through the natural image in Z_p^x on that quotient. Nontrivial Gamma image remains visible after passing to O^xmu(G).
This separation is an audit guardrail for indeterminacies: an Aut(G)-orbit and a nontrivial scalar orbit should not be merged as one undifferentiated automorphism slot.
Source: IUT II, Remark 1.8.1 Related: unit-orbit environment MLF-Galois indeterminacies

Graphs of Functorial Algorithms

Example 1.9 packages a functorial algorithm Xi : E -> F by its graph category: objects are pairs (E, Xi(E)) and morphisms are the compatible pairs (f, Xi(f)). For algorithms whose input is a group isomorphic to Pi_X^tp, the same graph can be viewed as multiradial or uniradial depending on the radial environment used.

Source guardrail. This is why "there exists a group-theoretic reconstruction algorithm" is not enough by itself. The radial/coric environment determines whether that algorithm is meaningful across alien spokes or only inside one spoke.
Source: IUT II, Example 1.9 Related: concrete radial examples radial taxonomy

IUT II Section 1: Mono-Theta Rigidity

Corollaries 1.10 through 1.12 turn mono-theta cyclotomic rigidity and constant-multiple rigidity into multiradial data. This is the local source for the distinction between an evaluation route that is already compatible with alien spokes and a base-field route that remains uniradial until log-shells enter.

Rigidity route from radial input to coric unit-quotient data
Source itemStatementWhy it matters
Example 1.7 and Remark 1.9.1 A functor from radial data to coric data is multiradial when it is full; otherwise it is uniradial. The point is to compute which outputs depend essentially on a spoke's arithmetic holomorphic structure and which can be described through the mono-analytic core. This supplies the formal meaning of "multiradial" used throughout the later theta and log-volume arguments.
Corollary 1.10 The mono-theta cyclotomic rigidity isomorphism sends the interior cyclotome l·DeltaTheta(Pi) to the exterior cyclotome Pi_mu of the mono-theta package and gives a multiradially defined functor relative to the coric G O×mu(G) environment. Roots of unity disappear in the unit quotient O×mu, so mono-theta cyclotomic rigidity is compatible with sharing this coric object across spokes.
Remark 1.10.2 The reason for Corollary 1.10 is not a generic identification: the exterior cyclotome maps trivially into O×mu(G). The formal model should distinguish a genuine coric quotient from a direct equality of cyclotomes across holomorphic structures.
Corollary 1.11 The base-field/Galois cyclotomic rigidity route can be represented multiradially only after allowing specified Aut(G)- or Gamma-orbit indeterminacies. This foreshadows why base-field numerical access is useful but not automatically compatible with alien-spoke comparison.
Corollary 1.12(i)-(ii) Pointed inversion automorphisms and restriction to the subgroup D yield invariant theta subsets and splittings after quotienting by torsion: M_TM×mu x {theta-invariants / torsion}. This is the constant-multiple rigidity input used later to control scalar ambiguity in theta-value comparison.
Remark 1.12.2 The multiradiality depends on constructing theta functions up to a mu_2l-indeterminacy, not up to a larger ring-dependent unit such as a chosen value 2 = 1+1. This is a source-level guardrail: a normalization tied to one ring structure would be uniradial and would fail at the log-shell stage.
Remark 1.12.4 A Kummer-compatible theta-evaluation algorithm must extend to coverings; functoriality then forces it to arise from a section G -> Pi_Ydd(Pi). This is the Galois-evaluation principle used in IUT II Section 2.

Coric unit quotient

Corollary 1.10 works because O×mu(G) has already divided out roots of unity. The exterior cyclotome can be rigidified without reintroducing a spoke-specific unit choice.

Cor. 1.10O×mu

Orbit indeterminacy

Corollary 1.11 permits the cyclotomic comparison through specified orbits. This is weaker than strict rigidity, but it is the price for a multiradial formulation of the base-field route.

Cor. 1.11orbits

Pointed inversion

A pointed inversion automorphism is reconstructed group-theoretically and isolates theta subsets invariant modulo torsion. This is the input for the direct-product splittings of Corollary 1.12.

Cor. 1.12(i)invariants

Constant-multiple split

The split separates a coric component M_TM×mu from a radial theta-orbit component. This is how scalar ambiguity is controlled before theta evaluation.

Cor. 1.12(ii)splitting

mu_2l normalization

The source normalization uses values over the two-torsion point mu- and gives only mu_2l-indeterminacy. A larger ring-dependent unit normalization would not be multiradial.

Rem. 1.12.2normalization

Galois evaluation

Remark 1.12.4 explains why the later evaluation at torsion points is expressed through Galois groups and sections, not through an external analytic choice of values.

Rem. 1.12.4sections
Formalization warning: mono-theta rigidity should be modeled as a package of algorithms, quotient choices, and permitted indeterminacies. A single field named "rigidity isomorphism" would hide the source distinction between multiradial mono-theta rigidity and the initially uniradial base-field route.
Sources: IUT II, Corollaries 1.10-1.12 Related: mono-theta environments Corollary 1.10 Corollary 1.11 Corollary 1.12 Galois theta evaluation theta evaluation route multiradiality

Multiradial Mono-Theta Cyclotomic Rigidity

Corollary 1.10 applies the exterior-cyclotomic radial environment of Example 1.8(vi). The cyclotomic rigidity isomorphism l·DeltaTheta(Pi) ~= Pi_mu(MTheta_*(Pi)) is constructed from the group-theoretic mono-theta system and determines a multiradially defined functor via the graph construction of Example 1.9.

InputRigidified dataMultiradial output
Pi in the exterior-cyclotomic radial environment Domain l·DeltaTheta(Pi), codomain Pi_mu(MTheta_*(Pi)), and the rigidity isomorphism. A graph category R^dagger whose natural functor from R is multiradially defined.
Source: IUT II, Corollary 1.10 Related: interior/exterior cyclotomes coric unit quotient

Coric Unit Quotient in Remark 1.10.2

Remark 1.10.2 explains why Corollary 1.10 is multiradial: the coric object O^xmu(G) is obtained by quotienting O^x(G) by roots of unity. The homomorphism from Pi_mu(MTheta_*) tensor Q/Z to this quotient is trivial, so rigidifying the exterior cyclotome is compatible with sharing the coric O^xmu(G) across distinct holomorphic spokes.

Guardrail. This is not an equality of the cyclotomes in different arithmetic holomorphic structures. It is compatibility after passing to a coric quotient where the roots-of-unity contribution has disappeared.
Source: IUT II, Remark 1.10.2 Related: Example 1.8 Corollary 1.10

MLF-Galois Rigidity with Indeterminacies

Corollary 1.11 gives a multiradial form of base-field/Galois cyclotomic rigidity, but only with explicit orbit indeterminacies: a Gamma-orbit for the G O^triangleright(G) route and an Aut(G)-orbit for the comparison from mu_Z(G) to l·DeltaTheta(Pi).

OrbitObject comparedReason it matters
Gamma-orbit mu_Z(G) ~= mu_Z(O^x(G)) Records the indeterminacy from forgetting the full anabelian construction of the monoid data.
Aut(G)-orbit mu_Z(G) ~= l·DeltaTheta(Pi) Records that G is shared only up to isomorphism.
Source: IUT II, Corollary 1.11 Related: algorithm content Corollary 1.10 contrast

Algorithm Content in Remark 1.11.3

Remark 1.11.3 is a methodological warning. It is not enough to know that some reconstruction algorithm exists; the content of the algorithm and the point at which anabelian structure is forgotten determine which indeterminacies appear in the multiradial statement.

Coric data must be specified

If G is coric, objects constructed from G are coric only while their anabelian construction history is retained or explicitly declared as coric data.

Corollary 1.10 is strict

Its strict rigidity comes from the trivial relationship between the cyclotomic portion and the coric O^xmu quotient.

Corollary 1.11 has orbits

Its orbits arise from the nontrivial relationship between O^triangleright input and O^xmu coric data.

Source: IUT II, Remark 1.11.3 Related: graphs of algorithms MLF-Galois orbits

Multiradial Constant Multiple Rigidity

Corollary 1.12 constructs pointed inversion automorphisms, restricts the theta package to the associated decomposition subgroup, and obtains splittings after quotienting torsion. The result separates a coric M_TM^xmu component from the theta-orbit component needed for constant-multiple rigidity.

Corollary 1.12 itemContentUse
(i) Group-theoretically constructs pointed inversion automorphisms (iota,D). Supplies the invariant subgroup for theta restriction.
(ii) Restricts to D and compares M_TM^x · infty theta with the environment version. Moves from standard theta to the exterior-cyclotome theta package.
(iii) Forms the direct-product-style quotient by torsion. Controls scalar ambiguity in the later theta-value comparison.
Source: IUT II, Corollary 1.12 Related: pointed inversion theta_env package

mu_2l Normalization

Remark 1.12.2 explains that the theta functions used in this multiradial construction are normalized only up to a mu_2l-indeterminacy. A stronger normalization by a ring-dependent unit, such as choosing a value tied to 2 = 1+1, would be uniradial and would fail at the log-shell stage.

Guardrail. For the wiki and for later formalization, this is a hard source constraint: do not replace the allowed mu_2l ambiguity by a normalization that remembers one spoke's ring structure.
Source: IUT II, Remark 1.12.2 Related: constant multiple rigidity log-shells

Galois Evaluation Principle

Remark 1.12.4 explains why theta evaluation in the next section is forced into a Galois-theoretic form. A Kummer-compatible evaluation algorithm must extend to coverings; by functoriality, it then arises from a section G -> Pi_Ydd(Pi) rather than from an external analytic choice of values.

Source: IUT II, Remark 1.12.4 Related: Galois theta evaluation tempered covering ladder

IUT II §2: Theta Evaluation

IUT II Section 2 develops the group-theoretic algorithm for evaluating the etale theta function at l-torsion labels. The route passes through tempered covering reconstruction, subgraph decomposition groups, signed cusp labels, two-torsion translates, theta-value orbits, and the single-basepoint conjugate synchronization needed for simultaneous Kummer theory.

Grouped route

This page is the unified Section 2 route. The first row below gives the larger conceptual units; the second row keeps the individually searchable source-level pages.

covering ladder signed labels evaluation orbits mono/base

Atomic source pages

Use these when checking a single proposition, corollary, or remark against the paper text.

2.1 2.2 2.3 2.4 2.5 2.6 2.8 2.9

Reading rule

Read the hub first to understand why a single basepoint is needed, then jump to the fine pages for exact statements such as the j^2 exponent or the uniradial warning.

grouped unitIUT II §2
Section 2 evaluation pipeline from tempered coverings to synchronized theta-value orbits
Source itemConstructionConceptual role
Proposition 2.1 Reconstructs the diagram of tempered covering groups Pi_Ydd -> Pi_Y -> Pi_X and its lower-level companion diagram functorially from the local tempered group Pi_v. Supplies the covering ladder on which theta functions and theta values are evaluated.
Remark 2.1.1 Describes the dual-graph chain, the inversion automorphisms, the finite tree Gamma_X, and the one-vertex subgraph Gamma_X^bullet. Identifies the connected subgraphs that later give the single evaluation basepoint.
Proposition 2.2 Constructs decomposition groups Pi_v^bullet subset Pi_vbar subset Pi_v and the inversion-invariant theta-function orbit theta_iota(Pi_v). Turns graph-theoretic components into group-theoretic domains for the evaluation algorithm.
Definition 2.3 Defines the ambient groups Pi_v, Pi_v^±, Pi_v^cor, signed cusp-label classes, and the induced F_l^±-torsor structure. Provides the label system that later matches the additive Theta±ell symmetry.
Corollary 2.4 From a signed cusp label t, constructs the inertia group I_t, decomposition group D_t, the two-torsion decomposition group D_mu-, and the translated evaluation group D_t,mu-, compatibly with F_l^±-symmetry. This is the group-theoretic replacement for choosing torsion-shifted analytic evaluation points.
Corollary 2.5 Restricts etale theta functions to the subgraph group and then to D_t,mu-, producing mu_2l- and mu-orbits theta_|t|(Pi_vdd^gamma) indexed by |t| in |F_l|. This is the actual group-theoretic theta evaluation algorithm.
Remark 2.5.1 Relative to the standard cyclotomic rigidity isomorphism, the label j in |F_l| gives the mu_2l-orbit of the theta value with exponent j^2. This is the source of the square-weighted value profile used in the later Gaussian and log-volume constructions.
Corollary 2.6 Defines M_TM^x on the subgraph and gives zero-label splittings after quotienting by torsion. Supplies the constant-multiple control needed before passing to mono-theta and Gaussian data.
Corollary 2.8 Uses mono-theta cyclotomic rigidity to replace l·DeltaTheta by Pi_mu, producing mono-theta-theoretic theta evaluation and compatible splittings. Converts group-theoretic evaluation into the multiradial mono-theta route.
Corollary 2.9 Uses base-field-theoretic cyclotomes instead of the mono-theta exterior cyclotome. Useful for base-field access, but Remark 2.9.1 says this route is only uniradial until later log-shell modifications.

Tempered covering diagram

Proposition 2.1 reconstructs the covering ladder Pi_Ydd -> Pi_Y -> Pi_X at a bad place. Both squares in the diagram are cartesian, and the construction is functorial in the topological group.

Prop. 2.1coverings

Subgraph decomposition groups

Proposition 2.2 uses the finite tree and one-vertex subgraph of the dual graph to define Pi_vbar and Pi_v^bullet, up to the stated conjugacy.

Prop. 2.2subgraphs

Signed cusp labels

Definition 2.3 recovers LabCusp± from cuspidal inertia groups and equips the relevant label set with its F_l^±-torsor structure.

Def. 2.3labels

Two-torsion translates

Corollary 2.4 constructs the decomposition group for the mu--translate of the cusp that gives rise to I_t. This group is the image of the evaluation section used by theta evaluation.

Cor. 2.4evaluation point

Theta-value orbits

Corollary 2.5 produces orbits theta_|t|(Pi_vdd^gamma) after restriction to the translated decomposition groups. The result depends only on |t|, not the sign representative.

Cor. 2.5orbits

Square-weight value

Remark 2.5.1 identifies the theta-value orbit for j with the orbit of the value whose exponent is j^2. This is source-level data, not later notation.

Rem. 2.5.1j²

Independent conjugacy

Remark 2.5.2 explains why conjugacy indeterminacies acting on cusp inertia groups and on the subgraph groups are independent. Figure 2.1 visualizes the two unsynchronized translation lines.

Rem. 2.5.2indeterminacy

Conjugate synchronization

Remark 2.6.1 says all theta values for |t| in |F_l| are computed relative to one Galois group and one cyclotome arising from one basepoint. This is what allows simultaneous Kummer theory for all theta values.

Rem. 2.6.1single basepoint

Theta±ell compatibility

Remark 2.6.2 identifies the same single-basepoint phenomenon with the basepoint behind the F_l^±-symmetry of D-Theta±ell-Hodge theaters.

Rem. 2.6.2F_l^±

Mono-theta evaluation

Corollary 2.8 is the multiradial route: it applies mono-theta cyclotomic rigidity to express the evaluated theta data through Pi_mu.

Cor. 2.8multiradial

Base-field evaluation

Corollary 2.9 gives the base-field cyclotome version. Remark 2.9.1 marks it as uniradial in this form, so it cannot simply replace the mono-theta route in multiradial arguments.

Cor. 2.9uniradial
Formalization warning: the single-basepoint condition is not cosmetic. The simultaneous Kummer description of all theta values depends on one shared Galois group and one shared cyclotome; independent local evaluations would lose conjugate synchronization.
Sources: IUT II Related: mono-theta environments distinguished subgraph theta evaluation route Theta±ell bridges Gaussian Frobenioids

IUT II Section 2: Single Basepoint Setup

Section 2 states the theta-evaluation problem as a group-theoretic algorithm for the etale theta function on l-torsion points. The technical constraint is not merely local evaluation: all evaluation points must be handled with respect to one basepoint, one ambient Galois group, and one cyclotome.

One ambient container for many labels Section 2 setup before Proposition 2.1
Source statementMeaningWiki use
Opening of Section 2 The target is a group-theoretic algorithm around Hodge-Arakelov evaluation of the etale theta function on l-torsion points. The later pages should be read as reconstruction steps, not as free analytic choices of theta values.
Conjugate synchronization Copies of base-field Galois objects at different evaluation points must be synchronized. This is why the page separates independent conjugacy actions from the final single-basepoint Kummer interpretation.
Collas orientation Collas' category-theoretic reading treats IUT data as information carried in containers up to isomorphism, not as one globally identified scheme. This is useful vocabulary for the wiki, but the source of the construction here remains IUT II Section 2.
Sources: IUT II, Section 2 opening Related: conjugate synchronization Collas category view tempered covering diagram

IUT II Proposition 2.1: Tempered Covering Diagram

Proposition 2.1 reviews a diagram of open injections of tempered fundamental groups associated to the coverings Ydd -> Y -> X and their companion objects. The diagram is reconstructed functorially from the temp-slim topological group Pi_X^tp = Pi_v.

Cartesian covering ladder Proposition 2.1
ClauseContentConstraint
2.1(a)-(c) Names the tempered groups attached to X_v, Y_v, and the double-covering object Ydd_v. The wiki treats these as the first layer of the evaluation domain, before signed cusp labels are introduced.
2.1(d) The arrows are natural inclusions and the two displayed squares are cartesian. Later restrictions of theta classes use this diagram, so collapsing it to a single group loses source structure.
Conclusion The whole diagram is reconstructed by a functorial group-theoretic algorithm from Pi_v. This is the first “no chosen model” step of Section 2.
Source: IUT II, Proposition 2.1 Related: dual graph subgraphs subgraph decomposition groups

IUT II Remark 2.1.1: Dual Graph Subgraphs

Remark 2.1.1 turns the stable special fiber into a graph-theoretic control object. The relevant graph is a chain of projective-line components whose vertices form a torsor over Z; after quotienting to the finite range {-l,...,l}, inversion fixes the central vertex.

Finite subgraphs selected from the chain Remark 2.1.1

Graph input

The dual graph of the relevant special fiber is a chain; maps induced by the coverings in Proposition 2.1 are bijective on vertices.

2.1.1(i)chain

Selected subgraphs

Gamma_X is the unique connected tree stabilized by inversion and containing all vertices of the finite graph; Gamma_X^bullet is the one-vertex subgraph at label 0.

2.1.1(ii)basepoint
Source: IUT II, Remark 2.1.1 Related: tempered covering diagram subgraph groups distinguished subgraph

IUT II Proposition 2.2: Subgraph Decomposition Groups

Proposition 2.2 assigns decomposition groups to the subgraphs selected in Remark 2.1.1. The data Pi_v^bullet subset Pi_vbar subset Pi_v together with inversion is reconstructed from Pi_v, up to the stated conjugacy.

Graph subobject to group subobject Proposition 2.2(i)
Audit point: the conjugacy qualifications are part of the construction. A wiki or formal model that records the subgroups without their stated indeterminacy would misrepresent Proposition 2.2.
Source: IUT II, Proposition 2.2(i) Related: dual graph subgraphs iota-invariant theta orbit

IUT II Proposition 2.2: Iota-Invariant Theta Orbit

The second part of Proposition 2.2 applies the theta-function algorithms of Proposition 1.4 together with invariance under inversion. The result is a specific mu_2l-orbit inside the theta set and a corresponding mu-orbit inside the enlarged theta set.

Selecting an invariant orbit Proposition 2.2(ii)
InputSelection ruleOutput
Proposition 1.4 theta algorithms Apply them with Pi = Pi_v. theta(Pi_v) subset infty theta(Pi_v).
Inversion automorphism iota Impose invariance with respect to iota. The specific theta_iota and infty theta_iota orbits.
Source: IUT II, Proposition 2.2(ii) Related: etale theta standard type theta evaluation orbits

IUT II Definition 2.3: Signed Cusp Label Classes

Definition 2.3 defines signed cusp-label classes from cuspidal inertia groups and their commensurators in larger signed ambient groups. This is a group-theoretic label system for the later evaluation points.

From inertia to labels Definition 2.3(ii)-(iv)

Cusp reconstruction

Definition 2.3 recalls that cuspidal inertia groups are reconstructed from the relevant topological group and transferred through intersections or commensurators across the ambient inclusions.

2.3(ii)inertia

Label-to-vertex step

A class t determines a unique vertex of Gamma_X, hence a one-vertex subgraph and a decomposition group Pi_v^bullet,t.

2.3(iv)vertex
Source: IUT II, Definition 2.3(ii)-(iv) Related: F_l^± torsor labels two-torsion translates

IUT II Definition 2.3: F_l^± Torsor Labels

Definition 2.3 carries the signed labels into larger signed and core ambient groups. For signed ambient groups, the quotient Pi_cor / Pi_± preserves the label torsor and gives a natural outer isomorphism to F_l^±.

ItemDefinition 2.3 contentReason it matters
Ambient inclusions Delta_v -> Delta_v^± -> Delta_v^cor and Pi_v -> Pi_v^± -> Pi_v^cor, with profinite completions and conjugacy conventions. The label action is not floating; it is attached to these ambient groups.
Zero and ±-canonical classes LabCusp±(Pi_v) has a zero element and a ±-canonical element, well-defined up to multiplication by ±1. The later use of absolute labels |t| does not erase the signed origin.
Outer quotient action The action of Pi_cor/Pi_± preserves the F_l^±-torsor structure and identifies the quotient with F_l^±. This is the local label symmetry used by Corollary 2.4 and by the Theta±ell side of IUT I.
Category-theoretic reading: this page is one of the places where Collas' container vocabulary is helpful. The labels are reconstructed and transported inside specified ambient categories/groups, rather than being globally named scheme points.
Source: IUT II, Definition 2.3(i), (iii), (v) Related: IUT I additive labels Theta±ell bridges Collas category view

IUT II Corollary 2.4: Two-Torsion Translates

Corollary 2.4 turns a signed cusp label into the group-theoretic data needed to evaluate at a two-torsion translate of a cusp. The objects D_t, D_mu-, and D_t,mu- are produced from inclusions involving the cuspidal inertia group I_t.

From cusp label to evaluation section Corollary 2.4(ii)
PartOutputCompatibility
2.4(i) Equivalent conditions for compatible conjugates of inertia and subgraph groups. Controls the independent conjugacy choices that enter evaluation.
2.4(ii) Constructs D_t, D_mu-, and D_t,mu-. Provides the group-theoretic evaluation point.
2.4(iii) Compatibility with conjugation by the core group quotient. Matches the F_l^±-symmetry of labels.
Source: IUT II, Corollary 2.4 Related: signed cusp labels theta evaluation orbits

IUT II Corollary 2.5: Theta Evaluation Orbits

Corollary 2.5 restricts the etale theta-function classes to the subgraph group and then to the translated decomposition group D_t,mu-. The output is an indexed collection of mu_2l- and mu-orbits theta_|t|(Pi_vdd^gamma) and infty theta_|t|(Pi_vdd^gamma).

Restrict, evaluate, forget sign representative Corollary 2.5
Corollary 2.5 partConstructionOutput
(i) Restricts the subquotient l·DeltaTheta from Pi_v to Pi_vdd and constructs the associated quotients. The cohomological target is preserved on the subgraph group.
(ii) Restricts theta_iota to Pi_vdd^gamma and then to D_t,mu-. Produces theta_t and infty theta_t.
(iii) Lets t range over signed cusp labels and records the independent conjugacy actions. Gives functorial collections indexed by |t| in |F_l|.
Source: IUT II, Corollary 2.5 Related: two-torsion translates square-weight exponent theta evaluation overview

IUT II Remark 2.5.1: Square-Weight Exponent

Remark 2.5.1 identifies, relative to the standard cyclotomic rigidity isomorphism and Kummer map, the theta-value orbit associated to j in |F_l|. The source-level exponent is the square j^2; this is the local seed for later Gaussian and log-volume value profiles.

Absolute labels produce square weights Remark 2.5.1(i)
Precision warning: the wiki records the square exponent as a statement of Remark 2.5.1, not as a generic convention for all theta values. The selected values depend on the preceding subgraph and Kummer setup.
Source: IUT II, Remark 2.5.1 Related: theta evaluation orbits Gaussian value profiles log-volume estimates

IUT II Remark 2.5.2: Independent Conjugacy

Remark 2.5.2 explains why the conjugacy indeterminacies acting on cusp inertia groups and on the subgraph groups are independent. Both live in a common ambient container, but their horizontal translation actions are not synchronized.

Two unsynchronized translation lines Figure 2.1 and Remark 2.5.2
Remark partContentModeling consequence
2.5.2(i) Inclusions I_t^gamma -> Pi_vdd^gamma are interpreted as morphisms between quotient-like families inside one ambient container. Record both families, not just the resulting theta orbit.
2.5.2(ii)-(iii) The global bridge Delta^± relates local labels to global data, while Pi_vdd does not globalize over multiple places. Do not identify local subgraph conjugacy with global label conjugacy.
2.5.2(iv) Figure 2.1 visualizes two independent translation actions. This is the diagrammatic source of the “no synchronized separator” warning.
Source: IUT II, Remark 2.5.2 Related: theta evaluation orbits conjugate synchronization subgraph criterion

IUT II Corollary 2.6: Zero-Label Splittings

Corollary 2.6 extends the M_TM^x construction to the subgraph group and records the splitting that occurs at the zero-labeled evaluation point. This is the place where the zero label connects the theta evaluation algorithm back to constant-multiple rigidity.

PartConstructionRole
2.6(i) Defines M_TM^x(Pi_vdd^gamma) and its products with theta_iota and infty theta_iota after restriction to the subgraph group. Transfers the mono-theta multiplicative module package to the evaluation domain.
2.6(ii) When t = 0, the theta-value orbit is the orbit of the identity element; after quotienting by torsion, restriction to D_t,mu- gives splittings. Supplies the zero-label comparison used in Remark 2.6.2 and later Gaussian constructions.
Precision point: Corollary 2.6 is not a general splitting for arbitrary labels. The splitting statement is attached to the zero-labeled evaluation point after quotienting by the torsion submodule.
Source: IUT II, Corollary 2.6 Related: constant-multiple rigidity Theta±ell basepoint

IUT II Remark 2.6.1: Conjugate Synchronization

Remark 2.6.1 names conjugate synchronization: all label-indexed theta values are computed relative to the same copy of G_v(Pi_vdd^gamma) and the same cyclotome l·DeltaTheta(Pi_vdd^gamma). This is what permits simultaneous Kummer theory.

One Galois group, one cyclotome, many theta values Remark 2.6.1
The point is not that there are no indeterminacies. The point is that the theta values for all labels are represented in one Kummer-theoretic ambient setting, rather than in unrelated copies of the base-field Galois data.
Source: IUT II, Remark 2.6.1 Related: single basepoint setup independent conjugacy Gaussian Frobenioids

IUT II Remark 2.6.2: Theta±ell Basepoint Compatibility

Remark 2.6.2 relates conjugate synchronization to the D-Theta±ell-Hodge theaters of IUT I. The single basepoint used for the theta-evaluation algorithm is compatible with the label synchronization behind the F_l^±-symmetry.

Compatible symmetry

The local group Pi_v, its signed commensurator Pi_v^±, and the Delta_v^cor/Delta_v^± -> F_l^± action are read through the D-Theta±ell theater.

2.6.2(i)F_l^±

Incompatible symmetry

The plain F_l-symmetry of D-ThetaNF theaters involves permuting multiple local copies and forces label crushing if one tries to identify them directly.

2.6.2(ii)label crushing

Zero-label comparison

The F_l^±-symmetry allows comparison with label zero, hence with the unit terms appearing in the Corollary 1.12 splittings.

2.6.2(iii)zero label

Formalization cue

A model should distinguish the additive signed symmetry used here from the unsigned symmetry that appears in D-ThetaNF contexts.

auditsymmetry type
Source: IUT II, Remark 2.6.2 Related: Theta±ell bridges zero-label splittings additive labels

IUT II Remark 2.6.3: Distinguished Subgraph Criterion

Remark 2.6.3 explains why the chosen subgraph is not arbitrary. Connectedness, the zero label, independent conjugacy, a square-average maximization, and product-formula compatibility all point back to the distinguished graph Gamma_Ydd.

ConditionSourceEffect
Connectedness Remark 2.6.3(i), using Remark 2.6.1 The alternative subgraph Gamma' must be connected.
Zero vertex Remark 2.6.3(i), using Corollary 2.6(ii) The subgraph must contain label 0.
Fiber restriction Remark 2.6.3(ii), using Remark 2.5.2 One cannot restrict to one representative of a fiber over |F_l| without the other representatives in that fiber.
Average maximization Remark 2.6.3(ii)-(iii) The quantity ||Gamma'|| reaches its relevant maximum at the distinguished size.
Product formula Remark 2.6.3(iv)-(v) Multiple representatives in a fiber are incompatible with the later globalization by product formula.
Source: IUT II, Remark 2.6.3 Related: distinguished subgraph overview square-weight exponent global Gaussian Frobenioids

IUT II Definition 2.7: Mono-Theta Projective System

Definition 2.7 introduces a projective system of mono-theta environments MTheta_* whose associated Pi_X(MTheta_*) is identified with Pi_v. It then pulls the Section 2 subgraph groups into the mono-theta environment.

PartDefinitionUse in Corollary 2.8
2.7(i) Defines Pi_MTheta* as the inverse limit of the induced topological groups. Its kernel over Pi_X(MTheta_*) is the exterior cyclotome Pi_mu(MTheta_*). Supplies the mono-theta cyclotome container.
2.7(ii) Defines inverse images of Pi_vdd subset Pi_vbar subset Pi_v and the subquotients Pi_mu, l·DeltaTheta, Pi_vdd, and G_v. Allows cyclotomic rigidity to replace l·DeltaTheta by Pi_mu on the subgraph.
Source: IUT II, Definition 2.7 Related: projective MTheta systems Theta_env package MTheta evaluation

IUT II Corollary 2.8: Mono-Theta Evaluation

Corollary 2.8 repeats the theta-evaluation algorithm after using the mono-theta cyclotomic rigidity isomorphism to replace l·DeltaTheta by the exterior cyclotome Pi_mu. This is the multiradial route out of Section 2.

Replace the cyclotome, keep the evaluation pattern Definition 2.7 and Corollary 2.8
PartCorollary 2.8 contentConsequence
(i) Restricts the mono-theta theta classes to subgraphs and evaluation points. Produces theta_env,|t| and infty theta_env,|t|.
(ii) Packages the construction as a functorial algorithm in MTheta_*. Maintains independent conjugacy compatibility.
(iii) Transports the zero-label splitting of Corollary 2.6. Connects mono-theta evaluation to the later Gaussian splitting data.
Source: IUT II, Corollary 2.8 Related: MTheta projective system Frobenioid route base-field cyclotomes

IUT II Remark 2.8.2: Frobenioid Significance of Mono-Theta Evaluation

Remark 2.8.2 explains why Corollary 2.8 is not just a notational variant of Corollary 2.5. The mono-theta version connects group-theoretic theta evaluation to Frobenioid-theoretic theta functions associated to tempered Frobenioids.

Special case

Remark 2.8.1 says Corollaries 2.5 and 2.6 are recovered when the projective mono-theta system is obtained from Pi_v by the algorithm of Proposition 1.2.

Rem. 2.8.1specialization

Frobenioid route

Remark 2.8.2 says the value of Corollary 2.8 is that it relates the Section 2 evaluation theory to Frobenioid-theoretic theta functions and Frobenioid cyclotomes.

Rem. 2.8.2Frobenioid
Source: IUT II, Remarks 2.8.1-2.8.2 Related: tempered Gaussian monoids Gaussian Frobenioid categories Collas category view

IUT II Remark 2.8.3: Archimedean Segment and Discrete Rigidity

Remark 2.8.3 interprets the finite archimedean line segment Gamma_X as selecting integer representatives -l,...,l of congruence classes modulo l. Mochizuki relates this selection to discrete rigidity and to cyclotomic rigidity.

Remark partContentDownstream role
2.8.3(i) The archimedean line segment singles out integer representatives with absolute value at most l. Explains why the value profile is not merely a residue-class list.
2.8.3(ii) The argument depends on discreteness of Z and on the square map modulo the {±1} action. Links the square-weighted profile to discrete and cyclotomic rigidity.
Source: IUT II, Remark 2.8.3 Related: dual graph subgraphs square-weight exponent cyclotomic compatibility

IUT II Corollary 2.9: Base-Field Cyclotome Evaluation

Corollary 2.9 repeats the theta-evaluation construction using base-field cyclotomes mu_Z(G_v(-)) instead of the mono-theta exterior cyclotome Pi_mu. The pattern is parallel to Corollary 2.8, but the radiality status is different.

PartConstructionOutput
(i) Applies base-field cyclotomic rigidity to replace l·DeltaTheta by mu_Z(G_v(-)), then restricts to subgraphs and evaluation groups. Produces theta_bs,|t| and infty theta_bs,|t|.
(ii) Packages the construction as a functorial group-theoretic algorithm in Pi_v. Maintains the independent conjugacy actions from Corollary 2.5.
(iii) Transports the zero-label splitting using the base-field cyclotome notation. Gives the base-field analogue of the Corollary 2.8 splitting.
Source: IUT II, Corollary 2.9 Related: MTheta evaluation uniradial price

IUT II Remark 2.9.1: Uniradial Price and Log-Shell Repair

Remark 2.9.1 records the tradeoff between the mono-theta cyclotome route and the base-field cyclotome route. The mono-theta route is multiradial; the base-field route gives useful maps to Z but is only uniradial until the log-shell modifications developed in IUT III.

Two cyclotome routes, different radiality Remark 2.9.1
Remark partClaimWiki consequence
2.9.1(i) Pi_mu admits a multiradial formulation, whereas mu_Z(G_v) is only uniradial unless one gives up the cyclotomic rigidity in question. Keep the two routes separate.
2.9.1(ii) The base-field cyclotome route allows use of the natural surjection from H^1(G_v, mu_Z(G_v)) to Z. It is useful, but not a replacement for the multiradial route.
2.9.1(iii) IUT III log-shell theory modifies the algorithms to obtain manifestly multiradial functors, with relatively mild indeterminacies. This is a bridge into the log-link/log-shell pages.
Source: IUT II, Remark 2.9.1 Related: base-field cyclotomes theta/log links synchronization/log-wall

IUT II Propositions 2.1-2.2: Tempered Covering Ladder and Subgraph Groups

The first step in IUT II Section 2 reconstructs the tempered covering diagram and the subgraph decomposition groups that support theta evaluation. This fixes the connected graph-theoretic basepoint before signed labels and theta values enter.

Covering groups and graph subgroups Proposition 2.1, Remark 2.1.1, Proposition 2.2
Source itemConstructionRole
Proposition 2.1 Reconstructs the diagram of open injections Pi_Ydd -> Pi_Y -> Pi_X and its companion lower row from the tempered group Pi_v. Supplies the covering ladder on which theta functions and theta values are evaluated.
Remark 2.1.1 The special-fiber dual graph is a chain; inversion fixes the central label 0. The finite connected subgraph Gamma_X and the one-vertex subgraph Gamma_X^bullet are reconstructed. This graph segment is the geometric source of the later single-basepoint constraint.
Proposition 2.2 Constructs Pi_vbullet subset Pi_vbar subset Pi_v and an inversion automorphism iota, up to the stated conjugacy indeterminacy, then extracts theta_iota(Pi_v) and infty theta_iota(Pi_v). Converts the graph subobjects into group-theoretic domains and inversion-invariant theta-function orbits.
Sources: IUT II, Proposition 2.1 through Proposition 2.2 Related: Section 2 atlas signed cusp labels theta evaluation orbits

IUT II Definition 2.3: Signed Cusp Labels and Ambient Groups

Definition 2.3 builds the ambient local groups, cusp inertia classes, and signed label torsors used by the theta-evaluation algorithm. It is the local IUT II version of the additive label discipline introduced in IUT I.

Local signed cusp-label package Definition 2.3
ClauseDefinitionDownstream use
2.3(i) Defines the local diagram Delta_v -> Delta_v± -> Delta_vcor and Pi_v -> Pi_v± -> Pi_vcor, with profinite completions and conjugacy conventions. Establishes the ambient containers in which cusp labels and conjugacy indeterminacies live.
2.3(ii)-(iii) Reconstructs cuspidal inertia groups and defines LabCusp±(Pi) via commensurators in the larger signed group. Makes signed labels group-theoretic rather than analytic choices of cusps.
2.3(iv) A label t determines a unique vertex and hence a decomposition group Pi_vbullet,t subset Pi_vbar subset Pi_v. Connects the signed label to the subgraph on which evaluation will occur.
2.3(v) For signed ambient groups, the quotient Pi_cor / Pi_± acts on labels and determines an outer isomorphism to F_l^±. Supplies the compatibility with IUT I additive F_l^±-symmetry.
Sources: IUT II, Definition 2.3 Related: IUT I additive labels tempered covering ladder theta evaluation orbits

IUT II Corollaries 2.4-2.6: Theta Evaluation Orbits and Synchronization

Corollaries 2.4-2.6 turn signed cusp labels into group-theoretic evaluation points, restrict the etale theta function to those points, and isolate the single-basepoint conjugate synchronization needed for simultaneous Kummer theory.

From labels to synchronized theta-value orbits Corollaries 2.4-2.6 and Remarks 2.5.1-2.6.3
Source itemConstructionConstraint
Corollary 2.4 From I_t and a compatible conjugate of the subgraph group, constructs D_t, D_mu-, and D_t,mu-, compatibly with F_l^±-symmetry. This replaces analytic evaluation-point choice by group-theoretic decomposition groups.
Corollary 2.5 Restricts theta functions first to Pi_vdd and then to D_t,mu-, producing mu_2l- and mu-orbits theta_|t| and infty theta_|t|. The resulting orbit depends only on the absolute label |t|, but the label discipline is not erased.
Remarks 2.5.1-2.5.2 The theta values correspond to the special collection q_v^(j^2). Independent conjugacy indeterminacies act on inertia and subgraph-group conjugates. The construction must be modeled as two independent conjugacy actions inside one ambient container, not as a single synchronized relabeling.
Corollary 2.6 and Remarks 2.6.1-2.6.2 Zero-labeled evaluation gives splittings compatible with Corollary 1.12. Conjugate synchronization means all labels are evaluated relative to one Galois group and one cyclotome. This is why the additive F_l^±-symmetry is compatible with theta evaluation whereas the multiplicative F_l-symmetry would crush labels.
Remark 2.6.3 The connected subgraph is distinguished by qualitative constraints, by maximizing the average involving j^2, and by compatibility with the global product formula. The value set is not arbitrary sampling of theta values; the selected subgraph is tied to later global realified Frobenioids.
Sources: IUT II, Corollaries 2.4-2.6 Related: signed cusp labels distinguished subgraph tempered Gaussian monoids

IUT II Definition 2.7 and Corollaries 2.8-2.9: Mono-Theta and Base-Field Evaluation

The final part of Section 2 repeats theta evaluation after replacing the interior cyclotome by either the mono-theta exterior cyclotome or the base-field cyclotome. Mochizuki explicitly distinguishes the multiradial mono-theta route from the useful but initially uniradial base-field route.

Two cyclotome replacements Definition 2.7, Corollary 2.8, Corollary 2.9
SourceConstructionStatus
Definition 2.7 For a projective mono-theta environment MTheta_* with Pi_X(MTheta_*) ~= Pi_v, defines the inverse limit group, the subgraph inverse images, and the restricted cyclotomic rigidity isomorphism lDeltaTheta(MTheta_*dd) ~= Pi_mu(MTheta_*dd). Sets up the mono-theta replacement of the coefficient cyclotome.
Corollary 2.8 Applies the mono-theta cyclotomic rigidity isomorphism to produce theta_env and infty theta_env orbits, including the zero-label splitting. This route relates group-theoretic theta evaluation to Frobenioid-theoretic theta functions from tempered Frobenioids.
Remarks 2.8.1-2.8.3 Corollaries 2.5-2.6 are the special case where MTheta_* comes from Pi_v. The chosen graph segment uses discrete rigidity and is dual to cyclotomic rigidity. Explains why the mono-theta version is the bridge to Frobenioid theta functions, not a redundant restatement.
Corollary 2.9 Replaces lDeltaTheta by the base-field cyclotome mu_Z(G_v), producing theta_bs and infty theta_bs versions of the evaluation algorithm and zero-label splittings. Useful because it gives access to the valuation map H^1(G_v,mu_Z(G_v)) -> Z.
Remark 2.9.1 The mono-theta cyclotome route has a multiradial formulation; the base-field route is only uniradial unless one later applies log-shell modifications with mild indeterminacies. This is a major source-level guardrail for the wiki: base-field numerical access and multiradial alien comparison are different interfaces.
Sources: IUT II, Definition 2.7 and Corollaries 2.8-2.9 Related: theta evaluation orbits tempered Gaussian monoids log-theta lattice theta/log links

IUT II Remark 2.6.3: Distinguished Subgraph

Remark 2.6.3 explains why the theta-value collection used later is not an arbitrary finite sample of labels. Connectedness, the zero-label splitting, independent conjugacy indeterminacy, the averaging quantity ||Gamma'||, and the product formula single out the full subgraph Gamma_Ydd.

Subgraph selection argument from local basepoint discipline to product-formula compatibility
Source itemStatementFormalization consequence
Remark 2.6.3(i) A replacement subgraph Gamma' must be connected because the theta evaluation algorithm is performed relative to a single basepoint. A formal model should not allow independent basepoints for independently selected theta labels.
Remark 2.6.3(i) The same Gamma' must contain the vertex labeled 0, since the splittings of Corollary 2.6(ii) use the zero-labeled evaluation point. Constant-multiple rigidity and theta-value sampling share the same subgraph constraint.
Remark 2.6.3(ii) Independent conjugacy indeterminacies force selection by fibers over |F_l|: one cannot restrict to a single chosen representative without the other representatives in the same fiber. The label interface must encode fiber-wise indeterminacy, not a free list of evaluation points.
Remark 2.6.3(ii)-(iii) The average ||Gamma'|| is monotone in the projected label count, reaches its maximum at l, and the infinite case adds no larger value. The later height optimization should refer to the distinguished full label collection, not to an arbitrary truncation.
Remark 2.6.3(iv)-(v) Multi-element fibers are incompatible with globalization by the product formula, so the subgraph Gamma_Ydd is singled out for the collection q_v^{j^2}. The passage from local theta evaluation to global Gaussian Frobenioids must preserve product-formula compatibility as a separate constraint.

Single-basepoint condition

The connectedness condition is not graph-theoretic decoration. It is the local geometric form of using one fundamental group, one Galois group, and one cyclotome for simultaneous Kummer theory.

Rem. 2.6.1connected

Zero-vertex condition

Corollary 2.6(ii) identifies zero-labeled theta-value orbits with the identity orbit after quotienting by torsion. Any candidate subgraph for the evaluation collection must retain this zero-labeled reference point.

Cor. 2.6(ii)zero label

Fiber indeterminacy

The source restriction is fiber-wise over |F_l|. This is how Remark 2.5.2 reappears inside the height-relevant subgraph discussion.

Rem. 2.5.2fibers

Average ||Gamma'||

The quantity averages minimum square representatives across projected label classes. Mochizuki uses its monotonicity to justify why the full projected set is optimal for the later height comparison.

Rem. 2.6.3(ii)height

Product-formula test

Globalization in IUT II Section 4 imposes product-formula compatibility. This removes the remaining fiber-rich alternatives even when the local average has already reached its maximum.

Rem. 2.6.3(iv)global

Distinguished collection

The resulting collection is the full set of values q_v^{j^2} determined by Gamma_Ydd. This is the input later treated as a Gaussian distribution, not an optional choice of sample.

Rem. 2.6.3(v)q^{j²}
Formalization warning: the subgraph argument must carry both local conditions and the later product-formula constraint. A record that only stores a finite list of labels would erase why this collection is distinguished.
Sources: IUT II, Remark 2.6.3 Related: Galois theta evaluation Theta±ell bridges Tempered Gaussian monoids Global Gaussian Frobenioids

Tempered Gaussian Monoids in IUT II 3

IUT II Section 3 converts the synchronized theta-value data of Section 2 into theta monoids, constant monoids, value-profile-indexed Gaussian monoids, and Frobenioid-theoretic Gaussian monoids. The section is also where Mochizuki explicitly separates the multiradial split-theta route from the uniradial constant-monoid route.

Section 3 monoid route theta monoids, constant monoids, and Gaussian value-profiles
Source itemConstructionWiki consequence
Proposition 3.1(i) A projective system of mono-theta environments gives split theta monoids Psi_env and infty Psi_env, together with conjugation actions and splittings up to torsion. These are the multiradial-friendly theta containers that should not be collapsed to bare numerical values.
Proposition 3.1(ii) The same system gives a constant monoid Psi_cns, naturally identified with the local base-field monoid O_Fv^triangleright. This supplies the constant part used by value-profiles, but it is the route that later carries the uniradial warning.
