It constructs theta-environment monoids and realified Frobenioids from D-side prime-strips, identifies their Frobenioid-theoretic versions by Kummer isomorphisms, and records the bad-prime, good-prime, archimedean, and constant-monoid compatibility clauses needed before Theorem 2.2 and Corollary 2.3.

Proposition 2.1 route D-side vertical coricity, Kummer readout, and local compatibility
Source itemConstructionWhy it matters
Proposition 2.1(i) Functorial algorithms in the associated D_> and D⊢_> prime-strips construct Psi_env(D_>), ∞Psi_env(D_>), and the global realified Frobenioid Denv(D⊢_>). Vertical arrows of the Gaussian log-theta lattice induce compatible isomorphisms. This gives the theta-environment monoids vertical coricity before the §2 multiradial theta-monoid construction continues.
Proposition 2.1(ii) Frobenioid-theoretic theta-environment data Psi_Fenv, ∞Psi_Fenv, and Cenv are constructed from the Θ±ellNF-Hodge theater and then identified with the D-side environment data by Kummer isomorphisms. This is the controlled Frobenius-like to D/etale-like readout for theta-environment monoids and F-prime-strips.
Proposition 2.1(iii) At bad primes, Kummer isomorphisms are obtained through mono-theta environments arising from tempered Frobenioids and from the tempered fundamental group. The exterior-cyclotome map tensored with Q/Z maps trivially into the relevant ×μ quotient. This zero-map clause insulates mono-theta rigidity from the Z×-type indeterminacies acting on coric unit-quotient data.
Proposition 2.1(iv) At good nonarchimedean primes, the unit portion is obtained from a labelled version of the relevant MLF-Galois topological-monoid isomorphism and from Kummer poly-isomorphisms of semi-simplifications. Good-prime compatibility uses the semi-simplification route already built in IUT II §4, rather than the bad-prime mono-theta route.
Proposition 2.1(v) At archimedean primes, the unit portion is obtained from a labelled version of the archimedean topological-monoid isomorphism and its Kummer semi-simplification orbit. The archimedean clause is separate from both the bad-prime and good nonarchimedean constructions.
Proposition 2.1(vi) The unit portion of the theta monoids gives natural isomorphisms between the constant and environment F⊢×-prime-strip data, compatible with the Kummer isomorphisms and Theorem 1.5(iii). This ties the §2 theta-environment route back to the constant F⊢×μ_△ interface preserved by the modified theta links.

D-side theta environment

The D-side prime-strips reconstruct Psi_env, ∞Psi_env, and Denv. This is the vertically coric environment data transported along log-links.

Prop. 2.1(i)D-side

F-side Kummer readout

The Frobenioid-theoretic data Psi_Fenv and Cenv are identified with the D-side data by Kummer isomorphisms and read as F-prime-strip isomorphisms.

Prop. 2.1(ii)Kummer

Bad-prime zero map

The exterior-cyclotome contribution, after tensoring by Q/Z and projecting to the ×μ quotient, is the zero map.

Prop. 2.1(iii)insulation

Good-prime labelled semi-simplification

At good nonarchimedean places, the Kummer data is supplied by labelled versions of MLF-Galois topological-monoid isomorphisms and semi-simplification poly-isomorphisms.

Prop. 2.1(iv)good primes

Archimedean labelled semi-simplification

At archimedean places, the construction uses labelled versions of the archimedean topological-monoid isomorphism and the associated Kummer semi-simplification orbit.

Prop. 2.1(v)archimedean

Constant/environment compatibility

The unit part of the theta monoids identifies constant and environment F⊢× data and matches the F⊢×μ isomorphism of IUT II Corollary 4.10.

Prop. 2.1(vi)constant side

Five mono-theta rigidity properties

Remark 2.1.1 lists cyclotomic rigidity, discrete rigidity, constant multiple rigidity, isomorphism class compatibility, and Frobenioid structure compatibility as the source rigidity package used in §2.

Rem. 2.1.1rigidity

First-power cyclotomic rigidity

Remark 2.1.1 emphasizes that cyclotomic rigidity is nonlinear and distinguished at the first power. Composing it with arbitrary N-power morphisms sacrifices constant multiple rigidity.

Rem. 2.1.1(iv)first power

Discrete rigidity and positivity

Remark 2.1.1 says discrete rigidity restricts attention to Z-multiples or powers, not a Zhat-style completion that would lose positivity and confuse effective with negative divisors.

Rem. 2.1.1(v)positivity
Source warning: Proposition 2.1 is not just a generic Kummer compatibility statement. Its local clauses differ by place type, and the bad-prime mono-theta zero-map clause is the mechanism that keeps theta monoids much more exact than the later Gaussian multiradial formulation.
Sources: IUT III, Proposition 2.1 and Remark 2.1.1 Related: log-theta lattice Theta×μ Frobenius-pictures F×μ Kummer prime-strips multiradiality log-Kummer correspondences

IUT III Proposition 2.1(i): D-Side Theta Environment

Proposition 2.1(i) constructs theta-environment monoids and a global realified Frobenioid from the D-side data of a Theta±ellNF-Hodge theater. These objects are vertically coric: vertical arrows of the Gaussian log-theta lattice induce compatible isomorphisms between them.

D-side inputConstructed outputVertical behavior
D_> Psi_env(D_>) and ∞Psi_env(D_>). Transported by the full poly-isomorphism induced by a vertical arrow.
D⊢_> Denv(D⊢_>) with Prime(Denv) ≃ V. Local isomorphisms and prime bijections remain compatible.
Gaussian log-theta lattice column Compatible data at (n,m) and (n,m+1). This is the theta-environment instance of vertical coricity.
Sources: IUT III, Proposition 2.1(i) Related: theta monoid vertical coricity vertical/horizontal coricity F-side Kummer readout

IUT III Proposition 2.1(ii): F-Side Kummer Readout

Proposition 2.1(ii) constructs the Frobenioid-theoretic theta-environment data Psi_Fenv, ∞Psi_Fenv, and Cenv, then identifies them with the D-side environment data by Kummer isomorphisms. The final readout is an isomorphism of F⊢- and F-prime-strips.

F-side objectD-side objectReadout
Psi_Fenv(HT_Theta) Psi_env(D_>) Kummer isomorphism of theta-environment monoids.
∞Psi_Fenv(HT_Theta) ∞Psi_env(D_>) Infinity theta-environment compatibility.
Cenv(HT_Theta) Denv(D⊢_>) Realified Frobenioid isomorphism compatible with prime bijections.
Sources: IUT III, Proposition 2.1(ii) Related: D-side theta environment Theta/Gaussian prime-strips bad-prime zero map

IUT III Proposition 2.1(iii): Bad-Prime Zero Map

At bad primes, Proposition 2.1(iii) routes the Kummer isomorphisms through mono-theta environments arising from both tempered Frobenioids and tempered fundamental groups. After passing through exterior cyclotomes, tensoring by Q/Z, and projecting to the relevant ×μ quotient, the composite is the zero map.

StageSource objectEffect
Mono-theta comparison Projective systems MTheta_*(D_>,v) and MTheta_*(F_v). Full poly-isomorphism induces exterior-cyclotome isomorphisms.
Exterior cyclotome Pi_mu(MTheta_*(D_>,v)) ⊗ Q/Z. Maps into the unit side before projection.
×μ quotient (Psi_FTheta_v)^{×μ}. The composite is the zero map.
This zero-map clause is the source-level insulation that keeps the theta-monoid Kummer theory far more exact than the later Gaussian multiradial formulation.
Sources: IUT III, Proposition 2.1(iii) Related: mono-theta rigidity package theta monoid multiradiality indeterminacies

IUT III Proposition 2.1(iv): Good-Prime Labelled Semi-Simplification

At good nonarchimedean primes, Proposition 2.1(iv) obtains the unit portion of the Kummer isomorphisms from labelled versions of MLF-Galois topological-monoid isomorphisms and the Kummer poly-isomorphisms of semi-simplifications developed in IUT II.

ComponentSource routeDifference from bad primes
Unit portion Labelled MLF-Galois topological-monoid isomorphism. Does not use the bad-prime mono-theta environment route.
Automorphism orbit Labelled Kummer poly-isomorphism of semi-simplifications. Tracks the Aut_F(Fenv)-orbit.
Local type v ∈ Vgood ∩ Vnon. Separate from both bad and archimedean clauses.
Sources: IUT III, Proposition 2.1(iv) Related: F-side Kummer readout good-prime Gaussian monoids archimedean semisimplification

IUT III Proposition 2.1(v): Archimedean Labelled Semi-Simplification

At archimedean primes, Proposition 2.1(v) obtains the unit portion from a labelled version of the archimedean topological-monoid isomorphism and the corresponding Kummer poly-isomorphism of semi-simplifications.

Archimedean objectRoleAudit point
Topological monoid isomorphism Supplies the unit-side Kummer route at v ∈ Varc. Not copied from the nonarchimedean local-field formula.
Labelled version Aligns the local construction with the global label apparatus. Preserves the label sensitivity needed by theta monoids.
Semi-simplification orbit Records the Aut_F(Fenv)-orbit of the Kummer readout. Separates archimedean behavior from good nonarchimedean behavior.
Sources: IUT III, Proposition 2.1(v) Related: good-prime semisimplification archimedean Kummer/log Frobenioids archimedean torsion system

IUT III Proposition 2.1(vi): Constant/Environment Compatibility

Proposition 2.1(vi) states that the unit portion of the theta monoids gives natural isomorphisms between constant and environment F⊢×-prime-strip data. These are compatible with the Kummer isomorphisms of Proposition 2.1(ii) and the bi-coric F⊢×μ comparison of Theorem 1.5(iii).

ComparisonSource statementUse
F⊢×_△ ≃ F⊢×_env Natural isomorphism of associated F⊢×-prime-strips. Connects constant data to theta-environment data.
F⊢×_△(D⊢_△) ≃ F⊢×_env(D_>) D-side version of the same constant/environment identification. Ties the comparison to the vertical-coric D-side route.
Theorem 1.5(iii) Compatibility with bi-coric F⊢×μ prime-strips. Prevents the constant side from becoming an unrelated auxiliary object.
Sources: IUT III, Proposition 2.1(vi) Related: constant F_△ prime-strip bi-coric F⊢×μ prime-strips theta monoid multiradiality

IUT III Remark 2.1.1: Five Mono-Theta Rigidity Properties

Remark 2.1.1 reviews the mono-theta environment rigidity package used in Section 2: cyclotomic rigidity, discrete rigidity, constant multiple rigidity, isomorphism class compatibility, and Frobenioid structure compatibility. Mochizuki treats these as the mechanism making Kummer-detachment indeterminacies mild enough for reconstruction algorithms.

PropertyRole in §2Failure mode if omitted
Frobenioid structure compatibility Relates Frobenius-like monoidal data to etale-like structures by Kummer theory. The theta/log links lose their non-scheme-theoretic monoid input.
Isomorphism class compatibility Works with objects known only up to isomorphism while preserving usable field/ring structure. Kummer towers become projective-system data without the single-field structure needed for log-links.
Constant multiple rigidity Supports canonical splittings of theta monoids by zero-section restriction. Splittings used in theta-monoid multiradiality disappear.
Cyclotomic rigidity Rigidifies the relation between interior and exterior cyclotomes. Only a Z×-orbit remains, which is too coarse.
Discrete rigidity Restricts to integral powers/multiples with positivity intact. A profinite-completed version would confuse effective and negative divisors.
Sources: IUT III, Remark 2.1.1 Related: bad-prime zero map first-power cyclotomic rigidity discrete rigidity/positivity

IUT III Remark 2.1.1(iv): First-Power Cyclotomic Rigidity

Remark 2.1.1(iv) says that mono-theta cyclotomic rigidity is nonlinear and distinguished at the first power. Composing the cyclotomic rigidity isomorphism with an arbitrary N-power morphism sacrifices constant multiple rigidity.

FeatureSource readingConsequence
Quadratic commutator Cyclotomic rigidity reflects the nondegenerate degree-two theta-group commutator. The rigidity is not a linear rescaling convention.
Interior/exterior cyclotomes A specific isomorphism rigidifies their relation. This distinguishes the mono-theta route from MLF-Galois-pair rigidity.
First power Arbitrary N-power composition breaks constant multiple rigidity. Explains why naive power replacements are not source-equivalent.
Sources: IUT III, Remark 2.1.1(iv) Related: mono-theta rigidity package no naive power map theta monoid multiradiality

IUT III Remark 2.1.1(v): Discrete Rigidity and Positivity

Remark 2.1.1(v) reads discrete rigidity as the rule that theta-environment divisors, line bundles, and rational functions are controlled by ordinary Z-powers or multiples, not by a profinite completion. Mochizuki explicitly says a completed theory would lose the positivity/negativity distinction needed for effective divisors.

ChoiceSource consequenceWhy it matters
Ordinary Z Preserves positivity and the line-bundle automorphism structure. Compatible with the Frobenioid-theoretic mono-theta environment.
Profinite completion Would allow negative directions as limits of positive integers. Would force birational confusion of effective and negative divisors.
Discrete rigidity Prevents that collapse in the theta-environment setting. Supports the exactness of theta-monoid multiradiality.
Sources: IUT III, Remark 2.1.1(v) Related: mono-theta rigidity package positive/negative pilot warning F⊥ prime-strips and pilots

IUT III Remark 2.1.2: Evaluation Discrete Rigidity

Remark 2.1.2 transfers the discrete-rigidity theme to evaluation-point bookkeeping. The F_l^{±}-symmetry requires global label operations on evaluation points, but the relevant global etale fundamental groups are profinite. Mochizuki says the complements on tempered coverings from IUT I §2 are used precisely to restrict to discrete Z-conjugates of theta functions rather than profinite Zhat-conjugates.

QuestionSource answerConsequence
Why another discrete rigidity? Evaluation labels live in global bookkeeping operations whose ambient fundamental groups are profinite. The theory needs a mechanism that keeps evaluation coordinates genuinely discrete.
Can one use a partially tempered group? No. The candidate subgroup Pi^dagger has a normalizer quotient isomorphic to Zhat/Z. It fails the absolute-anabelian properties needed by EtTh and IUT II §2.
What does the full tempered group protect? Using complements on tempered coverings avoids profinite translation indeterminacies on evaluation-point coordinates. Later evaluation pages should cite this as the reason for using the full tempered group, not a partially tempered shortcut.
Remark 2.1.2 says this evaluation-related issue is not used directly in IUT III §2, but becomes relevant in §3. It is therefore a bridge page from theta-monoid rigidity to the later Gaussian evaluation machinery.
Source: IUT III, Remark 2.1.2 Related: discrete rigidity/positivity IUT I tempered complements theta evaluation route conjugate synchronization

Theta Monoid Multiradiality in IUT III 2.2-2.3

Theorem 2.2 and Corollary 2.3 turn the theta-environment data of Proposition 2.1 into the first explicit multiradial theta-monoid interface. The key point is not merely that prime-strip data are isomorphic, but that bad-prime mono-theta rigidity is compatible with arbitrary automorphisms of the coric F⊢×μ side.

Theorem 2.2 to Corollary 2.3 Kummer compatibility, bad-prime insulation, and radial/coric output
Source itemFormal contentRole in the corridor
Theorem 2.2(i) Automorphism groups of F-prime-strips map to automorphism groups of the associated F×μ and F⊢×μ prime-strips. The second arrows are surjections and the route is compatible with the Kummer isomorphisms of Proposition 2.1 and Theorem 1.5. This records exactly where automorphism freedom enters the weakened prime-strip interfaces.
Theorem 2.2(ii) At each bad prime, the identity on the theta splitting objects, exterior-cyclotome torsion, projective mono-theta environments, and zero-labeled splittings is compatible with arbitrary automorphisms of F⊢×μ_△. This is the Kummer-compatible multiradiality statement: the purely radial mono-theta data are insulated from coric ×μ automorphisms.
Remark 2.2.1 Mono-theta environments amalgamate Π_v and Π_μ so that cyclotomic rigidity recovers an isomorphism, not just its Z×-orbit. This decouples geometric/radial data from base-field/coric unit quotients. This explains why the theta-monoid step is more exact than the later Gaussian-monoid multiradiality.
Remark 2.2.2 A naive correspondence q ↦ q^λ lacks the mono-theta cyclotomic rigidity and zero-labeled evaluation splittings required for the Kummer-compatible radial/coric decoupling. This is a source-level warning against replacing the theta-function construction by an arbitrary power map.
Corollary 2.3(i) The radial algorithm sends radial data to coric data and its associated functor is full and essentially surjective. The theta-monoid radial environment is multiradial in the source sense.
Corollary 2.3(ii)-(iv) Vertical arrows identify radial data in each column, horizontal arrows identify coric data across columns, the resulting etale-picture admits arbitrary spoke permutations, and the constructions are equivariant with respect to automorphisms of horizontal-arrow domains and codomains. This is the radial-spoke/common-coric-center picture used before the Gaussian monoid and log-volume stages.

Prime-strip automorphism route

Theorem 2.2(i) does not leave automorphism groups informal: it routes Aut_F through Aut_{F×μ} and Aut_{F⊢×μ}, with compatibility imposed by the preceding Kummer identifications.

Theorem 2.2(i)automorphisms

Bad-prime radial insulation

The bad-prime clause makes the identity on radial mono-theta data compatible with arbitrary coric automorphisms because the relevant torsion/exterior-cyclotome contribution maps trivially into the ×μ quotient.

Theorem 2.2(ii)insulation

Mono-theta vs MLF rigidity

Remark 2.2.1 contrasts mono-theta cyclotomic rigidity with MLF-Galois-pair rigidity: the latter recovers only a Z×-orbit once the unit quotient is involved.

Remark 2.2.1Z× orbit

No naive power map

Remark 2.2.2 says that a replacement q ↦ q^λ would not carry the theta-function machinery: no mono-theta rigidity, no zero-labeled splitting, and no compatible Kummer-class passage.

Remark 2.2.2warning

Radial/coric etale picture

Corollary 2.3 packages D-Theta theater data, theta-environment prime-strips, bad-prime rigidity data, and F⊢×μ_△ into radial data, then maps it to the coric pair (D⊢_△,F⊢×μ_△(D⊢_△)).

Corollary 2.3radial to coric

Spoke symmetry

The etale-picture identifies radial data along vertical lines and coric data horizontally. The resulting diagram has arbitrary permutation symmetries among the spokes labelled by n ∈ Z.

Corollary 2.3(ii)spokes
Use for formalization. A faithful Lean interface should keep the Theorem 2.2(i) automorphism route, the Theorem 2.2(ii) bad-prime identity-compatibility clause, and the Corollary 2.3 radial-to-coric functor separate. Collapsing these into one "multiradiality" predicate would hide the point at which the theta-function-specific Kummer machinery is used.
Sources: IUT III §2 Related: theta monoid vertical coricity multiradiality overview log-Kummer correspondences Theta×μ Frobenius-pictures Corollary 3.12 corridor

IUT III Theorem 2.2(i): Prime-Strip Automorphism Route

Theorem 2.2(i) records the automorphism passage from an F-prime-strip to its F×μ and F⊢×μ descendants. The second arrows are stated to be surjective and the whole route is compatible with the Kummer isomorphisms of Proposition 2.1(ii) and Theorem 1.5(iii).

Automorphism Route weakened prime-strip interfaces keep Kummer compatibility
PartSource statementWiki guardrail
Input Automorphisms of the F-prime-strip attached to Fenv(D>) or Fenv. Do not replace these by automorphisms of a single untyped object.
Middle and target Associated F×μ and F⊢×μ prime-strips, with the second arrows surjective. The weakened interfaces are where coric unit-quotient freedom enters.
Kummer condition Compatibility with Proposition 2.1(ii) and Theorem 1.5(iii). The route is not merely functorial; it is constrained by earlier Kummer identifications.
Sources: IUT III, Theorem 2.2(i) Related: F-side Kummer readout theta monoid multiradiality bad-prime radial insulation

IUT III Theorem 2.2(ii): Bad-Prime Radial Insulation

At a bad prime, Theorem 2.2(ii) isolates radial mono-theta data from arbitrary automorphisms of the coric F⊢×μ prime-strip. The key source clause is that the composite maps from the torsion/exterior-cyclotome side to the ×μ quotient are zero.

Bad-Prime Insulation radial mono-theta data remain fixed under coric ×μ motion
Formalization guardrail. This page should not be encoded as "the coric automorphism is trivial." The source statement is subtler: identity on the listed radial objects is compatible with arbitrary automorphisms of the coric ×μ quotient because the relevant torsion contribution maps trivially into that quotient.
Radial objectSource labelFunction
Theta splitting monoid and torsion subgroup(av)Domain of the bad-prime identity statement.
Exterior cyclotome tensor Q/Z(bv)Identified with the torsion subgroup before the zero map.
Projective mono-theta environments(cv)Carry the cyclotomic-rigidity data.
Zero-labeled evaluation splittings(dv)Supply the evaluation splitting used in radial/coric decoupling.
Sources: IUT III, Theorem 2.2(ii) Related: bad-prime zero map mono-theta rigidity radial/coric etale picture

IUT III Remark 2.2.1: Mono-Theta vs MLF Rigidity

Remark 2.2.1 explains why the mono-theta route is used in Theorem 2.2(ii). Mono-theta environments amalgamate Π_v and Π_μ so as to recover a cyclotomic-rigidity isomorphism itself, not merely its Z×-orbit. The MLF-Galois-pair route does not have this insulation once O×μ is involved.

Mono-theta route

The exterior cyclotome Π_μ is treated as radial data. The identity action on it sits under the ×μ quotient, so coric automorphism freedom does not disturb the cyclotomic-rigidity isomorphism.

Fig. 2.2insulated

MLF-Galois-pair route

The source input uses monoids whose ×μ quotient already carries Z×-indeterminacy. That indeterminacy lifts to unit and group-completion levels, so only a Z×-orbit remains.

Fig. 2.1orbit only
IssueMono-thetaMLF-Galois-pair
Cyclotomic rigidity Recovers the isomorphism l·ΔΘ ≃ Π_μ. Recovers only a Z×-orbit.
Relation to ×μ Π_μ is radial and insulated from ×μ automorphisms. The construction uses O×μ essentially.
Role in §3 Essential first step for Gaussian-monoid multiradiality. Too coarse for theta values because Z×-powers would be confused.
Sources: IUT III, Remark 2.2.1 Related: first-power cyclotomic rigidity bad-prime radial insulation theta monoid multiradiality

IUT III Remark 2.2.2: No Naive Power Map

Remark 2.2.2 rejects replacing the theta-value correspondence by an arbitrary map q ↦ q^λ. Such a map lacks the mono-theta cyclotomic rigidity, zero-labeled evaluation splittings, and F_l^±-symmetry used by the source construction.

Naive Power Map Failure the theta-function machinery is not optional source decoration
Missing from q ↦ q^λSource roleConsequence
Mono-theta cyclotomic rigidityInsulates theta Kummer classes from Z×-indeterminacy.No exact theta-monoid multiradiality.
Zero-labeled evaluation splittingsDecouple radial value-group data from coric ×μ log-shells.No Kummer-compatible radial/coric split.
F_l^±-symmetric evaluation packageControls symmetry among theta labels.Arbitrary evaluation points impose incompatible constraints.
Sources: IUT III, Remark 2.2.2 Related: theta-function rigidity mono-theta vs MLF rigidity 3.12 proof steps

IUT III Corollary 2.3 Cluster: Radial/Coric Etale-Picture

Corollary 2.3 and Remarks 2.3.1-2.3.4 form one source unit. The corollary states the radial-to-coric algorithm and the spoke symmetry of its etale-picture; the remarks explain why this is a canonical splitting of the theta-monoid portion of the Gaussian log-theta lattice, how an analogous ∞κ-coric statement fits, and why the theta and number-field outputs differ with respect to the j ∈ F_l labels. Remark 2.3.4 then reviews the essential EtTh decision: multiradial Kummer theory must use theta-function line-bundle data in mono-theta environments, not ordinary rational functions or conventional ring-dependent theta theory.

Radial-to-coric algorithm

The radial package consists of a D-Theta±ellNF theater, Fenv(D>), bad-prime data from Theorem 2.2(ii), an F⊢×μ target, and a full poly-isomorphism to that target. The algorithm returns the coric D⊢_△ and F⊢×μ_△ data.

Cor. 2.3(i) Theorem 2.2 input

Spoke symmetry

Vertical arrows identify radial data along a column; horizontal arrows identify the coric data. After this separation, the etale-picture has arbitrary permutation symmetries among the radial spokes indexed by n ∈ Z.

Cor. 2.3(ii)-(iv) log-theta lattice

Polar-coordinate analogy

Remark 2.3.1 compares Figure 2.4 to the polar-coordinate representation of the Gaussian integral. In this analogy, the etale-picture is the canonical splitting of the theta-monoid part of the Gaussian log-theta lattice, not a collapse of the lattice into one holomorphic coordinate system.

Remark 2.3.1 theta values

∞κ-coric analogue

Remark 2.3.2 says that a similar result may be formulated for ∞κ-coric structures by using the localization polymorphisms of IUT II Corollary 4.8. The reconstruction of ∞κ from ∞κ× via restrictions of Kummer classes is treated as an analogue of the radial/coric decoupling for theta data.

Remark 2.3.2 ∞κ pseudo-monoids ∞κ structures

Theta vs number-field containers

Remark 2.3.3 compares two multiradial geometric containers. In the theta case, etale theta functions on Tate curves supply a container for the values q_v^{j^2}. In the number-field case, absolute-anabelian Belyi cuspidalizations supply a container for copies of Fmod. Both pass from the geometric container to radial arithmetic data by Galois evaluation.

Remark 2.3.3(i)-(ii) Belyi cuspidalization

Label dependence

The theta output q^{j^2} depends essentially on labels j ∈ F_l, while the number-field output copies of Fmod do not. Remark 2.3.3 uses this distinction to emphasize the nontrivial compatibility between mono-theta cyclotomic rigidity, F_l^{±}-symmetry, and Galois evaluation.

Remark 2.3.3(iv)-(x) mono-theta rigidity no naive Kummer shortcut

EtTh and non-scheme filters

Remark 2.3.4(i) explains why EtTh enters the IUT series. Log-links and theta-links are non-ring/scheme-theoretic filters between distinct ring/scheme theories. Frobenius-like structures construct the filters; etale-like structures relate the two sides; Kummer theory then compares corresponding Frobenius-like and etale-like objects in a multiradial fashion.

Remark 2.3.4(i) log-Kummer Frobenius/etale versions

Theta Kummer line bundles

Remark 2.3.4(ii) rejects two simpler Kummer interfaces. Algebraic rational functions are incompatible with the profinite/tempered topology at bad primes. Truncated Kummer theory of theta functions is topologically compatible, but in its naive form lacks the shifting automorphisms needed for discrete rigidity and F_l^{±}-symmetry. The source therefore uses Kummer theory of the line bundles, equivalently first Chern classes, associated to theta functions.

Remark 2.3.4(ii) discrete rigidity theta evaluation

Mono-theta section choice

Remark 2.3.4(iii) is a four-way exclusion. No theta or algebraic section data leaves constant-unit indeterminacy and breaks constant multiple rigidity. Both theta and algebraic section data give bi-theta environments and break discrete rigidity and F_l^{±}-symmetry. Algebraic sections alone do not supply the commutator property needed for cyclotomic rigidity. Theta sections alone give mono-theta environments, compatible with tempered topology, F_l^{±}-symmetry, and the three rigidity properties listed in Remark 2.1.1.

Remark 2.3.4(iii) rigidity package mono-theta route

Conventional theta limits

Remark 2.3.4(iv) distinguishes the EtTh interface from classical series or representation-theoretic theta theory. Explicit series use addition and multiplication of functions; theta-group representations use modules over rings. Both are tied to a fixed ring/scheme model, whereas IUT needs multiplicative monoid data and Galois actions that survive the dismantling of additive and multiplicative ring structures.

Remark 2.3.4(iv) EtTh mono-theta dismantling
Source itemWhat it contributesWhy grouped here
Corollary 2.3 Radial algorithm, full and essentially surjective functor, vertical and horizontal identifications, spoke permutations, and equivariance. This is the structural theorem whose explanatory remarks are unpacked below.
Remark 2.3.1 Polar-coordinate analogy and canonical splitting interpretation of the theta-monoid part of the Gaussian log-theta lattice. It explains how to read the etale-picture geometrically.
Remark 2.3.2 Parallel ∞κ-coric formulation using IUT II Corollary 4.8 and Kummer-class restrictions. It prevents treating Corollary 2.3 as isolated from the number-field/Kummer side.
Remark 2.3.3 Comparison between theta-value containers and number-field containers, including the role of Belyi cuspidalization and label dependence. It links the theta-monoid split to the broader multiradial geometry of IUT.
Remark 2.3.4 EtTh decision tree: Frobenius-like/etale-like filters, theta-function line-bundle Kummer theory, mono-theta section choice, and rejection of conventional ring-dependent theta interfaces. It explains which theta/Kummer structure may be transported through the non-ring links without losing rigidity or symmetry.
Grouping rule. This page keeps the detailed pages searchable and directly linkable while giving the reader one coherent route through the corollary and its explanatory remarks. This is the preferred pattern for source units that otherwise fragment into many tiny pages.
Source: IUT III, Corollary 2.3 and Remarks 2.3.1-2.3.4 Related: theta monoid multiradiality radial/coric etale picture spoke symmetry F⊥ transition

IUT III Corollary 2.3: Radial/Coric Etale Picture

Corollary 2.3 packages Theorem 2.2 into an etale-picture of radial data. A radial object consists of a D-theater, the associated Fenv(D>), the bad-prime data of Theorem 2.2(ii), a target F⊢×μ-prime-strip, and a full poly-isomorphism to that target. The radial algorithm sends this package to the coric pair (D⊢_△, F⊢×μ_△(D⊢_△)).

Radial Algorithm full and essentially surjective functor from radial data to coric data
Radial componentSource labelCoric output role
D-Θ±ellNF Hodge theater(aR)Provides the D-side source theater.
Fenv(D>)(bR)Holds the F-prime-strip attached to the D-side data.
Bad-prime data from Theorem 2.2(ii)(cR)Keeps radial insulation visible.
F⊢×μ_△(D⊢_△) and full poly-isomorphism(dR)-(eR)Supplies the coric target and comparison map.
Sources: IUT III, Corollary 2.3(i) Related: Corollary 2.3 cluster bad-prime radial insulation spoke symmetry multiradiality overview

IUT III Corollary 2.3(ii)-(iv): Spoke Symmetry

Corollary 2.3 identifies radial data along vertical log-theta-lattice columns, identifies coric data along horizontal arrows, and obtains an etale-picture with arbitrary permutation symmetries among the n ∈ Z spokes. This is a source-level symmetry statement, not a claim that all underlying holomorphic structures have been merged.

Etale-Picture Spokes many radial spokes, one common coric center
ClauseSource contentAudit use
Vertical arrowsIdentify radial data n,mR along a column.Preserve radial packages before horizontal coric identification.
Horizontal arrowsInduce full poly-isomorphisms of D⊢-prime-strips and coric data.Construct the common coric center.
EquivarianceConstructions are stabilized/equivariant/functorial for arbitrary automorphisms of horizontal-arrow domains and codomains.Marks the symmetry that later §3 algorithms inherit.
Sources: IUT III, Corollary 2.3(ii)-(iv) Related: Corollary 2.3 cluster radial/coric etale picture Gaussian log-theta lattice theta monoid multiradiality

F-perp Prime-Strips and Pilot Objects in IUT III 2.4

Definition 2.4 is the short but important transition from the theta-monoid radial/coric picture of Corollary 2.3 to the Gaussian and log-volume machinery of §3. It introduces prime-strip variants built from O⊥ submonoids and records how pilot objects in global realified Frobenioids can be reconstructed from ordered real monoids.

Definition 2.4 and Remark 2.4.2 subquotient Frobenioids, pilot element, and positivity constraint
Source itemConstructionWhy it matters
Definition 2.4(i) From an F⊢-prime-strip, replace the split Frobenioid portion at bad primes by Frobenioids determined by O⊥ ⊆ O◃ and by the torsion quotient O. At good primes the unit subgroup is trivial, so O = O⊥. This isolates the subquotient Frobenioid structures used to pass from theta-monoid radial data toward Gaussian/log-shell objects.
Definition 2.4(ii) Defines F⊢⊥- and F⊢-prime-strips as local collections over V, using Frobenioids at nonarchimedean places and Frobenioid plus TM⊢ data at archimedean places. Morphisms are indexed collections of isomorphisms. This keeps the local type distinction explicit instead of treating "prime-strip" as a single untyped label.
Definition 2.4(iii) Defines F⊥- and F-prime-strips by replacing the local F⊢ portion in the usual global F-prime-strip package (C, Prime(C) ≃ V, local strip, ρ_v). These are the global prime-strip variants that carry the later pilot objects and realified Frobenioid comparisons.
Remark 2.4.1 Applying Definition 2.4(i) to the F_env(D>) prime-strips of Corollary 2.3 gives multiradiality for the associated F⊥_env(D>). At bad primes, the O⊥ submonoids sit naturally inside the ∞Ψ⊥_env(D>)_v monoids of Theorem 2.2(ii). This ties the new F⊥ interface back to the theta-monoid multiradiality already established in §2.
Remark 2.4.2(ii)-(iv) The weights r_v are defined from local degree factors and either log(p_v) at good primes or ord_v(q_v)log(p_v) at bad primes. An ordered monoid M ≃ R gives local copies M_v; a negative pilot element η_M gives submonoids with ρ_v(η_{M_v}) = r_v η_M. This is the numerical skeleton behind the pilot-object comparison and later weighted log-volume conventions.
Remark 2.4.2(v)-(vi) Taking M = Pic(C) reconstructs (C, Prime(C) ≃ V). Adding the negative pilot element reconstructs the further rigidification by local constant divisor monoids and the maps ρ_v. Distinct negative pilot choices can be compared inside the common Frobenioid container C; comparing positive and negative choices would reverse the order structure and destroy the meaningful height distinction.

Subquotient Frobenioids

Definition 2.4 starts with local split Frobenioids and replaces their relevant portions by Frobenioids attached to O⊥ and to its torsion quotient. The good-prime case is simpler because the unit subgroup is trivial.

Definition 2.4(i)O⊥

Global F⊥ and F variants

The global F⊥ and F variants keep the same global spine: a global realified Frobenioid C, a prime identification with V, local strip data, and comparison maps ρ_v.

Definition 2.4(iii)global strip

Inherited multiradiality

Remark 2.4.1 says that the theta-monoid multiradiality of Corollary 2.3 passes to associated F⊥-prime-strips. This prevents §2.4 from becoming a fresh unproved bridge.

Remark 2.4.1inheritance

Pilot-object common container

Remark 2.4.2 reconstructs the common Frobenioid container from Pic(C) as an ordered monoid. Different negative pilot elements then produce different rigidifications that remain comparable inside the same C.

Remark 2.4.2(v)-(vi)common container

Weights r_v

The local weights combine residue degree factors with either log(p_v) or ord_v(q_v)log(p_v). They make the local pilot-element images satisfy ρ_v(η_v)=r_vη.

Remark 2.4.2(ii)-(iv)weights

Positive/negative warning

Remark 2.4.2 warns that comparing positive and negative pilot choices reverses the order on M. The resulting Frobenioids have pre-steps in opposite directions; embedding them in an ambient category that inverts those pre-steps makes the height distinction meaningless.

Remark 2.4.2(vi)positivity
Formalization warning. The pilot-object discussion is not a generic "compare any two real numbers" interface. The source requires a fixed ordered-monoid orientation and negative pilot elements; allowing a positive versus negative comparison would erase precisely the arithmetic degree distinction the Frobenioid container is meant to preserve.
Sources: IUT III, Definition 2.4 and Remarks 2.4.1-2.4.2 Related: theta monoid multiradiality tensor/log-volume corridor theta/q pilot objects weighted log-volume measure Corollary 3.12 corridor

IUT III Definition 2.4(i): Subquotient Frobenioids

Definition 2.4(i) starts from an F⊢-prime-strip and replaces the split Frobenioid portion at each place by Frobenioids determined by the subquotient monoids O⊥ ⊆ O◃ and O. At bad primes, O is obtained by quotienting O⊥ by torsion; at good primes the unit subgroup is trivial and O = O⊥.

Local Replacement from split Frobenioid portions to O⊥ and torsion-quotient Frobenioids
Place typeSource constructionFormal distinction
Bad nonarchimedeanO⊥ ⊆ O◃, then quotient O⊥ by torsion.Torsion must remain explicit because it feeds the later ×μ separation.
Good nonarchimedeanThe unit subgroup is trivial, and O = O⊥.Do not import the bad-prime torsion quotient into this case.
Output notationCollections F⊢⊥ and F⊢.These are local replacements of the split Frobenioid portion, not new Hodge theaters.
Sources: IUT III, Definition 2.4(i) Related: F-perp overview bad-prime radial insulation log-shell variants

IUT III Definition 2.4(ii)-(iii): Global F⊥ Variants

Definition 2.4(ii) defines local F⊢⊥ and F⊢ prime-strips as collections over V. Definition 2.4(iii) then defines global F⊥ and F prime-strips by retaining the usual global spine (C, Prime(C) ≃ V, local strip, ρ_v) and replacing the local F⊢ portion by the corresponding F⊢⊥ or F⊢ variant.

Global Spine the global container is preserved while the local strip type changes
LevelDefinition 2.4 dataAudit point
Local nonarchimedeanA Frobenioid isomorphic to the corresponding F⊢⊥ or F⊢ local object.The placewise type is part of the data.
Local archimedeanA Frobenioid plus an object of TM⊢.Archimedean structure is not silently identified with the nonarchimedean case.
GlobalC, Prime(C) ≃ V, local strip data, and ρ_v.This is the global container used by pilot-object reconstruction.
Sources: IUT III, Definition 2.4(ii)-(iii) Related: subquotient Frobenioids pilot common container global realified theater

IUT III Remark 2.4.1: Inherited F⊥ Multiradiality

Remark 2.4.1 says that applying Definition 2.4(i) to the Fenv(D>) prime-strips of Corollary 2.3 gives corresponding multiradiality for F⊥env(D>). At bad primes, the O⊥ submonoids sit naturally inside the ∞Ψ⊥env(D>)_v monoids of Theorem 2.2(ii).

Inheritance Definition 2.4 does not create a new independent bridge
Formalization guardrail. The F⊥ layer should be linked back to Corollary 2.3 and Theorem 2.2(ii). Treating it as a fresh assumed multiradial bridge would hide the source dependency.
Sources: IUT III, Remark 2.4.1 Related: radial/coric etale picture subquotient Frobenioids bad-prime radial insulation

IUT III Remark 2.4.2(v)-(vi): Pilot-Object Common Container

Remark 2.4.2 uses Pic(C), with its ordered monoid structure, to reconstruct the common realified Frobenioid container C and its prime set. A negative pilot element then reconstructs the additional local constant-divisor monoids and ρ_v maps. Distinct negative pilot choices can be compared inside the same container.

Common Container Pic(C) reconstructs C; negative pilots add rigidification
InputReconstructed outputMeaning
M ≃ R as ordered monoidC, Prime(C) ≃ VThe common realified Frobenioid container.
(M, η_M<0)Local constant divisor monoids and ρ_v mapsThe pilot rigidification.
Distinct negative pilotsComparable rigidifications in the same CThe common-container comparison used downstream.
Sources: IUT III, Remark 2.4.2(v)-(vi) Related: pilot weights positive/negative warning common containers

IUT III Remark 2.4.2(ii)-(iv): Pilot Weights r_v

Remark 2.4.2 defines local weights r_v using residue-degree factors and either log(p_v) at good primes or ord_v(q_v)log(p_v) at bad primes. For an ordered monoid M ≃ R, the local maps ρ_{M_v} multiply by r_v and reverse the order; with a negative pilot element, ρ_v(η_v)=r_vη.

PlaceWeightUse
Good[(Fmod)_v : Q_{v_Q}] · log(p_v)Ordinary local residue-degree/log-prime factor.
Bad[(Fmod)_v : Q_{v_Q}] · ord_v(q_v) · log(p_v)Includes the Tate parameter valuation.
All placesρ_v(η_v)=r_vηRelates local pilot generators to the global pilot element.
Sign convention. The source uses a negative pilot element. This is why the local submonoids are generated by negative-direction data even though the weights r_v themselves are nonnegative real factors.
Sources: IUT III, Remark 2.4.2(ii)-(iv) Related: pilot common container weighted log-volume measure theta/q pilot objects

IUT III Remark 2.4.2(vi): Positive/Negative Pilot Warning

Remark 2.4.2 warns against comparing constructions built from positive and negative pilot elements. Reversing the order on M = Pic(C) reverses the direction of the Frobenioid pre-steps. Embedding both versions into a larger category where these pre-steps are invertible makes the arithmetic degree/height distinction meaningless.

Orientation Warning do not compare opposite pilot signs by inverting the pre-steps
Audit rule. A formal model should reject an interface that compares positive and negative pilot choices by passing to a category in which all pre-steps are isomorphisms. That move destroys the degree data needed by the later height inequalities.
Sources: IUT III, Remark 2.4.2(vi) Related: pilot common container pilot weights discrete rigidity/positivity

Tensor Packets in IUT III 3.1-3.3

Propositions 3.1-3.3 introduce tensor packets as the §3 amalgam of log-shells and processions. They first construct local holomorphic packets from F-prime-strip capsules, then local mono-analytic packets from D⊢-prime-strip capsules, and finally global tensor packets from labeled M_mod fields.

Local and global tensor packets holomorphic packets, mono-analytic packets, and log-linked localization
Source itemConstructionWhy it matters
Proposition 3.1 setup For an n-capsule {αF}_{α∈A}, the one-packet over v_Q is the direct sum of log(αF_v) over all v|v_Q. The n-packet is the tensor product over α∈A, equivalently a direct sum over all choices of places {v_α} over v_Q. This is the basic packet container used by the later procession and log-volume machinery.
Proposition 3.1(i) The ind-topological field structures on the factors determine an ind-topological ring structure on the packet. Decompositions as direct sums of fields are determined by that ring structure and are compatible with the local group actions on the relevant summands. The packet is not merely a formal tensor product; its ring structure recovers its field-summand decomposition.
Proposition 3.1(ii) For fixed α and v|v_Q, the summand log(A,αF_v) uses the v-summand in the α factor and all other factors. Tensoring with 1's gives log(αF_v) → log(A,αF_v), transporting integral structures to packet summands. This is the local holomorphic integral structure that later has to be compared with mono-analytic log-shell integral structures.
Remark 3.1.1 Locally, log(αF_v) behaves like an algebraic closure kbar and its integral structure like O_kbar. The remark fixes normalized weights for log-volumes over the chosen set V⊆V(K) and warns that weighted sums need not be logarithms of ordinary measures. This fixes the normalization convention before the log-volume estimates of Proposition 3.9 and Corollary 3.12.
Proposition 3.2 Repeats the packet construction for D⊢-prime-strip capsules. If the capsule comes from F-data, the poly-isomorphisms D⊢ → F⊢×μ → F induce compatible identifications with the holomorphic packets. Integral structures are built from mono-analytic log-shells, or Hermitian unit balls at archimedean places. This is the packet-level bridge from holomorphic data to the mono-analytic/log-shell side.
Proposition 3.3 For a subset A of Hodge-theater labels, the global packet (†M_mod)_A is the tensor product of the labeled global fields. Localization functors, composed with the chosen log-links, inject this global packet into the product of local holomorphic packets. This supplies the global object whose local images are measured in the packet/log-volume corridor.

Holomorphic n-packet formula

The n-packet over v_Q is a tensor over labels and a direct sum over all choices of places above v_Q. This is why packet log-volumes later require normalized sums over those choices.

Proposition 3.1local packet

Field-summand decomposition

Proposition 3.1(i) says the ind-topological ring structure determines the decomposition into direct sums of ind-topological fields. This prevents the packet from being treated as an unstructured product of modules.

Proposition 3.1(i)ring structure

Holomorphic integral structures

The injection log(αF_v) → log(A,αF_v) carries the integral structure of the factor into each field summand of the packet. This is one side of the discrepancy later estimated in IUT IV.

Proposition 3.1(ii)integers

Weighted-volume caution

Remark 3.1.1 says the weighted log-volume sum is not generally the log of an ordinary measure. Direct product regions are straightforward; an auxiliary weighted-measure construction handles broader Borel sets.

Remark 3.1.1measure

Mono-analytic packet compatibility

Proposition 3.2 makes the same packet construction for D⊢ data and identifies it with F⊢×μ and F packets when the data arise from an F-capsule.

Proposition 3.2(i)poly-isomorphism

Global packet localization

Proposition 3.3 links the global tensor packet (†M_mod)_A to local holomorphic packets by composing localization functors with the selected log-links.

Proposition 3.3global to local
Use for formalization. Tensor packets should be represented with their index set, place fibers, field-summand decomposition, and integral structures explicit. A bare tensor-product type would miss the decomposition and weighting data that are later used in log-volume comparisons.
Sources: IUT III, Propositions 3.1-3.3 and Remark 3.1.1 Related: F-perp prime-strips and pilot objects tensor/log-volume corridor local packet Frobenioids weighted log-volume measure Corollary 3.12 corridor

IUT III Proposition 3.1: Holomorphic n-Packet Formula

Proposition 3.1 defines local holomorphic tensor packets for an n-capsule of F-prime-strips. Over a rational place v_Q, the one-packet is the direct sum of log(αF_v) over v|v_Q. The n-packet is the tensor product over labels, equivalently the direct sum over all choices of places {v_α} above v_Q.

Local Packet Formula direct sums over places, tensor products over capsule labels
ObjectDefinitionWhy it matters
One-packetlog(αF_{v_Q}) = ⊕_{v|v_Q} log(αF_v).Collects local fields above the rational place.
n-packetlog(AF_{v_Q}) = ⊗_{α∈A} log(αF_{v_Q}).Creates the packet container used by processions.
Expanded formDirect sum over all choices of places {v_α} above v_Q.Explains later averaging and local summand bookkeeping.
Sources: IUT III, Proposition 3.1 Related: tensor packets overview field decomposition integral structures

IUT III Proposition 3.1(i): Packet Field Decomposition

Proposition 3.1(i) says that the ind-topological field structures on the factors determine an ind-topological ring structure on the packet. The resulting decompositions as direct sums of ind-topological fields are uniquely determined by that ring structure and are compatible with the local topological group actions on the relevant summands.

Ring structure is source data

The packet is not only a tensor product of modules. The source attaches a ring structure built from the factor field structures.

3.1(i)ring

Summands are recoverable

The decomposition into field summands is uniquely determined by the packet ring structure. This prevents arbitrary regrouping of packet factors in later comparisons.

3.1(i)decomposition
FeatureSource claimFormalization guardrail
Ind-topological ringDetermined by the field structures on the factors.Do not model packets as unstructured tensor containers.
Field-summand decompositionUniquely determined by the ring structure.Summand labels should be derivable from packet structure, not arbitrary metadata.
Group action compatibilityCompatible with the natural action of αΠ_v on the corresponding summand.The action target must be the correct label/place summand.
Sources: IUT III, Proposition 3.1(i) Related: holomorphic n-packet packet integral structures local LGP monoids

IUT III Proposition 3.1(ii): Packet Integral Structures

For a fixed label α and place v|v_Q, Proposition 3.1(ii) defines the summand log(A,αF_v) by taking the v-summand in the α factor and all other factors. Tensoring with 1's gives an injective homomorphism log(αF_v) → log(A,αF_v) that transports integral structures to packet summands.

Integral Structure Injection one factor enters a selected packet summand by tensoring with ones
StepSource constructionDownstream role
Choose label/placeFix α∈A, v|v_Q.Selects the packet summand receiving the factor.
Inject by onesTensor log(αF_v) with 1's in the other factors.Moves integral structures into packet summands.
Integral structuresSuitable cofinal objects induce corresponding integral structures.These are measured in the later packet log-volume layer.
Sources: IUT III, Proposition 3.1(ii) Related: field decomposition weighted-volume caution packet log-volume

IUT III Remark 3.1.1: Weighted-Volume Caution

Remark 3.1.1 fixes the weighted local-volume convention for packets and warns that the resulting weighted log-volume sum need not be the logarithm of an ordinary measure. Direct product regions behave in the expected way, but broader Borel sets require a separate weighted-measure construction.

Audit rule. Do not silently replace IUT's weighted log-volume convention by an ordinary product-measure logarithm. The source explicitly separates direct-product behavior from the auxiliary construction needed for general Borel sets.
Volume conventionSource statusFormal consequence
Local packet normalizationIntegral structures are normalized before global summation.Store the normalization with the packet, not only with the final sum.
Weighted sumNeed not be the log of an ordinary measure.A Lean model should keep a weighted log-volume map separate from ordinary measures.
Direct productsBehave naturally under the packet convention.Safe special case, not the whole definition.
Sources: IUT III, Remark 3.1.1 Related: packet integral structures weighted log-volume measure Corollary 3.12 corridor

IUT III Proposition 3.2: Mono-Analytic Packet Compatibility

Proposition 3.2 repeats the packet construction for D⊢-prime-strip capsules. When the capsule comes from F-data, the poly-isomorphisms D⊢ → F⊢×μ → F identify the mono-analytic packets with the holomorphic packets. The integral structures come from mono-analytic log-shells, or from Hermitian unit balls at archimedean places.

Packet sideSource constructionRole
D⊢ capsuleSame packet formula as Proposition 3.1, but for mono-analytic data.Builds packet containers on the log-shell side.
Compatibility with F-dataUses D⊢ → F⊢×μ → F poly-isomorphisms.Identifies mono-analytic packets with holomorphic packets when source data permit.
Integral structuresMono-analytic log-shells, or Hermitian unit balls at archimedean places.Supplies the comparison side for discrepancy estimates.
Sources: IUT III, Proposition 3.2 Related: holomorphic packets mono-analytic log-shells packet log-volume

IUT III Proposition 3.3: Global Packet Localization

Proposition 3.3 forms a global tensor packet (M_mod)_A from labeled global fields. Localization functors, composed with the selected log-links, inject this global packet into products of local holomorphic packets. This is the global-to-local bridge used before local packet Frobenioids and log-volume estimates.

Global to Local global packet localized through log-links into local packet products
StepSource constructionWhy it matters
Global packetTensor product of labeled global fields over A.Provides the global object before localization.
LocalizationComposed with selected log-links.The local image depends on the log-link context.
Injection into productMaps the global packet into products of local holomorphic packets.Prepares the local packet Frobenioid and log-volume constructions.
Sources: IUT III, Proposition 3.3 Related: local LGP monoids global packet Frobenioids log-theta lattice

Local Packet Frobenioids and LGP Monoids in IUT III 3.4

Proposition 3.4 turns tensor-packet data into local packet-theoretic Frobenioids and logarithmic Gaussian procession monoids. This is the first point in §3 where the packet formalism directly produces monoids that act on packet containers and carry the splitting data later used in the log-Kummer and log-volume arguments.

Proposition 3.4 single packet monoids, processions, log-link pullback, and local LGP monoids
Source itemConstructionWhy it matters
Remark 3.3.2 Local images of global tensor packets are considered for holomorphic and mono-analytic processions. In the |F_l| processions, the zero label is identified with the diagonal label by the isomorphisms cited from IUT II Corollaries 4.6 and 4.5. These local packet images are the packet-level inputs to Proposition 3.4 and later global Frobenioid constructions.
Proposition 3.4(i) The factor-to-packet map sends the logarithmic monoid of one factor, its units, and its realification to the corresponding packet monoid, packet unit monoid, and realified packet monoid inside the selected packet summand. This converts one factor's monoid data into monoids acting on the packet summand where later LGP actions are defined.
Proposition 3.4(i), local place clauses At nonarchimedean places, these monoids carry G_v(αΠ_v)-actions and determine Frobenioids naturally isomorphic to log(αF_v). At archimedean places, the analogous data are an Aut-holomorphic orbispace and a Kummer structure. The local construction keeps the nonarchimedean and archimedean types separate while preserving the packet action interpretation.
Proposition 3.4(ii) Given a log-link of Θ±ellNF-Hodge theaters, apply the single packet monoid construction to the F-prime-strip processions associated to the corresponding Θ±-bridges, then pull back the Gaussian monoids Ψ_Fgau and ∞Ψ_Fgau along the log-link poly-isomorphisms. This produces the local logarithmic Gaussian procession monoids Ψ_FLGP and ∞Ψ_FLGP.
Proposition 3.4(ii), symmetry condition The functorial algorithm requires compatibility of the log-link with the F_l^±-symmetrizing isomorphisms. This prevents LGP monoids from being a purely formal relabeling of Gaussian monoids; the procession symmetry is part of the construction.
Proposition 3.4(ii), packet action For bad primes, the component labelled j of the Galois-invariant submonoid sits inside I_Q(S_{j+1}^±,j;‡F_v) and acts multiplicatively. For every place, the unit portion has the analogous action on the same packet piece. This is the explicit "splitting monoids of LGP-monoids acting on tensor packets" interface shown in Fig. 3.1.

Single packet monoids

Proposition 3.4(i) takes the monoid, unit submonoid, and realification from one packet factor and views their images inside log(A,αF_v). These images are the local packet monoids.

Proposition 3.4(i)packet monoids

Local place types

Nonarchimedean places use group actions and Frobenioids; archimedean places use Aut-holomorphic orbispace and Kummer data. The page keeps these source clauses separate because they are not interchangeable.

Proposition 3.4(i)local clauses

LGP monoids act on packets

The local LGP monoids and their unit portions act on rational spans I_Q(S_{j+1}^±,j;‡F_v) of the packet integral structures. This is the source-level content behind Fig. 3.1.

Proposition 3.4(ii)action

Log-link pullback

The Gaussian monoids from IUT II are pulled back through the log-link poly-isomorphisms before becoming LGP monoids. This is why the construction depends on the log-link, not only on a procession label.

Proposition 3.4(ii)log-link

F_l^± symmetry condition

Proposition 3.4(ii) explicitly invokes compatibility with the F_l^±-symmetrizing isomorphisms. This links the LGP construction back to the procession combinatorics of IUT I.

symmetryprocessions

Bad-prime splittings

At bad primes, the local LGP monoids carry splittings up to torsion. These are the local objects that Proposition 3.5 will make vertically coric and Kummer-compatible.

Vbadto Prop. 3.5
Use for formalization. A local LGP-monoid interface should store the packet action target, the place type, the log-link used to pull back Gaussian data, and the procession label. Treating it as only a named monoid would lose the action-on-packets data needed for later log-volume estimates.
Sources: IUT III, Proposition 3.4 and Remarks 3.3.2-3.3.3 Related: tensor packets tensor/log-volume corridor vertically coric LGP monoids log-Kummer totality Corollary 3.12 corridor

IUT III Proposition 3.4(i): Single Packet Monoids

Proposition 3.4(i) applies the injection log(αF_v) → log(A,αF_v) from Proposition 3.1(ii) to the logarithmic monoid Ψ_log(αF_v), its unit submonoid, and its realification. Their images define packet monoids Ψ_log(A,αF_v), Ψ_log^×(A,αF_v), and Ψ_log^R(A,αF_v) acting on suitable subquotients of the packet summand.

Factor Monoid to Packet Monoid images under the factor-to-summand injection
Factor dataPacket imageUse
Ψ_log(αF_v)Ψ_log(A,αF_v)Main monoid acting on the packet summand.
Unit submonoidΨ_log^×(A,αF_v)Tracks unit portions needed by Kummer theory.
RealificationΨ_log^R(A,αF_v)Supplies the realified packet monoid used later.
Sources: IUT III, Proposition 3.4(i) Related: packet integral structures local LGP overview LGP packet actions

IUT III Proposition 3.4(i): Packet Local Place Types

Proposition 3.4(i) treats nonarchimedean and archimedean packet monoids differently. At nonarchimedean places, the first packet monoid carries a G_v(αΠ_v)-action and determines a Frobenioid naturally isomorphic to log(αF_v). At archimedean places, the corresponding data are an Aut-holomorphic orbispace and a Kummer structure.

Place typeSource packageDo not collapse into
NonarchimedeanPacket monoid plus G_v(αΠ_v)-action determining a Frobenioid.A bare monoid without group action.
ArchimedeanPacket monoid plus Aut-holomorphic orbispace and Kummer structure.A copy of the nonarchimedean Frobenioid clause.
BothNatural isomorphism to the source log(αF_v) data.An untyped local packet label.
Formalization guardrail. The local-place branch is part of the theorem statement. A future Lean interface should have separate constructors or fields for nonarchimedean group-action/Frobenioid data and archimedean Aut-holomorphic/Kummer data.
Sources: IUT III, Proposition 3.4(i) Related: single packet monoids archimedean local data Frobenioid package

IUT III Proposition 3.4(ii): LGP Actions on Packets

Proposition 3.4(ii) constructs local LGP monoids whose labelled components act multiplicatively on rational packet pieces I_Q(S_{j+1}^±,j;‡F_v). At bad primes, the labelled component of the Galois-invariant submonoid of the whole LGP monoid sits inside this packet piece; for every place, the labelled unit portion acts on the analogous packet piece.

Packet Action the Fig. 3.1 interface: labelled LGP monoids act on tensor packets
ComponentSource claimWhy it matters
Bad-prime whole LGP monoidLabelled Galois-invariant component sits inside I_Q(S_{j+1}^±,j;‡F_v).Source of the bad-prime packet action.
Unit portionFor every place, the labelled invariant unit portion acts on the same packet kind.Unit data remain active for Kummer theory.
Procession labelj∈{1,...,l*}, with capsule index set S_{j+1}^±.Connects the packet action to procession combinatorics.
Sources: IUT III, Proposition 3.4(ii) and Fig. 3.1 Related: local LGP overview bad-prime LGP splittings vertically coric LGP

IUT III Proposition 3.4(ii): Log-Link LGP Pullback

The local LGP monoids are constructed from a log-link ‡HT → †HT. Proposition 3.4(ii) applies the single-packet construction to the F-prime-strip processions of the ‡-side, then pulls back the Gaussian monoids Ψ_Fgau and ∞Ψ_Fgau from the †-side along the log-link poly-isomorphisms.

InputOperationOutput
Log-link ‡HT → †HTUse Definition 1.1(iii) poly-isomorphisms.Context for pulling Gaussian monoids back.
‡-processionsApply single packet monoid construction label-by-label.Packet action targets.
Ψ_Fgau(†HT), ∞Ψ_Fgau(†HT)Pull back via log-link poly-isomorphisms.Ψ_FLGP(†HT), ∞Ψ_FLGP(†HT).
Audit rule. LGP monoids are not just renamed Gaussian monoids. Their construction records the log-link and the direction of the pullback.
Sources: IUT III, Proposition 3.4(ii) Related: local log-link mechanism Gaussian Frobenioids LGP packet actions

IUT III Proposition 3.4(ii): F_l± Symmetry in LGP

Proposition 3.4(ii) states that the functorial LGP algorithm requires compatibility of the given log-link with the F_l^±-symmetrizing isomorphisms. This ties the construction to the procession combinatorics of IUT I and prevents treating LGP monoids as independent local gadgets.

Procession labels

The label j appears in the (j+1)-capsule with index set S_{j+1}^±.

Symmetry compatibility

The log-link must respect the symmetrizing isomorphisms; otherwise the packet construction does not match the source procession data.

Sources: IUT III, Proposition 3.4(ii) Related: F_l symmetry labels processions log-link pullback

IUT III Proposition 3.4(ii): Bad-Prime LGP Splittings

For v∈V_bad, Proposition 3.4(ii) constructs LGP monoids equipped with splittings up to torsion. These local splitting data are the objects that Proposition 3.5 later rebuilds in a vertically coric setting and subjects to upper-semi log-Kummer compatibility.

Bad-prime itemSource roleNext use
Ψ_FLGP(†HT)_vLocal LGP monoid with action and splitting up to torsion.Input to vertically coric reconstruction.
∞Ψ_FLGP(†HT)_vRealified/extended local LGP monoid data.Used in upper-semi containment statements.
Labelled packet actionActs on I_Q(S_{j+1}^±,j;‡F_v).Source of bad-prime action in Fig. 3.1.
Sources: IUT III, Proposition 3.4(ii) Related: LGP actions on packets vertically coric LGP root-of-unity becomes zero

Vertically Coric Local LGP Monoids in IUT III 3.5

Proposition 3.5 takes the local LGP monoids of Proposition 3.4 and rebuilds them in the vertically coric D-Theta±ellNF Hodge theater attached to a fixed column of the Gaussian log-theta lattice. Its central point is that compatibility with vertical log-links is upper-semi: one must use the totality of Kummer maps pre-composed with log-link iterates, not a single Kummer isomorphism.

Proposition 3.5 vertical coricity, Kummer maps, and upper semi-compatibility
Source itemConstructionAudit consequence
Proposition 3.5 setup Fix a family {n,m HT_Theta±ellNF} arising from a Gaussian log-theta lattice. For each n, use vertical coricity to determine a D-Theta±ellNF Hodge theater labelled n,◦. The target for comparison is the columnwise vertically coric object, not one of the ordinary n,m holomorphic theaters.
Proposition 3.5(i) Apply Proposition 3.4(ii) to the prime-strips F(n,◦D≻)_t and the associated full log-links, compatibly with the F_l^± symmetries, to construct vertically coric local LGP monoids Psi_LGP and infty Psi_LGP. A correct interface must remember both the prime-strip source and the log-link data used to make the construction vertical.
Proposition 3.5(i), Kummer displays For every n,m, Kummer isomorphisms compare the ordinary local FLGP monoids in the n,m theater with the vertically coric LGP monoids in the n,◦ theater. These maps are comparison inputs. They do not by themselves commute with the vertical log-links.
Proposition 3.5(ii)(a) At nonarchimedean primes, the target module contains images obtained directly from Kummer maps and images obtained after pre-composing Kummer maps with positive iterates of the column's log-links. The formal statement is a containment statement; replacing it by equality would erase the upper-semi feature.
Proposition 3.5(ii)(b) At archimedean primes, the closed unit ball contains images of unit groups and radius-pi balls. These balls contain subsets that surject onto the log-link domains for iterates. The archimedean clause is not a copy of the nonarchimedean one; it uses ball containment and surjectivity for partially defined log-link iterates.
Proposition 3.5(ii)(c) At bad primes and nonzero labels, the splitting submonoids Psi_perp and infty Psi_perp act on rational log-shell packet modules. Across the column, only root-of-unity indeterminacies in log-link domains can be related, and these become addition by zero at the next index. This is the bad-prime source of the "no new indeterminacy after the log-link" clause. It is not a global equality of all packet actions.
Final paragraph The action is not via a single Kummer isomorphism. It is via the totality of Kummer isomorphisms pre-composed with iterates of the log-links. This is the page's main audit rule for the 3.11 to 3.12 corridor: source-faithful models must encode total log-Kummer correspondence.

Columnwise target

The label n,◦ in this page denotes the vertically coric target determined by the column. It is the object whose rational log-shell packet modules receive the total correspondence.

3.5 setupvertical coricity

Kummer displays

The displayed Kummer isomorphisms compare Psi_FLGP(n,m) with Psi_LGP(n,◦), and similarly for the realified monoids. They preserve the local actions and splittings stated in 3.5(i).

3.5(i)Kummer

Single Kummer map not enough

Proposition 3.5 explicitly rejects using one Kummer isomorphism as the action. Such a map fails to be compatible with the column's log-links, so the construction uses all relevant log-link pre-composites.

3.5(ii)audit rule

Nonarchimedean containment

For v_Q in V_Q^non, the target I(S_{j+1}^±F(n,◦D≻)_{v_Q}) contains images from both direct Kummer maps and Kummer maps pre-composed with log-link iterates.

3.5(ii)(a)upper semi

Archimedean ball containment

For archimedean primes, the closed unit ball contains images of units and radius-pi balls. This is the local real-analytic version of the upper-semi containment.

3.5(ii)(b)closed balls

Root-of-unity becomes zero

At bad primes, root-of-unity ambiguity in the domain of a log-link corresponds, after the log-link, to addition by zero. The proposition uses this to state mutual compatibility of bad-prime splitting actions without turning upper-semi compatibility into equality.

3.5(ii)(c)Ind3 source
Formalization warning. A Lean structure for this page should not store only one Kummer isomorphism and a proof that it commutes with log-links. The source statement asks for containment data generated by direct Kummer maps and by their log-link iterates, with separate nonarchimedean, archimedean, and bad-prime clauses.
Sources: IUT III, Proposition 3.5 Related: local LGP monoids upper semi-compatibility log-Kummer totality bad-prime splitting compatibility Theorem 3.11 Corollary 3.12 corridor

IUT III Proposition 3.5(i): Columnwise Vertically Coric Target

Proposition 3.5 starts with a Gaussian log-theta-lattice {n,mHT}_{n,m∈Z}. For each column n, vertical coricity determines a D-Θ±ellNF Hodge theater n,◦HTD-Θ±ellNF, up to isomorphism, from all theaters n,mHT in that column.

Column Target the vertically coric object is not one ordinary n,m theater
InputSource constructionAudit point
Column nAll theaters n,mHT for m∈Z.The target is columnwise, not one selected row entry.
Vertical coricityUses Theorem 1.5(i) to determine n,◦HTD-Θ±ellNF.The result is determined up to isomorphism.
PurposeBuild vertically coric local LGP monoids.Prepares the target for Kummer displays and upper-semi compatibility.
Sources: IUT III, Proposition 3.5(i) Related: vertically coric LGP overview vertical coricity Kummer displays

IUT III Proposition 3.5(i): Kummer Displays

For each n,m∈Z, Proposition 3.5(i) gives Kummer isomorphisms from the local LGP monoids of the ordinary n,m theater to the vertically coric LGP monoids of the n,◦ D-theater. These isomorphisms preserve the topological group actions at nonarchimedean places and the splittings up to torsion at bad places.

Kummer Comparison ordinary n,m local LGP monoids to vertically coric n,◦ monoids
Displayed source mapPreserved structureUse
Ψ_FLGP(n,mHT)_v ≃ Ψ_LGP(n,◦HTD)_vTopological group actions when v∈V_non; splittings up to torsion when v∈V_bad.Connects row-level LGP data to the columnwise coric target.
∞Ψ_FLGP(n,mHT)_v ≃ ∞Ψ_LGP(n,◦HTD)_vSame structure preservation for the realified/extended monoids.Feeds the upper-semi compatibility clauses.
Sources: IUT III, Proposition 3.5(i) Related: columnwise target single Kummer map warning log-Kummer correspondences

IUT III Proposition 3.5(ii): Single Kummer Map Not Enough

Proposition 3.5 explicitly says that the unit groups and splitting monoids should be thought of as acting on the n,◦ packet modules not via a single Kummer isomorphism, which fails to be compatible with the vertical log-links, but through the totality of Kummer isomorphisms pre-composed with iterates of those log-links.

Total Log-Kummer Correspondence direct Kummer maps plus log-link iterates
Formalization warning. A structure containing one Kummer isomorphism plus a commutativity proof is not source-faithful here. The theorem asks for containment/action data generated by direct Kummer maps and their log-link pre-composites, separated by place type.
Sources: IUT III, Proposition 3.5(ii), final paragraph Related: Kummer displays nonarchimedean containment archimedean containment bad-prime root-of-unity clause

IUT III Proposition 3.5(ii)(a): Nonarchimedean Containment

At a nonarchimedean rational place v_Q, Proposition 3.5(ii)(a) states a containment in I(S_{j+1}^±F(n,◦D≻)_{v_Q}). This target contains images of Galois-invariant unit submodules via both direct tensor products of the Kummer isomorphisms and tensor products of Kummer isomorphisms pre-composed with positive iterates of the column's log-links.

Input imageRoute into targetSource status
Galois-invariant units (Ψ_cns(n,mF≻)_{|t|})^×_vTensor product of relevant Kummer isomorphisms.Contained in the vertically coric packet module.
Same units from earlier log-link domainsPre-compose Kummer isomorphisms with m'-th log-link iterates for m'≥1.Also contained; this is upper-semi compatibility.
TargetI(S_{j+1}^±F(n,◦D≻)_{v_Q}).Constructed by Proposition 3.4(ii) in the vertically coric context.
Sources: IUT III, Proposition 3.5(ii)(a) Related: single Kummer map warning upper-semi Log-Kummer archimedean containment

IUT III Proposition 3.5(ii)(b): Archimedean Ball Containment

At an archimedean rational place v_Q, the target is the closed unit ball I(S_{j+1}^±F(n,◦D≻)_{v_Q}). Proposition 3.5(ii)(b) says it contains images of unit groups and closed radius-π balls. Such balls contain subsets that surject, under positive log-link iterates, onto the domains where those iterates are defined.

Contained objectSource routeWhy it is distinct
Unit groupsTensor product of Kummer isomorphisms.Archimedean counterpart of the unit-image clause.
Closed radius-π balls in group completionsMapped by the same tensor Kummer route.Specific real-analytic ball condition, not a nonarchimedean copy.
Subsets of ballsSurject onto log-link domains for positive iterates.Explains how partial log-link iterates enter the archimedean clause.
Sources: IUT III, Proposition 3.5(ii)(b) Related: nonarchimedean containment total log-Kummer correspondence archimedean containment

IUT III Proposition 3.5(ii)(c): Root-of-Unity Becomes Zero

At bad primes and nonzero labels, Proposition 3.5(ii)(c) compares the actions of splitting submonoids Ψ_FLGP^⊥ and ∞Ψ_FLGP^⊥ on rational packet modules. As m varies, the only portions possibly related by log-links are root-of-unity multiplication indeterminacies in the domains; under the log-link, these correspond to addition by zero at the next index.

Bad-Prime Compatibility root-of-unity ambiguity in the domain maps to no additive indeterminacy
Bad-prime objectSource conditionMeaning
Ψ_FLGP^⊥ ⊆ Ψ_FLGPSplitting submonoid corresponding to O⊥.Acts on rational packet modules.
∞Ψ_FLGP^⊥ ⊆ ∞Ψ_FLGPSubmonoid whose unit subgroup contains the full torsion subgroup.Tracks the realified/extended splitting side.
Log-link compatibilityOnly root-of-unity domain indeterminacies can be related across m; they become addition by zero at m+1.Explains why no additional bad-prime indeterminacy is introduced here.
Sources: IUT III, Proposition 3.5(ii)(c) Related: bad-prime LGP splittings total log-Kummer correspondence Corollary 3.12 corridor

Global Packet Frobenioids and Pilot Objects in IUT III 3.6-3.8

Example 3.6 and Proposition 3.7 globalize the packet machinery. They compare two concrete models of the global Frobenioid, build single-packet and global realified Frobenioids from local packet data, and prepare Definition 3.8, where theta-pilot and q-pilot objects enter the LGP/lgp Gaussian log-theta-lattice.

Section index

This hub groups the source route from FMOD/Fmod models to realified LGP/lgp Frobenioids and finally to theta/q pilot objects.

FMOD/Fmod single packets realified LGP/lgp pilot objects

Construction order

Example 3.6 fixes the two global models; Proposition 3.7 globalizes packet data and bad-prime monoids; Definition 3.8 names the pilot objects that are later compared through Theorem 3.11 and Corollary 3.12.

3.6 -> 3.7 -> 3.8source order

Audit rule

Keep the multiplicative FMOD route, additive Fmod route, and realified LGP/lgp route distinct. Collapsing them hides the source distinction between torsor, fractional-ideal, and log-volume containers.

do not collapsepilot corridor
3.6-3.8 global packet route FMOD/Fmod models, global LGP/lgp Frobenioids, and pilot objects
Source itemConstructionWhy it matters
Example 3.6(i) FMOD is the rational-function torsor version: objects are Fmod^×-torsors with local trivializations, and morphisms are elementary integral morphisms plus a positive Frobenius degree. This model is strictly multiplicative and is naturally isomorphic to the Frobenioid of arithmetic line bundles.
Example 3.6(ii) Fmod is the local fractional-ideal version: objects are collections {J_v} of fractional ideals, trivial almost everywhere, with integral morphisms given by elements of Fmod^×. This model represents objects additively as local modules, which is needed for later comparison by mono-analytic log-shells.
Remark 3.6.2 The union Fmod^× ∪ {0} has a natural additive structure. This fails for the rational-function monoids of the perfection or realification, and for arbitrary subgroups not coming from subfields. A formal model must not replace Fmod by an arbitrary multiplicative subgroup when additive object-level comparison is required.
Proposition 3.7(i),(ii) For each packet label alpha, construct (FMOD)_alpha from the global packet image, and construct (Fmod)_alpha from the number field plus local monoid invariants inside the packet integral structures. The packet construction now has global Frobenioid objects, not only local monoids acting on tensor packets.
Proposition 3.7(iii),(iv) Applying realified Frobenioid isomorphisms to the Gaussian source prime-strip constructs C_LGP and C_lgp, together with tautological F-prime-strip isomorphisms from F_gau. This is the global realified target in which later pilot objects and log-volume computations live.
Proposition 3.7(v) The global realified LGP and lgp Frobenioids embed into products of the labeled realified FMOD and Fmod copies; local bad-prime monoid generators construct global objects compatibly with local isomorphisms. This is the bridge from bad-prime splitting monoids to global objects whose log-volumes can be compared.
Definition 3.8(i) A theta-pilot object is determined by generators, up to torsion, of the bad-prime splitting monoids in the LGP/lgp side. A q-pilot object is determined in the global realified Frobenioid C_delta by the bad-prime q_v. This supplies the two pilot objects whose log-volume comparison is central in the later Theorem 3.11 to Corollary 3.12 route.

Two global models

FMOD is multiplicative and torsor-based. Fmod is fractional-ideal based and carries the additive object representation. They are isomorphic as Frobenioids, but their roles in later comparison are not interchangeable.

Example 3.6FMOD/Fmod

Additive structure warning

Remark 3.6.2 says the additive structure belongs to Fmod^× ∪ {0}, not to arbitrary realified or subgroup replacements. This is a source-level constraint on any formalization that tries to simplify the global Frobenioid layer.

Remark 3.6.2do not collapse

Single packet Frobenioids

Proposition 3.7(i),(ii) constructs one global Frobenioid for each packet label alpha. The construction is tied to the global packet image and to local packet monoid invariants.

Proposition 3.7(i),(ii)packet global

Global realified LGP/lgp

The C_LGP and C_lgp Frobenioids are constructed functorially in a log-link of Theta±ellNF Hodge theaters and carry F-prime-strip isomorphisms from the Gaussian source.

Proposition 3.7(iii),(iv)realified

Bad-prime data to global objects

Proposition 3.7(v) uses local fractional ideals generated by elements of bad-prime monoids to construct global objects in the LGP/lgp Frobenioids. This is the object-level globalization of the splitting monoid data.

Proposition 3.7(v)globalization

Theta and q pilots

Definition 3.8(i) distinguishes the theta-pilot object, built from bad-prime splitting generators on the LGP/lgp side, from the q-pilot object determined by the bad-prime q_v in the delta-side global realified Frobenioid.

Definition 3.8(i)pilot objects
Formalization consequence. The global layer should distinguish the multiplicative FMOD route, the additive fractional-ideal Fmod route, and the realified product-embedding route. The pilot-object comparison downstream depends on this separation.
Sources: IUT III, Example 3.6, Proposition 3.7, Definition 3.8 Related: vertically coric LGP global packet Frobenioids theta/q pilot objects LGP Gaussian log-theta lattice Corollary 3.12 corridor

IUT III Example 3.6: FMOD/Fmod Two Models

Example 3.6 gives two concrete representations of the global Frobenioid attached to Fmod. The FMOD model is a rational-function torsor model with local trivializations and Frobenius-degree morphisms. The Fmod model is a local fractional-ideal model whose objects are collections of fractional ideals, trivial almost everywhere. Both are naturally isomorphic to the Frobenioid of arithmetic line bundles, but their formal roles are different.

Two Concrete Models multiplicative torsors versus additive fractional ideals
ModelObject dataMorphism data
FMODFmod^×-torsor plus local trivializations compatible almost everywhere.Positive Frobenius degree plus integral elementary morphism of torsors.
FmodFractional ideals J_v⊆K_v, trivial almost everywhere.Positive Frobenius degree plus multiplication by an integral f∈Fmod^×.
ComparisonBoth model the arithmetic-line-bundle Frobenioid.The induced maps on rational function monoids are tautological identities.
Sources: IUT III, Example 3.6(i)-(iii) Related: global packet overview additive structure warning single packet global Frobenioids

IUT III Remark 3.6.2: Additive Structure Warning

Remark 3.6.2 isolates a source-level constraint: the union Fmod^× ∪ {0} has a natural additive structure, but the corresponding rational-function monoids of the perfection or realification do not.
