Proposition 1.6 From the prime number theorem, for sufficiently large n, the n-th prime satisfies n <= 4p_n/(3 log(p_n)). This turns prime counting into an explicit reciprocal-log estimate that can be inserted into the final theta-pilot bound.
Proposition 1.6 There is a positive real threshold eta_prm such that the weighted sum over primes p <= eta is at least one for all eta >= eta_prm. The constant eta_prm is later referenced as a concrete large-prime cutoff, not as an unspecified analytic assumption.
Proposition 1.7 For a finite set E, positive weights lambda_e, and real values beta_e, the weighted average over E^n of the tuple sum beta_vec_e equals n beta_avg. This is the elementary identity that lets packet-normalized local log-volumes be averaged over repeated label choices.
Remark 1.7.1 When using the normalized log-volumes of Proposition 1.4, the weights from IUT III Remark 3.1.1 must be replaced by local degree weights [(F_mod)_v : Q_vQ]. This prevents mixing normalized and non-normalized log-volume conventions in Theorem 1.10.
Remark 1.7.1 The proposition is applied with E the set of valuations over a fixed rational place, n=|A|, lambda_e the local degree, and beta_e a normalized local log-volume. The abstract averaging formula is tied to the actual valuation-indexed packet data used in the IUT IV estimate.

Prime-number bound

Proposition 1.6 uses only the standard prime number theorem, but fixes an explicit inequality strong enough for the later proof. The wiki records it because it is where analytic prime distribution first enters IUT IV.

Prop. 1.6PNT

eta_prm threshold

The threshold eta_prm packages the sufficiently large range of the prime-number estimate. Later estimates can refer to this number instead of reopening the analytic proof.

Prop. 1.6large bound

Finite weight set

Proposition 1.7 starts with a finite nonempty set E, positive weights lambda_e, and real values beta_e. This abstraction is deliberately elementary, so the later packet averaging is a transparent calculation.

Prop. 1.7finite set

Tuple weight product

For a tuple vec e in E^n, the product lambda_Pi_vec_e multiplies the component weights, while beta_vec_e sums the component values. The identity computes their normalized average.

Prop. 1.7E^n

Normalized-weight replacement

Remark 1.7.1 is a convention checkpoint: because IUT IV uses normalized log-volume, the degree weights used in IUT III's non-normalized setting must be replaced by normalized local degree weights.

Rem. 1.7.1normalization

Packet log-volume application

In Theorem 1.10, E is the set of local valuations over a rational place, and beta_e is a normalized log-volume of a compact open subset. This is where the elementary average becomes a packet-normalized log-volume computation.

Rem. 1.7.1packets
Formalization warning: the averaging formula is elementary, but its use is convention-sensitive. A formal model must state whether log-volumes are normalized as in Proposition 1.4; otherwise the weights imported from IUT III Remark 3.1.1 have the wrong scale.
Sources: IUT IV, Propositions 1.6-1.7 and Remark 1.7.1 Related: local estimates weighted averages atlas card prime-number estimate atlas card theta-pilot estimate

IUT IV Proposition 1.6: Prime Estimate

Proposition 1.6 is the analytic-number-theory input of IUT IV Section 1. It uses the prime number theorem to produce an explicit large-prime inequality and a threshold eta_prm used later in the theta-pilot computation.

Prime counting

For the n-th prime p_n, the source obtains n <= 4p_n/(3 log(p_n)) for all sufficiently large n.

Threshold

The inequality implies existence of eta_prm > 0 such that the corresponding prime sum is at least 1 whenever eta >= eta_prm.

Theorem 1.10 use

The threshold is cited in Theorem 1.10 to absorb prime-sum contributions into an explicit bound rather than a qualitative asymptotic.

ComponentSource statusFormalization consequence
Prime number theorem Imported as a classical analytic estimate, cited through a standard reference. A Lean development can initially treat this as a named analytic-number-theory theorem or imported certificate.
Numerical constant 4/3 Chosen after passing to a sufficiently large range where the asymptotic ratio is controlled. The exact constant should remain visible because it feeds the explicit estimate.
eta_prm Existence threshold for the final prime-sum inequality. Downstream pages should reference this threshold rather than rephrasing it as "large enough" without a name.
Source: IUT IV, Proposition 1.6 Related: prime/averaging overview Theorem 1.10 Diophantine inequalities

IUT IV Proposition 1.7: Weighted Averages

Proposition 1.7 is elementary, but its role is structural: it computes the weighted average of tuple-sums over E^n. Remark 1.7.1 then identifies the correct weights when IUT IV uses normalized log-volumes rather than the non-normalized convention from IUT III.

Finite data

A nonempty finite set E, positive weights lambda_e, and real values beta_e.

Tuple calculation

For tuples in E^n, product weights and summed beta-values have average n beta_avg.

IUT specialization

In Remark 1.7.1, E is the valuation set over a fixed rational place, lambda_e is a local degree, and beta_e is a normalized log-volume.

ItemStatement contentAudit consequence
Proposition 1.7 Weighted average over E^n of tuple-sums equals n beta_avg. This can be formalized as finite-sum algebra before any IUT-specific objects are introduced.
Remark 1.7.1 Normalized log-volume changes the weights from the IUT III non-normalized convention. The formal interface must carry a normalization flag or separate weight construction.
Theorem 1.10 specialization The set E is valuations over v_Q; n=|A|; lambda_e=[(F_mod)_v:Q_{v_Q}]. The generic finite-sum theorem must be instantiated with valuation-indexed packet data.
Formalization checkpoint: this proposition is mathematically easy but convention-sensitive. Mixing the IUT III weights with IUT IV normalized log-volume would give a type-correct but source-inaccurate theorem.
Source: IUT IV, Proposition 1.7 and Remark 1.7.1 Related: normalized log-volume IUT III packet normalization Theorem 1.10

IUT IV Proposition 1.8: Elliptic Torsion Input

Proposition 1.8 collects the elliptic-curve facts needed before the explicit theta-pilot estimate: torsion detects automorphisms, descent data is expressed by sections over Galois subgroups, rational torsion forces uniqueness and semistability, 2-torsion gives the Legendre form, and reduction type controls ramification on torsion.

Section index

This parent page groups the elliptic-curve gates used before Theorem 1.10 and Corollary 2.2. The child pages isolate the three main subpackages.

torsion rigidity descent sections reduction gates

Proof flow

Assertions (i) and (iv) make torsion-level data rigid; (ii) and (iii) turn descent into Galois-section data; (v)-(vii) convert rational torsion and reduction type into semistability and ramification control.

torsion-to-reductionapplication gate

Reading rule

Do not read Proposition 1.8 as generic elliptic-curve background. In IUT IV it supplies the exact gates that make the tripodal field, Legendre model, bad/good place split, and later different estimates usable.

grouped unitnot folklore
Elliptic input route torsion, descent, Legendre form, and ramification constraints
Source itemStatementRole in IUT IV
Proposition 1.8(i) For l >= 3, the action of automorphisms of a polarized abelian variety on l-torsion is injective. This makes torsion-level structure rigid enough to control models and descent in the later application.
Proposition 1.8(ii) For an elliptic curve over a perfect field, automorphisms over the algebraic closure fit into an exact sequence over G_k; models over extensions are equivalent to sections over the corresponding closed subgroups. The proposition translates model/descent questions into Galois-section data, matching the anabelian style of the surrounding IUT machinery.
Proposition 1.8(iii) If the geometric automorphism group is {+/-1}, then the 2-torsion representation factors through the field-of-moduli Galois image. This isolates the 2-torsion information used to pass to a Legendre-form model in the application setup.
Proposition 1.8(iv) If two descents to k have all l-torsion rational over k, then the descended elliptic curves are isomorphic over k. Rational torsion eliminates a model ambiguity that would otherwise affect the initial-theta-data construction.
Proposition 1.8(v) Over a complete discrete valuation field, rational l-torsion with l >= 3 and l invertible implies semistable reduction. This supplies the reduction-type control required by the later bad/good place estimates.
Proposition 1.8(vi) If the field-of-moduli image is all of G_k and the 2-torsion action is trivial, then the curve descends to a Legendre-form model; over a discrete valuation field, semistability is reached after an extension of degree at most two. This is the local source for the lambda-line/Legendre model used in IUT IV Corollary 2.2.
Proposition 1.8(vii) For torsion prime to the residue characteristic, good reduction gives unramified Galois action on torsion, while bad multiplicative reduction gives tame ramification. These ramification alternatives feed the explicit prime and different estimates in Theorem 1.10 and Section 2.

Serre criterion

The first assertion is a rigidity statement: a nontrivial automorphism of a polarized abelian variety cannot act trivially on l-torsion for l >= 3.

Prop. 1.8(i)torsion

Descent via sections

A model over an extension field is the same as a section of the automorphism-to-Galois map over the corresponding subgroup. This keeps descent explicit instead of treating the model as a label.

Prop. 1.8(ii)sections

Field-of-moduli image

The finite extension determined by the Galois image is the field generated by the j-invariant. This is why the later field-of-moduli notation is tied to actual descent data.

Prop. 1.8(ii)j

2-torsion factorization

When the only geometric automorphisms are +/-1, their action on 2-torsion is trivial, so the 2-torsion representation descends to the field-of-moduli Galois image.

Prop. 1.8(iii)E[2]

Rational torsion uniqueness

If all l-torsion is rational, the section is forced by lying in the kernel of the torsion representation. This gives uniqueness of the descended model.

Prop. 1.8(iv)unique model

Semistable reduction from level

Full rational l-torsion for l >= 3 gives an integral point on a proper level cover of the compactified elliptic moduli stack, hence semistable reduction.

Prop. 1.8(v)level

Legendre-form descent

Trivial 2-torsion action gives rational 2-torsion, hence a Legendre equation after at most a quadratic twist. This is the source for the lambda-line model in the IUT IV application.

Prop. 1.8(vi)Legendre

Good reduction

For torsion order invertible in the valuation ring, good reduction makes the Galois action on torsion unramified. This becomes a clean local input at good places.

Prop. 1.8(vii)unramified

Multiplicative reduction

Bad multiplicative reduction gives a tamely ramified torsion action. This separates the bad multiplicative places from the good ones in the arithmetic estimates.

Prop. 1.8(vii)tame
Formalization warning: Proposition 1.8 is not just background elliptic-curve folklore. It supplies named gates for uniqueness of models, field-of-moduli descent, Legendre-form input, and ramification cases used by the later construction of suitable initial theta data.
Sources: IUT IV, Proposition 1.8 Related: prime/averaging input arithmetic divisors theta-pilot estimate IUT IV output route

IUT IV Proposition 1.8: Torsion Rigidity

The torsion-rigidity part of Proposition 1.8 consists of Serre's criterion and the uniqueness consequence for elliptic-curve descents with rational l-torsion. It is the point where finite torsion data becomes strong enough to pin down model ambiguity.

Serre criterion

For l >= 3, an automorphism of a polarized abelian variety over an algebraically closed field is detected by its action on l-torsion.

Kernel condition

A descended elliptic model with rational l-torsion corresponds to a section whose image lies in the kernel of the torsion representation.

Uniqueness

Since the geometric automorphism subgroup injects into torsion automorphisms, the descended model with rational l-torsion is unique up to isomorphism.

AssertionPaper contentWhy it is a separate gate
1.8(i) The natural map Aut(A,lambda) -> Aut(A[l]) is injective for l >= 3. It prevents hidden automorphisms from surviving after passage to level structure.
Proof input The proof uses the l-adic Tate module and the fact that an algebraic-integer root of unity congruent to 1 mod l is trivial for l >= 3. The statement is classical but not definitional; formalization should import or prove it as an elliptic/abelian-variety lemma.
1.8(iv) Two descended elliptic curves over k with all l-torsion rational are isomorphic over k. This is the uniqueness gate used before constructing the fields and models in Theorem 1.10 and Corollary 2.2.
Source: IUT IV, Proposition 1.8(i),(iv) Related: Proposition 1.8 overview descent sections Theorem 1.10

IUT IV Proposition 1.8: Descent Sections

Proposition 1.8(ii)-(iii) converts elliptic-curve descent into sections of an automorphism-to-Galois exact sequence. This is why the later tripodal and field-of-moduli fields are not just labels: they are tied to an actual Galois image and torsion representation.

Exact sequence

Automorphisms of the geometric elliptic curve fit into 1 -> Aut_k(E) -> Aut_kbar(E) -> G_k, with open image G_E.

Models as sections

A model over the extension corresponding to a closed subgroup H subset G_k is equivalent to a section over H.

2-torsion factor

If Aut_kbar(E)={+/-1}, the 2-torsion representation factors through G_E.

AssertionPaper contentUse in the wiki route
1.8(ii) The finite extension determined by G_E is the minimal field of definition, generated by the j-invariant. Links the field-of-moduli notation to a concrete elliptic-curve invariant.
1.8(ii) Descent data over k_H is equivalent to a section of Aut(E) -> G_k over H. Provides a typed interface for model choices instead of treating descents as opaque labels.
1.8(iii) When geometric automorphisms are {+/-1}, the representation on E[2] factors through G_E. Feeds the Legendre-form descent condition in 1.8(vi) and the tripodal field in Theorem 1.10.
Source: IUT IV, Proposition 1.8(ii)-(iii) Related: torsion rigidity reduction gates suitable theta data

IUT IV Proposition 1.8: Reduction Gates

Proposition 1.8(v)-(vii) supplies the reduction and ramification gates used after the torsion/descent setup: rational level structure gives semistable reduction, trivial 2-torsion gives a Legendre-form model, and reduction type controls ramification on prime-to-residue-characteristic torsion.

Rational level

Full rational l-torsion over a complete discrete valuation field implies semistable reduction.

Legendre gate

Trivial G_E -> Aut(E[2]) action and G_E=G_k give a Legendre-form model, after at most a quadratic extension for semistability.

Ramification split

Good reduction gives unramified torsion action; bad multiplicative reduction gives tame ramification.

AssertionReduction statementDownstream consequence
1.8(v) Full rational l-torsion with l >= 3 and invertible in the valuation ring implies semistable reduction. Supports the reduction hypotheses needed when Theorem 1.10 estimates bad and good places separately.
1.8(vi) Trivial 2-torsion action yields a Legendre-form model; semistability is reached after degree at most two, and no extension is needed if good reduction already holds. Provides the Legendre/lambda-line input used in Corollary 2.2 and suitable initial theta data.
1.8(vii) Good reduction makes torsion action unramified; bad multiplicative reduction makes it tame. Feeds the ramification and different estimates that appear in Theorem 1.10.
Formalization checkpoint: the reduction gates should remain separate from the torsion-rigidity gates. The former control ramification and semistability; the latter control uniqueness and descent.
Source: IUT IV, Proposition 1.8(v)-(vii) Related: different estimates Theorem 1.10 Diophantine inequalities

IUT IV Definition 1.9: Arithmetic Divisors

Definition 1.9 fixes the global bookkeeping language used in Theorem 1.10: arithmetic divisors on a number field, support and effectivity, the degree and normalized degree maps, and the localized normalized E-degree over a rational valuation.

Section index

This parent page groups the degree bookkeeping used by Theorem 1.10. The child pages separate the global degree map from the localized E-degree operation.

degree map localized degree

Proof flow

Finite valuation sums become arithmetic divisors; the degree map assigns finite and archimedean weights; normalized degree makes the scale invariant under finite field extension; localized degree isolates one rational-place contribution.

global-to-localdegree scale

Reading rule

Do not replace Definition 1.9 by fractional ideals. The later symbols log(d), log(q), and log(f) are normalized degrees of arithmetic divisors, with archimedean and localized conventions built in.

grouped unitnot ideal degree
Arithmetic divisor bookkeeping global valuation sums and base-extension-invariant degrees
Source itemDefinitionUse in IUT IV
Definition 1.9 setup For a number field F, valuations split into nonarchimedean and archimedean parts. At nonarchimedean v, the notation includes ord_v, ramification index e_v, and residue cardinality q_v. This is the valuation language used by the later different, conductor, and q-parameter divisors.
Definition 1.9(i) An R-arithmetic divisor is a finite formal sum sum c_v v over valuations. Its support is where c_v != 0; it is effective if all coefficients are nonnegative. The theorem can speak uniformly about finite and archimedean height data without leaving the divisor formalism.
Definition 1.9(i) The degree map weights nonarchimedean valuations by log(q_v) and archimedean valuations by 1. The normalized degree divides by [F:Q]. This fixes the global scale for the explicit estimates in Theorem 1.10 and Section 2.
Definition 1.9(i) For every finite extension K/F, normalized degree is invariant under pullback: deg(a|K)=deg(a). This allows the proof to move through the field extensions appearing in initial theta data without changing the normalized degree scale.
Definition 1.9(ii) If E is a nonempty set of valuations of F above one rational valuation v_Q, then a_E is the supported part and deg_E(a) is the normalized E-degree. This is the local-over-global degree used when estimates are localized at a chosen rational place.
Definition 1.9(ii) The localized degree is also stable under finite base extension: deg_{E|K}(a|K)=deg_E(a). This preserves the local contribution when passing from F to larger fields in the theta-data tower.

Valuation split

Definition 1.9 starts by separating nonarchimedean and archimedean valuations. Only nonarchimedean valuations carry ord_v, e_v, and q_v; archimedean valuations enter through the degree weight 1.

Def. 1.9V(F)

Finite formal sum

Arithmetic divisors are finite sums over all valuations, not ideals alone. This is why the same object can carry finite q-parameter data and archimedean contributions.

Def. 1.9(i)ADiv

Support and effectivity

Support records the valuations with nonzero coefficient. Effectivity is coefficientwise nonnegativity and later applies to q-parameter and conductor-like divisors.

Def. 1.9(i)effective

Degree map

The degree map sends nonarchimedean v to log(q_v) and archimedean v to 1. The normalized version divides by [F:Q].

Def. 1.9(i)degree

Base-extension invariance

Pulling an arithmetic divisor from F to a finite extension K preserves normalized degree. This is a scaling convention, not an automatic property of unnormalized degree.

Def. 1.9(i)pullback

Localized E-degree

The E-degree isolates the contribution from valuations above a fixed rational valuation. It is the bookkeeping device for localizing global divisor estimates.

Def. 1.9(ii)local
Formalization warning: arithmetic divisors in Definition 1.9 combine finite and archimedean valuation data and use normalized degrees. A formalization should not silently replace them by fractional ideals or by unnormalized divisor degrees.
Sources: IUT IV, Definition 1.9 Related: elliptic torsion input theta-pilot estimate IUT IV overview IUT IV output route

IUT IV Definition 1.9: Degree Map

Definition 1.9(i) turns valuation-indexed data into real arithmetic divisors and then defines the degree and normalized degree maps used in Theorem 1.10. The key point is that finite and archimedean places live in one formal object, but with different degree weights.

Arithmetic divisor

An R-arithmetic divisor on F is a finite sum sum c_v v over valuations, with support where c_v != 0.

Degree weights

Nonarchimedean v is weighted by log(q_v); archimedean v is weighted by 1.

Normalization

The normalized degree divides by [F:Q] and is invariant under finite base extension.

Definition itemSource contentFormalization consequence
Support/effectivity Support is the set of valuations with nonzero coefficient; effectivity is coefficientwise nonnegativity. Effective q-parameter, different, and conductor divisors should be represented by a predicate on coefficients.
Degree map deg_F sends finite valuations to log(q_v) and archimedean valuations to 1. A formal model must not replace this by the degree of an ideal, because archimedean coefficients are part of the object.
Normalized degree deg(a)=deg_F(a)/[F:Q] and deg(a|K)=deg(a) for finite K/F. The invariant normalized scale is what allows field extensions in Theorem 1.10 without changing units.
Source: IUT IV, Definition 1.9(i) Related: arithmetic divisors overview localized degree Theorem 1.10

IUT IV Definition 1.9: Localized Degree

Definition 1.9(ii) localizes the normalized degree to a nonempty set of valuations lying above one rational valuation. This is the bookkeeping operation that lets Theorem 1.10 isolate the contribution of a specified rational place while keeping the same base-extension invariance.

Choose a rational place

Fix v_Q in V(Q) and a nonempty set E subset V(F) of valuations above v_Q.

Restrict support

For a=sum c_v v, define a_E=sum_{v in E} c_v v.

Normalize locally

Divide the degree of a_E by the local degrees over Q_{v_Q}; the result is stable under finite base extension.

Definition itemSource contentUse in Theorem 1.10
a_E The part of the arithmetic divisor supported on valuations in E. Allows different, conductor, and q-parameter divisors to be evaluated at one rational place.
deg_E(a) The normalized E-degree of a, using local degree denominators. This is the notation later used for log(d_v), log(q_v), and rational-place variants.
Base extension For finite K/F, deg_{E|K}(a|K)=deg_E(a). Local contributions survive passage to the field towers used in the theta-pilot estimate.
Formalization checkpoint: the localized degree is not a new divisor. It is a projection and normalized measurement of an existing arithmetic divisor over a chosen rational place.
Source: IUT IV, Definition 1.9(ii) Related: degree map Theorem 1.10 IUT IV output route

IUT IV Theorem 1.10: Theta-Pilot Estimate

Theorem 1.10 is the explicit numerical meeting point of IUT III Corollary 3.12 and the local estimates of IUT IV Section 1. It starts with initial theta data satisfying the Corollary 3.12 hypotheses, adds torsion and field-of-moduli assumptions, and converts the theta-pilot log-volume bound into a concrete inequality for log(q).

Section index

This parent page gives the whole Theorem 1.10 route. The child pages isolate the hypotheses, the local estimates, and the final inequality.

hypotheses local steps final bound

Proof flow

Corollary 3.12 supplies CTheta >= -1; the field and arithmetic-divisor package define the quantities; local p-adic and archimedean estimates bound the theta-pilot hull; averaging and prime estimates produce the explicit constant.

3.12-to-numberexplicit estimate

Reading rule

Do not treat Theorem 1.10 as an alternate proof of Corollary 3.12. It is the downstream calculation that turns the assumed Corollary 3.12 comparison into the numerical q-height inequality used in IUT IV §2.

grouped unitassumes 3.12
Theta-pilot estimate route Corollary 3.12 plus local numerical estimates
Theorem 1.10 itemPaper contentWiki/formalization consequence
Hypotheses The theorem assumes initial theta data in the situation of IUT III Corollary 3.12, with good reduction at good nonarchimedean places not dividing 2*ell, and with specified torsion-field conditions. The page is downstream of the Corollary 3.12 corridor; it does not independently prove that corridor.
Tripodal field The field tower includes Fmod, the tripodal field Ftpd = Fmod(E[2]), and F, together with 2, 3, and 5 torsion conditions used in the explicit estimates. Field data and torsion data must be represented before the inequality can be read as a Diophantine estimate.
Divisor inputs For an intermediate field F' between Fmod and K, Galois over Fmod, the theorem uses arithmetic divisors d_ADiv^F', q_ADiv^F', and f_ADiv^F' for differents, q-parameters, and bad-place conductor support. These are Definition 1.9 objects with normalized degrees, not informal height labels.
Theta-pilot target Step (iv) estimates the procession-normalized mono-analytic log-volume of the holomorphic hull of possible images of the theta-pilot object, relative to the Kummer isomorphisms of IUT III Theorem 3.11 and subject to Ind1, Ind2, and Ind3. This is the bridge from the IUT III comparison corridor to the explicit IUT IV numerical calculation.
Local cases Step (v) handles distinguished nonarchimedean valuations using Proposition 1.4(iii); Step (vi) gives zero contribution at nondistinguished nonarchimedean valuations; Step (vii) applies Proposition 1.5 to archimedean valuations. The local estimates should remain as three separate formal gates, because their hypotheses and error terms differ.
Averaging and primes Step (viii) sums the local bounds using the weighted-average formalism and absorbs prime-counting terms by Proposition 1.6 and eta_prm. The final constant is a composed estimate, not a single unexplained numerical axiom.
Final inequality Taking the displayed value of CTheta and using CTheta >= -1 yields an upper bound for (1/6) log(q) by normalized different/conductor degrees and the explicit e*_mod ell + eta_prm term. The formal target is a theorem transforming certified Corollary 3.12 input plus local Section 1 estimates into this explicit inequality.

Field tower

The theorem works over Fmod, Ftpd, F, and K. The tripodal intermediate field is generated by 2-torsion, while the 3- and 5-torsion hypotheses enter the explicit bound through the chosen initial theta data.

Thm. 1.10field data

Divisor inputs

The theorem's d, q, and f terms are normalized arithmetic-divisor degrees. This keeps ramification, bad places, and theta q-parameters on the same global scale.

Def. 1.9normalized degree

Theta-pilot hull

The object estimated is a holomorphic hull of possible theta-pilot images under the multiradial comparison machinery of Theorem 3.11. Ind3 is used as an upper-semi containment, matching the sign-sensitive log-volume convention.

Step (iv)theta-pilot

Distinguished local case

At distinguished nonarchimedean valuations, the proof applies Proposition 1.4(iii) to bound automorphism-distorted local regions by different, q-parameter, prime, and ramification terms.

Step (v)Prop. 1.4

Nondistinguished case

At nondistinguished nonarchimedean valuations, Proposition 1.4(iv) gives an exactly stable normalized tensor-product region, hence zero upper contribution to the relevant log-volume.

Step (vi)zero bound

Archimedean case

At archimedean valuations, Proposition 1.5 bounds the tensor-product region by a power of the integral ball B_I, giving the archimedean term used in the total estimate.

Step (vii)metric

Procession averaging

The proof averages over the packet/procession indices using Proposition 1.7, so the local log-volume bounds become a single procession-normalized global estimate.

Step (viii)weighted average

Prime absorption

Proposition 1.6 controls the prime-sum contribution by eta_prm. This is where the elementary analytic estimate enters the otherwise arithmetic divisor calculation.

Prop. 1.6eta_prm

Final inequality

The theorem derives (1/6)log(q) bounded by a multiple of log(d)+log(f) plus the explicit torsion/prime term. The sign-sensitive input is |log(q)| = (1/(2*ell))log(q) in the IUT convention.

outputlog(q)
Formalization warning: Theorem 1.10 is not a substitute proof of IUT III Corollary 3.12. It assumes the Corollary 3.12 lower bound CTheta >= -1 and then performs the explicit local, averaged, and prime-counting estimates needed to turn that lower bound into a Diophantine numerical inequality.
Sources: IUT IV, Theorem 1.10 Related: Corollary 3.12 Theorem 3.11 algorithms local estimates prime/averaging input elliptic torsion input arithmetic divisors suitable initial theta data IUT IV output route

IUT IV Theorem 1.10: Hypotheses

Theorem 1.10 begins downstream of IUT III Corollary 3.12. Its hypotheses add good-reduction, field-tower, torsion, and arithmetic-divisor data so that the abstract theta-pilot log-volume comparison can be converted into explicit numerical estimates.

3.12 input

The theorem assumes initial theta data in the situation of IUT III Corollary 3.12; it does not reprove the Corollary 3.12 comparison.

Field tower

It uses Fmod subset Ftpd subset F, with Ftpd obtained from 2-torsion and F obtained by adjoining sqrt(-1) and 3,5-torsion.

Divisor package

For intermediate fields Galois over Fmod, it defines arithmetic divisors for differents, q-parameters, and the bad-place conductor support.

Hypothesis blockSource contentAudit consequence
Corollary 3.12 situation Initial theta data is assumed to be in the situation of IUT III Corollary 3.12. Theorem 1.10 is a downstream numerical application, not an independent validation of the disputed corridor.
Good reduction The elliptic curve has good reduction at every good nonarchimedean valuation not dividing 2 ell. This gates the later use of unramified local estimates.
Torsion fields Ftpd=Fmod(E[2]) and F=Ftpd(sqrt(-1),E[3*5]), with the model tied to the Legendre form. This is where Proposition 1.8 feeds into the explicit theta-data field tower.
Arithmetic divisors The theorem names d_ADiv, q_ADiv, and f_ADiv for differents, q-parameters, and conductor support. All later logarithms in the estimate are normalized degrees of Definition 1.9 objects.
Source: IUT IV, Theorem 1.10 statement Related: Corollary 3.12 Proposition 1.8 gates Definition 1.9 degree map

IUT IV Theorem 1.10: Local Steps

The proof of Theorem 1.10 splits the theta-pilot log-volume estimate into distinguished nonarchimedean, nondistinguished nonarchimedean, and archimedean cases. Each case uses a specific earlier local proposition before the contributions are averaged.

Distinguished nonarchimedean

Uses Proposition 1.4(iii), hence the full different/logarithm upper-bound machinery.

Nondistinguished nonarchimedean

Uses Proposition 1.4(iv), the odd unramified zero-loss case.

Archimedean

Uses Proposition 1.5(iii)-(iv) to place tensor vectors in the appropriate archimedean integral ball.

Proof stepSource dependencyWhat must remain visible
Step (v) Distinguished nonarchimedean valuations use Proposition 1.4(iii), with different and ramification terms. This is the nonzero local upper-bound contribution; it cannot be summarized as "bounded locally".
Step (vi) Nondistinguished nonarchimedean valuations use Proposition 1.4(iv). The zero contribution depends on odd unramified hypotheses, not on a general cancellation principle.
Step (vii) Archimedean valuations use Proposition 1.5 to control tensor-product metric containers. The archimedean contribution is metric and tensorial, not p-adic.
Weighted sum Proposition 1.7 and Remark 1.7.1 supply the normalized packet weights. Normalization convention must match Proposition 1.4; otherwise the average has the wrong scale.
Source: IUT IV, Theorem 1.10 proof steps Related: nonarchimedean log-volume archimedean metrics weighted averages

IUT IV Theorem 1.10: Final Bound

The final part of Theorem 1.10 combines the local estimates with the procession average and the prime-number estimate. The role of Proposition 1.6 is to absorb prime-sum terms into an explicit choice of constants, while Corollary 3.12 supplies the theta-pilot lower bound.

Lower bound

The starting inequality is the theta-pilot lower bound inherited from IUT III Corollary 3.12.

Upper computation

Local upper estimates, normalized degrees, and weighted averages produce the explicit expression involving log(q) and divisor terms.

Prime absorption

Proposition 1.6 supplies eta_prm, allowing the prime contribution to be absorbed in the final displayed inequality.

Final-bound ingredientPaper roleWiki/formalization reading
Corollary 3.12 Provides the theta-pilot comparison/lower bound used at the start of the explicit estimate. The disputed input is upstream; Theorem 1.10 shows how it would be used numerically.
Local upper estimate Steps (v)-(vii) bound local log-volume contributions from nonarchimedean and archimedean places. The final inequality should be audited back to the exact local proposition used in each case.
Prime-number term Proposition 1.6 and eta_prm control the distinguished-prime summation. Analytic input enters here, after the IUT comparison and local arithmetic estimates.
Explicit consequence The result is an explicit theta-pilot log-volume estimate for log(q) with constants defined from the initial-theta-data field tower. This page is the immediate source for the Section 2 Diophantine applications.
Formalization checkpoint: the final bound should be represented as a theorem whose inputs include the Corollary 3.12 comparison certificate and the local estimates, so that the disputed upstream step remains explicitly visible.
Source: IUT IV, Theorem 1.10 final estimate Related: hypotheses local steps Diophantine inequalities

IUT IV Proposition 2.1: Well-Known Estimates

Proposition 2.1 supplies the elementary analytic estimates used in the proof of Corollary 2.2. It combines the linearization log(x) <= x with a prime-number-theorem bound for Chebyshev's theta function and a prime-avoidance consequence.

Log linearization

For real x >= 1, the paper uses log(x) <= x.

Prime theta bound

There is xi_prm >= 5 such that (2/3)x <= theta(x) <= (4/3)x for x >= xi_prm.

Avoid finite primes

For a finite set A of primes, one finds a prime outside A bounded by 2(theta_A+xi_prm).

ItemPaper contentUse in Corollary 2.2
2.1(i) The elementary inequality log(x) <= x for x >= 1. Used repeatedly to replace logarithmic factors by polynomial-looking bounds when enlarging exceptional sets.
2.1(ii) A prime-number-theorem estimate for theta(x)=sum_{p<=x}log(p). Controls the size of the finite set of forbidden primes and the choice of auxiliary ell.
Prime avoidance For finite A, some prime outside A is at most 2(theta_A+xi_prm). This is the direct input to the proof properties (P1)-(P3).
Source: IUT IV, Proposition 2.1 Related: Corollary 2.2 prime/Galois gate Proposition 1.6 Corollary 2.3

IUT IV Corollary 2.2: Suitable Initial Theta Data

Corollary 2.2 is the application gate. It starts from the lambda-line P^1 - {0,1,infty}, imposes compact-boundedness and a 2-adic j-invariant condition, removes controlled exceptional points, chooses a large auxiliary prime ell, and produces initial theta data satisfying the hypotheses of Theorem 1.10.

Section index

Read this page as the unified route through Corollary 2.2. The linked subpages remain searchable and individually addressable.

lambda/j gate BD heights exceptions prime/Galois C1/C2

Adjacent remarks

The following remarks explain how the estimate interacts with Riemann-hypothesis style weights, semi-twists, uniform abc language, and p-adic Teichmuller analogies.

2.2.1 2.2.2 2.2.3 2.2.4

Reading rule

The parent page gives the whole proof route at once; the child pages isolate details that are easy to lose, such as bounded-discrepancy equality, exceptional-set enlargement, and auxiliary-prime selection.

grouped unitIUT IV §2
Dependency orderSource stepWhere to read next
1 Start with the lambda-line, compactly bounded subset KV, support at 2, and bounded 2-adic j-image. lambda/j gate
2 Use part (i) to identify q-parameter heights and ht_{ω_X(D)} only up to bounded-discrepancy class. BD heights
3 Enlarge finite exceptional sets while keeping the dependence on KV, d, and error parameters explicit. exceptions
4 Choose the auxiliary prime ell, exclude l-cyclic subgroup pathologies, and obtain the large Galois-image condition. prime/Galois gate
5 Apply Theorem 1.10 to the resulting initial theta data and record conditions (C1) and (C2), then use part (iii) to force epsilon_E small outside a further finite set. C1/C2 output
Corollary 2.2 application gate lambda-line data to Theorem 1.10 hypotheses
Corollary 2.2 itemPaper contentWiki/formalization consequence
Lambda line The source curve is P^1_Q with divisor {0,1,infty}, viewed as the lambda-line for the Legendre equation y^2=x(x-1)(x-lambda). The application is first proved in the concrete thrice-punctured-line setting before GenEll transfers it to general hyperbolic curves.
Compact set A compactly bounded subset KV is chosen with support containing the prime 2. The extra condition (*j-inv) requires the 2-adic j-invariant image to be bounded. This is a real application hypothesis, not a stylistic restriction: it controls the comparison of all-prime and odd-prime q-parameter heights.
BD identity Part (i) identifies, up to bounded discrepancy on KV, the normalized q-parameter height away from 2, the all-prime q-height, the height at infinity, and ht_{omega_X(D)}. The output is an equality of bounded-discrepancy classes, not pointwise equality of functions.
Exceptions Part (ii) removes a finite set depending on KV, d, and the chosen error parameter. It contains points whose elliptic curves have automorphisms of order greater than 2. The formal object should record which constants are uniform in KV and which depend on it.
Prime ell The proof chooses a prime ell with sqrt(h) <= ell and an explicit upper bound, avoiding divisibility by local height terms and avoiding l-cyclic subgroup pathologies after enlarging the exceptional set. This is the arithmetic source of condition (C1), and it explains the square-root scale later visible in the error term.
Theta data The prime-choice, bad multiplicative place, and torsion-image properties give initial theta data satisfying IUT I Definition 3.1 and the hypotheses of Theorem 1.10. This page is the link from explicit Diophantine input to the inter-universal machinery; it is not part of the Corollary 3.12 proof itself.
C2 bound Applying Theorem 1.10 yields the displayed q-height bound by (1+epsilon_E) times log-diff + log-cond plus a constant depending on KV. Corollary 2.2 is the immediate numerical precursor to Corollary 2.3.

Lambda line

The point lambda classifies a Legendre elliptic curve. The removed points 0,1,infty are exactly the degenerations excluded from the open curve.

Cor. 2.2Legendre

2-adic j condition

The condition (*j-inv) controls the image of KV under the j-invariant at the prime 2. This is what makes the away-from-2 and all-prime q-height functions equivalent up to bounded discrepancy on KV.

conditionprime 2

BD-class identity

The identity (1/6)log(q_2) ~= (1/6)log(q_all) ~= ht_{omega_X(D)} is the translation from q-parameter data to the Vojta height language.

part (i)BD class

Prime choice

Proposition 2.1 supplies a prime outside a finite prohibited set. Its size is controlled by the q-height and degree parameter, which is why ell is comparable to a square root of the height up to logarithmic factors.

Prop. 2.1large ell

Galois image gate

After excluding an l-cyclic subgroup case and ensuring a bad multiplicative place remains, GenEll gives the required large image condition on ell-torsion.

SL2(F_ell)torsion

C1/C2 output

Condition (C1) records the prime range. Condition (C2) records the q-height estimate by log-different and log-conductor terms, with epsilon_E controlled after enlarging the exceptional set.

part (ii)output
Formalization warning: Corollary 2.2 mixes ordinary arithmetic gates with inter-universal input. The prime-choice, exceptional-set, BD-class, and torsion-image arguments should remain separate from the assumed Theorem 1.10 estimate, which itself is downstream of Corollary 3.12.
Sources: IUT IV, Corollary 2.2 Related: Theorem 1.10 Diophantine inequalities IUT IV output route initial theta data elliptic torsion input IUT IV overview

IUT IV Corollary 2.2: Lambda and j Gate

Corollary 2.2 starts in a very concrete setting: the thrice-punctured lambda-line over Q, interpreted through the Legendre equation. The compactly bounded subset KV must include the prime 2 in its support and satisfy a 2-adic boundedness condition under the j-invariant.

Open curve

X=P^1_Q, D={0,1,infty}, and U_X=X-D.

Legendre meaning

The coordinate is lambda in y^2=x(x-1)(x-lambda), giving a map to the elliptic moduli stack.

2-adic j condition

The image of the local set at the prime 2 under the j-invariant is bounded in Q_2.

GateSource contentWhy it matters
Lambda-line setup The application begins on P^1-{0,1,infty}, not on an arbitrary curve. GenEll later transfers this special case to general hyperbolic curves.
Compactly bounded subset KV subset U_X(Qbar) is compactly bounded and has support containing the prime 2. The compactly bounded domain controls the bounded-discrepancy constants.
(*j-inv) The 2-adic image under the j-invariant lies in a bounded subset of Q_2. This is the local input used to compare the all-prime and away-from-2 q-height functions.
Source: IUT IV, Corollary 2.2 setup Related: bounded-discrepancy heights Legendre reduction gate Corollary 2.3

IUT IV Corollary 2.2: Bounded-Discrepancy Heights

Part (i) of Corollary 2.2 translates q-parameter data on the lambda-line into the height language needed for Vojta. The equality is an equality of bounded-discrepancy classes on KV, not a pointwise identity of functions.

Away from 2

The (*j-inv) condition gives log(q_2) ~= log(q_all) on KV.

Height at infinity

The all-prime q-height is identified with the height from the divisor at infinity on the compactified elliptic moduli stack.

Vojta height

Pullback degree gives (1/6)ht_infty ~= ht_{omega_X(D)}.

ComparisonPaper contentAudit note
q_2 versus q_all The bounded 2-adic j-condition makes their difference bounded on KV. This depends on the compactly bounded local condition, not on a global identity.
q_all versus ht_infty The q-height is identified with the moduli height at infinity up to bounded discrepancy. This is the passage from elliptic q-parameters to a moduli-height function.
ht_infty versus ht_{omega_X(D)} The pullback of infinity has degree 6 while omega_X(D) has degree 1. The factor 1/6 is structural and must remain visible.
Source: IUT IV, Corollary 2.2(i) Related: lambda/j gate bounded discrepancy C1/C2 output

IUT IV Corollary 2.2: Exceptional Sets

Corollary 2.2 repeatedly enlarges a finite exceptional set before applying Theorem 1.10. The construction controls automorphism exceptions, low-height cases, l-cyclic subgroup pathologies, and error-size failures, while tracking which constants are uniform and which depend on KV.

Automorphism exceptions

Points whose elliptic curves admit automorphisms of order greater than 2 are included in the finite exceptional set.

Low-height cases

If h^{1/2} is below the prime thresholds, the possible j-invariants are finite on KV.

Error control

Part (iii) enlarges to Exc_{epsilon,d} so that the curve-dependent epsilon_E is at most the desired epsilon.

Exception typeSource roleFormalization consequence
Automorphism order > 2 Excluded so the once-punctured elliptic curve admits the required core and no extra automorphism ambiguity remains. Model-construction gates should expose this finite removal explicitly.
Bounded-height finite cases Several estimates imply bounded log(q_all), hence finitely many possible j-invariants. The proof uses finiteness after height bounds; these are not discarded silently.
Uniform constants H_unif is independent of KV; C_K,H_K depend on KV. Uniformity is part of the theorem statement and must be represented in the quantifier order.
Source: IUT IV, Corollary 2.2(ii)-(iii) Related: prime/Galois gate C1/C2 output Corollary 2.3

IUT IV Corollary 2.2: Prime and Galois Gate

The proof of Corollary 2.2 chooses an auxiliary prime ell outside a finite prohibited set and then uses nonexistence of l-cyclic subgroups plus a bad multiplicative place to obtain the large Galois-image condition required by IUT I Definition 3.1.

Forbidden primes

The set A contains small primes, primes dividing nonzero local heights, and residue primes at large local height.

Choose ell

Proposition 2.1 gives ell notin A satisfying the lower and upper bounds recorded as (P1)-(P3).

Large image

After excluding the l-cyclic subgroup case and retaining a bad multiplicative place, GenEll gives an image containing SL_2(F_ell).

Proof markerSource contentDownstream use
(P1)-(P3) Bounds on ell, nondivisibility of local heights, and avoidance of residue primes with large local height. Supplies condition (C1) and keeps local bad-prime contributions controlled.
(P4) Exclude elliptic curves admitting an ell-cyclic subgroup scheme by enlarging the exceptional set. Prepares the large-image argument from GenEll.
(P5)-(P6) Ensure a bad multiplicative place not dividing 2ell, then obtain SL_2(F_ell) in the l-torsion image. This gives the Galois-theoretic part of the initial theta data conditions.
(P7) The resulting data satisfy IUT I Definition 3.1 and Theorem 1.10 hypotheses. This is the precise gate where the ordinary arithmetic setup enters IUT.
Source: IUT IV, proof of Corollary 2.2(ii), (P1)-(P7) Related: Proposition 2.1 initial theta data Theorem 1.10 hypotheses

IUT IV Corollary 2.2: C1 and C2 Output

Conditions (C1) and (C2) are the concrete output of Corollary 2.2(ii). Condition (C1) controls the auxiliary prime in terms of the q-height, while condition (C2) converts Theorem 1.10 into a bound for the all-prime q-height by log-different and log-conductor terms.

C1

(log(q_all))^{1/2} <= ell <= 10 delta (log(q_all))^{1/2} log(2 delta log(q_all)).

Theorem 1.10 input

Apply Theorem 1.10 to the initial theta data obtained at gate (P7).

C2

(1/6)log(q_all) is bounded by (1+epsilon_E)(log-diff+log-cond)+C_K.

OutputSource contentWhat to preserve
epsilon_E Defined from delta, log(q_all), and logarithmic factors; it depends on the curve. Do not replace it by a generic small parameter before applying part (iii).
Different/conductor bridge log-diff_X(x_E)=log(d_{Ftpd}) and log(f_{Ftpd}) <= log-cond_D(x_E) <= log(f_{Ftpd})+log(2ell). This identifies Theorem 1.10's field-divisor terms with GenEll's Diophantine functions.
Part (iii) After enlarging the exceptional set depending on epsilon, one has epsilon_E <= epsilon. This is the final step needed before Corollary 2.3's arbitrary epsilon statement.
Formalization checkpoint: C2 is the place where Theorem 1.10 is consumed. The proof should keep a visible input theorem for Theorem 1.10 rather than folding the IUT III/IV comparison into the ordinary Diophantine estimates.
Source: IUT IV, Corollary 2.2(ii)-(iii), conditions (C1),(C2) Related: Theorem 1.10 final bound exceptional sets Corollary 2.3

IUT IV Remark 2.2.1: Weight One-Half and RH Analogies

Remark 2.2.1 interprets the error term in Corollary 2.2(ii) as a weight-one-half phenomenon. Mochizuki explicitly compares the square-root term in the height estimate with classical formulations of the Riemann hypothesis and asks whether a future inter-universal analogue of the Mellin transform could relate the theory to zeta functions.

Simplified bound

With F_tpd=Q, write h=log(q_all) and delta=log-cond_D; the bound has an error term of size delta^{1/2} log(delta).

Weight interpretation

A logarithmic line-bundle height is assigned the weight of one Tate twist; square roots of such heights are therefore read as weight 1/2.

Arithmetic duality

The square-root balance is traced to the competition between an archimedean term decreasing with ell and a nonarchimedean term increasing with ell.

PointPaper contentWiki consequence
RH analogy The critical-line value s=1/2 is compared with square roots of logarithmic heights. This is an analogy about asymptotic form, not an asserted proof of RH.
Compactly bounded limitation Corollary 2.2 controls abc sums in the chosen compactly bounded subset KV. The wiki must keep the KV restriction visible before discussing uniform abc.
Inter-universal Mellin transform Mochizuki speculates about an extension relating the theory to the Riemann zeta function. This belongs on an analogy page, not in the proof dependency chain.
Canonical number The paper asks whether a RH-type inequality could be viewed as an ABC inequality restricted to a suitable abstract canonical abc sum. This is a future-direction heuristic and should not be promoted to a theorem.
Source: IUT IV, Remark 2.2.1 Related: C1/C2 output semi-twist analogy p-adic Teichmuller analogy

IUT IV Remark 2.2.2: Gaussian Semi-Twist

Remark 2.2.2 relates the weight-one-half interpretation to the Gaussian integral, scheme-theoretic Hodge-Arakelov theory, IUT, and the Riemann hypothesis. The common motif is a Tate semi-twist, heuristically represented by arithmetic analogues of sqrt(pi).

Weight 1/2 analogy cluster Gaussian, Hodge-Arakelov, IUT, and RH
PhenomenonPaper roleAudit note
Gaussian integral Serves as a concrete model for a Tate semi-twist. The analogy is explanatory; no Gaussian integral is used as a proof step in Corollary 2.2.
Hodge-Arakelov theory Presented as a scheme-theoretic representation of the same semi-twist motif. This links IUT II theta-function technology to the later estimate pages.
Quadratic forms The paper points to theta-series exponents, theta-group commutators, and the quadratic shape of the error term. These should be cross-linked to theta-value and multiradiality pages.
Source: IUT IV, Remark 2.2.2 Related: RH weight analogy theta values q^j^2 multiradiality

IUT IV Remark 2.2.3: Weak Uniform abc

Remark 2.2.3 says that if the degree parameter in Corollary 2.2(ii)-(iii) is allowed to vary, the estimates resemble a weak form of the uniform abc conjecture. The weakness is explicit: the estimates are restricted to compactly bounded subsets and the exceptional bounds depend on the degree.

Uniform-abc aspectWhat Corollary 2.2 givesWhy it is weak
Variable degree One may consider arbitrary positive integers d in the Corollary 2.2 context. Corollary 2.3 later fixes d for bounded-degree points.
Height control The C2 inequality controls log(q_all) by different and conductor terms. The control is stated on the selected compactly bounded set KV.
Exceptional sets Bounds for Exc_d and Exc_{epsilon,d} are available. Those bounds depend on d; this prevents a full uniform statement.
This page is intentionally conservative: Remark 2.2.3 is a comparison with uniform abc, not the final abc/Szpiro consequence route. The latter runs through Corollary 2.3 and its GenEll boundary.
Source: IUT IV, Remark 2.2.3 Related: C1/C2 output exceptional sets Corollary 2.3 statement

IUT IV Remark 2.2.4: p-adic Teichmuller Analogy

Remark 2.2.4 compares the gap between Corollary 2.2's compactly bounded arithmetic domains and a hypothetical canonical arithmetic measure with the gap between specialized canonical curves and full modular Frobenius liftings in p-adic Teichmuller theory.

Canonical number

The speculative object from Remark 2.2.1 is compared with modular Frobenius liftings in p-adic Teichmuller theory.

Log-link distortion

The log-link corresponds, under the analogy, to Frobenius distortion in positive characteristic.

From discrete to continuous

Letting ell grow is compared with passing from an ell-torsion discretization to a fuller continuous theory.

Analogy pointPaper contentWiki consequence
Perfect versus non-perfect bases The p-adic theory distinguishes canonical curves over perfect bases from the nonspecialized moduli-stack theory. This parallels the gap between compactly bounded KV and a putative arithmetic measure over all additions.
Prime number theorem term The * ell contribution is related to summing log-shell volumes over nonarchimedean primes. The estimate pages should keep prime-counting input separate from IUT comparison input.
GenEll bridge The gap from KV to broader algebraic-point domains is later bridged by GenEll Theorem 2.1. This prepares the reader for Corollary 2.3's dependency on GenEll.
Bernoulli/difference analogy The passage from discrete difference operators to continuous derivatives is linked to Bernoulli numbers and zeta values. This remains analogy material and should not be mixed with formal theorem dependencies.
Source: IUT IV, Remark 2.2.4 Related: canonical number analogy theta/log links GenEll boundary

IUT IV Corollary 2.3: Diophantine Inequalities

Corollary 2.3 is the stated Diophantine output of IUT IV. It takes the suitable-theta-data estimate from Corollary 2.2, applies the reduction theorem from GenEll, and states a bounded-discrepancy Vojta-type inequality for hyperbolic curves. In Mochizuki's presentation, the abc and Szpiro consequences are reached through this output route.

Corollary 2.3 dependency route Corollary 2.2 plus GenEll gives the Vojta-form inequality
Corollary 2.3 itemPaper contentWiki/formalization consequence
Curve Let X be a smooth, proper, geometrically connected curve over a number field, let D be a reduced divisor, and set U_X = X \\ D. The output is not only a lambda-line statement; GenEll transfers the lambda-line application to general hyperbolic curves.
Hyp. The line bundle omega_X(D) must have positive degree. This is the hyperbolic-curve condition in the statement. A formal page should keep the hyperbolicity hypothesis explicit rather than folding it into the word "curve".
Degree d The inequality is stated on U_X(Q)_{<=d}, the points defined over extensions of degree at most d. Bounded degree is part of the theorem's quantifier structure and must be represented separately from compact-boundedness.
BD bound The conclusion is an inequality of bounded-discrepancy classes: ht_{omega_X(D)} is bounded by (1+epsilon)(log-diff_X+log-cond_D), up to an additive bounded term. The conclusion is not pointwise equality; it is a lower bound for the difference between the right-hand side and the height.
GenEll The proof says the statement is exactly GenEll Theorem 2.1(i), so it suffices by GenEll to verify the lambda-line case of Theorem 2.1(ii). The wiki records this as a dependency boundary: Corollary 2.3 relies on GenEll plus Corollary 2.2, not on a fresh IUT construction.
Lambda case In the reduced case, one assumes X=P^1_Q, D={0,1,infty}, a compactly bounded KV whose support includes 2, and the (*j-inv) condition. This is exactly where Corollary 2.2 supplies the needed bounded-discrepancy class equality and C2 estimate.
Consequences Mochizuki identifies the Vojta statement for hyperbolic curves, abc, and Szpiro-type statements as consequences of this Diophantine inequality route. These consequences remain downstream of the Corollary 3.12 corridor through Theorem 1.10 and Corollary 2.2.

Hyperbolic input

The statement applies to U_X=X-D when deg(omega_X(D)) > 0. This includes the thrice-punctured projective line and the hyperbolic curves to which GenEll reduces the problem.

Cor. 2.3hyperbolic

Bounded-degree points

The quantifier d fixes the degree of the number fields over which the points are defined. The bounded-discrepancy constant may depend on the theorem's fixed parameters.

domainU_X(Q)_{<=d}

Bounded discrepancy

The notation records a comparison up to an additive bounded term. In Corollary 2.3, this means the right-hand side minus the height is bounded below on the bounded-degree domain.

BD classnot pointwise

GenEll boundary

The proof invokes the equivalence in GenEll Theorem 2.1. Therefore the IUT IV contribution at this stage is to verify the lambda-line statement via Corollary 2.2, then let GenEll propagate it.

GenEllreduction

Compact-bounded correction

Remark 2.3.1 clarifies that a compactly bounded subset of an open curve must have its local compact domains contained in the open complement, both archimedean and nonarchimedean.

Remark 2.3.1domain control

Coefficient one

Remark 2.3.2 says the coefficient 1 in the displayed Corollary 2.3 inequality cannot be improved in the framework under discussion, referring back to the asymptotic examples and the theta-power discussion near Corollary 3.12.

Remark 2.3.2sharpness

Faltings comparison

Remark 2.3.3 compares Corollary 2.3 with Faltings' proof of Mordell: Faltings stays scheme-theoretic and arithmetically holomorphic, whereas IUT claims to work by dismantling that structure into multiradial and mono-analytic data.

Remark 2.3.3Mordell

Bogomolov and Parshin

Remarks 2.3.4 and 2.3.5 compare the IUT output with Bogomolov's geometric Szpiro proof and Parshin's function-field Mordell proof, emphasizing the real-analytic versus holomorphic contrast.

Remarks 2.3.4-2.3.5analogies

abc and Szpiro output

The wiki treats abc and Szpiro as downstream consequences of the Vojta-form bounded-discrepancy inequality, not as separate proofs detached from the IUT III/IV corridor.

outputabc/Szpiro
Formalization warning: Corollary 2.3 has two distinct dependency layers. The inter-universal layer supplies Corollary 2.2 through Theorem 1.10 and Corollary 3.12; the propagation from the lambda-line statement to arbitrary hyperbolic curves is delegated to GenEll Theorem 2.1. These should be separate modules or theorem interfaces in a proof assistant.
Sources: IUT IV, Corollary 2.3 and Remarks 2.3.1-2.3.5 Related: suitable theta data Theorem 1.10 IUT IV output route Bogomolov-Szpiro analogy probabilistic Szpiro route Scholze-Stix and responses

IUT IV Corollary 2.3: Statement

Corollary 2.3 states the final Vojta-form Diophantine inequality of IUT IV: for a hyperbolic open curve U_X=X-D over a number field, points of bounded degree satisfy a bounded-discrepancy inequality comparing the height of omega_X(D) with log-different plus log-conductor.

Geometric input

X is smooth, proper, geometrically connected; D is reduced; deg omega_X(D)>0.

Arithmetic domain

The inequality is on U_X(Qbar)_{<=d}, points defined over extensions of degree at most d.

BD inequality

ht_{omega_X(D)} is bounded above, up to bounded discrepancy, by (1+epsilon)(log-diff_X+log-cond_D).

QuantifierSource contentFormalization consequence
d A positive integer bounding the degree of algebraic points. The bounded-discrepancy constant may depend on fixed degree data; the domain is not all algebraic points at once.
epsilon An arbitrary positive real number in the coefficient 1+epsilon. This is obtained by the exceptional-set refinement in Corollary 2.2(iii).
BD class The right-hand side minus the height is bounded below on the bounded-degree domain. The conclusion is a bounded-discrepancy inequality, not a literal pointwise formula.
Source: IUT IV, Corollary 2.3 statement Related: GenEll boundary Corollary 2.2 C1/C2 IUT IV output route

IUT IV Corollary 2.3: GenEll Boundary

The proof of Corollary 2.3 explicitly delegates the passage from the lambda-line case to general hyperbolic curves to GenEll Theorem 2.1. The IUT IV contribution is to verify the lambda-line case using Corollary 2.2.

GenEll equivalence

Mochizuki says the Corollary 2.3 statement coincides with GenEll Theorem 2.1(i).

Reduced case

It suffices to prove the lambda-line case P^1-{0,1,infty} with a compactly bounded KV.

Corollary 2.2 use

The equality of BD classes from Corollary 2.2(i) and the C2 estimate from Corollary 2.2(ii)-(iii) give the required inequality.

BoundaryPaper contentAudit consequence
General curve propagation Handled by GenEll Theorem 2.1, described elsewhere as a Belyi-map analytic-continuation route. This should be a separate imported theorem in a formalization, not hidden inside IUT IV.
Lambda-line verification Uses X=P^1_Q, D={0,1,infty}, compactly bounded KV, and the (*j-inv) condition. This is the exact place where Corollary 2.2 is consumed.
Fixed-degree note The (*j-inv) condition is vacuous in fixed-degree situations of GenEll Theorem 2.1(ii). The wiki should not overstate (*j-inv) as a global condition in the final Vojta statement.
Source: IUT IV, proof of Corollary 2.3 Related: lambda/j gate BD heights IUT IV output route

IUT IV Remark 2.3.1: GenEll Corrections

Remark 2.3.1 records corrections to GenEll and clarifies the convention for compactly bounded subsets of open curves. The most relevant wiki point is that compactly bounded local domains must lie inside the open curve, not only inside its compactification.

BD notation correction

The non-respective and respective items in GenEll Definition 1.2(ii) are reversed.

Height comparison correction

A displayed equality in the proof of GenEll Theorem 2.1 should be an inequality with an extra factor 2.

Open-curve compactness

For U subset X, local compact domains defining a compactly bounded subset of U(Qbar) must be contained in U locally.

CorrectionSource contentWiki consequence
BD direction The display in GenEll Definition 1.2(ii) has the two directions reversed. All wiki explanations of bounded discrepancy should state the direction explicitly.
Compactly bounded subset For open U, both archimedean and nonarchimedean compact domains must lie in U. This prevents accidentally allowing compact domains that meet the removed divisor.
Height inequality A GenEll height equality should be weakened to an inequality with a factor 2. The final Corollary 2.3 page must avoid overstating the imported GenEll comparison.
Source: IUT IV, Remark 2.3.1 Related: Corollary 2.3 statement compact-bounded correction card GenEll boundary

IUT IV Remark 2.3.2: Sharpness and N-th Powers

Remark 2.3.2 says that stronger estimates are not pursued and that the coefficient 1 in Corollary 2.3 cannot be improved in the framework under discussion. Mochizuki relates this to the failure of replacing the first power of the etale theta function by an N-th power in the Corollary 3.12 discussion.

Possible stronger estimates

The paper notes that sharper versions of estimates in Theorem 1.10 and Corollaries 2.2-2.3 are possible but outside scope.

Coefficient one

The coefficient 1 in the final inequality is asserted not to be improvable, citing examples and Remark 1.10.5.

N-th power warning

The remark points back to the Step (xi)/Corollary 3.12 discussion where replacing theta by an N-th power breaks the intended argument.

ThemePaper content3.12 relevance
Sharpness The coefficient 1 cannot be improved. Prevents reading the final inequality as a loose estimate that could be arbitrarily strengthened.
Theta powers Replacing the first power of the etale theta function by an N-th power for N>=2 breaks the theory. This is directly tied to the disputed Step (xi) / Corollary 3.12 corridor.
Isogeny height stability The global height under such isogeny-like operations changes only by relatively small discrepancy. Explains why the naive N-th power route cannot simply amplify the final inequality.
Source: IUT IV, Remark 2.3.2 Related: 3.12 tensor-power warning generalized theta links Corollary 2.3

IUT IV Remarks 2.3.3-2.3.5: Comparison Layer

Remarks 2.3.3 through 2.3.5 compare the IUT IV output with Faltings' proof of Mordell, Bogomolov's geometric Szpiro proof, and Parshin's function-field Mordell proof. These remarks are not new theorem steps, but they clarify Mochizuki's view of how IUT differs from scheme-theoretic and holomorphic methods.

Faltings comparison

Faltings is read as scheme-theoretic and arithmetically holomorphic; IUT is framed as multiradial and mono-analytic after dismantling this structure.

Bogomolov comparison

Bogomolov's geometric Szpiro proof is described as a useful analogue or guide for portions of IUT.

Parshin comparison

Parshin's function-field proof motivates the question whether IUT could avoid Belyi maps; Mochizuki's answer in the text is no.

RemarkPaper contentWiki use
2.3.3 Lists ingredients in Faltings and compares them to IUT ingredients: Kummer theory, Frobenioids, anabelian geometry, prime-number estimates, and cyclotomic rigidity. Provides a structured comparison page between classical scheme-theoretic Mordell/Szpiro methods and IUT.
2.3.4 Describes Bogomolov's geometric Szpiro proof as an arithmetic analogue or blueprint for aspects of IUT. Links the wiki's Bogomolov-Szpiro analogy page to the source IUT IV remark.
2.3.5 Compares Parshin's function-field Mordell proof and says the number-field IUT route still needs Belyi maps as in GenEll. Keeps the GenEll/Belyi dependency visible in the application layer.
Scope note: these remarks are interpretive source material. They should guide orientation pages, but formal theorem pages should still cite the exact proposition, corollary, or GenEll theorem used in the proof.
Sources: IUT IV, Remarks 2.3.3-2.3.5 Related: Bogomolov-Szpiro analogy GenEll boundary Collas category view

IUT IV and Consequences

IUT IV converts the IUT III log-volume corridor into explicit estimates and then into Diophantine inequalities. Section 1 is the numerical engine: local different estimates, normalized nonarchimedean log-volume, archimedean metric estimates, prime-number-theorem input, weighted averages, arithmetic divisors, and finally Theorem 1.10 for theta-pilot objects. Section 2 applies this output to suitable initial theta data and then to Vojta, abc, and Szpiro-type consequences.

IUT IV estimate pipeline from the Corollary 3.12 corridor to Diophantine inequalities

Local estimate layer

Propositions 1.1-1.5 estimate tensor products of local rings, different ideals, p-adic logarithms, normalized nonarchimedean log-volume, and archimedean metric containers. This is the local numerical substrate under the global theta-pilot estimate.

Props. 1.1-1.5local fields

Averaging layer

Proposition 1.6 supplies the prime-number-theorem estimate used to sum over primes in the explicit computation, while Proposition 1.7 supplies the weighted-average formalism for packet-normalized log-volumes.

Props. 1.6-1.7global sums

Arithmetic divisors

Definition 1.9 packages finite and archimedean valuation data as arithmetic divisors. The theorem then expresses the q-parameter and theta-pilot estimates in terms of degrees, supports, and height-like quantities.

Definition 1.9heights

Theorem 1.10

Theorem 1.10 estimates the procession-normalized mono-analytic log-volume of the holomorphic hull attached to theta-pilot objects. It is the direct numerical continuation of IUT III's Corollary 3.12 setting.

theta-pilotlog-volume

Suitable initial theta data

Corollary 2.2 constructs initial theta data from points on the thrice punctured projective line, outside controlled exceptional loci, so that the hypotheses needed for Theorem 1.10 are available in Diophantine applications.

Corollary 2.2application input

Diophantine inequalities

Corollary 2.3 applies the estimate to Vojta-type inequalities for hyperbolic curves; the IUT series identifies abc and Szpiro-type statements as consequences through this route.

Corollary 2.3Vojta/abc/Szpiro
IUT IV stepRole in the wiki mapNearby concept pages
Propositions 1.1-1.5 Local estimates for differents, logarithms, and nonarchimedean/archimedean log-volume containers. local estimate route, different estimates, normalized log-volume
Propositions 1.6-1.7 Analytic and averaging input used to assemble local estimates into global bounds. prime/averaging route, prime number theorem input, weighted averages
Definition 1.9 and Theorem 1.10 Arithmetic divisors and the explicit theta-pilot log-volume estimate. elliptic torsion input, arithmetic divisor route, arithmetic divisors, theta-pilot estimate
Corollaries 2.2-2.3 Construction of suitable initial theta data and extraction of Diophantine inequalities. suitable initial theta data, Diophantine inequalities, lambda-line setup, prime choice, GenEll reduction
Section 3 Set-theoretic language of species, mutations, and copies used to describe inter-universal movement. species, mutation, set-theoretic copies
Reading rule: IUT IV does not replace the disputed Corollary 3.12 corridor. It assumes the earlier log-volume comparison machinery and computes its numerical consequences. A faithful wiki therefore keeps the estimate pipeline linked back to Corollary 3.12 rather than presenting the Diophantine inequalities as independent elementary estimates.
Sources: IUT IV Explicit Estimates WRAP version Related: IUT IV atlas tensor/log-volume corridor source library

IUT IV Output Route

IUT IV Section 2 is the application layer. It converts the explicit theta-pilot estimate of Theorem 1.10 into bounded-discrepancy Diophantine inequalities by constructing suitable initial theta data and then invoking the reduction theorem from GenEll.

Application route IUT III comparison data to Diophantine inequalities
ItemIUT IV roleWiki status
Corollary 2.2 Constructs initial theta data satisfying the hypotheses of Theorem 1.10 for the application setting. standalone page
Large prime and torsion image conditions Use the hypotheses on the lambda-line point to obtain the needed bad-place and Galois-image input. expanded below
Corollary 2.3 Transfers the output to bounded-discrepancy Vojta-type inequalities for hyperbolic curves. standalone page
GenEll reduction Reduces the general hyperbolic-curve statement to the thrice-punctured projective line setting. expanded below

Corollary 2.2 input package

Corollary 2.2 starts from a point in the lambda-line setting and, after excluding controlled exceptional cases, chooses data that form initial theta data in the sense of IUT I Definition 3.1. This is the bridge from explicit Diophantine input to the hypotheses required by Theorem 1.10.

IUT IV Cor. 2.2initial theta data

Torsion-image condition

The application checks that the Galois image on the selected l-torsion contains the required SL_2(F_l)-type subgroup. In IUT IV this is not a new inter-universal construction; it is an arithmetic input verified by the prime-choice and general-position hypotheses.

large lGenEll input

Epsilon estimate

After the theta-pilot estimate is applied, IUT IV rewrites the bound using a small error term of the form epsilon_E = (60 delta)^2 h^{-1/2} log(2 delta h). Enlarging the exceptional set allows the application to assume this error term is at most one.

error termheight h

BD-class inequality

Corollary 2.3 states the output as a bounded-discrepancy class inequality: the log-canonical height attached to omega_X(D) is bounded, up to 1+epsilon and an additive bounded term, by the log-different and log-conductor terms on points of bounded degree.

IUT IV Cor. 2.3Vojta form

Compactly bounded subsets

The application is stated on compactly bounded subsets of the open curve. Locally, the selected compact domains must lie inside the open complement rather than crossing the removed divisor; this is part of how the bounded-discrepancy formulation controls the application locus.

BD-class domainlocal condition

GenEll reduction route

The proof of Corollary 2.3 invokes the reduction theorem from GenEll: it is enough to treat the thrice-punctured projective line P^1 - {0,1,infty} with bounded-degree points satisfying the compact-boundedness and j-invariant conditions used by Corollary 2.2.

GenEll Thm. 2.1lambda line

abc and Szpiro special cases

IUT IV presents the Vojta-type inequality as the general output. The familiar abc and Szpiro consequences are treated as special cases reached through the same bounded-discrepancy inequality rather than as separate estimates with independent input.

abcSzpiro

Weight-one-half term

Mochizuki highlights that the application estimate has a square-root flavor, schematically a main term plus a delta^{1/2} log(delta)-type contribution. The wiki records this as an output-side feature, not as a substitute for the internal theta-link comparison.

Remark layerasymptotic shape

Belyi and analytic continuation

The reduction from general hyperbolic curves to the lambda-line case is mediated by the arithmetic-analytic continuation machinery cited from GenEll. For the wiki this marks a dependency boundary: the IUT IV output page records the use of that theorem and links the general statement back to the lambda-line calculation.

Belyi mapsdependency
Verification note: this page deliberately separates the downstream Diophantine application from the disputed comparison corridor. The output route uses Corollary 3.12 and Theorem 1.10 as inputs; it is not an alternate proof of the multiradial theta comparison.
Sources: IUT IV Related: IUT IV IUT IV atlas probabilistic Szpiro route tensor/log-volume corridor

Explicit Estimates: μ6 Evaluation Points

The Explicit Estimates paper modifies the etale-theta-function input by replacing the original 2-torsion evaluation points with 6-torsion evaluation points. The purpose is narrow and technical: make the theta-function normalization work at arbitrary bad places, including bad places above 2.

Why the μ6 modification is introduced Explicit Estimates, Introduction and §3
Source itemPaper contentWiki consequence
Original issue 2-torsion evaluation points do not function properly at bad places above 2. Keep the p=2 obstruction visible in the output-layer map.
Porowski observation The two desired conditions for n-torsion evaluation points hold exactly for n=6. Explain why the modification is μ6, not an arbitrary torsion replacement.
μ6 standard type Definition 3.3 introduces etale theta functions normalized at 6-torsion points. Use as a downstream modification of theta evaluation, not as a new 3.12 proof step.
Sources: Explicit Estimates, Introduction and §3 WRAP version Related: theta evaluation points IUT IV Theorem 1.10 μ6 Theorem 5.1

Explicit Estimates: μ6 Theorem 5.1

Theorem 5.1 is the μ6 analogue of IUT IV Theorem 1.10. It takes the modified theta-function setup and produces a numerically effective theta-pilot log-volume estimate, then Corollary 5.2 uses it to build suitable μ6 initial theta data without the earlier p=2 compactness restrictions.

§1 height input

Faltings, toric, and Weil-height comparisons; the mono-complex product-formula feature appears later.

§2 numerical input

Explicit estimates for arithmetic elliptic curves and prime-number-theorem choices.

§3-4 μ6 theta input

6-torsion evaluation points and minor IUT I-III modifications remove the odd-residue restriction.

§5 output

Theorem 5.1 and Corollary 5.2 provide effective θ-pilot and suitable-data estimates.

ResultRoleBoundary
Theorem 5.1 μ6 version of IUT IV Theorem 1.10, with an explicit CTheta-type bound under a large-prime hypothesis. Downstream of the IUT III comparison corridor.
Corollary 5.2 μ6 version of IUT IV Corollary 2.2; constructs effective suitable initial theta data. Uses Theorem 5.1 plus §1-§2 numerical estimates.
p=2 removal Eliminates the earlier CBS1/CBS2 restrictions tied to the nonarchimedean place 2. Does not alter the logical status of Corollary 3.12.
The page should be read as the effective-output branch of IUT IV: it strengthens numerical applicability if the IUT III/IUT IV source pipeline is accepted.
Sources: Explicit Estimates §5 Related: μ6 evaluation points IUT IV output route Corollary 3.12 corridor

Explicit ABC/Szpiro over Mono-Complex Fields

Theorem 5.3 applies Corollary 5.2 to mono-complex number fields, meaning Q or an imaginary quadratic field. The source then derives explicit ABC/Szpiro inequalities with very large constants; Theorem 5.4 specializes this to an effective Szpiro-style inequality over Q.

Effective output route Explicit Estimates, Theorems 5.3-5.4
ObjectPaper contentWiki handling
Mono-complex field A number field with exactly one archimedean place: Q or an imaginary quadratic field. Explain why the archimedean/product-formula step is special.
Theorem 5.3 Bounds the nonarchimedean height of the associated j-invariant and the height product H_L(a,b,c). Use as an explicit downstream ABC/Szpiro page.
Theorem 5.4 Specializes to nonzero coprime integers a+b+c=0 and an explicit Szpiro-style radical bound. Keep separate from IUT IV Corollary 2.3's non-effective output.
The source itself notes that the constants are astronomically large; the wiki should present them as effective constants, not as numerically sharp estimates.
Sources: Explicit Estimates, Theorems 5.3-5.4 Related: μ6 Theorem 5.1 IUT IV output route Fermat application

Explicit Fermat Application

Corollary 5.8 applies the effective ABC/Szpiro inequality to Fermat-type equations. The paper states that no positive integer solution exists for prime exponents p > 1.615 * 10^14, and then combines this with external classical and computational estimates for first- and second-case Fermat statements.

Elementary lower bound

Lemmas 5.5-5.7 give lower bounds on a hypothetical solution size.

Apply Theorem 5.3

Set (a,b,c)=(x^p,y^p,-z^p) over Q with epsilon=1.

Contradict the threshold

The lower and upper bounds conflict once p > 1.615 * 10^14.

External inputs

Coppersmith, Vandiver, and Mihailescu-Rassias estimates are cited for broader FLT consequences.

ItemSource roleBoundary
Corollary 5.8 States the explicit prime-exponent no-solution result for p > 1.615 * 10^14. Depends on Theorem 5.3 and the IUT pipeline behind it.
Remarks 5.8.1-5.8.3 Combine the result with Coppersmith, Vandiver, and Mihailescu-Rassias estimates. These are application-layer combinations, not new IUT constructions.
Corollary 5.9 Applies Theorem 5.4 to a generalized Fermat-type equation. Useful for the output map, peripheral to the Corollary 3.12 dispute.
Sources: Explicit Estimates, Corollaries 5.8-5.9 Related: explicit ABC/Szpiro IUT IV output route probabilistic Szpiro route

Probabilistic Szpiro Route

Dupuy and Hilado recast the output of Mochizuki's Corollary 3.12 as a probabilistic inequality for random measurable sets attached to multiradial theta-pilot hulls. Their paper is useful for this wiki because it separates two layers: the assumed IUT input, namely Corollary 3.12 plus an archimedean claim, and the subsequent elementary bounding route toward Szpiro-type inequalities.

Conditional downstream route from Corollary 3.12 to Szpiro-style estimates

Assumed input

The paper explicitly assumes Mochizuki's Corollary 3.12 and an additional archimedean assertion labeled Claim 5.0.1. The wiki therefore treats the paper as a conditional downstream analysis, not as a source for resolving the Corollary 3.12 dispute.

conditionalCor. 3.12

Field-of-moduli theta data

The elliptic curve is required to sit in initial theta data built from the field of moduli. This specializes the general IUT input to a form where the l-division field, bad multiplicative places, and valuation section can be used in explicit ramification averages.

Definition 1.0.1initial theta data

Random measurable sets

The multiradial theta-pilot hull is represented through random measurable sets indexed by places and procession labels. The main probabilistic inequality bounds the normalized q-degree by expected logarithmic radii of these hull components.
