Theorem 7.5.1expectation

Hull radius estimate

Sections 2-6 estimate the radius of a polydisc containing a stand-in for the multiradial hull. The bound uses tensor-product integral closures, different orders, p-adic logarithm estimates, archimedean log-shell estimates, and automorphisms modeling the indeterminacy action.

Sections 2-6hulls

Probabilistic Szpiro

Theorem 7.12.1 derives a Szpiro-type inequality whose right side records the average different, average ramification degree, unramified probability, and an archimedean contribution. The coefficient on the minimal discriminant is 1/6 + epsilon_l, with epsilon_l tending to zero as l grows.

Theorem 7.12.1average data

Baby Szpiro

Theorem 7.13.1 gives a coarser consequence that depends only on the degree and discriminant of the relevant division field. Its value in the wiki is expository: it shows how the probabilistic inequality collapses to a simpler but weaker field-invariant bound.

Theorem 7.13.1division field

Explicit Szpiro

Section 8 refines the estimates by separating infinite, large finite, and small finite primes. The final theorem gives an explicit bound for the minimal discriminant in terms of conductor and field discriminant, with large numerical constants.

Theorem 8.2.1explicit constants

Ramification breakdown

The explicit argument divides rational primes by size relative to a bound depending on l and the field-of-moduli degree. This isolates where tame, wild, and small-prime behavior must be controlled in the conductor/discriminant comparison.

Section 8.1ramification

Formalization use

For Lean work, this paper suggests a clean downstream module boundary: assume a typed Corollary 3.12 inequality and archimedean input, then formalize the probabilistic and local-field estimates separately. Failure or success in this downstream layer would not by itself decide the IUT comparison step.

module boundaryLean
Sources: Dupuy-Hilado probabilistic Szpiro Dupuy-Hilado initial theta data Related: IUT IV consequences Corollary 3.12 corridor initial theta data

IUT IV §3: ZFCG Universes

IUT IV Section 3 begins by fixing the set-theoretic environment for species. Mochizuki works with ZFC-models and allows Grothendieck universes satisfying the additional universe-existence axiom used by the Grothendieck school. This is the foundational stage on which species can be interpreted in different models without being reduced to one chosen category of outputs.

Universe ladder IUT IV §3 set-theoretic staging for species
Source itemContentWiki consequence
ZFC-model A model consists of sets with a membership relation satisfying standard ZFC axioms. Species pages should distinguish the rule from its interpretation in one model.
Universe axioms A Grothendieck universe is transitive, contains natural numbers, and is closed under powers, unions, and small surjective images. Universe changes are not informal metaphors; they are part of the formal staging.
ZFCG axiom Given any set, a universe containing it exists; consequently, a family of universes can be placed inside a larger universe. This supports the comparison of species-data across enlarged ambient contexts.
Absoluteness proviso Set-theoretic formulas used below are required to be absolute under universe enlargement. This is the technical guardrail behind model-free species descriptions.
Source: IUT IV, Section 3 preamble and Definition 3.1 Related: 0/1-species model-free specimens Collas universes/species

IUT IV Definition 3.1: 0/1-Species Data

Definition 3.1 defines a species in two layers. A 0-species gives formulas for object data. A 1-species gives formulas for morphism data, composition, associativity, and identities. The pair is what IUT IV calls a species.

0-species

A set-theoretic formula Phi_0(E) defines the permitted ordered collections of sets called 0-specimens.

1-species

Formulas Phi_1(E,E',F) and Phi_{1 o 1} define arrows and their composites.

Species

A species is the pair (S_0,S_1), with specimens interpreted as species-objects and species-morphisms.

Definition componentSource conditionFormal reading
0-specimen A concrete ordered collection of sets in a ZFC-model satisfying the object formula. An object is an interpreted output of a formula, not an isolated label.
1-specimen A concrete ordered collection of sets satisfying the morphism formula between two 0-specimens. Morphisms carry their domain/codomain data and are also formula outputs.
Composite For composable 1-specimens, the composite exists uniquely and composition is associative. The species data must encode category-like composition explicitly.
Identity Each 0-specimen has a unique identity 1-specimen with the usual unit property. Definition 3.1 builds a category after interpretation, rather than assuming one beforehand.
Smallness 0-smallness and 1-smallness assert that relevant specimens form sets in any ZFC-model. Size conditions should be visible when formalizing species categories.
This page is deliberately mechanical. It is the source-level reason the wiki treats IUT concepts as typed routes: the formula defining the kind of object matters as much as any one displayed specimen.
Source: IUT IV, Definition 3.1(i)-(iv) Related: species versus categories mutation histories Collas category view

IUT IV Definition 3.1: Model-Free Specimens

A model-free specimen is an interpreted species specimen described by a formula that gives the same specimen after passing from one universe to a larger universe. This is one precise IUT IV mechanism for preventing an object from being tied to an arbitrary representative inside a single set-theoretic model.

LayerSource statementAudit consequence
Formula independence The description must be independent of the ZFC-model in the sense of compatibility for universes V_1 in V_2. Do not identify model-free with merely canonical-sounding prose; it is a formula-stability condition.
Program view Remark 3.1.2 says the essence of a species lies in rules or deterministic algorithms, not one output set. Wiki and Lean interfaces should expose construction rules where possible.
Choice warning Remark 3.1.3 warns that conventional descriptions must not depend on arbitrary choices not encoded by formulas. This is a direct anti-drift rule for formalization and exposition.
Inter-universal meaning Remark 3.1.4 ties formula-defined species to invariance under universe extension. The inter-universal language here is a type/formula discipline, not a license to identify all copies.

Good use

Use model-free terminology when the same set-theoretic formula genuinely determines the same specimen after universe enlargement.

formulaabsolute

Bad use

Do not use it for an arbitrary chosen representative of a species-object, even if all representatives are isomorphic in a later category.

choicenot enough

Formalization use

A Lean development can initially expose model-free status as a hypothesis, but the long-term target is a construction or theorem proving formula stability.

Leanaudit hook
Source: IUT IV, Definition 3.1(iii) and Remarks 3.1.2-3.1.4 Related: ZFCG universes species versus categories LeanForm species/mutations

IUT IV Example 3.2: Category Species

Example 3.2 is the first concrete species example in IUT IV §3. The species has small categories as 0-specimens and isomorphism classes of covariant functors as 1-specimens. Species-isomorphisms in this example correspond to equivalences of categories up to isomorphism class.

Dictionary entrySource descriptionWhy it matters
0-species The notion of a small category, represented set-theoretically by arrows and composition relations. Categories themselves become specimens of a species, not the ambient language.
1-species Isomorphism classes of covariant functors between two small categories. The 1-morphism layer is intentionally quotienting functors by isomorphism.
0-specimen A particular small category in a particular ZFC-model. This is one model's output of the category-species formula.
1-specimen A particular isomorphism class of functors in a particular ZFC-model. The wiki should not silently replace this by an individual selected functor.
Species-isomorphism Corresponds to an isomorphism class of equivalences of categories in the usual sense. This clarifies why category geometry is natural for IUT's reconstruction language.
Remark 3.2.1 says one could introduce 2-species using functor isomorphisms, but Mochizuki avoids that extra level and works with functors up to isomorphism.
Source: IUT IV, Example 3.2 and Remark 3.2.1 Related: 0/1-species species versus categories Collas category view

IUT IV Definition 3.3: Mutations

A mutation is the species-level analogue of a functor. It is not just a mapping between already chosen categories: it is a pair of set-theoretic formulas assigning outputs to species-objects and species-morphisms, with compatibility for composites and identities.

Object formula

Psi_0(E,Ebar) assigns a unique output species-object M(E) to each input species-object.

Morphism formula

Psi_1(E,E',F,Fbar) assigns M(F):M(E)->M(E') to each input species-morphism.

Functor after interpretation

In a fixed ZFC-model, the mutation determines an ordinary functor between the categories determined by the two species.

ConditionDefinition 3.3(i)Audit consequence
Uniqueness on objects For each specimen E, the object formula has a unique output. A formal interface should not allow multiple untracked output objects.
Uniqueness on morphisms For each species-morphism F:E->E', the morphism formula has a unique output. Transport of morphisms is part of the mutation, not post-hoc bookkeeping.
Composition The assignment is compatible with composites. Mutation data must preserve the categorical structure produced by species.
Identity Identity species-morphisms are sent to identity species-morphisms. This is the exact point where a mutation becomes functorial after interpretation.
Source: IUT IV, Definition 3.3(i) Related: mutation morphisms mutation histories/cores species and mutations overview

IUT IV Definition 3.3: Mutation Morphisms and Equivalences

Definition 3.3(ii)-(iii) adds the analogue of natural transformations and equivalences. A morphism of mutations assigns a comparison morphism Z(E):M(E)->M'(E) naturally in species-morphisms. A mutation-equivalence has a quasi-inverse up to isomorphism of mutations.

ObjectDefinitionCategory-level analogue
Morphism of mutations A formula assigning Z(E):M(E)->M'(E) for every species-object E. Natural transformation after choosing a ZFC-model.
Naturality equation M'(F) o Z(E_1) = Z(E_2) o M(F) for each F:E_1->E_2. Commutative naturality square.
Isomorphism of mutations Every component Z(E) is a species-isomorphism. Natural isomorphism.
Mutation-equivalence A mutation with a mutation quasi-inverse, up to isomorphism of mutation composites with identities. Equivalence of categories, but lifted to formula-defined species transformations.
Fully-faithful effect A mutation-equivalence gives bijections between collections of species-isomorphisms. This is the species-level form of the isomorphism comparison used later in anabelian examples.
This page is central for formalization: a comparison should specify whether it is merely a morphism of mutations, an isomorphism of mutations, or a full mutation-equivalence. These are different source notions.
Source: IUT IV, Definition 3.3(ii)-(iii) Related: mutations absolute anabelian mutation Collas audit guide

IUT IV Remark 3.3.1: Simulated Membership Loops

Remark 3.3.1 explains a foundational motivation for species. Actual membership loops are forbidden by the axiom of foundation, but species-isomorphisms allow one to relate objects occurring at different levels of the set-theoretic hierarchy without identifying them as the same set. This is one of the formal meanings of moving across universes without collapsing histories.

Foundation blocks loops; species simulate comparisons Remark 3.3.1(i)-(iii)
Remark itemSource contentWiki consequence
3.3.1(i) Species-isomorphisms compare species-objects at different membership levels, roughly simulating membership loops without violating foundation. Do not collapse distinct copies into equality; record the authorized species-isomorphism.
3.3.1(ii) Simpler species, especially category species, make loop construction easier than more structured species such as schemes. This explains the move toward geometry of categories and absolute anabelian geometry.
3.3.1(iii) Cores capture portions left unaltered by mutations and help describe non-coric observables. Later theta/log comparison pages must distinguish coric readout from altered non-coric data.
Source: IUT IV, Remark 3.3.1 Related: category species mutation histories/cores Collas universes/species

IUT IV Remark 3.3.2: Nested Local Systems

Remark 3.3.2 gives a motivating picture for successive mutation histories. Starting from local systems on a scheme, one can ask about local systems whose fibers are themselves schemes, then local systems on those fibers, and so on. Mochizuki presents this as a naive route to systems of nested local systems, abstracted by iterated mutation histories.

Local systems on a scheme

The source begins from the ordinary idea of local systems on a connected scheme, without restricting values to modules over a finite ring.

Local systems of schemes

If fibers may be schemes isomorphic to the base, one may repeat the construction over the fibers themselves.

Successive mutation histories

The resulting nested-local-system picture is abstracted as successive iteration of a mutation-history.

ConceptSource roleFormalization consequence
Nested local systems A local system over a local system over a local system provides the motivating image. The wiki should treat mutation histories as structural, not decorative terminology.
Successive iteration Repeated application of a given mutation-history abstracts the nesting process. Iterated link/lattice pages should expose the indexing history.
Core A core is interpreted as an object invariant under the operations that generate the nested system. This is the conceptual source for invariant core objects in later log-theta pages.
Source: IUT IV, Remark 3.3.2 Related: mutation histories/cores bi-cores Hoshi link/indeterminacy route

IUT IV Definition 3.1: Species Versus Categories

IUT IV introduces species to formalize "types of mathematical objects" by set-theoretic formulas. A category determined in one ZFC-model is only a representation of such a type. The species carries the rules for producing objects and morphisms across universes, which is why it is stronger than one ordinary category of specimens.

Rules versus one model's output Definition 3.1 and Remarks 3.1.2-3.1.4
Source itemDefinition or warningWiki consequence
Definition 3.1(i)-(iii) A 0-species is a formula for object data. A 1-species supplies formula defined morphism data, composition, associativity, and identities. A species is the pair of these two layers. Do not describe a species as just "a category"; the formulas are part of the object.
Definition 3.1(iv) A species determines a category after interpretation inside a chosen ZFC-model. The category is output of the species program, not the whole program.
Remark 3.1.2 The essence of a species lies in the rules or programs, not in the particular output sets in one model. This is the formal counterpart of Collas's category-theoretic orientation.
Remark 3.1.3 Conventional descriptions are acceptable only when they avoid arbitrary choices not expressible by well-defined formulas. Formal wiki pages should mark which objects are canonical and which require choices.
Remark 3.1.4 Formula-defined species are immune to universe extension; this is the sense attached here to "inter-universal". Inter-universal movement is tracked at the rule/species level, not by one ambient set.
Sources: IUT IV, Definition 3.1 and Remarks 3.1.2-3.1.4 Related: Collas category view species and mutations set-theoretic copies

IUT IV Definition 3.3: Mutation Histories, Observables, and Cores

A mutation is the species-level analogue of a functor. A mutation history is an oriented graph of species and mutations. An observable reads such a history into a fixed species, while a core is an observable whose transition morphisms are isomorphisms. This is the formal source of the word coric in the later IUT architecture.

What survives a mutation history Definition 3.3 and Remark 3.3.1
Source itemContentIUT use
Definition 3.3(i)-(iii) Mutations, morphisms of mutations, and mutation-equivalences are formula-defined analogues of functors, natural transformations, and equivalences. Reconstruction data should be represented as transformations with history, not only as isomorphisms.
Definition 3.3(iv) A mutation history is an oriented graph with a species at each vertex and a mutation on each directed edge. The log-theta-lattice is naturally read as a mutation-history-shaped object.
Definition 3.3(v) An observable maps vertices into a fixed species. A covariant observable is a core if all edge comparison morphisms are isomorphisms. This is the formal counterpart of invariant, or coric, data.
Remark 3.3.1(i) Species-isomorphisms let one simulate membership loops across levels of the set-theoretic hierarchy without violating foundation. This explains why copies can be related without forcing them into one set-theoretic identity.
Remark 3.3.1(iii) Cores can be used to describe non-coric observables, i.e. portions altered by mutations, in terms of invariant portions. This is the species-level pattern behind coric containers for altered theta/log data.
Sources: IUT IV, Definition 3.3 and Remark 3.3.1 Related: vertical/horizontal coricity bi-cores of the log-theta-lattice Frobenius/etale mutation

IUT IV Example 3.6: Same-Species Parity of Source and Target

Example 3.6 explains the Frobenius mutation as the prototype for the log-theta-lattice. The construction of a target may be subordinate to the source, but the resulting source and target are still treated as objects of the same species. Mochizuki uses this as the formal model for treating the domain and codomain of log-links and Theta×mu_LGP links as Hodge theaters on a par, even when their construction is asymmetric.

Asymmetric construction, equal species status Example 3.6 and Remark 3.6.1
Source itemStatementWiki consequence
Example 3.6(i) For a reduced scheme S/F_p, S^(p) has the same topological space but structure sheaf of p-th powers. The etale site and perfection are formula-defined mutations. The example provides the formal template for altered structures with invariant cores.
Example 3.6(ii) The etale-site mutation and perfection mutation exhibit cores of the Frobenius mutation-history, while the natural map S -> S^(p) is generally non-coric. This separates invariant readout from non-coric structure transport.
Example 3.6(iii) The monoid version uses M -> pM, p-perfection, and a non-coric inclusion observable. This is the immediate combinatorial model for log/theta monoid deformation.
Introduction to §3 Although S^(p) is constructed from S, both lie in the same species; this models log- and Theta×mu_LGP-links between Theta±ellNF Hodge theaters. The source and target of an IUT link must not be collapsed into one conventional scheme.
This page is a foundations guardrail. Same-species status does not mean equality of histories; it means that after the mutation has been formed, the source and target are read as specimens of the same type.
Sources: IUT IV, Introduction to §3 and Example 3.6 Related: Frobenius/etale mutation coric/non-coric principle Collas category view log-theta lattice

Species, Mutations, and Inter-universal Formalism

IUT IV Section 3 introduces species to make explicit a kind of mathematical bookkeeping that is often left informal: a "type of object" is described by set-theoretic formulas, interpreted in ZFC-models, rather than by one preferred set of representatives. Mutations are formula-defined transformations between species. Mutation histories, observables, and cores then describe what changes and what remains invariant when the theory moves through distinct copies.

Foundations cluster

Start with ZFCG universes, 0/1-species, model-free specimens, and the program-not-output rule. This cluster explains why species are formula-governed notions rather than one category of representatives.

universes 0/1-species model-free programs

Category and mutation cluster

Then read category species, mutations, mutation morphisms, and mutation-equivalences. These pages give the functor-like language used for reconstruction without replacing species-level formulas by final objects.

category species mutations morphisms equivalences

History and coricity cluster

Finally read membership loops, nested local systems, Frobenius examples, and coric/non-coric observables. This is the bridge from §3 foundations to the log-theta-lattice and the Corollary 3.12 comparison.

histories loops same species coric/non-coric
Species-level movement rules, specimens, mutations, and cores
IUT IV Section 3 itemPaper contentWiki/formalization consequence
ZFC-models and universes Section 3 begins by working with ZFC-models and Grothendieck universes. The additional ZFCG existence axiom says that every set is contained in some universe; this implies that indexed families of universes can be placed inside a larger universe. Universe enlargement is explicit background for the formalism, not an informal metaphor. A formal model should state which construction is invariant under universe extension.
Formula absoluteness The set-theoretic formulas used for species are required to be absolute: validity for data in one universe agrees with validity after interpreting the same data in a larger universe. This is the technical gate behind model-free specimens and behind using the same construction across alien universes.
Definition 3.1 A 0-species is a formula for object data; a 1-species adds formula-defined morphism data, composition, associativity, and identities; a species is the pair of object and morphism species. "Type of object" in this page means formula-governed object and morphism data, not a single category chosen in one set-theoretic universe.
Remarks 3.1.2-3.1.4 The essence of a species is the rule or program given by formulas, not the particular sets output in a ZFC-model. This is compared with schemes versus point sets and abstract groups versus matrix representations. The wiki should avoid collapsing species-level assertions into statements about one representative object.
Definition 3.3 Mutations, morphisms of mutations, mutation-equivalences, mutation-histories, observables, and cores are defined by set-theoretic formulas. In a fixed model, mutations behave like functors and morphisms of mutations like natural transformations. A proof-assistant interface should track the history and the observable or core that authorizes comparison, not merely the final isomorphism.
Remark 3.3.1 Species allow comparisons between objects occurring at different levels of the membership hierarchy, thereby simulating membership loops without violating the axiom of foundation. This explains why species are used to compare outputs of iterated constructions without identifying their set-theoretic histories.

ZFCG universe assumption

Section 3 distinguishes ordinary ZFC-models from ZFCG-models satisfying the Grothendieck-school universe existence axiom. This lets one enlarge to a universe containing a whole indexed family of smaller universes.

Section 3 setupuniverses

0-species and 1-species

Definition 3.1 starts with a 0-species: a set-theoretic formula for object data. A 1-species adds formula-defined morphism data, unique composites, associativity, and identities. A species is the pair consisting of these object and morphism formulas.

Definition 3.1(i)-(iii)formulas

Programs, not output data

Remark 3.1.2 says the point of a species is the formula-program that constructs specimens, not the particular sets obtained in one model. This is the species-level analogue of using equations or functors instead of one chosen point set.

Remark 3.1.2rules

Formula absoluteness

The formulas used in §3 are required to be absolute under universe extension: if the relevant data lies in a universe, interpreting the same formula in a larger universe gives the same validity. This is the technical discipline behind model-free specimens.

Definition 3.1 setupuniverses

Choice-free canonicality

Remark 3.1.3 warns that conventional descriptions are acceptable only when they do not depend on arbitrary choices unavailable as set-theoretic formulas. Formula-defined species give a way to measure deviation from a canonical norm.

Remark 3.1.3canonicality

Species

A species is a pair consisting of a 0-species of objects and a 1-species of morphisms acting on them. Each side is given by formulas whose specimens are realized inside a chosen ZFC-model.

Definition 3.1objects/morphisms

Model-free specimens

A model-free specimen is described by a formula that gives the same specimen when interpreted after passing to a larger universe. This is the formal meaning of "inter-universal" in IUT IV Section 3.

Remarks 3.1.2-3.1.4ZFCG

Mutations

A mutation is a formula-defined transformation from one species to another, compatible with identities and composites. In a fixed ZFC-model it behaves like a functor between the corresponding categories.

Definition 3.3(i)functorial

Morphisms of mutations

A morphism of mutations is a formula that assigns, to each source object, a morphism between the two mutated outputs, compatibly with every source morphism. In a fixed ZFC-model it is the species-theoretic analogue of a natural transformation.

Definition 3.3(ii)natural transformation

Mutation equivalence

A mutation-equivalence has a mutation quasi-inverse up to isomorphism of mutations. Mochizuki uses this to express reconstruction-style equivalence without choosing one privileged concrete representative.

Definition 3.3(iii)quasi-inverse

Mutation histories

A mutation history is an oriented graph whose vertices carry species and whose edges carry mutations. This is the species-theoretic analogue of a diagram of categories.

Definition 3.3(iv)graph

Observables and cores

An observable reads a mutation history into a fixed target species. A core is a covariant observable whose transition morphisms are isomorphisms, so it records an invariant portion of the history.

Definition 3.3(v)coricity

Category species example

Example 3.2 takes small categories as species-objects and isomorphism classes of functors as species-morphisms. This gives the first template for reading ordinary category-level constructions as species-level data.

Next example: fundamental groups as mutations

Example 3.2categories

Absolute anabelian species

Example 3.5 formulates anabelian reconstruction as a mutation from universal profinite etale-cover data to topological-group data, together with a quasi-inverse mutation back to schemes. This records reconstruction algorithmically, not only as a fully faithful comparison.

Open the Example 3.5 page

Example 3.5anabelian

Frobenius and etale-site cores

Example 3.6 treats the etale site and perfection as cores of a Frobenius mutation-history. The natural Frobenius morphism itself is non-coric, but the perfection core relates this non-coric observable to invariant data.

The monoid version, based on multiplication by p and p-perfection, is the formal model for the coric/non-coric distinction later used to read log-links and theta-links.

Open the Example 3.6 page

Example 3.6coric/non-coric

Simulated membership loops

Remark 3.3.1 explains that species-isomorphisms allow one to compare objects occurring at different levels of the membership hierarchy without violating foundation. This is one reason the framework is useful for iterated mutation histories.

Remark 3.3.1foundation

Cores versus non-coric observables

Remark 3.3.1 explains why cores matter: mutations may alter mathematical structure, but a core records the invariant portion that can be used to describe altered non-coric observables.

Remark 3.3.1coricity
Example in IUT IVWhat it illustratesWiki link
0/1-species Object and morphism formulas with unique composites, identities, and associativity. 0/1-species
Formula absoluteness Descriptions must survive universe extension without depending on arbitrary choices. formula absoluteness
Topological spaces and fundamental groups The fundamental group can be treated as a mutation from universal-cover data to group data. fundamental group mutation
Absolute anabelian geometry Anabelian reconstruction is expressed as a mutation quasi-inverse to the fundamental-group mutation. absolute anabelian mutation
Frobenius and etale sites The etale site and perfection are cores of Frobenius-type mutation histories. Frobenius/etale mutation
Monoid Frobenius Coric versus non-coric observables motivate the log-link and theta-link distinction. coric/non-coric principle
IUT copies The framework prevents an uncontrolled collapse of alien copies into one ambient representation. set-theoretic copies
Formalization warning: a species-level assertion should not be encoded merely as an isomorphism between final objects. The relevant data includes the formulas defining the species, the mutation history, and the observable or core that authorizes the comparison.
Sources: IUT IV Related: IUT IV atlas log-theta lattice Corollary 3.12 corridor

IUT IV Remark 3.1.2: Programs, Not Output Data

Remark 3.1.2 is one of the basic guardrails for reading species. The species is not the particular set of specimens obtained in one ZFC-model. Its essence is the set-theoretic formula, or program, that constructs specimens in an unspecified model.

Formula-program

The source compares a species to software: the rules determine what is constructed, while the output sets depend on the model in which the rules are interpreted.

Model output

Objects and morphisms in a fixed ZFC-model are representations of the abstract species, just as matrices can represent an abstract group.

Do not quotient programs away

Identifying all programs with the same output would introduce internal program indeterminacy that the paper explicitly leaves outside its scope.

Remark itemSource contentWiki consequence
3.1.2(i) The mathematical content of a species lies in formulas governing construction in an indeterminate ZFC-model. Concept pages should record construction rules, not merely one specimen or final isomorphism class.
3.1.2(ii) The fixed-model/species distinction is compared to varieties/schemes and concrete matrix groups/abstract groups. When the wiki says "same species", it means same rule-governed type, not equality of representatives.
3.1.2(iii) Quotienting programs by equality of final output creates intractable internal-program indeterminacy. A formalization should avoid replacing species-level algorithms by bare output equivalence.
Source: IUT IV, Remark 3.1.2 Related: species and mutations choice-free canonicality LeanForm species/mutations

IUT IV Remark 3.1.3: Choice-Free Canonicality

Remark 3.1.3 explains how the paper intends to use conventional mathematical descriptions without writing every formula. A conventional description is admissible only when it is well-defined and does not depend on arbitrary choices that are not themselves formula-describable.

CheckAcceptedRejected or flagged
Conventional description A standard construction whose underlying set-theoretic formula is understood. A construction that silently chooses representatives without a formula-level rule.
Canonicality Formula-defined data has a canonical status across models and universe extensions. Objects selected by an invocation of choice in one model are not automatically inter-universal.
Audit role The wiki may summarize a standard construction, but should expose where canonicity is being used. A page that says "choose an isomorphism" must specify whether the choice is formula-governed or an indeterminacy.
This page is a local accuracy rule for the whole wiki. Mochizuki does not require every formula to be written out in IUT IV, but he does require that the conventional description determine a well-defined formula without hidden arbitrary choice.
Source: IUT IV, Remark 3.1.3 Related: programs not outputs choice explosion alien algorithms

IUT IV Remark 3.1.4: Immunity Under Universe Extension

Remark 3.1.4 gives the local meaning of "inter-universal" in IUT IV §3: because a species is formula-defined, the mathematical notion is unaffected by enlarging the universe in which the specimens are interpreted.

Same rule across larger universes Remark 3.1.4
Source requirementMeaningIUT use
Formula absoluteness Validity for data in one universe agrees after interpretation in a larger universe. Species-level data can be compared across universe extension.
Immunity under extension The mathematical notion constituted by the species is unaffected by ascending chains of universes. This supplies the formal foundation-language sense of inter-universal geometry.
Distinct geometries Formula-defined notions allow one to relate geometries occurring in distinct universes. Later alien-copy pages should distinguish this from ordinary scheme-theoretic gluing.
Source: IUT IV, Remark 3.1.4 Related: ZFCG universes model-free specimens set-theoretic copies

IUT IV Example 3.2 and Remark 3.2.1: Category Species

Example 3.2 supplies the first concrete species. Small categories are 0-specimens; isomorphism classes of functors are 1-specimens. Remark 3.2.1 explains why the paper does not develop a full hierarchy of 2-species, 3-species, and higher species for its purposes.

LevelSpecies readingReason for the convention
0-species The notion of a small category, represented set-theoretically by arrows and composition relations. This makes ordinary categories specimens of a formula-defined species.
1-species Isomorphism classes of covariant functors between small categories. Species-isomorphisms between 0-specimens correspond to equivalences of categories up to isomorphism.
Higher species One could introduce 2-specimens via functor isomorphisms and continue to n-species. The paper avoids this extra complexity by taking functors up to isomorphism at the 1-species level.
This convention matters downstream: when a page says a category-valued object is a species-level core, the morphism side is already quotienting functors by isomorphism in the sense of Example 3.2.
Source: IUT IV, Example 3.2 and Remark 3.2.1 Related: category species fundamental group mutation etale-site core

IUT IV Definition 3.3: Mutation Equivalence

Definition 3.3 does not stop at mutations and morphisms of mutations. It also defines mutation-equivalence: a mutation equipped with a quasi-inverse up to isomorphism of mutations. This is the species-theoretic form of reconstruction.

Mutation

A formula-defined transformation between species, functorial inside any fixed ZFC-model.

Isomorphism of mutations

A mutation morphism whose components are species-isomorphisms, equivalently invertible by another mutation morphism.

Mutation-equivalence

A mutation with a quasi-inverse so that both composites are isomorphic to the relevant identity mutations.

Definition 3.3 itemSource contentUse in the wiki
(ii) An isomorphism of mutations is a morphism of mutations whose objectwise components are species-isomorphisms. Natural-transformation-like data must be retained, not replaced by a bare equality.
(iii) A mutation-equivalence has a mutation quasi-inverse with composite isomorphisms to identity mutations. Absolute anabelian reconstruction pages should present reconstruction as algorithmic quasi-inverse data.
(iii) A mutation-equivalence induces bijections between species-isomorphisms of source objects and of their images. This is the exact bridge to the usual fully-faithful language, but with formula-defined algorithmic history retained.
Source: IUT IV, Definition 3.3(ii)-(iii) Related: absolute anabelian mutation mutation histories/cores cores and telecores

IUT IV Remark 3.3.1: Simple Species and Loops

Remark 3.3.1 explains why categories and group-like data are useful in a theory that wants to simulate loops through a membership hierarchy. The simpler the species, the easier it is to form loop-like comparisons without violating foundation.

Remark itemSource contentIUT role
3.3.1(i) Species-isomorphisms can compare objects at different membership levels, simulating membership loops without actual membership loops. Alien copies can be compared by type and reconstruction data without collapsing their histories.
3.3.1(ii) Loops are easier to construct for simpler species, such as categories, than for schemes. This motivates the geometry of categories and absolute anabelian geometry as a reconstruction medium.
3.3.1(iii) Cores record portions left unaltered by mutations and can be used to describe altered non-coric observables. This is the local species-theoretic origin of using coric containers for non-coric theta/log data.
The page separates two claims that are easy to conflate: species let one simulate loop-like comparison across membership levels, while cores explain how invariant portions can describe non-invariant portions along such mutation histories.
Source: IUT IV, Remark 3.3.1 Related: membership loops nested local systems coric/non-coric principle

IUT IV Example 3.6: Monoid Frobenius and p-Perfection

The monoid version of Example 3.6 is the closest species-theoretic toy model for the log/theta monoid phenomena later used in IUT. It replaces schemes by torsion-free abelian monoids, Frobenius by multiplication by p, and the invariant core by p-perfection.

Combinatorial Frobenius prototype Example 3.6(iii)
ObjectDefinition in Example 3.6(iii)Conceptual function
S_mon Species of torsion-free abelian monoids and monoid morphisms. Combinatorial/log-scheme analogue of the Frobenius scheme story.
M(p) = pM Submonoid obtained by multiplying elements by p. Defines the monoid-Frobenius mutation history.
Non-coric observable The inclusion M(p) -> M with identity observation mutations. Models altered data that is not invariant under the mutation.
p-perfection core The inductive limit along multiplication-by-p inclusions. Provides a coric object through which the non-coric observable is related.
Source: IUT IV, Example 3.6(iii) Related: Frobenius/etale mutation coric/non-coric principle theta monoid multiradiality

IUT IV Remark 3.6.2: Choice Explosion

Remark 3.6.2(iii) gives a concrete warning against replacing formula-defined algorithms by catalogues of arbitrary choices. Such catalogues grow by set-theoretic exponentiation and destroy the clean inter-universal calculability that species are meant to preserve.

ComparisonFormula-defined routeArbitrary-choice route
Set-theoretic size Use a canonical rule whose interpretation is stable under universe extension. Catalogue possible choices, producing large sets such as power sets of already large data.
p-adic analogy Canonical connections resemble canonical liftings in p-adic Teichmuller theory. Arbitrary integration resembles denominators with rapidly growing p-adic valuations.
IUT consequence Track the specific reconstruction or rigidity algorithm that controls component interrelations. Do not replace the algorithm by the set of all possible outcomes unless the source says that indeterminacy is intended.
This is not just a philosophical preference. In Remark 3.6.2 the choice-free algorithm is what allows an operation performed in one universe to be calculated from the viewpoint of an alien universe.
Source: IUT IV, Remark 3.6.2(iii) Related: choice-free canonicality alien algorithms cyclotomic rigidity

Topological Spaces and the Fundamental Group Mutation

IUT IV Example 3.4 is the elementary model for later species-theoretic reconstruction. It packages topological spaces, universal covers, and groups as species, then reads the deck-transformation group of a universal cover as a mutation.

Example 3.4 universal-cover species to group species
IUT IV itemPaper contentWiki/formalization consequence
Example 3.4(i) Topological spaces and continuous maps form a species. Universal covers of pathwise connected spaces form another species, with morphisms taken modulo deck transformations of the codomain cover. The quotient by deck transformations is part of the species-morphism interface, not an after-the-fact equality of selected maps.
Example 3.4(ii) Groups and outer homomorphisms form a species; morphisms are homomorphism classes modulo inner automorphisms of the codomain group. This is the group-side prototype for later absolute Galois group comparisons, where outer structure is the natural level.
Example 3.4(iii) The assignment from a universal cover to its deck-transformation group defines a mutation from the universal-cover species to the group species. The fundamental group is represented as a formula-defined transformation between species, setting up the anabelian example that follows.

Universal-cover species

A specimen contains a cover X~ -> X. Morphisms are compatible maps of covers, but they are identified after composition with a deck transformation on the target.

Outer group species

The group species records homomorphisms only up to inner automorphism. This matches the intrinsic ambiguity in recovering groups from covering theory without chosen basepoints.

Mutation reading

The mutation sends a cover to its deck group. This is intentionally more structured than saying that a particular fundamental group exists.

Source: IUT IV, Example 3.4 Related: species and mutations absolute anabelian mutation tempered fundamental group

Absolute Anabelian Geometry as a Mutation Quasi-Inverse

IUT IV Example 3.5 recasts absolute anabelian reconstruction as a pair of mutations. The fundamental-group mutation goes from a species of universal profinite etale covers of schemes to a species of topological groups; an anabelian reconstruction algorithm is a quasi-inverse mutation back to schemes.

Example 3.5 algorithmic reconstruction, not only full faithfulness
IUT IV itemPaper contentWiki/formalization consequence
Example 3.5(i) A class of connected normal schemes, closed under isomorphism and represented by a formula-describable set, gives a species of universal profinite etale coverings. The scheme side is not arbitrary: the class must have formula-describable representatives, so the reconstruction problem remains species-level.
Example 3.5(ii) A class of topological groups, closed under isomorphism and represented by a formula-describable set, gives a group species with bicontinuous outer isomorphisms. The group species is designed to receive fundamental groups without smuggling in basepoint choices.
Example 3.5(iii) The deck-group assignment defines a mutation Pi. Absolute anabelianity may be formulated by specifying a quasi-inverse mutation A. The paper emphasizes an algorithmic reconstruction mutation, not merely the existence of bijections between Hom-sets.
Example 3.5(iv) A profinite group considered only as a profinite group differs from the same group equipped with data arising from its construction as an etale fundamental group. This is a direct warning for the wiki and the Lean project: do not collapse construction history into a bare final group when the history is relevant.
Collas' categorical perspective is useful here: the key object is not just a global subspace of group elements, but the categorical data that records which labels, basepoints, and reconstruction algorithms authorize a comparison.
Source: IUT IV, Example 3.5 Related: Collas category view fundamental group mutation basepoints and inter-universality

Etale Sites, Perfections, and Frobenius Mutation Histories

IUT IV Example 3.6 supplies the motivating species-theoretic model for the later log-theta-lattice: Frobenius changes the underlying ring-theoretic structure, while the etale site and perfection provide coric observables.

Example 3.6 Frobenius mutation history and invariant observables
IUT IV itemPaper contentWiki/formalization consequence
Example 3.6(i) For a reduced scheme over F_p, the scheme S(p) has the same topological space and structure sheaf of p-th powers. The small etale site and the perfection are formula-defined mutations. The etale-site mutation lands in the category species, while the perfection mutation remains on the scheme species.
Example 3.6(ii) The Frobenius mutation history is generated by repeated S -> S(p). The etale-site mutation and perfection mutation are cores; the natural Frobenius morphism is a non-coric observable. This is the cleanest source for the wiki distinction between invariant data and data altered by a mutation.
Example 3.6(iii) The monoid version uses torsion-free abelian monoids, the submonoid pM, and p-perfection as a core of the monoid-Frobenius mutation history. The monoid model is the closest bridge from this example to the log-link and theta-link language later used in IUT III.
Source: IUT IV, Example 3.6 Related: coric/non-coric principle log-theta lattice theta and log links

Coric Descriptions of Non-Coric Data

Remarks 3.6.1-3.6.3 explain why the Frobenius example is not incidental. It is Mochizuki's model for the vertical and horizontal lines of the log-theta-lattice: altered, non-coric data must be described through formula-defined coric containers that remain meaningful from alien arithmetic holomorphic structures.

Etale-type versus Frobenius-type

Remark 3.6.1 reads invariance of etale sites and perfections under Frobenius as a species-theoretic distinction between coric observables and non-coric observables. Mochizuki explicitly points to this as the motivating pattern for both vertical log-links and horizontal theta-type links.

Formula-defined calculation across universes

Remark 3.6.2 says arbitrary choices inside one model do not let one calculate operations performed in an alien universe. The required descriptions must come from specific set-theoretic formulas or conventional descriptions that determine such formulas.

No ring-homomorphic bridge

Remark 3.6.3 says vertical and horizontal arrows of the log-theta-lattice are fundamentally unlike ring or scheme morphisms. The only natural formula-defined correspondence between local Galois groups across such arrows is an indeterminate isomorphism of topological groups.

Vertical versus horizontal distinction

For vertical arrows, the full arithmetic fundamental group is identified up to isomorphism. For horizontal arrows, the geometric groups attached to labels are kept distinct while local base-field Galois groups are identified up to isomorphism.

IUT IV itemPaper contentWiki/formalization consequence
Remark 3.6.1 Frobenius mutation histories are the underlying motivating examples for the vertical lines of log-links and the horizontal theta-type links of the log-theta-lattice. Pages on the log-theta-lattice should reference this species-theoretic prototype instead of presenting the lattice as isolated notation.
Remark 3.6.2 Calculations across alien universes require formula-defined algorithms. The paper explicitly says it will rely on conventional mathematical descriptions, with the reader responsible for checking well-definedness and choice-independence. This is a precise source for treating species data as audit obligations in our Lean work: the formulas may be implicit, but the obligation is real.
Remark 3.6.3(i) Galois groups are automorphism groups of rings or fields, but the log-theta-lattice arrows are not ring homomorphisms. Across such arrows, the natural correspondence is an indeterminate isomorphism of topological groups. This is the paper's local reason that ring/scheme history cannot simply be carried across the disputed comparison.
Remark 3.6.3(ii) Vertical arrows identify full arithmetic fundamental groups; horizontal arrows distinguish geometric groups attached to labels while identifying local base-field Galois groups up to isomorphism. The distinction is directly relevant to the Corollary 3.12 corridor, where label-dependent theta values and label-free data are compared.
For the Scholze-Stix/Mochizuki dispute, this page isolates one exact hinge: whether the comparison is allowed to pass through formula-defined coric containers after forgetting ring/scheme history, or whether that passage collapses the data to a trivial common object.
Source: IUT IV, Remarks 3.6.1-3.6.3 Related: Corollary 3.12 corridor log-theta lattice Collas category view

IUT IV Remark 3.6.1: Coric Descriptions of Non-Coric Data

Remark 3.6.1 says the Frobenius and monoid-Frobenius examples are not just analogies. They are the motivating examples for the vertical log-link lines and horizontal theta-type lines of the log-theta-lattice. The recurring pattern is to describe non-coric data through coric data that remains meaningful across mutation histories.

Source itemContentDownstream target
Remark 3.6.1(i) Etale-site and perfection invariance under Frobenius are read as coric observables; the Frobenius morphism itself is non-coric. Model for Frobenius-like versus etale-like structure in IUT.
Vertical lines Log-links in the log-theta-lattice are compared to the vertical Frobenius mutation-history pattern. IUT III log-link pages
Horizontal lines The theta-type links are also read through the coric/non-coric observable distinction. theta-link pages
Log-Kummer correspondences Vertically coric log-shells serve as a common receptacle for arrows of the log-Kummer correspondences in IUT III Theorem 3.11(ii). Theorem 3.11 log-Kummer route
Multiradial representations Theorem 3.11(i) describes aspects of one vertical line's arithmetic holomorphic structure from the viewpoint of another alien vertical line. Theorem 3.11 multiradial route
Formalization consequence: a theorem claiming a non-coric datum is described by coric data must expose both sides. A single proposition saying "the data is invariant" would erase the distinction made in the source.
Source: IUT IV, Remark 3.6.1 Related: Frobenius/etale mutation bi-cores Corollary 3.12 corridor

IUT IV Remark 3.6.2: Algorithms Across Alien Universes

Remark 3.6.2 states a key methodological rule: arbitrary choices made inside one set-theoretic model do not allow calculations in an alien universe. The required descriptions must be given by set-theoretic formulas, or by conventional mathematical descriptions that determine such formulas without hidden arbitrary choices.

Alien operation

Vertical and horizontal arrows have distinct scheme-theoretic basepoints corresponding to distinct ring theories.

Formula-defined algorithm

A specific algorithm, not merely the same final output data, lets one track interrelations among components during mutations.

Cyclotomic rigidity

Mochizuki names cyclotomic rigidity isomorphisms behind Theta x mu_LGP-link compatibility as a central example.

Remark itemPaper contentAudit consequence
3.6.2(i) Descriptions depending on arbitrary choices in one model do not support calculation of operations performed in an alien universe. Wiki and Lean pages should record formula/canonicity obligations for cross-universe comparison.
3.6.2(i) Different algorithms may have the same output, but specific algorithms reveal interrelations between components under mutations. A record of only final isomorphic output is too weak for IUT's claimed comparisons.
3.6.2(ii) The paper says formulas will usually remain implicit, supplied by conventional descriptions, but readers must check well-definedness and absence of arbitrary choices. This is an explicit source for audit pages: implicit formulas are obligations, not optional commentary.
3.6.2(iii) Cataloguing arbitrary choices leads to large unwieldy sets; this is compared with noncanonical p-adic integration versus canonical connections. Choice explosion is treated as a mathematical obstruction to clean inter-universal calculation.
Source: IUT IV, Remark 3.6.2 Related: model-free specimens cyclotomic rigidity LeanForm species/mutations

IUT IV Remark 3.6.3: Ring Incompatibility

Remark 3.6.3 gives a direct reason for inter-universality: etale fundamental groups are automorphism groups of field or ring structures, while the vertical and horizontal arrows of the log-theta-lattice do not arise from ring homomorphisms. Therefore ordinary functoriality of etale fundamental groups along ring or scheme morphisms is not available.

ComparisonRing/scheme morphism caseLog-theta arrow case
Underlying operation Compatible with ring structures on domain and codomain. Not compatible with a single ring-structure history.
Etale fundamental group Functorial morphisms arise tautologically from ring/scheme morphisms. No such functorial bridge is available.
Local base-field Galois groups Compared through the induced morphism when present. The natural formula-defined correspondence is an indeterminate isomorphism of topological groups.
Geometric fundamental groups Can be transported when the geometric data is functorially tied to the morphism. For vertical arrows, only an indeterminate topological-group isomorphism with outer action is natural; horizontal arrows behave differently.
This is one of the wiki's central guardrails for the Corollary 3.12 dispute: the source explicitly denies that the log-theta-lattice arrows are ordinary ring-homomorphic transports.
Source: IUT IV, Remark 3.6.3(i) Related: theta/log links Scholze-Stix critique Mochizuki response

IUT IV Remark 3.6.3: Vertical and Horizontal Galois Data

Remark 3.6.3(ii) distinguishes vertical and horizontal arrows. Vertical arrows identify full local arithmetic fundamental groups up to isomorphism. Horizontal arrows identify local base-field Galois groups up to isomorphism but keep the geometric groups, hence label data, distinct.

Vertical versus horizontal arrow data Remark 3.6.3(ii): what is identified, what remains distinct
MarkerSource contentWhy it matters
(V1) Across a vertical arrow, isomorphs of the full arithmetic fundamental group Pi_v are identified up to isomorphism. Vertical log-link construction needs the full local ring structure recoverable from Pi_v.
(H1) Across a horizontal arrow, the Delta_v's are distinguished; only local base-field Galois groups G_v are identified up to isomorphism. Horizontal theta comparisons must preserve the label-dependent geometric data.
(V2) The p-adic logarithm at a place requires local ring structures, reconstructed from full Pi_v, not merely G_v stripped from its quotient context. This explains the vertical use of full arithmetic fundamental groups.
(H2) The theta correspondence compares label-dependent values q^{j^2} with label-free q; such an isomorphism is possible only if labels remain distinct. This is directly relevant to the Corollary 3.12 route through theta labels and q-pilot data.
Source: IUT IV, Remark 3.6.3(ii) Related: theta values q^j^2 label-crushing warning Corollary 3.12 membership step

Essential Logical Structure

Mochizuki's 2024 logical-structure report presents IUT as a chain of simultaneous theta-link relations together with controlled enlargements by indeterminacies. The source slogan is not that all copies become one copy, but that one maintains an AND relation while replacing one side by larger collections of possibilities.

Boolean skeleton theta-link AND plus indeterminacy XOR

AND/XOR chain

Section 3.10 summarizes the proof architecture as a chain A ∧ (B1 ⊻ B2 ...) enlarged by later possibility terms. The AND terms correspond to simultaneous theta-link execution; the XOR terms correspond to alternatives produced by indeterminacy and projection/subquotient operations.

§3.10∧/⊻

Redundant-copy warning

The report's central warning is that arbitrarily identifying copies of Frobenius-like, etale-like, log-linked, or theta-linked objects changes the logical form of IUT. Such identifications can make coricity look trivial while erasing the labels that make the comparison meaningful.

§3.1-3.4labels

Boolean carry analogy

Mochizuki compares the AND/XOR pattern to carry addition for truncated Witt vectors: multiplication-like common structure and addition-like non-common structure are intertwined. The analogy is explanatory; the wiki treats it as a guide to the logical shape, not as a substitute for the source constructions.

IntroductionWitt-vector analogy
Source labelMeaning in the reportWiki consequence
RC-FrEt Claim that Frobenius-like and etale-like versions are redundant. Keep Frobenius-like labels and etale-like coricity as distinct entries.
RC-log Claim that Hodge theaters on the two sides of a log-link may be identified. Represent log-links as structural moves, not as erasure of the source theater.
RC-Theta Claim that the two sides of the theta-link may be identified. Do not collapse theta-pilot and q-pilot labels before the licensed descent route.
ORAch A diagram-commutativity reading that tries to put both sides in one external container too early. Use the container chain below instead of a one-shot commutative square.
Stp1-Stp8 route descent, hulls, determinant, log-shift, log-volume

Stp1-Stp8

Section 3.10 decomposes the route into eight steps: log-Kummer/Galois evaluation, descent to mono-analytic etale-like data, descent to mono-analytic Frobenius-like data, holomorphic hull, hull-approximants, determinant, log-shift adjustment, and log-volume.

§3.10route map

Log-Kummer centrality

Section 3.11 emphasizes that Stp1 is the technically heavy part: it uses log-Kummer correspondence and Galois evaluation to place the theta-pilot in a collection of possibilities. Ind1 and Ind2 are described as subordinate to the Ind3/log-Kummer stabilization.

§3.11Ind3

Common-container chain

Instead of a single commutative square into a container, the report uses a chain in which the theta-pilot side is embedded into larger containers until the q-pilot side is engulfed. This is compared to using sufficiently large thickenings in the theory of crystals.

§3.10containers

Label reconstruction

The reconstruction algorithms do not recover labels such as (0,0) by literal set-theoretic equality. The point is instead that the forgetting operation can be inverted up to suitable indeterminacies and that only the weakened prime-strip data is glued to the other column.

§3.11labels
Formalization warning: a Lean interface for this page should not replace the chain of labeled descents by a single equality in a fixed ambient type. The source claim is about preserving a logical AND relation through specified enlargements, descents, and log-volume passage.
Sources: Essential Logical Structure Essential Logical Structure I-V IUT III Mutually Alien Copies Related: log-theta lattice indeterminacies multiradiality Corollary 3.12 corridor RIMS Lean effort

Essential Logic 2.1: Limits and Integration

Section 2.1 uses the history of limits and integration to separate productive mathematical criticism from false contradictions caused by ignoring the intended formalism. The model prepares the reader for later claims that identifying symbols or copies outside their source context can manufacture contradictions.

ModelSource pointIUT reading
Grandi/integration history Real paradoxes led to refined definitions because the criticized claims were understood precisely. Disagreement must target the exact formal source route, not an invented simplification.
Example 2.1.1 Substituting arbitrary positive real numbers for integral notation creates a fake contradiction. Do not treat IUT labels, infinitesimals, or links as free variables detached from their definitions.
Source: Essential Logical Structure §2.1 Related: two-dimensionality Scholze-Stix critique page

Essential Logic 2.2: Derivative/Integral Non-Redundancy

Section 2.2 argues that even when two kinds of data are interconvertible in one sense, one of them may have structural properties that the other lacks. The derivative example models why Frobenius-like and etale-like versions are not automatically redundant.

ExampleSource pointIUT reading
Derivative symmetry A function may lack a symmetry that its derivative has. Etale-like symmetry/coricity can be meaningful even if the data is related to Frobenius-like data.
Discrete variation bound One bounds |f(n)-f(0)| by accumulating allowed variations, not by identifying endpoints. Indeterminacy enlarges containers; it is not endpoint equality.
Source: Essential Logical Structure §2.2 Related: Frobenius/etale copies gluings and indeterminacies

Essential Logic 2.3: Segments and Loops

Section 2.3 gives a topological model for why isomorphic pieces are not automatically redundant. Identifying endpoints of an interval changes the space into a loop; identifying adjacent copies of an interval changes the fundamental group and the orientation structure.

ConstructionSource pointIUT reading
Interval endpoints The endpoints are isomorphic one-point spaces, but identifying them changes an interval into a circle. Isomorphic local labels may carry non-redundant global position.
Adjacent intervals Gluing two copies into a line segment chain differs from identifying the two copies. Theta/log-linked copies should be glued as the source prescribes, not quotient-collapsed first.
Source: Essential Logical Structure §2.3 Related: theta-link copies log-link copies

Essential Logic 2.4: AND Versus OR

Section 2.4 reformulates the line-segment examples in Boolean language. The central phenomenon is that identifying redundant-looking copies can turn a valid AND relation into an OR relation while destroying the original AND relation.

Copy identification changes logical form from adjacent segments to one loop-like quotient
FormulaSource meaningIUT reading
AOL1 The glued point is simultaneously the endpoint of the left copy and the endpoint of the right copy. Models a source AND relation across distinct labeled copies.
AOL2/AOL3 After identifying copies, the point is one endpoint or the other. Copy identification creates an OR-style proposition.
AOL4 The original AND statement no longer holds after the identification. This is the elementary model for why RCS-style collapse is not harmless.
Source: Essential Logical Structure §2.4 Related: AND chain AND/XOR chain redundant-copy warning

Essential Logic 3.1: Two Dimensions

Section 3.1 sets the basic logical target. IUT studies the variable relation between two combinatorial/arithmetic dimensions of a ring, represented in the local picture by unit-group and value-group directions. The redundant-copies criticism is formulated here as the move that crushes these two dimensions into one fixed relation.

Source itemPaper contentWiki/formalization consequence
2-Dim The theta-intertwining on F⊢×μ-prime-strips is to be described in terms of the q-intertwining through log-links and Kummer theory. Keep q-pilot and theta-pilot histories as linked but non-identical data.
1-Dim A fixed one-dimensional identification of the two directions produces a superficial contradiction by changing the problem before IUT begins. Do not encode the target corridor as equality in one ambient real line.
Example 3.1.1 Gluing two labeled copies is harmless; identifying the copies first can force unwanted equalities such as x = y. Labels are not cosmetic. They prevent source histories from being erased too early.
Source: Essential Logical Structure §3.1 Related: logical overview theta/log links F×μ Kummer prime-strips

Essential Logic 3.2: Frobenius/Etale Copies

Section 3.2 explains why Frobenius-like and etale-like versions of objects cannot be treated as redundant copies. Etale-like objects have horizontal and vertical coricity and symmetry properties obtained from abstract fundamental groups; Frobenius-like objects depend on their Hodge-theater coordinates.

DistinctionSource roleDownstream use
Frobenius-like Depends on the coordinate (n,m) in the log-theta lattice. Supplies the holomorphic/ring-theoretic side whose history cannot be freely transported.
Etale-like Reconstructed from arithmetic fundamental groups treated as abstract topological groups. Provides coric/symmetric data used by multiradial algorithms.
RC-FrEt The redundant-copies move makes Frobenius-like data appear artificially coric. Formalization should expose whether coricity is proved or assumed by identification.
3.12 relevance. Theorem 3.11 relies on etale-like coricity/symmetry while retaining Frobenius-like source information. A model that makes Frobenius-like data coric by identification has changed the source situation.
Source: Essential Logical Structure §3.2 Related: Frobenius-picture chain vertical coricity Theorem 3.11 algorithms

Essential Logic 3.3: Log-Link Copies

Section 3.3 analyzes the claim that the domain and codomain of a log-link are redundant. The source point is that a log-link rotates or juggles unit and value directions, so identifying its two sides destroys the local splitting needed to define the neighboring theta-links.

Why RC-log is not neutral the theta-link requires splittings that the log-link moves
ClaimSource contentFormalization consequence
RC-log Identifying the two sides of a log-link makes local unit/value splittings ill-defined. Keep log-link domain and codomain as distinct theaters with explicit transport.
Ind3 The failure of the log-Kummer diagram to commute is controlled by upper-semi commutativity. Model Ind3 as one-sided comparison data, not equality.
Source: Essential Logical Structure §3.3 Related: local log-link mechanism upper-semi log-Kummer log-wall incompatibility

Essential Logic 3.4: Theta-Link Copies

Section 3.4 turns to the theta-link itself. The theta-link glues the theta-pilot side to the q-pilot side through a full poly-isomorphism of F⊢×μ-prime-strips. This does not identify the surrounding ring theories or erase the difference between q_v and the collection q_v^{j^2}.

ObjectSource roleGuardrail
q-pilot Line-bundle data determined by the root of the q-parameter at bad places. Keep fixed on the q side when the theta side is enlarged.
Theta-pilot Collection of line-bundle data determined by q_v^{j^2}. Do not replace the collection by the single q-pilot value.
Full poly-isomorphism The gluing is the set of all isomorphisms of the abstract prime-strip data. Represent the theta-link as relation-like poly-data, not a chosen map.
Source: Essential Logical Structure §3.4 Related: IUT I theta links F×μ to theta links square-weight exponent

Essential Logic 3.5: Gluings and Indeterminacies

Section 3.5 explains the apparent discrepancy between the q-pilot and theta-pilot. IUT does not assert equal log-volumes at the start. Instead, it uses the multiradial representation, indeterminacies, and the holomorphic hull of possible theta-pilot images before taking the final log-volume comparison.

Source labelMeaning3.12 consequence
ThetaGl Both sides of the theta-link gluing can be treated in one ring theory after the multiradial/hull passage. The common setting is an output of the route, not an initial equality.
NonInf The q-pilot Kummer theory is held fixed while the theta-pilot side ranges over allowed possibilities. Encodes simultaneous executability, not collapse.
CrAND / CrOR / CrRCS The crystal analogy separates a meaningful AND relation from vacuous OR or diagonal-only collapse. Use containers/thickenings as a model for why naive commutative squares are insufficient.
Source: Essential Logical Structure §3.5 Related: hull/determinant/log-volume step common-container chain Corollary 3.12

Essential Logic 3.6: Chain of AND Relations

Section 3.6 says the proof architecture is not a chain of intermediate inequalities. The bulk of IUT I-III preserves the AND relation that begins at the theta-link and reaches the multiradial representation; after that, the passage to Corollary 3.12 is described as relatively formal.

Symptom/structureSource pointWiki consequence
Syp1 The 3.11 to 3.12 passage can feel abrupt if the preceding AND chain is missed. Document all upstream AND-preserving transitions.
Syp2 One should not expect a false equality of log-volumes across the theta-link. Do not formalize the corridor as a commutative log-volume square.
Syp3 The final estimate is not obtained by concatenating ordinary inequalities. Separate logical relation preservation from final ordered-real algebra.
Source: Essential Logical Structure §3.6 Related: Theorem 3.11 Corollary 3.12 setup IPL/SHE/APT source

Essential Logic 3.7: Poly-Morphisms

Section 3.7 explains poly-morphisms as explicit enumerations of possibilities. A full poly-isomorphism such as the theta-link can be read as an AND relation together with an OR/XOR family of possible gluing isomorphisms.

FeatureSource roleFormalization consequence
Poly-morphism A set of morphisms between objects, composed by composing all compatible members. Use relation/set-valued interfaces where the source uses poly-data.
Possibility enumeration Poly-morphisms encode OR/XOR alternatives created by indeterminacy. Do not pick an arbitrary representative unless the source supplies a choice.
Stack analogy Gluing by poly-morphisms is compared with stack-like gluing rather than spaces. Keep isomorphism-class and quotient status explicit.
Source: Essential Logical Structure §3.7 Related: theta links poly-isomorphisms AND/XOR chain

Essential Logic 3.8: Inter-Universality

Section 3.8 places IUT's universe changes beside the familiar use of Galois categories in SGA 1. The novelty is not merely that distinct universes occur; it is that the relations between the relevant schemes are log- and theta-links, not morphisms of schemes or rings.

ComparisonSource pointConsequence
SGA 1 Distinct Galois categories are related by scheme/ring morphisms, with inner automorphism indeterminacy. Inner automorphism indeterminacy is normal, not a defect.
IUT Log- and theta-links are non-ring/scheme-theoretic relations between contexts. Specify which structures are coric/common across each relation.
Coric as AND Being common to distinct objects is read as a logical AND relation. Represent common structures separately from non-coric holomorphic histories.
Source: Essential Logical Structure §3.8 Related: Collas universes/species IUT IV species coric holomorphic log-shells

Essential Logic 3.9: Descent to Underlying Structures

Section 3.9 distinguishes output data treated independently from output data equipped with its input and construction algorithm. This is essential for q-/theta-intertwinings: the same apparent object may have different status depending on whether its source algorithm remains attached.

LabelMeaningUse
UdOut Output data treated as underlying structure, independently of source input. Permits descent only after the source route justifies it.
InOut Output data together with input data and the algorithm that produced it. Records source history needed to avoid accidental identification.
ItwOut q-/theta-intertwinings on F⊢×μ-prime-strips depend on how the prime-strip was constructed. Keep q-pilot and theta-pilot intertwinings as source-dependent interfaces.
Source: Essential Logical Structure §3.9 Related: label reconstruction F×μ pilot object Remark 3.12.2 intertwining

Essential Logic 3.10: AND/XOR Chain

Section 3.10 gives the detailed Boolean form of the proof route. AND corresponds to simultaneous execution/gluing; OR, and under disjointness XOR, corresponds to indeterminacy, poly-morphism, projection, subquotient, and splitting alternatives.

Boolean componentIUT readingRoute consequence
AND Common multiplicative structure in the theta-link and simultaneous execution. Preserve both sides of the source relation.
OR/XOR Alternatives created mainly by log-Kummer indeterminacies and quotient/splitting operations. Represent possibility containers explicitly.
Stp1-Stp8 Log-Kummer/Galois evaluation, descents, hulls, determinant, log-shift loop, log-volume. Do not compress the proof route into a one-shot numerical inequality.
Closed-loop point. The source says set-theoretic membership in a set of possibilities is obtained only after the closed-loop/log-volume passage. Before that, possibility is stack-like or quotient-like, not naive set containment.
Source: Essential Logical Structure §3.10 Related: Stp1-Stp8 membership step (xi-f) hull/determinant/log-volume

Essential Logic 3.11: Log-Kummer Centrality

Section 3.11 identifies the log-Kummer correspondence and Galois evaluation as the technically heavy core. Steps 2 through 8 are described as comparatively elementary; Step 1 is where IUT uses the anabelian/log-Kummer machinery that makes the theta-pilot multiradial output possible.

Source itemContent3.12 consequence
Stp1 Log-Kummer correspondence and Galois evaluation occupy the bulk of IUT I-III. This is the main source-obligation target before the final ordered-real algebra.
Ind3 > Ind1+2 Once local tensor-packet output is stable under Ind3, Ind1 and Ind2 stability is essentially subordinate. Do not treat all indeterminacies as equal labels; Ind3 is the central one-sided mechanism.
logORInd The theta-pilot is saturated by possibilities from the 0-column log-link sequence. This saturation is potent, unlike theta-domain/codomain confusion.
Source: Essential Logical Structure §3.11 Related: Log-Kummer correspondences Ind1/Ind2/Ind3 3.11 coarse space step (x)

Galois-Orbit IUT Preview

The 2025 Anabelian Gateway notes briefly preview a Galois-orbit version of IUT. Mochizuki presents it as part of new enhanced versions related to ongoing work on the Section Conjecture and Grothendieck-Teichmuller theory. The wiki records the source claim and dependency shape, while marking the page as preview material rather than established core IUT I-IV infrastructure.

One height-bounding theme, three application directions Gateway 2025 Section 4

Status in the source

Gateway 2025 explicitly presents this as a brief preview of new enhanced versions of IUT, closely related to manuscripts in preparation and recent progress. The wiki therefore records it as current source context, not as a completed theorem page.

Gateway 2025 §4preview

Section-conjecture route

The source states that local geometricity at nonarchimedean primes plus three global conditions should reduce geometricity of Galois sections in the indicated hyperbolic-curve setting. Galois-orbit IUT is said to imply the intersection-finiteness condition among these global conditions.

SCintersection-finiteness

Siegel-zero application

Mochizuki states that GalOrbIUT implies nonexistence of Siegel zeroes for Dirichlet L-functions associated to imaginary quadratic number fields, via work of Colmez, Granville-Stark, and Tafula.

analytic number theorysource claim

Stronger abc/Szpiro preview

The same page lists a numerically stronger version of abc/Szpiro inequalities as another consequence. This should be kept distinct from the published IUT IV route until the enhanced version has a precise source text available for formalization.

abc/Szpiroenhanced route

Shared height theme

Mochizuki describes the three application directions as essentially the same mathematical phenomenon of bounding heights, or local denominators. This is why the page belongs near IUT's log-volume and height-estimate material even though it is not part of the four-paper proof spine.

height boundslocal denominators

Wiki and Lean obligation

A future formal page would need exact statements of GalOrbIUT, the intersection-finiteness condition, and the analytic-number-theory bridge. Until then, Lean work should treat this as an external roadmap, not as a verified dependency for Corollary 3.12.

formalizationnot core dependency
Sources: Anabelian Gateway 2025 Mutually Alien Copies Related: IUT IV output route log-theta lattice Collas category view formalization

Galois-Theoretic Context

IUT I Introduction §I5 situates IUT among other Galois-theoretic approaches to Diophantine geometry. Mochizuki's comparison separates the historical Grothendieck section-conjecture route, representation-theoretic developments of p-adic Teichmuller theory, and IUT's use of absolute anabelian geometry to control asymptotic Diophantine estimates.

Three Galois-theoretic routes IUT I §I5: historical positioning, not a proof substitute

Grothendieck section route

Mochizuki recalls Grothendieck's anabelian motivation for Diophantine geometry: use sections of arithmetic fundamental groups to control rational points. IUT is presented as related in spirit but not the same strategy.

IUT I §I5historical route

IUT anabelian route

The four-paper route applies absolute anabelian geometry to compare structures inside Hodge theaters and eventually obtain Diophantine inequalities. The paper stresses the role of p-adic absolute anabelian results rather than only number-field section conjectures.

IUT I §I5IUT route

p-adic absolute/relative gap

Mochizuki identifies a fundamental gap between relative and absolute anabelian geometry over p-adic fields as a key difference from the historical number-field section-conjecture viewpoint. This gap is tied to arithmetic Teichmuller deformation of the number field itself.

IUT I §I5p-adic anabelian

Addition versus multiplication

The relationship between additive and multiplicative structure is described as central both in anabelian proofs and in the Diophantine estimates of IUT IV. This connects the historical discussion back to the log-theta lattice's two combinatorial dimensions.

IUT I §I5+ / x

Anabelian versus representation route

IUT I contrasts an anabelian development of p-adic Teichmuller theory with representation-theoretic generalizations into groups such as SL_n(Z_p). The source argues that representation-theoretic targets cannot access the same asymptotic phenomena as anabelian fundamental groups.

IUT I §I5route split

Asymptotic output

The Diophantine results derived in IUT IV concern asymptotic behavior of varying rational points over varying number fields. This is different from approaches centered on a specific rational point over a distinguished number field.

IUT I §I5IUT IV output

Finite-extension invariance

Because the output is asymptotic, Mochizuki says no distinguished number field occurs in the theory; the framework is invariant under passing to finite extensions. This source claim belongs with the IUT IV height-output pages.

IUT I §I5extensions

Formalization boundary

This page records motivation and positioning. It should not be encoded as a proof dependency for Corollary 3.12, except where it points to precise constructions already represented elsewhere: anabelian reconstruction, addition/multiplication separation, and asymptotic height output.

wiki scopecontext
Sources: IUT I Related: IUT I Teichmuller analogy anabelian background Galois-orbit IUT preview IUT IV output route log-theta lattice

Current Situation Reports

Mochizuki's 2023 and 2025 situation reports are context documents, not new source theorems in IUT I-IV. They are still useful for this wiki because they state how Mochizuki frames mathematical accountability, set-theoretic copy issues, recent anabelian developments, and Lean formalization as the next communication layer for IUT.

Situation-report context accountability, copies, anabelian context, and Lean
SourceMain contentWiki treatment
Brief report, August 2023 The 2023 report describes IUT as published, surveyed, and discussed in workshops, then argues that public objections should be stated as precise mathematical claims with complete written details. This is recorded as Mochizuki's accountability framing. It is not itself evidence for a source theorem, but it explains why the wiki separates formal claims from social summaries.
